title: An Evaluation of Decentralized Group Formation Techniques for Flying Light Specks
comment: Appeared in ACM Multimedia Asia 2023 (MMAsia '23), December 06-08, 2023, Tainan, Taiwan. ACM, New York, NY, USA, 7 pages
summary: Group formation is fundamental for 3D displays that use Flying Light Specks, FLSs, to illuminate shapes and provide haptic interactions. An FLS is a drone with light sources that illuminates a shape. Groups of G FLSs may implement reliability techniques to tolerate FLS failures, provide kinesthetic haptic feedback in response to a user's touch, and facilitate a divide and conquer approach to challenges such as localizing FLSs to render a shape. This paper evaluates four decentralized techniques to form groups. An FLS implements a technique autonomously using asynchronous communication and without a global clock. We evaluate these techniques using synthetic point clouds with known optimal solutions and real point clouds. Obtained results show a technique named Random Subset (RS) is superior when constructing small groups (G $\leq$ 5) while a different technique named Closest Available Neighbor First (CANF) is superior when constructing large groups (G $\geq$ 10).

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates four decentralized group formation techniques for Flying Light Specks (FLSs) in 3D displays, finding that Random Subset (RS) works best for small groups (G ≤ 5) while Closest Available Neighbor First (CANF) is superior for large groups (G ≥ 10).
AI.motivation: Group formation is fundamental for 3D displays using Flying Light Specks (FLSs) to illuminate shapes and provide haptic interactions. Groups enable reliability techniques to tolerate failures, provide kinesthetic haptic feedback, and facilitate divide-and-conquer approaches to challenges like localization.
AI.method: The paper evaluates four decentralized group formation techniques where FLSs operate autonomously using asynchronous communication without a global clock. Evaluation uses synthetic point clouds with known optimal solutions and real point clouds.
AI.result: Random Subset (RS) is superior for small groups (G ≤ 5), while Closest Available Neighbor First (CANF) is superior for large groups (G ≥ 10).
AI.conclusion: Different decentralized group formation techniques are optimal depending on group size: RS for small groups and CANF for large groups.

title: WQ-Fusion: Dynamic Gated Attention for Cross-Domain Audio Representation
summary: While pre-trained models excel in specialized tasks, learning universal representations across diverse acoustic domains remains challenging. To address this, we propose WQ-Fusion, a robust dual-encoder framework for cross-domain audio representation learning. Overcoming the limitations of static concatenation, WQ-Fusion integrates whisper and qwen via an Adaptive Feature Modulation module and a novel element-wise gated attention mechanism. This design enables dynamic feature selection, allowing the model to selectively emphasize relevant acoustic and semantic dimensions. Extensive experiments on the Interspeech 2026 Audio Encoder Capability Challenge (Track A) benchmark demonstrate that by effectively routing heterogeneous information, WQ-Fusion achieves a superior overall score of 0.836, significantly outperforming the strongest single-encoder baseline.

AI.tldr: WQ-Fusion is a dual-encoder framework combining Whisper and Qwen via Adaptive Feature Modulation and element-wise gated attention for cross-domain audio representation learning, achieving a score of 0.836 on the Interspeech 2026 benchmark, outperforming single-encoder baselines.
AI.motivation: Pre-trained models struggle to learn universal representations across diverse acoustic domains; static concatenation of heterogeneous features is insufficient.
AI.method: A dual-encoder framework integrating Whisper and Qwen with an Adaptive Feature Modulation module and a novel element-wise gated attention mechanism for dynamic feature selection.
AI.result: Achieved an overall score of 0.836 on the Interspeech 2026 Audio Encoder Capability Challenge (Track A) benchmark, significantly outperforming the strongest single-encoder baseline.
AI.conclusion: Effective routing of heterogeneous information through dynamic feature selection enables superior cross-domain audio representation learning.

title: NaviCache: Test-Time Self-Calibration Caching for Video Generation
comment: Published at ICML 2026: Proceedings of the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning, Seoul, South Korea. PMLR 306, 2026
summary: Video Diffusion Models (VDMs) is constrained by immense computational costs. While offline calibration-based acceleration suffers from calibration data dependency, prohibitive calibration duration, and susceptibility to distribution shifts, offline calibration-free methods eliminate these hurdles. However, since they rely on instantaneous zero-order approximations where the mapping between input and output differences varies in real-time, they are susceptible to observational noise and ignore the intrinsic momentum within the diffusion trajectory. In this paper, we propose NaviCache, a plug-and-play test-time self-calibration method re-conceptualizing feature evolution as an Inertial Navigation System (INS) problem. NaviCache bridges the fundamental domain gap and the non-stationary nature of diffusion by modeling the relative coupling between input and output variations. We introduce a dual-state estimation architecture that adaptively tracks the feature change ratio and its latent drift, initialized via a specialized Initial Alignment phase. By integrating a time-dependent noise schedule with an uncertainty-aware Measurement Update mechanism, NaviCache provides a theoretically grounded mechanism for error-bounded computation skipping. Extensive experiments on the HunyuanVideo, Wan, and Open-Sora series demonstrate that NaviCache exhibits more accurate error judgment for computation skipping and achieves outstanding comprehensive performance.

AI.tldr: NaviCache is a plug-and-play test-time self-calibration method for accelerating Video Diffusion Models by re-conceptualizing feature evolution as an Inertial Navigation System problem, using dual-state estimation with uncertainty-aware measurement updates to enable error-bounded computation skipping.
AI.motivation: Video Diffusion Models suffer from immense computational costs. Existing offline calibration-based acceleration methods have issues with calibration data dependency, long calibration duration, and susceptibility to distribution shifts. Offline calibration-free methods eliminate these hurdles but suffer from observational noise and ignore intrinsic momentum in diffusion trajectories due to reliance on instantaneous zero-order approximations.
AI.method: The method bridges the domain gap between input and output variations by modeling relative coupling through a dual-state estimation architecture that adaptively tracks feature change ratio and latent drift, initialized via an Initial Alignment phase. It integrates a time-dependent noise schedule with uncertainty-aware Measurement Update for error-bounded computation skipping.
AI.result: Experiments on HunyuanVideo, Wan, and Open-Sora series show NaviCache exhibits more accurate error judgment for computation skipping and achieves outstanding comprehensive performance.
AI.conclusion: NaviCache provides a theoretically grounded mechanism for error-bounded computation skipping in Video Diffusion Models, outperforming existing methods across multiple video generation model series.

title: HiPR: Hierarchical Progressive Rendering for Immediate Feedback
summary: Hierarchical Progressive Rendering (HiPR) is a dynamic render-scheduling algorithm that makes interactive path tracing finally feel real-time. While most renderers recompute the entire frame after any change to the scene, our method updates some of the pixels based on a priority order while keeping the others unchanged. Rather than relying on error-driven or temporal reuse heuristics, it amortizes rendering costs by organizing pixels into a hierarchy of light-path dependencies from changed elements outward, prioritizing by perceptual impact and delivering instant visual feedback, while eventually converging to an unbiased result.

AI.tldr: HiPR is a dynamic render-scheduling algorithm that enables interactive path tracing to feel real-time by prioritizing pixel updates based on perceptual impact through a hierarchy of light-path dependencies, converging to unbiased results without heuristics.
AI.motivation: Current renderers recompute the entire frame after any scene change, which prevents interactive path tracing from achieving real-time responsiveness.
AI.method: HiPR organizes pixels into a hierarchy of light-path dependencies from changed elements outward, updating pixels in priority order based on perceptual impact rather than recomputing the entire frame.
AI.result: The method delivers instant visual feedback while eventually converging to an unbiased result, without relying on error-driven or temporal reuse heuristics.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical progressive rendering makes interactive path tracing feel real-time by amortizing rendering costs through perceptual prioritization and hierarchical dependency tracking.

title: Appearance-Preserving Refinement of Generated 3D Assets for Monochromatic Fabrication
summary: Recent advances in 3D mesh generation have enabled the creation of visually realistic assets. However, much of their visual fidelity is encoded in textures rather than geometry. When such assets are fabricated using monochromatic materials, texture information is largely lost, causing visually important details to disappear even when the original geometry is faithfully preserved. A key challenge is that the geometric perturbations required to recover texture-dependent appearance cues often introduce sharp local features and high-frequency surface structures, which may increase stress concentration and fabrication risk. In this paper, we present GenMF, an appearance-oriented geometry refinement framework for monochromatic fabrication. GenMF transforms texture-dependent visual cues into geometry-induced shading effects and formulates geometry refinement as a balance between appearance preservation and fabrication-oriented robustness. To discourage structurally and narrow the gap between simulation and physical manufacturing, we further introduce a differentiable stress-aware regularization based on a learned thermal-stress predictor. Experimental results demonstrate that GenMF significantly improves appearance preservation under monochromatic rendering while reducing stress concentration under a consistent thermo-mechanical simulation setting. Physical 3D printing examples further show that the refined geometries preserve more recognizable visual details while remaining suitable for fabrication. These results suggest that appearance-aware geometry refinement provides an effective bridge between generated 3D assets and fabrication-ready monochromatic objects.

AI.tldr: GenMF is a geometry refinement framework that transforms texture-dependent visual cues into geometry-induced shading for monochromatic 3D printing, balancing appearance preservation with fabrication robustness via differentiable stress-aware regularization.
AI.motivation: Visually realistic 3D assets rely heavily on textures, which are lost in monochromatic fabrication. Even with faithful geometry preservation, texture-dependent details disappear, creating a gap between generated assets and fabrication-ready objects.
AI.method: GenMF formulates geometry refinement as balancing appearance preservation and fabrication robustness. It introduces a differentiable stress-aware regularization using a learned thermal-stress predictor to discourage fragile structures and bridge simulation-physical manufacturing gaps.
AI.result: GenMF significantly improves appearance preservation under monochromatic rendering while reducing stress concentration in thermo-mechanical simulations. Physical 3D printing examples show refined geometries preserve more recognizable visual details while remaining suitable for fabrication.
AI.conclusion: Appearance-aware geometry refinement effectively bridges generated 3D assets and fabrication-ready monochromatic objects, proving that geometric perturbations can recover texture-dependent cues without compromising fabrication feasibility.

title: Orchestrating Black-Box Schema Converters: An Empirical Study of Automated, Quality-Ranked Conversion Across Heterogeneous Schema Languages
summary: Modern software systems routinely need the same data model in several schema languages: a model may exist as JSON Schema for a web API, as XSD for data exchange, and as SHACL for a knowledge graph. Keeping these representations consistent as the model evolves is a recurring construction and maintenance burden, because converters between schema languages are hard to find, scattered across ecosystems, of uneven quality, and frequently lossy. We study, empirically, to what extent such imperfect, heterogeneous converters can be orchestrated into automated, reproducible, and quality-ranked conversions, and where the current converter landscape reaches its limits. Our approach models schema languages as nodes and converters, treated as black boxes, as directed edges, so that conversions become paths that are discovered, executed, ranked, and reported with full per-step provenance, with failures handled by trying alternatives. We realize it as the open-source Schema Conversion Orchestrator, integrate it into MetaConfigurator, and evaluate it on 60 conversion tasks built from real-world schemas across five schema languages, using agent-assisted, human-reviewed quality annotations. Orchestration surfaces a usable result for 43 of 60 tasks; the remaining failures localize concrete gaps in the converter landscape. We discuss implications for tool builders and for measuring conversion quality.

AI.tldr: An open-source Schema Conversion Orchestrator that automates, ranks, and provides provenance for conversions between different schema languages (JSON Schema, XSD, SHACL, etc.) using black-box converters, successfully handling 43/60 real-world tasks and identifying gaps in the converter landscape.
AI.motivation: Modern software systems need the same data model in multiple schema languages (JSON Schema, XSD, SHACL), but keeping these representations consistent is burdensome because converters are hard to find, scattered, of uneven quality, and frequently lossy.
AI.method: Models schema languages as nodes and converters as directed edges (black boxes), enabling path discovery, execution, ranking, and reporting with full per-step provenance. Implemented as open-source Schema Conversion Orchestrator, integrated into MetaConfigurator, evaluated on 60 conversion tasks across five schema languages with agent-assisted, human-reviewed quality annotations.
AI.result: Orchestration produced a usable result for 43 of 60 tasks; remaining 17 failures identified concrete gaps in the converter landscape.
AI.conclusion: Imperfect heterogeneous converters can be orchestrated into automated, reproducible, quality-ranked conversions, but the current converter landscape has limits; the findings have implications for tool builders and conversion quality measurement.

title: Physics-guided Convolutional Neural Network for Domain Growth Prediction in Systems with Conserved Kinetics
summary: The spatiotemporal evolution of many physical, chemical, and biological systems is described by nonlinear partial differential equations (PDEs). Recently, deep neural network-based surrogate models have gained increasing interest as efficient alternatives to computationally expensive traditional numerical solvers. In this work, we propose an attention-based, physics-guided convolutional neural network as a surrogate model to learn the microstructural evolution of such systems. We train the model to accurately predict the full time-evolution of phase separation in binary mixtures governed by the Cahn-Hilliard equation. We show that predictions from our trained surrogate model remain stable and accurate over long-time rollouts for both critical and off-critical mixtures and preserve the mixture composition throughout evolution. We also show that our model accurately captures the growth of domain size and is consistent with the Lifshitz-Slyozov domain-growth law. The prediction results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework for modeling systems with conserved kinetics and can be extended to other complex dynamical systems.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an attention-based, physics-guided convolutional neural network as a surrogate model to learn microstructural evolution governed by the Cahn-Hilliard equation. The model accurately predicts phase separation dynamics in binary mixtures over long time periods, preserving composition and following the Lifshitz-Slyozov growth law.
AI.motivation: Nonlinear PDEs describing spatiotemporal evolution in physical, chemical, and biological systems are computationally expensive to solve with traditional numerical methods, motivating the need for efficient deep learning-based surrogate models.
AI.method: An attention-based, physics-guided convolutional neural network (CNN) surrogate model trained to predict the full time-evolution of phase separation governed by the Cahn-Hilliard equation.
AI.result: The surrogate model produces stable and accurate long-time predictions for both critical and off-critical mixtures, preserves mixture composition throughout evolution, and accurately captures domain growth consistent with the Lifshitz-Slyozov law.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively models systems with conserved kinetics and can be extended to other complex dynamical systems.

title: Neural Speaker Diarization via Multilingual Training: Evaluation on Low-Resource Nepali-Hindi Speech
summary:
Speaker diarization, the task of determining "who spoke when" in a multi-speaker recording, is a critical component in applications such as meeting transcription, accessibility tools, and multilingual information retrieval. While end-to-end neural diarization systems have achieved strong performance for English and other high-resource languages, their effectiveness degrades substantially for underrepresented languages where annotated speech data is scarce.
  This paper investigates speaker diarization for low-resource Nepali-Hindi speech through a multilingual training approach, comparing two modern architectures: EEND with encoder-decoder attractors (EEND-EDA) and EEND with Perceiver-based attractors (DiaPer). Both models are trained on a multilingual corpus combining English speech from LibriSpeech, diverse speaker recordings from VoxCeleb, and separately collected Nepali and Hindi audio, a setup designed to reduce language bias and encourage cross-lingual generalization. We evaluate both models across 2-speaker, 3-speaker, 4-speaker, and mixed-speaker scenarios on LibriSpeech, VoxCeleb, and Nepali-Hindi (NeHi) test sets. DiaPer achieves stronger overall performance than EEND-EDA, particularly in more challenging multi-speaker conditions, obtaining DERs of 3.28%, 2.02%, 4.05%, and 4.76% on NeHi 2-speaker, 3-speaker, 4-speaker, and mixed-speaker settings, respectively, compared to 1.50%, 9.68%, 16.17%, and 11.19% for EEND-EDA. These results demonstrate the viability of Perceiver-based end-to-end neural diarization for low-resource multilingual speech processing.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates speaker diarization for low-resource Nepali-Hindi speech using multilingual training, comparing EEND-EDA and DiaPer (Perceiver-based) architectures. DiaPer outperforms EEND-EDA, particularly in challenging multi-speaker scenarios, achieving lower DERs on Nepali-Hindi test sets.
AI.motivation: Speaker diarization systems perform poorly for underrepresented languages due to scarce annotated speech data. The authors aim to address this gap by developing effective diarization for low-resource Nepali-Hindi speech through multilingual training.
AI.method: Two end-to-end neural diarization architectures (EEND-EDA and DiaPer) are trained on a multilingual corpus combining English (LibriSpeech, VoxCeleb) and separately collected Nepali-Hindi audio. Models are evaluated across 2/3/4-speaker and mixed-speaker scenarios on LibriSpeech, VoxCeleb, and Nepali-Hindi (NeHi) test sets.
AI.result: DiaPer achieves DERs of 3.28%, 2.02%, 4.05%, and 4.76% on NeHi 2/3/4-speaker and mixed settings, respectively, versus EEND-EDA's 1.50%, 9.68%, 16.17%, and 11.19%. DiaPer shows stronger overall performance, especially in challenging multi-speaker conditions.
AI.conclusion: Perceiver-based end-to-end neural diarization (DiaPer) is viable for low-resource multilingual speech processing, demonstrating superior cross-lingual generalization compared to EEND-EDA.

title: DocArena: Turning Raw Documents into Controllable Training Environments for Document Search Agents
summary: Recent methods train search agents via reinforcement learning from (question, answer, evidence) tuples without requiring expert trajectories. The tuples serve as the training environment, and whose properties directly shape what search strategies and generalization abilities the agent can develop. While prior works have made encouraging progress in improving training data quality, existing environments remain predominantly text-based and existing approaches can struggle to construct training environments that are controllable, scalable, and account for multimodal data. Given this, we propose DocArena, a fully automated data curation pipeline building on the practical need for multimodal document search and question-answering. It transforms raw document collections into training environments for search agents without any human annotation. The pipeline first structures and indexes documents through MLLM-based visual perception, then profiles and leverage the cross-page information distribution to construct reasoning-intensive QA pairs, as well as performs cascaded quality assurance operations via MLLM. We introduce DocArena-79K with QA pairs from 8,336 documents spanning 16 domains and 49 languages. We further design a Doc-Search agent infrastructure that decouples visual perception from the policy model, allowing text-based LLMs to serve as the reasoning backbone for multimodal document retrieval and QA. Under a unified evaluation framework where only the policy model differs, experiments on six multimodal document scenarios and seven text-based QA benchmarks show that agents trained on DocArena data achieve the best performance on both retrieval accuracy and QA quality. Further analysis on agent search behaviors confirms the effectiveness and controllability of the constructed training environment.

AI.tldr: DocArena automates the curation of multimodal document training environments for search agents via MLLM-based visual perception, cross-page reasoning QA construction, and cascaded quality assurance, achieving SOTA retrieval and QA performance across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing search agent training environments are predominantly text-based, lack controllability and scalability, and cannot handle multimodal data. The goal is to build a fully automated data curation pipeline that transforms raw document collections into training environments without human annotation.
AI.method: DocArena pipeline: (1) Structure and index documents via MLLM-based visual perception; (2) Profile cross-page information distribution to construct reasoning-intensive QA pairs; (3) Perform cascaded quality assurance via MLLM. Also proposes a Doc-Search agent that decouples visual perception from the policy model, enabling text-based LLMs to serve as the reasoning backbone for multimodal document retrieval and QA.
AI.result: DocArena-79K dataset with QA pairs from 8,336 documents across 16 domains and 49 languages. Experiments on six multimodal document scenarios and seven text-based QA benchmarks show agents trained on DocArena achieve best performance on both retrieval accuracy and QA quality.
AI.conclusion: The constructed training environment is effective and controllable for training search agents, and the decoupled architecture enables text-based LLMs to handle multimodal retrieval and QA tasks successfully.

title: Detecting and Controlling Sycophancy with Cascading Linear Features
summary: Interpreting and controlling model behaviors through activation steering methods requires many pairs of contrastive samples that clearly exhibit desired or undesired behavior. These data pairs determine the degree to which interpretability frameworks can reliably detect model features responsible for a behavior, and therefore the ability to steer models toward or away from such behavior. In this work, we present an iterative data generation pipeline that isolates cascading linear features responsible for a behavior. Specifically, we show how moving beyond simple binary pairs of samples, and instead isolating samples that show degrees of features that scale linearly with behavior, allows for better disentanglement of features. We focus on detecting and steering away from sycophancy -- the tendency of language models to prioritize user validation. We demonstrate that sycophancy features discovered through cascading samples form linearly separable subspaces, and allow for selection of model activations that more clearly correspond to the desired behavior than baseline approaches. We also evaluate their ability to enable detection, deterministic scoring, and robust steering, and see that they either match or outperform LLM-as-a-judge and system prompting baselines while providing lower computational demand and more interpretability guarantees. Code & Data: https://cascading-feats.github.io/

AI.tldr: An iterative data generation pipeline that uses cascading linear features (with graded contrastive samples) to better disentangle, detect, and steer model behaviors—demonstrated on sycophancy reduction—outperforming LLM-as-a-judge and prompting baselines with lower compute and higher interpretability.
AI.motivation: Existing activation steering methods rely on binary contrastive sample pairs, which often fail to fully disentangle the linear features responsible for a target behavior, limiting detection reliability and steering effectiveness.
AI.method: An iterative data generation pipeline that produces cascading samples exhibiting degrees of features that scale linearly with a behavior, enabling better isolation of linearly separable subspaces for that behavior. Applied to sycophancy in language models.
AI.result: Sycophancy features discovered via cascading samples form linearly separable subspaces; the approach matches or outperforms LLM-as-a-judge and system prompting baselines in detection, scoring, and steering while requiring lower computational cost and offering stronger interpretability guarantees.
AI.conclusion: Moving beyond binary contrastive pairs to graded, cascading samples improves feature disentanglement for interpretability and steering, with practical advantages in efficiency and transparency.

title: Neural Texture Compression using Hypernetworks
summary: Recent work on neural texture compression has demonstrated that it is possible to learn small, per-material texture representations (composed of latent textures and a small Multi-Layer Perceptron decoder) that can be decoded in real-time during shading to reproduce the input to a physically based shading model. However, existing methods require performing gradient-descent optimization per material for a given MLP and latent configuration. In this work, we train a single hypernetwork that outputs both the latent features and the MLP's weights and biases. Though the solution space is high-dimensional, this approach produces results comparable in quality to the current reference neural texture compressors. We further extend this approach to infer multiple decoders at once or even produce decoders that learn super-resolution.

AI.tldr: This paper trains a single hypernetwork to output both latent features and MLP weights/biases for neural texture compression, achieving comparable quality to per-material optimization without requiring per-material gradient descent, and extends to multi-decoder inference and super-resolution.
AI.motivation: Existing neural texture compression methods require per-material gradient-descent optimization for a given MLP and latent configuration, which is computationally expensive and lacks generalization.
AI.method: Trains a single hypernetwork that outputs both latent features and the MLP's weights and biases for neural texture compression, replacing per-material optimization with a learned mapping.
AI.result: Produces results comparable in quality to current reference neural texture compressors, and extends to inferring multiple decoders at once or producing decoders that learn super-resolution.
AI.conclusion: A single hypernetwork approach can match the quality of per-material optimized neural texture compression while avoiding expensive per-material optimization, with additional capabilities for multi-decoder and super-resolution inference.

title: Augmentation with Dilution: A Large-Scale Empirical Study of Human Contributor Ecosystems After AI Coding Agent Adoption
summary: AI coding agents are penetrating open-source software development at an unprecedented pace, yet existing research predominantly treats human contributors as a static backdrop rather than as the subject of inquiry. This paper presents the first large-scale empirical study that takes the human contributor ecosystem as its dependent variable, examining how the number, composition, and behavior of human participants change following AI coding agent adoption in open-source projects. Using a staggered difference-in-differences design on a dataset of 11,097 GitHub repositories spanning January 2023 to May 2026, we provide causal evidence via the Sun and Abraham estimator. Our results show that AI agent adoption does not significantly change the absolute number of human contributors (ATT = 0.014, p = 0.224), but significantly reduces human contributor density (ATT = -0.019, p = 0.002), indicating that the relative share of human participation declines as AI-generated pull requests accumulate. The relative participation share of newcomers declines significantly by 3.7 percentage points (ATT = -0.037, p < 0.001), with the effect emerging immediately after adoption and remaining stable throughout the observation window. Review depth increases significantly by 5.3% (ATT = +0.0168, p < 0.001), indicating that AI agents shift burden from the code production stage to the review stage. Moderator analysis reveals that these effects vary systematically with project size, programming language, and project maturity. Together, these findings present a pattern of augmentation with dilution: AI agents are not displacing human contributors, but are systematically reshaping the participation structure of open-source ecosystems.

AI.tldr: This large-scale empirical study uses a staggered difference-in-differences design on 11,097 GitHub repositories to examine how AI coding agent adoption changes human contributor ecosystems. Results show AI agents don't reduce absolute contributor counts but dilute relative human participation density, reduce newcomer share by 3.7 percentage points, and increase review depth by 5.3%, revealing a pattern of "augmentation with dilution" where AI reshapes participation structures without displacing humans.
AI.motivation: Existing research treats human contributors as a static backdrop rather than as the subject of inquiry, while AI coding agents are rapidly penetrating open-source development. This study fills the gap by taking the human contributor ecosystem as its dependent variable.
AI.method: Staggered difference-in-differences design using the Sun and Abraham estimator on a dataset of 11,097 GitHub repositories spanning January 2023 to May 2026, examining changes in number, composition, and behavior of human participants following AI coding agent adoption.
AI.result: AI agent adoption does not significantly change absolute human contributor count (ATT=0.014, p=0.224), but significantly reduces human contributor density (ATT=-0.019, p=0.002). Newcomer relative participation share declines by 3.7 percentage points (ATT=-0.037, p<0.001). Review depth increases by 5.3% (ATT=+0.0168, p<0.001). Effects vary with project size, programming language, and project maturity.
AI.conclusion: AI agents present a pattern of augmentation with dilution—they are not displacing human contributors but are systematically reshaping the participation structure of open-source ecosystems, shifting burden from code production to review stages and reducing newcomer participation opportunities.

title: \chisao{}: A GPU-Native Parallel Optimizer for Multimodal Black-Box Functions via Convergence-Anticonvergence Oscillation
summary: Finding all modes of a multimodal black-box function is a fundamental challenge in optimization, Bayesian inference, and scientific computing. Existing approaches -- basin-hopping, CMA-ES, multistart gradient descent -- operate sequentially and cannot exploit the massive parallelism of modern GPU hardware. We introduce \chisao{} (\textbf{C}onvergence-\textbf{H}alt-\textbf{I}nvert-\textbf{S}tick-\textbf{A}nd-\textbf{O}scillate), a GPU-native population optimizer that runs an entire sample batch simultaneously and exploits a deliberate convergence-anticonvergence oscillation cycle to escape local traps while freezing confirmed modes. The structural move is asymmetric: samples that reach true peaks are frozen (``stuck'') and preserved, while the rest keep exploring via momentum-based anti-convergence and stochastically smoothed gradients. Adaptive reseeding via two complementary strategies (Repulse Monkey and Golden Rooster) maintains population diversity throughout. On all 42 functions of the Simon Fraser University optimization benchmark suite across dimensions $d \in \{2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64\}$, \chisao{} achieves \textbf{100\%} mode recovery where all CPU baselines collapse at $d \geq 8$ on the hardest multimodal functions, at up to \textbf{$34\times$} speedup over basin-hopping on functions where all methods succeed (Michalewicz $d=64$) and up to \textbf{$39\times$} on unimodal functions (Rotated Hyper-Ellipsoid $d=64$, pure GPU dividend). All benchmarks evaluate the objective by value alone -- gradients come from finite differences -- so the reported speedups are a derivative-free worst case. Under substantial likelihood noise ($σ_{\mathrm{noise}}$ up to 1.0), mode detection remains 100\% reliable. The algorithm is available as a standalone open-source Python package on PyPI.

AI.tldr: χισάο is a GPU-native population optimizer that uses a convergence-anticonvergence oscillation cycle to find all modes of multimodal black-box functions, achieving 100% mode recovery on 42 benchmark functions across dimensions up to 64, with up to 39× speedup over basin-hopping, and 100% reliability under substantial likelihood noise.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal optimization methods (basin-hopping, CMA-ES, multistart gradient descent) operate sequentially and cannot leverage the massive parallelism of modern GPU hardware, limiting their scalability and speed.
AI.method: CHISAO runs an entire sample batch simultaneously on GPU using a deliberate convergence-anticonvergence oscillation cycle. Samples at true peaks are frozen ("stuck") and preserved, while others explore via momentum-based anti-convergence and stochastically smoothed gradients. Adaptive reseeding via two strategies (Repulse Monkey and Golden Rooster) maintains population diversity.
AI.result: On all 42 SFU benchmark functions across d ∈ {2,4,8,16,32,64}, CHISAO achieves 100% mode recovery where CPU baselines collapse at d≥8 on hardest multimodal functions, with up to 34× speedup over basin-hopping (Michalewicz d=64) and up to 39× on unimodal functions (Rotated Hyper-Ellipsoid d=64). Under likelihood noise σ_noise up to 1.0, mode detection remains 100% reliable.
AI.conclusion: CHISAO demonstrates that GPU-native population-based optimization with deliberate oscillation dynamics can dramatically outperform sequential CPU methods for multimodal black-box optimization, achieving both superior mode recovery and significant speedups. The method is robust to noise and available as an open-source Python package.

title: Soroll-IA: A Weakly Labeled Audio Dataset for Real-World Industrial Port Monitoring
comment: Paper being under review at Journal on Audio, Speech, and Music Processing
summary: Soroll-IA is a weakly labeled environmental audio dataset recorded in a real-world industrial port environment in Valencia (Spain) using two fixed sensing nodes. The dataset comprises approximately 22 hours of audio segmented into 7,396 clips and covers 26 sound event classes representative of industrial port acoustic activity commonly observed in such environments, such as crane sirens, train movements, traffic, and other logistical and industrial sounds. Recordings were captured under highly challenging acoustic conditions, including strong background noise, long-distance sources, and frequent event overlap. All audio clips were annotated by domain experts following a weak labeling strategy, where tags indicate the presence of sound events within a clip without temporal localization. To account for inter-annotator variability, two ground-truth versions are released: one without cross-validation, where a class is considered present if annotated by at least one expert, and a second, more conservative version based on cross-validation, where agreement by at least two-thirds of the annotators is required. The dataset is intended to support research in audio tagging, weakly supervised sound event detection, and machine learning under realistic industrial acoustic conditions. Benchmark results are provided using two complementary architectures: CNN14 representing high-capacity convolutional models for audio tagging, and MobileNetV2, selected for its suitability in real-time classification on low-resource edge devices. To the best of current knowledge, Soroll-IA constitutes an available dataset dedicated exclusively to industrial port acoustic environments, aiming to foster advances in robust environmental sound analysis for safety-critical and operational monitoring applications. The dataset is available online and collected under Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 International license.

AI.tldr: Soroll-IA is a ~22-hour weakly labeled environmental audio dataset from an industrial port in Valencia, with 7,396 clips across 26 sound event classes, designed to support audio tagging and weakly supervised sound event detection under challenging real-world acoustic conditions.
AI.motivation: To fill the gap of a dedicated, publicly available dataset for industrial port acoustic environments, enabling research in robust environmental sound analysis for safety-critical and operational monitoring applications.
AI.method: Two fixed sensing nodes recorded 22 hours of audio in a real port environment; 7,396 clips were weakly labeled by domain experts with two ground-truth versions (without cross-validation and with two-thirds agreement cross-validation). Benchmarking used CNN14 (high-capacity) and MobileNetV2 (edge-device suitable) architectures.
AI.result: Benchmark results from CNN14 and MobileNetV2 are provided, establishing baselines for audio tagging and weakly supervised sound event detection on this dataset.
AI.conclusion: Soroll-IA provides a unique, openly available resource under Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 International license, fostering advances in robust environmental sound analysis for industrial port settings.

title: Predicting Fruit Quality with a Hybrid Machine Learning and Image Processing Approach
summary: Fruit spoilage is a significant issue in agriculture, leading to substantial economic losses. Addressing this, our study introduces a hybrid approach combining image processing and deep learning to assess fruit freshness. We developed an image processing algorithm that quantifies spoilage on a scale from 0 (fully fresh) to 100 (fully rotten). Alongside, we trained a convolutional neural network (CNN) to perform binary classification (fresh or rotten) using a large dataset of fruit images. The outcomes of both methods were synthesized using logistic regression to enhance the accuracy of freshness predictions. Subsequently, this logistic regression model was utilized to enable the image processing algorithm to provide binary classification based on its percentage output, thus eliminating the need for the CNN in real-time applications. Our approach, which does not require high computational resources, achieved real-time performance and was validated with over 90% accuracy on a dataset comprising apples and oranges. The primary limitation lies in the requirement for fruits to be isolated on a background that must be either white or transparent, suggesting future improvements could include advanced segmentation models to automate background removal. This study's results highlight the potential of integrating simple image processing techniques with machine learning to provide practical solutions in the agricultural sector.

AI.tldr: A hybrid image processing and deep learning approach was developed to assess fruit freshness, achieving >90% real-time accuracy on apples and oranges by combining CNN classification with image processing, then using logistic regression to enable standalone image processing for binary classification.
AI.motivation: Fruit spoilage causes substantial economic losses in agriculture, motivating the need for an efficient, low-cost automated freshness assessment system.
AI.method: (1) Image processing algorithm quantifies spoilage on a 0-100 scale; (2) CNN performs binary fresh/rotten classification; (3) Logistic regression synthesizes outputs from both methods; (4) The logistic regression model enables the image processing algorithm to directly perform binary classification from its percentage score, removing CNN dependency in deployment.
AI.result: Achieved real-time performance with over 90% accuracy on apples and oranges, without requiring high computational resources.
AI.conclusion: Integrating simple image processing with machine learning can provide practical, low-cost solutions for agricultural quality assessment. Future work should focus on advanced segmentation models to automate background removal.

title: Life After Benchmark Saturation: A Case Study of CORE-Bench
summary: When a benchmark's accuracy saturates, it is often retired and replaced with a more challenging version. We show that this approach privileges accuracy and misses the opportunity to study six other key dimensions of agent performance: construct validity issues such as shortcuts, out-of-distribution generalizability, efficiency, reliability, the relative importance of the model versus the scaffold, and uplift from human-agent collaboration. We use CORE-Bench Hard, a benchmark for computational reproducibility of scientific code, as a case study to demonstrate that measuring agents along these dimensions yields meaningful insights into agent performance even after accuracy saturates. First, we surface threats to construct validity in CORE-Bench Hard that are difficult to anticipate with less capable agents. We introduce an improved benchmark, CORE-Bench v1.1, and an out-of-distribution task suite, CORE-Bench OOD. Second, we find that despite accuracy saturation, CORE-Bench v1.1 remains useful for measuring efficiency, reliability, model performance, and scaffold performance. Finally, we conduct a small-scale randomized experiment to measure uplift from human-agent collaboration on real-world computational reproducibility tasks. We find a statistically significant speedup by about a factor of two -- likely underestimated due to one-fifth of human-only reproductions reaching the time limit before completing -- and describe various other findings. Together, our contributions present a more rigorous alternative to the dominant accuracy-centric evaluation paradigm.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that accuracy saturation shouldn't retire benchmarks; instead, six additional dimensions (construct validity, generalizability, efficiency, reliability, model vs. scaffold importance, human-agent uplift) should be measured. Using CORE-Bench Hard as a case study, they introduce CORE-Bench v1.1 and OOD, find meaningful insights even after accuracy saturates, and show human-agent collaboration yields ~2x speedup.
AI.motivation: The dominant accuracy-centric paradigm for evaluating agents leads to retiring benchmarks once accuracy saturates, missing the opportunity to study six other key dimensions of agent performance (construct validity, out-of-distribution generalizability, efficiency, reliability, model vs. scaffold importance, and human-agent collaboration uplift).
AI.method: The authors use CORE-Bench Hard (computational reproducibility of scientific code) as a case study. They surface construct validity threats, introduce improved benchmark CORE-Bench v1.1 and an out-of-distribution suite CORE-Bench OOD. They measure efficiency, reliability, model vs. scaffold performance under accuracy saturation, and conduct a small-scale randomized experiment to measure human-agent collaboration uplift.
AI.result: CORE-Bench v1.1 remains useful for measuring efficiency, reliability, model performance, and scaffold performance despite accuracy saturation. Human-agent collaboration shows a statistically significant ~2x speedup, likely underestimated because one-fifth of human-only reproductions hit the time limit.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents a more rigorous alternative to the accuracy-centric evaluation paradigm by demonstrating that multiple dimensions beyond accuracy provide meaningful insights into agent performance even after accuracy saturates.

title: Vis4GS: A Visual Analytic Tool for 3D Gaussian Splatting Reconstruction
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) supports fast training and real-time rendering, but its optimization process remains difficult to interpret. Existing viewers mainly expose the final reconstructed scene and offer limited support for explaining how Gaussian properties contribute to visible artifacts or evolve during training. We present Vis4GS, a multi-view visual analytics tool for primitive-level diagnosis of 3DGS reconstruction artifacts. Built on the original 3DGS viewer and training framework, Vis4GS links rendered artifacts to Gaussian properties, View Coverage, training progress, and Gaussian genealogy through four linked views: an interactive Gaussian analysis view, a property timeline view, a Gaussian densification tree view, and a log and control panel. The system supports Gaussian selection, blur and needle-like artifact scoring, View Coverage analysis, and multiscale genealogy exploration of clone, split, prune, and clone-split events. By connecting scene-level artifacts with primitive-level evidence and optimization history, Vis4GS enables a structured workflow for diagnosing reconstruction failures beyond final-image inspection and global metrics. A user study also shows that Vis4GS provides stronger support for usability and artifact understanding than the original 3DGS viewer.

AI.tldr: Vis4GS is a multi-view visual analytics tool that diagnoses reconstruction artifacts in 3D Gaussian Splatting by linking rendered artifacts to Gaussian properties, View Coverage, training progress, and Gaussian genealogy through four linked views, enabling primitive-level understanding and structured failure diagnosis beyond final-image inspection.
AI.motivation: 3DGS optimization is difficult to interpret; existing viewers expose only the final scene and offer limited support for explaining how Gaussian properties contribute to visible artifacts or evolve during training.
AI.method: Vis4GS extends the original 3DGS viewer and training framework with four linked views: (1) interactive Gaussian analysis view, (2) property timeline view, (3) Gaussian densification tree view, and (4) log and control panel. It supports Gaussian selection, blur/needle-like artifact scoring, View Coverage analysis, and multiscale genealogy exploration of clone, split, prune, and clone-split events.
AI.result: Connects scene-level artifacts with primitive-level evidence and optimization history, enabling structured workflow for diagnosing reconstruction failures. A user study shows stronger support for usability and artifact understanding compared to the original 3DGS viewer.
AI.conclusion: Vis4GS enables primitive-level diagnosis of 3DGS reconstruction artifacts through a linked multi-view approach, improving interpretability and usability beyond final-image inspection and global metrics.

title: Towards Safety-Aware Mutation Testing for Autonomous Driving Systems
summary:
Simulation-based testing is essential for ensuring the safety of Autonomous Driving Systems (ADS), yet the community lacks a systematic criterion for determining when we can safely stop additional test scenario generation. Existing coverage metrics typically focus on individual component reliability or treat the ADS as a black box, failing to capture certain component interactions that cause most ADS accidents. While traditional mutation testing provides a falsifiable measure of test adequacy, directly porting code- and deep learning model-level mutations to the corresponding modules of ADS is insufficient.
  In this vision paper, we propose a paradigm shift toward Safety-Aware Mutation Testing (SAMT). Unlike traditional mutation testing, which creates mutants (i.e., faulty versions of the software under test) by injecting artificial faults into individual components, SAMT systematically injects temporally bounded faults into the messages exchanged between ADS modules to simulate realistic interaction failures. To ensure these mutants represent genuine hazards, we propose deriving mutant generation rules directly from top-down safety engineering frameworks, such as System-Theoretic Process Analysis (STPA). By embedding systems thinking into the mutation testing pipeline, SAMT provides a rigorous mechanism for evaluating test adequacy, enabling automated scenario generation, and guiding ADS repair. We also outline critical open challenges.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Safety-Aware Mutation Testing (SAMT), a paradigm shift from traditional mutation testing, which injects temporally bounded faults into inter-module messages in Autonomous Driving Systems (ADS) based on safety engineering frameworks like STPA, to provide a systematic and falsifiable test adequacy criterion for simulation-based testing.
AI.motivation: Current simulation-based testing for ADS lacks a systematic criterion for when to stop generating test scenarios. Existing coverage metrics fail to capture component interactions that cause most ADS accidents, and traditional mutation testing is insufficient when directly applied to ADS modules.
AI.method: SAMT injects temporally bounded faults into messages exchanged between ADS modules (rather than into individual components), with mutant generation rules derived from top-down safety engineering frameworks like System-Theoretic Process Analysis (STPA).
AI.result: The paper presents the SAMT paradigm as a vision/position paper, outlining how it provides a rigorous mechanism for evaluating test adequacy, enabling automated scenario generation, and guiding ADS repair. It also outlines critical open challenges.
AI.conclusion: SAMT embeds systems thinking into mutation testing, offering a more principled approach to ADS safety testing by focusing on interaction failures rather than isolated component faults, though challenges remain to be addressed.

title: Implementation of reinforcement learning in chemical reaction networks: application to phototaxis as curiosity-driven exploration
comment: This paper is accepted as talk at ALIFE 2026 (Waterloo, Canada)
summary: Living systems navigate environments using noisy and incomplete sensory signals. In unicellular algae, phototaxis is often modeled as a mechanistic run--tumble process driven by stimulus--response rules. However, such descriptions overlook how organisms actively sample their environment to reduce sensory ambiguity. From a minimal cognition perspective, we reframe this navigation as a subjective, information-driven sensorimotor process. To this end, we propose a framework linking a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) with biochemical reaction dynamics. Environmental variables are hidden, while the cell updates a minimal internal state from each observation through a memoryless Bayesian step. These internal dynamics balance orienting toward light with exploratory reorientation and can be implemented through Chemical-Reaction-Network Ordinary Differential Equations (CRN--ODEs). Our model includes a biophysical observation process for photoreception and a chemically computable polynomial bound on information gain. Using Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL) on 30 experimentally recorded Chlamydomonas trajectories, we infer the behavioral objective consistent with observed phototactic motion and benchmark the resulting dynamics with standard Stochastic Simulation Algorithm (SSA) baselines. Our model reproduces the empirical alignment-to-light distribution, comparable to objective SSA baselines on this dataset. Within this framework, run--tumble alternation emerges as an information-acquisition strategy: tumbling reorients the cell to sample new sensory configurations and resolve sensor ambiguity, demonstrating how intracellular biochemical networks can support adaptive information-seeking behavior in cellular navigation.

AI.tldr: The paper reframes unicellular algae phototaxis as an information-driven sensorimotor process using a POMDP framework linked to biochemical reaction dynamics, showing that run-tumble behavior emerges as an information-acquisition strategy to reduce sensory ambiguity, validated on Chlamydomonas trajectories via Inverse Reinforcement Learning.
AI.motivation: Traditional mechanistic run-tumble models of phototaxis overlook how organisms actively sample their environment to reduce sensory ambiguity; the authors seek to reframe navigation from a minimal cognition perspective as a subjective, information-driven process.
AI.method: The authors propose a framework linking a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) with Chemical-Reaction-Network Ordinary Differential Equations (CRN-ODEs), incorporating a memoryless Bayesian update, a biophysical observation process for photoreception, and a chemically computable polynomial bound on information gain. Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL) is applied to 30 experimentally recorded Chlamydomonas trajectories to infer the behavioral objective.
AI.result: The model reproduces empirical alignment-to-light distribution comparably to objective SSA baselines on the dataset. Run-tumble alternation emerges as an information-acquisition strategy where tumbling reorients the cell to sample new sensory configurations and resolve sensor ambiguity.
AI.conclusion: Intracellular biochemical networks can support adaptive information-seeking behavior in cellular navigation, demonstrating that minimal cognitive principles can explain how unicellular organisms balance orienting toward light with exploratory reorientation.

title: Listening Like a Judge: A Music-Aware Framework for Automatic Singing Performance Evaluation
comment: Accepted at Interspeech 2026. Supplementary material: https://neelam472.github.io/MusicJudge/Supp.pdf (backup mirror: https://sourav-ghosh.github.io/MusicJudge/Supp.pdf )
summary: Automatic singing quality assessment (SQA) requires evaluating lyrical correctness and musical fidelity while handling expressive variations. However, existing systems largely rely on either acoustic cues or lyric transcriptions exclusively, limiting holistic performance evaluation. Furthermore, their integration is non-trivial due to challenges in robust singing transcription amid melisma, vibrato, and tempo elasticity. To this end, we propose MusicJudge, a modality-guided framework for automated SQA that performs block-aligned multimodal analysis by coupling lyric correctness with pitch-rhythm fidelity. It detects semantically meaningful lyric blocks using multi-signal matching that integrates semantic embeddings, lexical similarity, and phonetic alignment. To improve singing audio transcription, we introduce Modality-Guided LoRA for ASR fine-tuning. Experiments across datasets demonstrate strong agreement with human expert judgments and validate the generalizability of MusicJudge.

AI.tldr: MusicJudge is a modality-guided framework for automatic singing quality assessment that integrates lyrical correctness and pitch-rhythm fidelity through block-aligned multimodal analysis, using multi-signal matching for lyric block detection and Modality-Guided LoRA for improved singing transcription.
AI.motivation: Existing SQA systems rely solely on either acoustic cues or lyric transcriptions, limiting holistic evaluation; integrating both is challenging due to melisma, vibrato, and tempo elasticity in singing.
AI.method: Proposes a modality-guided framework with block-aligned multimodal analysis coupling lyric correctness with pitch-rhythm fidelity. Uses multi-signal matching (semantic embeddings, lexical similarity, phonetic alignment) for lyric block detection and Modality-Guided LoRA for ASR fine-tuning on singing audio.
AI.result: Achieves strong agreement with human expert judgments and demonstrates generalizability across datasets.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal integration via modality-guided frameworks can effectively address the limitations of unimodal SQA systems, even under challenging singing-specific acoustic phenomena.

title: LCG: Long-Context Consistent Image Generation with Sparse Relational Attention
summary: Recent image generation models achieve impressive quality in single-image synthesis, but often fail to maintain consistency across sequential outputs, as required in comics, storyboards, and visual narratives. We propose Long-Context Generation (LCG), a framework for long-context multi-image text-to-image generation, to improve consistency and scalability in long-context multi-image generation. LCG employs the Sparse Relational Attention (SRA) mechanism to selectively attend to core features across extended visual contexts, ensuring that the propagation of semantic and layout information remains computationally tractable. To enforce semantic alignment, we introduce the Routing Consistency Constraint (RCC), which leverages identity-aware masks to align structural patterns across generation branches, effectively mitigating drift in appearance even in complex multi-character scenes. To support training and evaluation in this setting, we construct the Long-Context Consistency Dataset (LCCD), a large-scale synthetic dataset comprising character-centric multi-image sequences spanning varied situational contexts. LCCD contains 600K training sequences and a separate 1K test set, with each sequence containing 6 to 20 images. The experiments demonstrate that LCG outperforms the compared baselines in prompt alignment and character consistency for long-context image generation, including multi-character scenes.

AI.tldr: LCG is a framework for long-context multi-image text-to-image generation that uses Sparse Relational Attention (SRA) and Routing Consistency Constraint (RCC) to improve consistency across sequential images, evaluated on a new synthetic dataset LCCD. It outperforms baselines in prompt alignment and character consistency.
AI.motivation: Existing image generation models produce high-quality single images but fail to maintain consistency across sequential outputs needed for comics, storyboards, and visual narratives.
AI.method: LCG introduces (1) Sparse Relational Attention (SRA) to selectively attend to core features across extended visual contexts, and (2) Routing Consistency Constraint (RCC) using identity-aware masks to align structural patterns across generation branches. A new dataset LCCD is constructed with 600K training + 1K test sequences (6–20 images each).
AI.result: LCG outperforms compared baselines in prompt alignment and character consistency for long-context image generation, including multi-character scenes.
AI.conclusion: The proposed LCG framework effectively addresses long-context multi-image consistency challenges, with SRA and RCC enabling scalable and semantically aligned generation across extended sequences.

title: Refusal Lives Downstream of Persona in Chat Models
comment: Accepted to the ICML 2026 Mechanistic Interpretability workshop
summary: Linear directions in activation space have been identified for both refusal and persona traits in instruction-tuned chat models, but the two have been studied as separate mechanisms. We show they interact: a compliant persona gates refusal. In Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, we extract a compliant model-persona direction and a refusal direction and intervene on both. Compliant persona steering suppresses refusal -- in Llama, the refusal rate falls from 97% to 2%. Reintroducing the refusal direction partially restores refusal at late layers but not at early ones. Projecting out the persona direction in a late-layer window restores it to baseline; projecting out a random direction does not. Refusal is therefore gated at the late-layer expression stage, downstream of where it is computed. Treating refusal as a single isolated direction misses its dependence on persona.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that refusal and persona directions in activation space interact: a compliant persona gates refusal. In two 8B-parameter instruction-tuned models, steering the persona direction suppresses refusal (e.g., Llama refusal rate drops from 97% to 2%). Refusal restoration via refusal direction steering is only effective at late layers. Projecting out the persona direction in late layers restores refusal, while random projection does not. Thus, refusal is gated at late-layer expression, downstream of where it is computed, not as an isolated mechanism.
AI.motivation: Prior work identified separate linear directions for refusal and persona traits in chat models, but their interaction was unexplored. The authors aim to show that these mechanisms are not independent but that persona gates refusal expression.
AI.method: The authors extract compliant model-persona and refusal directions from Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, then intervene on both via steering and projection at different layers to observe refusal rate changes.
AI.result: Compliant persona steering suppresses refusal (Llama: 97%→2%). Reintroducing refusal direction partially restores refusal only in late layers. Projecting out persona direction in late-layer window restores refusal to baseline, while random projection does not.
AI.conclusion: Refusal expression is gated by persona at the late-layer stage, downstream of computation. Treating refusal as an isolated linear direction is insufficient; it is dependent on the persona direction.

title: SpaceRipple: Lightweight Semantic Delivery for Mission-Oriented LEO Earth Observation Satellite Networks
summary: Earth observation satellite networks generate massive volumes of high-resolution imagery, whereas inter-satellite and downlink resources remain limited. In many time-sensitive missions, ground users require mission-relevant semantic information rather than a full raw-image downlink. This paper proposes SpaceRipple, a lightweight framework for mission-oriented semantic delivery and on-board processing in Earth observation satellite networks. A sensing satellite performs adaptive compression and metadata generation to reduce inter-satellite traffic, while an edge computing satellite restores the received representation and extracts task-relevant semantic information. Unlike fidelity-driven image transmission, SpaceRipple coordinates compression, forwarding, restoration, and semantic inference within a collaborative pipeline, enabling semantic-oriented delivery instead of pixel-level image delivery. A compression-aware MoE enhancement module is further introduced to improve robustness under degraded visual inputs. Experimental results show that SpaceRipple achieves favorable reconstruction quality, improved semantic detection performance, and substantial bandwidth savings, demonstrating its potential for efficient and reliable Earth observation under constrained satellite-network resources.

AI.tldr: SpaceRipple is a lightweight framework for mission-oriented semantic delivery in Earth observation satellite networks. It uses adaptive compression, metadata generation, and on-board edge computing to extract and transmit task-relevant semantic information instead of full raw images, reducing bandwidth while maintaining reconstruction quality and semantic detection performance.
AI.motivation: Earth observation satellite networks generate massive high-resolution imagery but have limited inter-satellite and downlink resources. Time-sensitive missions require semantic information rather than full raw-image downlinks, motivating a shift from fidelity-driven to semantic-oriented delivery.
AI.method: The framework employs: (1) adaptive compression and metadata generation on sensing satellites to reduce inter-satellite traffic; (2) an edge computing satellite for representation restoration and task-relevant semantic extraction; (3) a compression-aware Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) enhancement module for robustness under degraded visual inputs; (4) a collaborative pipeline coordinating compression, forwarding, restoration, and semantic inference.
AI.result: SpaceRipple achieves favorable reconstruction quality, improved semantic detection performance, and substantial bandwidth savings compared to traditional fidelity-driven image transmission approaches.
AI.conclusion: SpaceRipple demonstrates potential for efficient and reliable Earth observation under constrained satellite-network resources by enabling semantic-oriented delivery instead of pixel-level image transmission.

title: An Empirical Study of LLM-Generated Specifications for VeriFast
summary: Static verification tools can assure industrial scale software, but require significant human labor to write specifications. This is particularly true of static verifiers based on separation logic (SL verifiers), which excel at verifying heapmanipulating programs, but require many complex auxiliary specifications to reason about heap structure. Recent work applies large language models (LLMs) to generate code, tests, and proofs, including specifications for verifiers, but mostly targeting non-SL verifiers. To address this gap, this paper thoroughly evaluates how well LLMs perform when prompted to generate specifications for verifying 303 C functions with the SL verifier VeriFast. We explored eight prompting approaches, ten LLMs, and three input types in two stages. Quantitative and qualitative analyses are used to assess the LLM-generated code and specifications for functional behavior, verifiability and errors. The results show that LLMs preserve functional behavior in source code and specifications (both over 91%), but achieve modest verification success (31.4%). Using Gemini 2.5 Pro and providing formal contracts lead to higher success rates in our setting. Moreover, most errors (94%) come from LLMs' mistakes in the domainspecific knowledge of SL verifiers such as VeriFast. These findings provide guidance for optimizing LLM-generated specifications for SL verifiers.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates how well LLMs generate specifications for the separation logic verifier VeriFast across 303 C functions, finding that while LLMs preserve functional behavior well (over 91%), verification success is modest (31.4%) and most errors stem from domain-specific SL knowledge gaps.
AI.motivation: Static verification tools based on separation logic (SL) are powerful for verifying heap-manipulating programs but require significant human effort to write complex auxiliary specifications. Recent LLM-based work on specification generation targets mostly non-SL verifiers, leaving a gap for SL verifiers like VeriFast.
AI.method: The authors evaluated eight prompting approaches, ten LLMs, and three input types across two stages on 303 C functions using the SL verifier VeriFast, employing both quantitative and qualitative analyses of generated code and specifications for functional behavior, verifiability, and errors.
AI.result: LLMs preserve functional behavior in source code and specifications at over 91%, but achieve only 31.4% verification success. Gemini 2.5 Pro and providing formal contracts yield higher success rates. 94% of errors stem from LLMs' mistakes in domain-specific knowledge of SL verifiers like VeriFast.
AI.conclusion: These findings provide guidance for optimizing LLM-generated specifications for SL verifiers, highlighting the need to address domain-specific knowledge gaps.

title: Neural Architecture Search for Generative Adversarial Networks: A Comprehensive Review and Critical Analysis
summary: Neural Architecture Search (NAS) has emerged as a pivotal technique in optimizing the design of Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), automating the search for effective architectures while addressing the challenges inherent in manual design. This paper provides a comprehensive review of NAS methods applied to GANs, categorizing and comparing various approaches based on criteria such as search strategies, evaluation metrics, and performance outcomes. The review highlights the benefits of NAS in improving GAN performance, stability, and efficiency, while also identifying limitations and areas for future research. Key findings include the superiority of evolutionary algorithms and gradient-based methods in certain contexts, the importance of robust evaluation metrics beyond traditional scores like Inception Score (IS) and Fréchet Inception Distance (FID), and the need for diverse datasets in assessing GAN performance. By presenting a structured comparison of existing NAS-GAN techniques, this paper aims to guide researchers in developing more effective NAS methods and advancing the field of GANs.

AI.tldr: This paper reviews Neural Architecture Search (NAS) methods applied to Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), categorizing approaches by search strategies, evaluation metrics, and performance. It finds evolutionary algorithms and gradient-based methods effective, emphasizes robust metrics beyond IS and FID, and identifies future research directions.
AI.motivation: To address the challenges of manual GAN design by systematically reviewing and categorizing NAS methods applied to GANs, highlighting their benefits and limitations to guide future research.
AI.method: A comprehensive literature review categorizing and comparing NAS-GAN approaches based on search strategies (e.g., evolutionary algorithms, gradient-based methods), evaluation metrics (e.g., IS, FID, and beyond), and performance outcomes.
AI.result: Evolutionary algorithms and gradient-based methods show superiority in certain contexts; robust evaluation metrics beyond IS and FID are needed; diverse datasets are essential for assessing GAN performance.
AI.conclusion: NAS improves GAN performance, stability, and efficiency, but future work should focus on better metrics, diverse datasets, and addressing current limitations.

title: VoiceTTA: Enhancing Zero-Shot Text-to-Speech via Reinforcement Learning-Based Test-Time Adaptation
summary: Recently, zero-shot text-to-speech (TTS) has enabled high-fidelity and expressive speech synthesis, but it often fails to imitate unseen speaking styles from uncommon scenarios (e.g., crosstalk, dialects). Moreover, fine-tuning pretrained models requires large, high-quality datasets, limiting rapid personalization. We propose VoiceTTA, a reinforcement learning-based test-time adaptation (TTA) method that improves voice imitation of pretrained zero-shot TTS models. VoiceTTA introduces two style rewards based on coefficient-of-variation differences of F0 and energy, combined with speaker similarity and intelligibility (WER from a pretrained Whisper model), and optimizes learnable prefixes via group relative preference optimization (GRPO) in a flow matching-based model at inference time. Extensive experiments demonstrate substantial improvements on uncommon speech prompts, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines. Audio samples are available at https://voicetta.pages.dev/

AI.tldr: VoiceTTA is a reinforcement learning-based test-time adaptation method that improves zero-shot TTS voice imitation for uncommon speaking styles by optimizing learnable prefixes via GRPO using style rewards (F0/energy CV differences), speaker similarity, and WER-based intelligibility.
AI.motivation: Zero-shot TTS fails to imitate unseen speaking styles from uncommon scenarios (e.g., crosstalk, dialects), and fine-tuning requires large high-quality datasets, limiting rapid personalization.
AI.method: VoiceTTA uses reinforcement learning-based test-time adaptation (TTA) with two style rewards (coefficient-of-variation differences of F0 and energy), speaker similarity, and WER from a pretrained Whisper model for intelligibility. It optimizes learnable prefixes via group relative preference optimization (GRPO) in a flow matching-based model at inference time.
AI.result: Substantial improvements on uncommon speech prompts, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: Test-time adaptation via reinforcement learning effectively enhances zero-shot TTS for unseen styles without requiring large fine-tuning datasets.

title: Self-Supervised Tree-level Biomass Estimation in Urban Environments From Airborne LiDAR and Optical Observations
summary: Urban tree biomass remains less spatially explicitly quantified than biomass in managed forests because many estimates rely on inventories or coarse products that cannot resolve individual crowns or fine-scale heterogeneity. We present a crown-level above-ground biomass (AGB) framework for an 810~km$^2$ landscape in Ontario, Canada, using leaf-off airborne LiDAR (8--10~pulses~m$^{-2}$) and near-infrared RGB orthophotography (0.16--0.20~m) from 2018 and 2023. A dual-stream cross-attention network trained on rule-based pseudo-labels produced semantic marks for buildings, needleleaf trees, and deciduous trees, supporting crown delineation and functional-type assignment. On independently annotated withheld tiles, global/mean precision, recall, and Dice scores were 0.86, 0.83, and 0.84. Crowns were delineated with multiscale watershed segmentation in mapped tree areas, and AGB was estimated from a crown area--height power-law proxy calibrated to species-specific allometry (Lambert et al., 2005) for 21,921 inventory trees. For 18,713 inventory--segment matched pairs from a 90,726-tree held-out test set, AGB prediction achieved $R^2=0.609$ using inventory crown geometry and $R^2=0.570$ under operational segmentation, identifying crown delineation as the remaining uncertainty source. Aggregated to 30~m, estimates yielded total AGB stocks of 1.73~Tg in 2018 and 1.81~Tg in 2023 (811--850~Gg~C), local densities up to ${\sim}140$~Mg~ha$^{-1}$ along the Niagara Escarpment, and a net carbon gain of 39~Gg~C over five years. Deep-ensemble uncertainty maps highlighted high-epistemic-uncertainty areas linked to underrepresented land covers and guided assignment of uncertain crowns to a pooled allometric equation. The framework uses standard provincial data, requires no manual annotation, and produces a public bitemporal crown-level AGB database for trees outside forests at management-relevant resolution.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a crown-level above-ground biomass (AGB) estimation framework for urban trees across an 810 km² landscape in Ontario, Canada, using leaf-off airborne LiDAR and orthophotography from 2018 and 2023. A dual-stream cross-attention network with rule-based pseudo-labels classifies trees as needleleaf or deciduous, followed by watershed crown delineation and allometry-based AGB prediction. The method achieves R²=0.570 under operational segmentation, estimates total carbon stocks of 811–850 Gg C, and produces a public bitemporal database without manual annotation.
AI.motivation: Urban tree biomass remains less spatially explicitly quantified than biomass in managed forests because existing estimates rely on inventories or coarse products that cannot resolve individual crowns or fine-scale heterogeneity.
AI.method: A dual-stream cross-attention network trained on rule-based pseudo-labels classifies buildings, needleleaf, and deciduous trees from leaf-off airborne LiDAR (8–10 pulses/m²) and near-infrared RGB orthophotography (0.16–0.20 m). Crowns are delineated via multiscale watershed segmentation in mapped tree areas, and AGB is estimated using a crown area–height power-law proxy calibrated to species-specific allometry for 21,921 inventory trees. Deep ensembles provide uncertainty maps.
AI.result: Global/mean precision (0.86), recall (0.83), and Dice score (0.84) on withheld tiles. AGB prediction achieved R²=0.609 with inventory crown geometry and R²=0.570 under operational segmentation for 18,713 matched pairs. Total AGB: 1.73 Tg in 2018 and 1.81 Tg in 2023 (811–850 Gg C), local densities up to ~140 Mg/ha, net carbon gain of 39 Gg C over five years.
AI.conclusion: The framework uses standard provincial data, requires no manual annotation, and produces a public bitemporal crown-level AGB database for trees outside forests at management-relevant resolution. Crown delineation is identified as the primary remaining uncertainty source.

title: AlgoEvolve: LLM-driven Meta-evolution of Algorithmic Trading Programs
summary: Recent work shows that Large Language Models (LLMs) can act as semantic mutation operators for the evolutionary discovery of programs and proofs. Most current applications focus on static coding benchmarks. We extend this paradigm to algorithmic trading. This domain is uniquely challenging because it is noisy, non-stationary, and highly discontinuous. We present AlgoEvolve, an LLM-driven evolutionary framework that generates, evaluates, and iteratively improves executable trading strategies. These strategies are expressed as Python code and evaluated through a rigorous testing protocol. Across multiple experiments, the system exhibits emergent regime-adaptive strategy logic, including autonomous shifts in trading rules. We further introduce a meta-evolutionary outer loop that evolves the prompts guiding program synthesis in the inner loop. This outer loop discovers improved search heuristics. These heuristics balance exploration and exploitation while reducing zero-trade failures. They consistently outperform initial human-designed instructions. The results demonstrate that LLM-based semantic evolution provides a viable approach for continual program synthesis in complex environments.

AI.tldr: AlgoEvolve uses LLM-driven semantic evolution to generate, evaluate, and iteratively improve executable trading strategies in Python, demonstrating emergent regime-adaptive behavior and a meta-evolutionary outer loop that discovers superior search heuristics beyond human-designed prompts.
