AI.motivation: To extend LLM-based semantic evolutionary program synthesis from static coding benchmarks to the challenging domain of algorithmic trading, which is noisy, non-stationary, and highly discontinuous.
AI.method: AlgoEvolve uses an LLM-driven evolutionary framework with two loops: (1) an inner loop that generates, evaluates, and iteratively improves Python-coded trading strategies, and (2) a meta-evolutionary outer loop that evolves the prompts guiding program synthesis to discover improved search heuristics balancing exploration and exploitation.
AI.result: The system exhibits emergent regime-adaptive strategy logic with autonomous shifts in trading rules. The meta-evolutionary outer loop discovers search heuristics that outperform initial human-designed instructions and reduce zero-trade failures.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based semantic evolution provides a viable approach for continual program synthesis in complex, dynamic environments like algorithmic trading.

title: Extracting Neural Materials from Multi-view Images
summary: Neural materials can represent complex specular reflections and scattering effects in a compact, universal basis. However, acquiring and authoring such materials remains challenging. We present NeuMatEx, a differentiable inverse rendering method for extracting spatially varying neural materials from images. The nonlinear structure of neural material latent spaces makes optimization with naive inverse rendering infeasible. To address this, we train a Large Material Reconstruction Model (LMRM) that directly predicts initialbase color, neural material latents, and aleatoric uncertainty guides from images. This material prior provides a good initialization and better constrains our subsequent optimization using inverse path tracing. The predicted uncertainty further helps by anchoring high-confidence regions more tightly to the LMRM prediction, preventing lighting and complex specular effects from being baked into materials. Experiments on synthetic and real assets show that NeuMatEx extracts complex materials with better visual quality and material decomposition than PBR-based methods.

AI.tldr: NeuMatEx is a differentiable inverse rendering method that uses a Large Material Reconstruction Model (LMRM) to predict initial material latents and uncertainty guides, enabling optimization via inverse path tracing for extracting spatially varying neural materials from images.
AI.motivation: Neural materials can represent complex specular reflections and scattering effects, but acquiring and authoring such materials remains challenging, and naive inverse rendering is infeasible due to the nonlinear structure of neural material latent spaces.
AI.method: Train a Large Material Reconstruction Model (LMRM) to predict initial base color, neural material latents, and aleatoric uncertainty guides from images. Use this as a prior to constrain subsequent optimization via inverse path tracing, where uncertainty anchors high-confidence regions to LMRM predictions to prevent baking lighting/specular effects into materials.
AI.result: NeuMatEx extracts complex materials with better visual quality and material decomposition compared to PBR-based methods on synthetic and real assets.
AI.conclusion: The LMRM-based initialization and uncertainty-guided optimization effectively address the optimization challenges of neural material latent spaces, producing superior material extraction results over traditional PBR approaches.

title: Evaluation-Strategy Gap in Fault Diagnosis of Deep Learning Programs
comment: Accepted research track paper in the 42nd IEEE International Conference on Software Maintenance and Evolution (ICSME 2026)
summary: Deep Learning (DL) programs can fail during training for many reasons, and diagnosing the cause is a costly and time-consuming maintenance task. Techniques for diagnosing such failures are commonly assessed using within-program cross-validation, which may be inadequate for deployment settings involving previously unseen programs. It is therefore necessary to assess how performance differs across these settings and to identify the causes of any performance gap in established fault diagnosis techniques for DL. We investigate this gap using DynFault, a corpus of 5,542 fault-injected training traces from 38 real-world DL programs. We found a gap of 0.190 in balanced accuracy for existing fault diagnosis techniques between within-program evaluation and holding out whole programs. We also found the gap comes from program-level structure in the features, which led us to examine two runtime feature sets, curvature features and optimizer features, and their behavior on unseen programs. We found that curvature features are useful for instability detection on unseen programs, while optimizer and activation features help only on programs seen during training.

AI.tldr: Existing fault diagnosis techniques for Deep Learning programs show a 0.190 balanced accuracy gap between within-program evaluation and cross-program evaluation, primarily due to program-level feature structure; curvature features generalize well to unseen programs while optimizer and activation features do not.
AI.motivation: Diagnosing failures in DL programs during training is costly, and existing techniques are typically evaluated using within-program cross-validation, which may not reflect real-world deployment on unseen programs. Understanding the performance gap across program settings is essential for reliable fault diagnosis.
AI.method: Using DynFault, a corpus of 5,542 fault-injected training traces from 38 real-world DL programs, the study measures the balanced accuracy gap between within-program evaluation and holding out whole programs, and analyzes the impact of program-level structure on runtime feature sets (curvature, optimizer, activation features).
AI.result: A gap of 0.190 in balanced accuracy exists between within-program and cross-program evaluation. Curvature features generalize to unseen programs, while optimizer and activation features are only effective on programs seen during training.
AI.conclusion: Program-level feature structure is the primary source of the performance gap; curvature features are recommended for cross-program fault diagnosis, whereas optimizer and activation features should be used with caution when generalizing to unseen DL programs.

title: KG-TRACE: A Neuro-Symbolic Framework for Mechanistic Grounding in Antimicrobial Resistance Prediction
summary:
While WGS-based AMR prediction has reached high accuracy, existing models lack a mechanism to ground neural attributions in established biological pathways. We present KG-TRACE, a novel neuro-symbolic framework that integrates the WHO mutation knowledge graph (KG) as a structured biological constraint on a neural genomic model. Unlike existing methods that learn statistical patterns in isolation, KG-TRACE fuses genomic features and RotatE-based KG embeddings through a learned epistemic trust gate, dynamically weighting neural evidence against symbolic biological knowledge.
  Evaluated on the CRyPTIC M. tuberculosis cohort, KG-TRACE achieves an AUROC of 0.9760 for isoniazid, achieving competitive accuracy while its primary value lies in symbolic grounding, not predictive uplift. More importantly, we introduce the Biological Grounding Ratio (BGR), a dataset-level metric that quantifies alignment between neural attributions and established biology. Our framework achieves a 92.5% symbolic coverage of isoniazid-resistant predictions and effectively identifies MDR co-occurrence artifacts by issuing laboratory follow-up flags for 'UNCERTAIN' cases. We demonstrate that neuro-symbolic grounding provides a verifiable audit trail for clinicians, bridging the gap between predictive accuracy and clinical trust.

AI.tldr: KG-TRACE integrates WHO mutation knowledge graphs with neural genomic models via a trust gate mechanism, achieving high symbolic grounding (92.5% coverage) and competitive AUROC (0.9760) for M. tuberculosis AMR prediction, while introducing BGR as a metric for biological alignment and enabling verifiable clinical audit trails.
AI.motivation: Existing WGS-based AMR prediction models lack mechanisms to ground neural attributions in established biological pathways, hindering clinical trust and interpretability.
AI.method: A neuro-symbolic framework (KG-TRACE) that fuses genomic features with RotatE-based KG embeddings via a learned epistemic trust gate, dynamically weighting neural evidence against symbolic biological knowledge from the WHO mutation KG. Introduces Biological Grounding Ratio (BGR) as a dataset-level evaluation metric.
AI.result: AUROC of 0.9760 for isoniazid on CRyPTIC M. tuberculosis cohort; 92.5% symbolic coverage of isoniazid-resistant predictions; effective identification of MDR co-occurrence artifacts with laboratory follow-up flags for 'UNCERTAIN' cases.
AI.conclusion: Neuro-symbolic grounding provides a verifiable audit trail for clinicians, bridging the gap between predictive accuracy and clinical trust, with primary value in symbolic grounding rather than predictive uplift.

title: A multi-task spatiotemporal deep neural network for predicting penetration depth and morphology in laser welding
summary: In laser penetration welding, the assessment of penetration state and weld seam morphology plays a crucial role in determining the weld quality. This paper presents a comprehensive introduction of the innovative muti-task deep learning model that has the capability to predict penetration state, depth, and weld seam morphology with high accuracy. The monitoring platform relies on weld pool images captured during the laser welding process using a complementary metal-oxide-semiconductor camera. The proposed model integrates spatiotemporal features extracted from top weld pool images along with welding parameters, establishing a deep learning framework based on convolutional neural networks and state space models for more efficient extraction and processing of spatial-temporal information. Furthermore, a reliable method for constructing the dataset is proposed to enhance both robustness and generalization capability of the developed model. Validation results on the test set demonstrate that prediction accuracy for penetration state can reach 99.35%, while prediction error for penetration depth is 1.79 millimeter, and accuracy of reconstructing the weld cross-section is 95.65%. This study provides new insights and methodologies for in-situ quality control strategies in laser penetration welding systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a multi-task deep learning model that uses weld pool images and welding parameters to predict penetration state (99.35% accuracy), penetration depth (1.79mm error), and weld seam morphology (95.65% accuracy) in laser penetration welding, employing CNNs and state space models for spatiotemporal feature extraction.
AI.motivation: In laser penetration welding, accurately assessing penetration state and weld seam morphology is critical for determining weld quality, but existing methods lack the capability to simultaneously predict multiple weld quality metrics with high precision.
AI.method: The model integrates spatiotemporal features from top weld pool images (captured by a CMOS camera) with welding parameters using a deep learning framework combining convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and state space models. A reliable dataset construction method is proposed to enhance robustness and generalization.
AI.result: Prediction accuracy for penetration state reaches 99.35%, prediction error for penetration depth is 1.79mm, and accuracy of reconstructing the weld cross-section is 95.65% on the test set.
AI.conclusion: This multi-task deep learning approach provides new insights and methodologies for in-situ quality control strategies in laser penetration welding systems.

title: Agentic Analysis for Agentic Infrastructure: An LLM-Powered Pipeline for Comparative Governance of DAO and Corporate AI Protocols
summary: As AI agent protocols proliferate, the governance structures shaping their interoperability standards remain empirically underexamined. We introduce an LLM-powered comparative pipeline for large-scale governance discourse analysis, integrating automated annotation, neural topic modeling, and multi-layer network analysis to study socio-technical power structures at scale. We validate it on two contrasting standards for agent interoperability: ERC-8004 (permissionless, on-chain) and Google A2A (corporate-led). Analyzing 4,323 governance participation records, we combine LLM-assisted coding, topic modeling, and multi-layer network analysis to examine how institutional design shapes thematic priorities and community structure. We find that while governance form influences substantive focus, both regimes exhibit comparable levels of participation inequality and community fragmentation. Discourse alignment is denser in the permissionless setting, suggesting that open governance may foster greater thematic convergence despite decentralized participation. These findings illustrate how LLM-assisted methods can advance the empirical study of technology governance, with implications for designing more equitable agentic AI standards. All data and code are openly available.

AI.tldr: An LLM-powered comparative pipeline combining automated annotation, neural topic modeling, and multi-layer network analysis is introduced to study governance structures in AI agent interoperability standards, comparing permissionless (ERC-8004) and corporate-led (Google A2A) regimes across 4,323 records.
AI.motivation: Governance structures shaping AI agent interoperability standards remain empirically underexamined despite the proliferation of agent protocols.
AI.method: LLM-assisted coding, neural topic modeling, and multi-layer network analysis applied to 4,323 governance participation records comparing ERC-8004 (permissionless, on-chain) and Google A2A (corporate-led) standards.
AI.result: Governance form influences substantive focus, but both regimes show comparable participation inequality and community fragmentation. Discourse alignment is denser in the permissionless setting, suggesting open governance fosters thematic convergence despite decentralized participation.
AI.conclusion: LLM-assisted methods can advance empirical study of technology governance, with implications for designing more equitable agentic AI standards.

title: Same Scrutiny, More Time: Eye Tracking Insights into Reviewing LLM-Labelled Code
comment: Accepted at the 41st IEEE/ACM International Conference on Automated Software Engineering (ASE 2026)
summary: Modern software development increasingly involves the use of large language models (LLMs) to generate code. Despite their rapid advancement, LLMs remain prone to errors and hallucinations, emphasizing the importance of careful code inspection. However, in practice, developers' trust in LLM-generated code and their willingness to review it thoroughly may differ from these recommendations. How developers actually behave when reviewing LLM-generated code remains largely unexplored. In this study, we conduct a Wizard-of-Oz experiment to examine how software engineers behave when code is explicitly labeled as LLM-generated during a code review task. We collect both behavioral data and participant feedback through eye-tracking and exit interviews. Combining Bayesian data analysis with qualitative analysis, we found that while the thoroughness of code review did not change for participants, they spent more time fixating on LLM-labelled code, indicating that the label itself influences attention. Practitioners also adapted their review strategy for LLM-labelled code by assessing the code based on specific criteria (e.g., logical correctness), or using the prompt to guide their review. These findings inform LLM-based tool design on labelling while incorporating the prompt as a software artifact. Our study reveals a gap between reviewers' intentions and actual reviewing behaviour, highlighting the need for software companies to revisit their AI policies (particularly regarding LLM-assisted development) to better support developers in reviewing LLM-generated code.

AI.tldr: A Wizard-of-Oz experiment reveals that while labeling code as LLM-generated does not change review thoroughness, it increases developers' visual attention on such code and prompts strategy adaptations, highlighting a gap between review intentions and actual behavior.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used for code generation but remain error-prone, necessitating careful code review. However, how developers actually behave when reviewing code explicitly labeled as LLM-generated is largely unexplored.
AI.method: Wizard-of-Oz experiment with software engineers performing code review on LLM-labeled vs. unlabeled code; data collected via eye-tracking and exit interviews; analyzed using Bayesian data analysis and qualitative analysis.
AI.result: Review thoroughness did not change, but participants spent more time fixating on LLM-labeled code. Practitioners adapted review strategies by evaluating based on specific criteria (e.g., logical correctness) or using prompts to guide review.
AI.conclusion: A gap exists between reviewers' intentions and actual behavior. Findings inform LLM-based tool design regarding labeling and prompt integration, and highlight the need for companies to revisit AI policies to better support LLM-generated code review.

title: Necessary but Not Sufficient: Temperature Control and Reproducibility in LLM-as-Judge Safety Evaluations
summary: LLM-as-judge ("grader") components are now standard in evaluation harnesses, including safety evaluations where a pass/fail verdict may gate downstream deployment decisions. A widespread assumption is that setting the grader's sampling temperature to 0 makes grading deterministic. We test this assumption against a real safety-evaluation codebase (Japan AISI's open-source aisev) and show it fails on two levels. First, the harness invokes its grader without setting temperature or seed; the underlying provider silently applies its default of 1.0, so items near the decision boundary flip pass/fail across identical runs (per-item disagreement up to ~50% over 20 runs). Second, pinning temperature=0 reduces but does not eliminate flips: across 690 API calls spanning two providers, three model tiers, and five sampling configurations, 1-2 of 7 borderline items remain non-reproducible even under forced greedy decoding (top_k=1). Claude Opus 4.7/4.8 has since deprecated temperature entirely, rendering the primary mitigation inapplicable to newer model generations. These findings expose a structural gap: evaluation harnesses that report single-run verdicts without variance or grader-disagreement metrics can present noise as a safety property. We release a reproduction harness (690 calls, 7 conditions) and recommend that harnesses treat grader disagreement as a first-class health metric alongside the scores themselves.

AI.tldr: Setting LLM grader temperature to 0 does not guarantee deterministic safety evaluations; non-reproducibility persists even under greedy decoding, and newer models have deprecated temperature control entirely. The paper recommends treating grader disagreement as a first-class metric.
AI.motivation: The common assumption that setting temperature=0 makes LLM-as-judge grading deterministic is widespread in safety evaluation harnesses, where pass/fail verdicts can gate deployment decisions. The authors test this assumption and find it fails.
AI.method: The authors test Japan AISI's open-source aisev evaluation harness. They analyze two levels: (1) the harness invokes the grader without setting temperature or seed, so the provider defaults to 1.0, causing per-item disagreement up to ~50% across 20 runs near decision boundaries; (2) even with temperature=0 and top_k=1 (forced greedy decoding), 1-2 of 7 borderline items remain non-reproducible across 690 API calls spanning two providers, three model tiers, and five sampling configurations.
AI.result: (1) Without explicit temperature/seed settings, per-item disagreement reached ~50% over 20 runs near the decision boundary. (2) Even with forced greedy decoding (temperature=0, top_k=1), 1-2 out of 7 borderline items remained non-reproducible. (3) Claude Opus 4.7/4.8 deprecated temperature entirely, making the primary mitigation inapplicable to newer models.
AI.conclusion: Evaluation harnesses that report single-run verdicts without variance or grader-disagreement metrics can present noise as a safety property. The authors release a reproduction harness and recommend treating grader disagreement as a first-class health metric alongside scores.

title: wav2tok 2.0: Scalable Audio Tokenization Maintaining Explicit Pairwise Token Alignment for Efficient Audio Retrieval
summary: Learning discrete speech representations that preserve similarity across variable-length utterances is central to query-by-example spoken term detection (QbE-STD). While wav2tok introduced CTC-based sequence alignment to enforce token consistency, its tightly coupled clustering and alignment training recipe limits scalability. We propose wav2tok 2.0, a scalable alignment-aware speech tokenizer built on the BEST-STD backbone. wav2tok 2.0 employs staged training, first learning discriminative, speaker-invariant representations via contrastive learning and vector quantization, and then enforcing pairwise token consistency using a CTC alignment loss and a novel DTW-aligned framewise prediction objective with adaptive weighting. Experiments show that wav2tok 2.0 consistently outperforms BEST-STD and general-purpose tokenizers on QbE-STD while remaining efficient and scalable.

AI.tldr: wav2tok 2.0 is a scalable alignment-aware speech tokenizer for query-by-example spoken term detection that uses staged training (contrastive learning + VQ, then CTC alignment loss + DTW-aligned framewise prediction) and outperforms BEST-STD and general-purpose tokenizers.
AI.motivation: Existing wav2tok has tightly coupled clustering and alignment training that limits scalability. There is a need for a scalable, alignment-aware speech tokenizer that preserves similarity across variable-length utterances for QbE-STD.
AI.method: Staged training: (1) learn discriminative, speaker-invariant representations via contrastive learning and vector quantization; (2) enforce pairwise token consistency using CTC alignment loss and a novel DTW-aligned framewise prediction objective with adaptive weighting.
AI.result: wav2tok 2.0 consistently outperforms BEST-STD and general-purpose tokenizers on QbE-STD while remaining efficient and scalable.
AI.conclusion: The proposed staged training approach with decoupled representation learning and alignment objectives effectively addresses the scalability limitations of prior work, achieving state-of-the-art QbE-STD performance.

title: Beyond Single-Source Cognitive Taskonomy:Multi-Source Task Relations through fMRI Transfer Learning
summary: Cognitive tasks are organized by shared and specialized neural processes. Masked fMRI reconstruction provides a common self-supervised objective for quantifying transfer relations among task states, but existing reconstruction-based taskonomies mainly study one-to-one transfer from a single source task to a target. Here, we extend an fMRI cognitive taskonomy from single-source to multi-source transfer across 23 Human Connectome Project task states and use Boolean Integer Programming (BIP) to analyze budget-constrained task allocation. We train 1,127 task-specific and transfer models. Single-source transfer is directional and paradigm structured: motor states transfer well within the motor paradigm but provide limited support to most non-motor targets, consistent with a shared sensorimotor execution system and effector-specific representations. Multi-source transfer depends on the composition of the source set, suggesting that many-to-one task relations are not fully captured by pairwise taskonomy alone. Across supervision budgets, BIP repeatedly allocates direct supervision to several 0-back and 2-back working-memory states, although these states are not consistently the strongest individual sources. This pattern may reflect the integration of perceptual, attentional, and executive processes in working-memory tasks. Together, these findings reveal a cross-paradigm-limited motor cluster and working-memory states with high priority under the specified global allocation objective. Our study extends reconstruction-based fMRI taskonomy from one-to-one transfer to many-to-one task relations and budget-constrained task dependencies.

AI.tldr: This study extends fMRI cognitive taskonomy from single-source to multi-source transfer across 23 HCP task states using Boolean Integer Programming, revealing that motor states transfer well within-paradigm but poorly across paradigms, working-memory states are prioritized under budget constraints, and many-to-one task relations are not fully captured by pairwise taskonomy alone.
AI.motivation: To extend existing reconstruction-based fMRI taskonomies beyond one-to-one transfer to more realistic many-to-one (multi-source) transfer settings and budget-constrained task allocation, and to understand the structure of neural transfer relations among cognitive tasks.
AI.method: Trained 1,127 task-specific and transfer models on masked fMRI reconstruction across 23 HCP task states. Used Boolean Integer Programming (BIP) to analyze budget-constrained task allocation and compared single-source transfer (directional, paradigm-structured) with multi-source transfer (composition-dependent).
AI.result: Single-source transfer is directional and paradigm-structured: motor states transfer well within the motor paradigm but poorly to non-motor targets. Multi-source transfer depends on source set composition, not fully captured by pairwise metrics. Under budget constraints, BIP repeatedly allocates direct supervision to 0-back and 2-back working-memory states, despite them not being the strongest individual sources, suggesting integration of perceptual, attentional, and executive processes.
AI.conclusion: There exists a cross-paradigm-limited motor cluster and working-memory states with high priority under global allocation objectives. The study extends fMRI taskonomy from one-to-one to many-to-one transfer and budget-constrained task dependencies.

title: Knowledge-augmented Agentic AI for Mental Health Medication Information Seeking
summary: Patients increasingly seek medication information online, yet safety knowledge for psychiatric drugs is split between regulatory adverse-event records, which are authoritative but abstract, and patient narratives, which are experience-near but unvalidated. Integrating them without conflating evidence and anecdote is especially consequential in psychiatry, where poorly contextualised information can amplify fear, nocebo responses, and non-adherence. Here we develop a provenance-aware, knowledge-graph-based multi-agent framework unifying 466,525 Reddit posts, 60,782 WebMD reviews, and twenty years of U.S. FDA Adverse Event Reporting System records for nine antidepressants. A large-language-model entity-recognition pipeline benchmarked against physician annotations reached highest F1 scores of 0.969 for medications and 0.973 for conditions. The two community platforms were far more concordant with each other (overlap up to a Jaccard similarity of 0.905) than with regulatory reports, indicating that patient-generated data form a partly independent safety signal. For sertraline, many adverse events appeared in community sources hundreds of days before the corresponding FDA date. A Neo4j knowledge graph grounded in ATC-N, ICD-10, and MedDRA vocabularies preserves provenance, keeping every claim traceable and regulatory facts distinct from patient experience. These results establish source-aware integration as a route to more auditable psychiatric medication information, with usefulness and patient benefit to be tested prospectively.

AI.tldr: A multi-agent knowledge-graph framework integrating Reddit posts, WebMD reviews, and FDA adverse-event records for nine antidepressants, showing patient-generated data provide partly independent safety signals that often precede regulatory reports, while preserving provenance to distinguish evidence from anecdote.
AI.motivation: Patients seek online medication information, but psychiatric drug safety knowledge is fragmented: regulatory records are authoritative yet abstract, while patient narratives are experiential but unvalidated. Poorly contextualized information can amplify fear, nocebo responses, and non-adherence, motivating a need for integration without conflating evidence and anecdote.
AI.method: A provenance-aware, knowledge-graph-based multi-agent framework integrating 466,525 Reddit posts, 60,782 WebMD reviews, and 20 years of FDA FAERS data for nine antidepressants. An LLM-based entity recognition pipeline was benchmarked against physician annotations, and a Neo4j knowledge graph grounded in ATC-N, ICD-10, and MedDRA vocabularies preserves provenance to keep regulatory facts distinct from patient experience.
AI.result: LLM entity recognition achieved F1 scores of 0.969 for medications and 0.973 for conditions. Community platforms showed high concordance (Jaccard similarity up to 0.905) but were far less concordant with FDA reports, indicating patient-generated data form a partly independent safety signal. For sertraline, many adverse events appeared in community sources hundreds of days before the corresponding FDA date.
AI.conclusion: Source-aware integration of patient-generated and regulatory data establishes a route to more auditable psychiatric medication information; usefulness and patient benefit require prospective testing.

title: ConcoLixir: Reactive LLM Discovery Oracles for Python Concolic Testing
summary: Concolic testing combines concrete execution with symbolic constraint solving, but Python programs expose recurring limits. Library calls can cause symbolic variables to downgrade to concrete values. Regular expressions, checksums, parsers, and other semantic operations can be hard to solve, and exploration can plateau on already covered paths. We present ConcoLixir, a reactive LLM extension for Python concolic execution. The LLM acts as a discovery oracle, not a replacement for the solver or a correctness oracle. It generates initial seeds, proposes concrete inputs after solver failures, and targets uncovered code when coverage stalls. Each candidate is executed concolically, and only observed coverage and collected path constraints guide later exploration. Across synthetic, real-world, and library targets, ConcoLixir improves mean line coverage over the baseline concolic tester without an LLM oracle by 8.6, 15.1, and 17.0 percentage points. The gains are strongest near semantic barriers and library boundaries, and the full evaluation costs \$1.63 in API charges. These results show that bounded LLM discovery can complement symbolic reasoning without replacing it.

AI.tldr: ConcoLixir is a reactive LLM extension for Python concolic execution that acts as a discovery oracle to overcome common limitations (symbolic value downgrading, unsolvable constraints, coverage plateaus). It generates seeds, proposes concrete inputs after solver failures, and targets uncovered code. Across synthetic, real-world, and library targets, it improves mean line coverage by 8.6, 15.1, and 17.0 percentage points over baseline, costing only $1.63 in API charges, showing LLM discovery complements rather than replaces symbolic reasoning.
AI.motivation: Concolic testing of Python suffers from recurring limitations: library calls degrade symbolic variables to concrete values, complex semantic operations (regex, checksums, parsers) are hard to solve, and coverage exploration plateaus on already covered paths. The authors aim to address these gaps without replacing the symbolic solver.
AI.method: ConcoLixir integrates an LLM as a discovery oracle alongside a concolic execution engine. The LLM (1) generates initial seeds, (2) proposes concrete inputs when the solver fails, and (3) targets uncovered code when coverage stalls. All candidates are executed concolically; only observed coverage and path constraints guide further exploration.
AI.result: ConcoLixir improves mean line coverage over the baseline concolic tester (without LLM oracle) by 8.6, 15.1, and 17.0 percentage points across synthetic, real-world, and library targets respectively. Gains are strongest near semantic barriers and library boundaries. Total API cost for full evaluation is $1.63.
AI.conclusion: Bounded LLM discovery can complement symbolic reasoning in concolic testing without replacing it, offering cost-effective coverage improvements where symbolic solvers struggle.

title: Clue-Guided Money Laundering Group Discovery
summary: Money Laundering Group Discovery (MLGD) aims to identify hidden criminal groups and recover their complete structures in large-scale financial networks. Existing graph anomaly detection methods mainly produce node-level risk alerts, while global group discovery methods passively search for suspicious groups over the whole network. Both are mismatched with real Anti-money-laundering (AML) investigations, where analysts usually start from a concrete clue and gradually expand the investigation to recover the responsible group. To address this gap, we propose Clue-Guided Group Discovery (CGGD), where a laundering group is progressively recovered from an initial clue set through analyst interaction. We further propose Clue2Group, a framework that first constructs a compact local investigation context to reduce noise and preserve chain-like and cycle-like laundering structures. It then estimates a clue-conditioned local risk field with a multi-semantic local-temporal GNN, and finally integrates risk, structural, and prior-pattern evidence to recover a coherent laundering group. Experiments on two large-scale AML benchmarks show that Clue2Group provides a practical clue-driven analysis framework for AML investigations, offering a feasible step toward bridging the gap between graph-based AML research and real investigation workflows.

AI.tldr: Clue2Group is a framework for clue-guided money laundering group discovery that progressively recovers criminal structures from initial clues using interaction, local-temporal GNNs, and multi-evidence integration.
AI.motivation: Existing graph anomaly detection methods only produce node-level alerts and global group discovery methods passively search the whole network, both mismatched with real AML investigations where analysts start from a concrete clue and expand gradually.
AI.method: Clue2Group constructs a compact local investigation context to reduce noise while preserving chain-like and cycle-like structures, estimates a clue-conditioned local risk field using a multi-semantic local-temporal GNN, and integrates risk, structural, and prior-pattern evidence to recover coherent laundering groups.
AI.result: Experiments on two large-scale AML benchmarks show Clue2Group provides a practical clue-driven analysis framework for AML investigations.
AI.conclusion: Clue2Group offers a feasible step toward bridging the gap between graph-based AML research and real investigation workflows.

title: Elastic Time: Dynamic Frame Rate Bottlenecks for Neural Audio Coding
summary: Neural audio autoencoders have become a core component of compression, feature extraction, and generation. However, while existing systems support variable bitrate, the vast majority of models still operate at a fixed latent frame-rate, allocating equal temporal budget to regions with very different information density, which can result in unnecessarily long sequences. We introduce Elastic Time, a dynamic frame-rate bottleneck that converts fixed-frame-rate autoencoders to dynamic ones. Our method learns a lightweight latent predictor used to decide which frames can be skipped and later reconstructed, enabling efficient greedy boundary selection at inference. Experiments show our method enables deployment-time rate control while improving efficiency-quality tradeoffs relative to baselines. Overall, we provide a flexible mechanism for adjusting temporal resolution in audio autoencoders, potentially facilitating more efficient downstream modeling for generation and long-context tasks.

AI.tldr: Elastic Time is a dynamic frame-rate bottleneck that converts fixed-frame-rate neural audio autoencoders to variable temporal resolution, using a lightweight predictor to skip/reconstruct frames, enabling deployment-time rate control with improved efficiency-quality tradeoffs.
AI.motivation: Existing neural audio autoencoders operate at a fixed latent frame-rate, allocating equal temporal budget to regions with very different information density, which can result in unnecessarily long sequences and inefficient representation.
AI.method: Elastic Time learns a lightweight latent predictor that decides which frames can be skipped and later reconstructed, enabling greedy boundary selection at inference to dynamically adjust the temporal frame-rate.
AI.result: The method enables deployment-time rate control while improving efficiency-quality tradeoffs relative to baselines.
AI.conclusion: Elastic Time provides a flexible mechanism for adjusting temporal resolution in audio autoencoders, potentially facilitating more efficient downstream modeling for generation and long-context tasks.

title: GeMoE: Gating Entropy is All You Need for Uncertainty-aware Adaptive Routing in MoE-based Large Vision-Language Models
summary: With the increase in model parameters and training data, the instruction following and generalization capabilities of Large VisionLanguage Models (LVLMs) have been significantly improved. Based on the Mixture of Experts (MoE) architecture, LVLMs expand their parameter capacity while maintaining the inference cost. However, traditional MoE methods employ a Top-k static routing strategy, which fails to account for variations in the input and adaptively select the number of experts, resulting in suboptimal resource utilization. In this paper, we propose viewing token routing as an information encoding task, framing dynamic routing as a Minimum Description Length (MDL) problem in encoding By validating the connection between MDL and gating entropy in the MoE scenario, we introduce Gating Entropy-based Uncertainty-aware Adaptive Routing (GeMoE) for MoE. Unlike traditional static or heuristic-based dynamic routing methods, GeMoE explicitly models the trade-off between model complexity and performance. By using gating entropy to assess the complexity of tokens, GeMoE adaptively determines the number of experts each token should engage. On a wide range of backbones and benchmarks, our method achieves 99.5% average performance retention compared to the original static routing, while improving average expert activation sparsity by 36.5%.

AI.tldr: GeMoE introduces an adaptive, uncertainty-aware routing mechanism for Mixture of Experts in LVLMs that treats token routing as a Minimum Description Length problem, using gating entropy to dynamically determine the number of experts per token, achieving 99.5% performance retention while improving expert activation sparsity by 36.5%.
AI.motivation: Traditional Top-k static routing in MoE-based LVLMs fails to account for input variations and cannot adaptively select the number of experts, leading to suboptimal resource utilization.
AI.method: Frame token routing as an MDL problem; establish a connection between MDL and gating entropy; use gating entropy to assess token complexity and adaptively determine the number of experts per token.
AI.result: Achieves 99.5% average performance retention compared to static routing while improving average expert activation sparsity by 36.5% across various backbones and benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling the trade-off between model complexity and performance via gating entropy-based adaptive routing yields better resource utilization without significant performance degradation.

title: Accelerating Skill Assessment in Chess: A Drift-Diffusion-Enhanced Elo Rating System
comment: Accepted at the IEEE Conference on Games (IEEE CoG) 2026
summary: Rating systems such as Elo serve as the gold standard for matchmaking in competitive chess. However, they inherently suffer from response lag due to their exclusive reliance on match outcomes, neglecting the granular quality of gameplay. Nevertheless, incorporating move-by-move information into rating adjustments presents a significant challenge given the substantial noise and the vastness of the game-state space. To address this, we propose the Drift-Diffusion-Enhanced Elo Rating System (DD-Elo), a novel skill assessment framework inspired by the drift diffusion model (DDM) from cognitive neuroscience. By modeling skill expression as a decision-making process, our model integrates move-level data to capture rapid skill fluctuations. We provide a rigorous mathematical derivation proving that DD-Elo maintains a bounded deviation from the traditional Elo system, ensuring theoretical alignment. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DD-Elo adapts to skill changes faster than Elo. Our findings suggest that DD-Elo offers an explainable, highly responsive, and backward-compatible solution for chess rating ecosystems. The implementation code is publicly available at https://github.com/Aquila-zhou1/DD-Elo .

AI.tldr: DD-Elo is a novel chess rating system that enhances traditional Elo by incorporating move-by-move gameplay data via a drift diffusion model, achieving faster skill adaptation while maintaining theoretical alignment with Elo.
AI.motivation: Traditional Elo ratings suffer from response lag because they rely solely on match outcomes and ignore granular gameplay quality. Incorporating move-level information into rating adjustments is challenging due to noise and the vast game-state space.
AI.method: The Drift-Diffusion-Enhanced Elo Rating System (DD-Elo) is inspired by the drift diffusion model (DDM) from cognitive neuroscience. It models skill expression as a decision-making process, integrating move-level data to capture rapid skill fluctuations, with rigorous mathematical derivation ensuring bounded deviation from traditional Elo.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that DD-Elo adapts to skill changes faster than the traditional Elo system while maintaining bounded deviation from it.
AI.conclusion: DD-Elo provides an explainable, highly responsive, and backward-compatible solution for chess rating ecosystems.

title: Quantum Mutant Equivalence via Transpilation
summary: Mutation testing evaluates test suite quality by introducing artificial faults (mutants) and checking whether tests detect (kill) them. A central challenge is the equivalent mutant problem: some mutants are syntactically different from the original program but semantically identical to it and therefore cannot be killed by any test. If left unidentified, such mutants waste testing effort and distort mutation scores. In quantum software, mutation testing is increasingly used, but the equivalent mutant problem remains unsolved. A recent study generated more than 700,000 quantum circuit mutants and found that roughly half survived the available tests, making it unclear whether these survivors reflect weak tests or semantic equivalence. We propose Transpiler-Based Equivalence (TBE), a lightweight approach that identifies equivalent quantum mutants by transpiling original and mutated circuits under the same configuration and comparing their resulting OpenQASM code. We evaluate TBE on 348,299 surviving mutants, 92,011 of which are equivalent; TBE identifies 29,536 of them (32.1%) as equivalent while achieving 100% precision and 82% accuracy.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Transpiler-Based Equivalence (TBE), a lightweight method to identify equivalent mutants in quantum software mutation testing by comparing transpiled OpenQASM code of original and mutated circuits. Applied to 348,299 surviving mutants, TBE identified 29,536 equivalent ones with 100% precision and 82% accuracy.
AI.motivation: The equivalent mutant problem remains unsolved in quantum software mutation testing, where roughly half of over 700,000 generated quantum circuit mutants survived tests, making it unclear whether survivors indicate weak tests or semantic equivalence.
AI.method: TBE transpiles both original and mutated quantum circuits under the same configuration and compares their resulting OpenQASM code to identify equivalent mutants.
AI.result: Applied to 348,299 surviving mutants (92,011 equivalent); TBE identified 29,536 (32.1%) as equivalent with 100% precision and 82% accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Transpiler-Based Equivalence is a precise and accurate lightweight approach for detecting equivalent quantum mutants, addressing a key challenge in quantum mutation testing.

title: Federated Hash Projected Latent Factor Learning
summary: Hash Learning (HL) is an efficient representation learning approach that maps real-valued data into compact binary representations. Traditional HL methods typically require users to upload personal data to a central server, which is incompatible with increasingly stringent data security regulations. Federated Learning (FL) provides a decentralized paradigm for learning globally optimal models without centralizing private data. However, most FL methods rely on transmitting large-scale real-valued gradient information, leading to high communication overhead and potential privacy risks. Integrating HL into FL is a promising solution. Nevertheless, existing HL methods suffer from limited representational capacity of binary codes, which may degrade model accuracy. To address this challenge, we propose a Federated Hash Projected Latent Factor (FHPLF) model. FHPLF introduces three key innovations: (a) replacing real-valued gradient matrices with binary gradient-like matrices, significantly reducing computation, storage, and communication costs while enhancing privacy protection; (b) leveraging Projected Hamming Distance for similarity modeling, which captures the importance of individual binary bits to improve representation capability; and (c) proposing a Secure Binary Gradient Reassembly and Privacy-Enhanced Upload (SBG-PEU) strategy to further reduce the risk of user interaction leakage during transmission. Extensive experiments on four real-world datasets demonstrate that FHPLF consistently outperforms state-of-the-art HL and FL methods, achieving a favorable trade-off among accuracy, efficiency, and privacy preservation.

AI.tldr: FHPLF integrates Hash Learning into Federated Learning by replacing real-valued gradients with binary gradient-like matrices, using Projected Hamming Distance for better similarity modeling, and introducing a secure upload strategy (SBG-PEU) to improve efficiency, privacy, and accuracy trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Traditional Hash Learning requires centralizing personal data, violating data privacy regulations; Federated Learning reduces privacy risks but incurs high communication costs from transmitting real-valued gradients; existing HL methods have limited binary code representation capacity, degrading accuracy.
AI.method: (a) Replaces real-valued gradient matrices with binary gradient-like matrices to reduce computation, storage, and communication costs while enhancing privacy; (b) Uses Projected Hamming Distance for similarity modeling to capture importance of individual binary bits; (c) Proposes Secure Binary Gradient Reassembly and Privacy-Enhanced Upload (SBG-PEU) strategy to reduce interaction leakage risk.
AI.result: On four real-world datasets, FHPLF consistently outperforms state-of-the-art HL and FL methods, achieving a favorable trade-off among accuracy, efficiency, and privacy preservation.
AI.conclusion: FHPLF effectively addresses the representational capacity limitation of binary codes in federated hash learning, demonstrating that binary gradient matrices combined with projected Hamming distance can achieve better accuracy-efficiency-privacy balance than existing approaches.

title: Beyond Aesthetics: Quantifying Information Loss in Turbid Scenes
summary: Visibility in underwater environments degrades rapidly under turbid conditions, yet the effects on computer-vision models remain unclear. This issue is compounded by reliance on synthetic turbidity datasets, which may misrepresent real-world information loss. To address this gap, we introduce the Turbid Underwater Baseline (TUB) dataset, comprising 1,320 images captured under extreme turbidity and over 16,000 high-confidence ground-truth segmentation masks. We additionally propose PCD, a metric derived from phase congruency maps that is invariant to contrast and aims to capture the loss of structural information in real turbidity. We show that PCD correlates strongly with the performance of instance segmentation models on both real and synthetic turbid images, whereas common metrics in the field show weak to no correlation at all. The dataset and relevant code can be found on the project page: https://vap.aau.dk/pcd

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the TUB dataset (1,320 real extreme-turbidity images with 16,000+ segmentation masks) and the PCD metric (phase-congruency-based, contrast-invariant) to measure structural information loss in turbid water. PCD strongly correlates with instance segmentation model performance on both real and synthetic turbid images, unlike common metrics.
AI.motivation: Underwater visibility degrades rapidly under turbid conditions, but the effects on computer-vision models are unclear. Existing synthetic turbidity datasets may misrepresent real-world information loss, creating a gap in reliable evaluation.
AI.method: (1) TUB dataset: 1,320 real extreme-turbidity images with over 16,000 high-confidence ground-truth segmentation masks. (2) PCD metric: derived from phase congruency maps, invariant to contrast, designed to capture structural information loss in real turbidity.
AI.result: PCD correlates strongly with instance segmentation model performance on both real and synthetic turbid images, whereas common metrics in the field show weak to no correlation.
AI.conclusion: The TUB dataset and PCD metric provide a more reliable foundation for evaluating computer-vision models under real turbid underwater conditions, addressing the limitations of synthetic-only evaluation.

title: Governing Actions, Not Agents: Institutional Attestation as a Governance Model for Autonomous AI Systems
summary: Autonomous AI agents may begin to perform consequential, irreversible actions such as clinical prescribing and production software deployment. This paper observes that human institutions have governed powerful autonomous actors not by monitoring their reasoning but by requiring independently attested evidence at the point of consequential action. We formalise this institutional pattern as a computational governance model for AI agent systems. Under the proposed model, an agent retains full autonomy over planning and reasoning but holds no execution authority over designated high-risk actions. Execution is conditional on preconditions that are each independently attested by a separate authoritative source, cryptographically bound to a declared intent, and evaluated by a deterministic policy. Decisions are recorded in a tamper-evident log amenable to independent re-verification. We present a proof-of-concept implementation and illustrate the model with examples from software deployment and clinical prescribing.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a computational governance model for AI agents where they retain full autonomy over planning/reasoning but require independently attested evidence from separate authoritative sources to execute high-risk actions, with decisions recorded in tamper-evident logs.
AI.motivation: Autonomous AI agents may soon perform consequential, irreversible actions (e.g., clinical prescribing, production software deployment), creating a need for governance mechanisms that prevent harm while preserving agent autonomy.
AI.method: The authors formalize a governance model inspired by human institutions: agents have full autonomy over planning but no execution authority over high-risk actions. Execution requires independently attested preconditions from authoritative sources, cryptographically bound to a declared intent, evaluated by a deterministic policy, with decisions logged in a tamper-evident system.
AI.result: A proof-of-concept implementation is presented, with illustrative examples from software deployment and clinical prescribing.
AI.conclusion: The proposed model offers a viable computational governance framework that balances AI agent autonomy with safety, by requiring independent external attestation for consequential actions rather than monitoring internal reasoning.

title: Knowledge-Based Pull Requests: A Trusted Workflow for Agent-Mediated Knowledge Collaboration
summary: AI coding agents are changing the bottleneck in software collaboration: code is increasingly cheap, while understanding intent, negotiating scope, and governing long-term project responsibility remain costly. This paper proposes \emph{Knowledge-Based Pull Requests} (KPR), a trusted workflow for agent-mediated software collaboration across trust boundaries, including open source, enterprise, vendor, contractor, and customer-driven settings. In KPR, an external collaborator's local code, tests, and cleaned agent interaction trace are treated as knowledge sources rather than as the default merge candidate. Agents distill these sources into a human-confirmed knowledge package and render it into reviewer-facing forms such as design memos, risk checklists, test plans, or implementation briefs. A project-owned inner trusted coding agent then regenerates candidate code inside the receiving project's environment under repository context, engineering conventions, tests, and security policy. KPR therefore separates two decisions that traditional pull requests often collapse: whether the knowledge should enter the project, and whether a particular implementation should be merged. We contribute the KPR workflow, a candidate artifact schema, a cost-accounting view, a collaboration gateway architecture, a minimal controlled simulation pilot over seven merged public pull requests, and an evaluation agenda. The pilot shows that KPR packages can be instantiated from real PR material and stress-tested under description ablation, diff ablation, and synthetic poisoned-patch conditions. We position KPR as an empirically testable workflow: its value depends on whether auditable extraction, transformation, and project-side regeneration reduce the cost of understanding and reworking high-context external changes.

AI.tldr: Proposes Knowledge-Based Pull Requests (KPR), a workflow where external code and agent traces are distilled into knowledge packages, then regenerated by a trusted inner agent, separating knowledge acceptance from implementation merging.
AI.motivation: Code is increasingly cheap due to AI coding agents, but understanding intent, negotiating scope, and governing long-term project responsibility remain costly. Traditional PRs collapse knowledge and implementation decisions, causing inefficiency across trust boundaries.
AI.method: KPR workflow: (1) external collaborator's code, tests, and agent traces are treated as knowledge sources; (2) agents distill them into a human-confirmed knowledge package (design memos, risk checklists, test plans, implementation briefs); (3) a project-owned inner agent regenerates code in the project's environment under repository context, conventions, tests, and security policy.
AI.result: A minimal controlled simulation pilot over 7 merged public PRs shows KPR packages can be instantiated from real PR material and stress-tested under description ablation, diff ablation, and synthetic poisoned-patch conditions.
AI.conclusion: KPR is positioned as an empirically testable workflow whose value depends on whether auditable extraction, transformation, and project-side regeneration reduce the cost of understanding and reworking high-context external changes. Contributions include the workflow, artifact schema, cost-accounting view, collaboration gateway architecture, pilot results, and evaluation agenda.

title: Statistical and Structural Approaches to Algorithmic Fairness
summary: Modern machine learning systems have outgrown their origins as isolated predictive constructs, evolving into complex socio-technical architectures that actively mediate human opportunity. As algorithms increasingly determine access to economic and social opportunities, it has become widely recognized that these systems are deeply embedded with the structural inequalities and prejudices of their environments. The field of algorithmic fairness emerged in response to the growing recognition that models optimized for predictive accuracy can systematically disadvantage marginalized groups. Early mitigation strategies, however, rested on fragile simplifications that limited their effectiveness in complex socio-technical environments. This thesis identifies and addresses two fundamental limitations of contemporary fairness paradigms: the reliance on deterministic point estimates for auditing and the treatment of individuals as isolated entities devoid of structural context.

AI.tldr: This thesis identifies and addresses two fundamental limitations of contemporary algorithmic fairness paradigms: reliance on deterministic point estimates for auditing and treating individuals as isolated entities devoid of structural context.
AI.motivation: Modern ML systems mediate human opportunity but are embedded with structural inequalities and prejudices, necessitating a rethinking of algorithmic fairness approaches.
AI.method: The author identifies two key limitations of current fairness paradigms and proposes solutions to address the reliance on deterministic point estimates and the lack of structural context in fairness auditing.
AI.result: Two fundamental limitations identified: (1) deterministic point estimates for auditing are fragile, and (2) treating individuals as isolated entities ignores structural context.
AI.conclusion: Current fairness mitigation strategies rest on fragile simplifications; the field needs to move beyond deterministic point estimates and incorporate structural context to effectively address bias in complex socio-technical systems.

title: Layer-Specific Prompt Fusion Discovery via Differentiable Search in Vision Foundation Models
summary: Visual prompt tuning has emerged as a parameter-efficient fine-tuning approach for adapting large-scale Vision Transformers (ViTs) to downstream tasks. As its learnable prompts are applied in input and feature spaces, prior to jointly going through attention in transformer layers, the most commonly used scheme for fusing image and prompt tokens is concatenation or addition. In this paper, we aim to study a fundamental yet essential problem in visual prompt tuning: whether a single fusion scheme tends to yield better results, and whether that would be beneficial to develop a hybrid fusion scheme. To this end, we formulate the task as a bi-level optimization problem, and solve it leveraging differentiable architecture search. In this context, the learnable prompts and their fusion schemes are jointly optimized. To enrich the search space in the architecture search, we propose two additional fusion schemes, namely, affine transformation and cross-attention, in addition to concatenation and addition. Extensive experiments on 34 datasets spanning VTAB-1k, FGVC, and HTA show consistent gains over prompt-tuning baselines. With a frozen ViT backbone, our method delivers a favorable accuracy--latency--parameter trade-off compared with VPT-Deep and recent variants. Our findings reveal that how prompts fuse with image tokens plays a significant role in visual prompt tuning, and a hybrid fusion fashion can more effectively leverage layer semantics of ViTs, contributing a novel perspective for visual prompt-tuning research.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether a hybrid fusion scheme for combining visual prompts with image tokens outperforms single fusion schemes in visual prompt tuning. The authors formulate this as a bi-level optimization problem solved via differentiable architecture search, proposing two new fusion methods (affine transformation and cross-attention) alongside standard concatenation and addition. Experiments across 34 datasets show consistent improvements, demonstrating that hybrid fusion better leverages ViT layer semantics and achieves favorable accuracy-latency-parameter trade-offs.
AI.motivation: To study whether a single fusion scheme (e.g., concatenation or addition) is optimal for visual prompt tuning, and whether a hybrid fusion scheme that jointly optimizes learnable prompts and their fusion strategies can yield better performance.
AI.method: Formulates the visual prompt tuning task as a bi-level optimization problem, solved via differentiable architecture search (DARTS). The search space includes four fusion schemes: concatenation, addition, affine transformation, and cross-attention. Learnable prompts and fusion schemes are jointly optimized during training.
AI.result: Consistent gains over prompt-tuning baselines across 34 datasets (VTAB-1k, FGVC, HTA). The method delivers a favorable accuracy–latency–parameter trade-off compared with VPT-Deep and recent variants.
AI.conclusion: The way prompts fuse with image tokens significantly impacts visual prompt tuning performance. A hybrid fusion scheme can more effectively leverage layer semantics of Vision Transformers, offering a novel perspective for visual prompt-tuning research.

title: COrigami: An AI Pipeline for Co-Designing Flat-Foldable Visually Recognisable Origami
summary: While generative AI has achieved remarkable success in solving problems with verifiable solutions, generating physical art that satisfies both strict geometric constraints and subjective visual aesthetics remains a challenge. This paper presents an approach to tackle these difficulties in the domain of computational origami, a mathematically rigid environment that grounds artistic design within the equations of flat foldability. We present COrigami, an end-to-end AI-driven pipeline that assists the design cycle by generating crease patterns from natural language. Our pipeline involves generating a semantic stick figure, computing a base packing, solving for a flat-foldable crease pattern, shaping the flat-folded crease pattern, and refining the generated model using reinforcement learning driven by an autonomous aesthetic evaluation loop. Our system acts as a highly effective collaborative assistant, generating structural starting points that human artists can further expand and shape. By integrating algorithmic optimisation with autonomous aesthetic critique, this work demonstrates how AI systems can satisfy multi-objective physical constraints to enable reliable, mathematically grounded co-creativity.

AI.tldr: COrigami is an end-to-end AI pipeline that generates flat-foldable origami crease patterns from natural language descriptions, combining geometric constraint solving with reinforcement learning-driven aesthetic evaluation.
AI.motivation: Existing generative AI excels at verifiable-solution problems but struggles with physical art generation that must simultaneously satisfy strict geometric constraints and subjective visual aesthetics; computational origami provides a mathematically rigid environment to ground artistic design.
AI.method: The pipeline consists of: (1) generating a semantic stick figure from natural language, (2) computing a base packing, (3) solving for a flat-foldable crease pattern, (4) shaping the flat-folded crease pattern, and (5) refining via reinforcement learning driven by an autonomous aesthetic evaluation loop.
AI.result: The system acts as a highly effective collaborative assistant, generating structural starting points that human artists can further expand and shape, demonstrating multi-objective constraint satisfaction.
AI.conclusion: By integrating algorithmic optimisation with autonomous aesthetic critique, this work demonstrates how AI systems can satisfy multi-objective physical constraints to enable reliable, mathematically grounded co-creativity.

title: A Deterministic Control Plane for LLM Coding Agents
comment: 45 pages, 9 figures, 13 tables. Dataset and reproduction scripts: Zenodo DOI 10.5281/zenodo.20780913. Ancillary files include report.json, organizations.txt, and figure-reproduction scripts
summary:
LLM coding harnesses grant agents broad file and shell access, yet the configuration layer that steers them -- rules files, agent definitions, IDE-specific markdown -- is largely unmanaged. A prevalence study of 10,008 public GitHub repositories (n=6,145 agent config files) finds that agent configurations propagate as undeclared shared components: 10.1% of tracked paths are SHA-256 exact duplicates across independent repositories (fork-adjusted, threshold-independent), with 75.5% of clone pairs crossing organisational boundaries. Two further patterns are indicative: configurations are rarely revised (58% single-commit; 0.4 vs 0.6 commits/month age-normalised against CI/CD workflows), and rarely declare permission boundaries (<1% of agent configs vs 33% of Actions workflows, n=31 true positives).
  We propose a deterministic control plane above the harness that maps one-to-one to these gaps. Rel(AI)Build treats agent definitions as a managed supply chain (SHA-256 content addressing, HMAC-stamped lockfiles, hash-chained audit logs); enforces tiered permissions and attack-derived blocklists before LLM invocation; gates feature work through a phase state machine with requirement-to-file-to-test traceability; compiles a single canonical definition to seven IDE targets; and detects prompt drift via Jaccard similarity. Conformance tests on injected violations confirm each mechanism enforces its stated invariant; developer outcomes remain future work. Governance of this layer must be deterministic and tool-agnostic -- not delegated to further LLM orchestration.

AI.tldr: Agent configuration files (rules, agent definitions, IDE markdown) are largely unmanaged, with 10.1% SHA-256 duplicates across repos, 75.5% cross-org clones, 58% single-commit, and <1% permission declarations. Rel(AI)Build proposes a deterministic control plane with content-addressing, HMAC lockfiles, tiered permissions, phase state machines, multi-IDE compilation, and prompt drift detection.
AI.motivation: Agent configurations (rules files, agent definitions, IDE-specific markdown) that steer LLM coding harnesses are unmanaged — they propagate as undeclared shared components, are rarely revised, and rarely declare permission boundaries, creating a governance gap.
AI.method: Prevalence study of 10,008 public GitHub repositories (n=6,145 agent config files) analyzing SHA-256 exact duplicates, fork-adjusted clone rates, commit frequency, and permission declaration rates. Then proposal of Rel(AI)Build: a deterministic control plane with SHA-256 content addressing, HMAC-stamped lockfiles, hash-chained audit logs, tiered permissions, attack-derived blocklists, phase state machine with traceability, multi-IDE compilation, and Jaccard-based prompt drift detection.
AI.result: 10.1% of tracked paths are SHA-256 exact duplicates across independent repos; 75.5% of clone pairs cross organizational boundaries; 58% of configs are single-commit; <1% of agent configs declare permissions vs 33% of Actions workflows (n=31 true positives). Conformance tests confirm each Rel(AI)Build mechanism enforces its invariant.
AI.conclusion: Governance of the agent configuration layer must be deterministic and tool-agnostic — not delegated to further LLM orchestration. Developer outcomes remain future work.

title: Topology-Informed Neural Networks for Flood Detection in Optical and Synthetic Aperture Radar Imagery
summary: Floods frequently impact regions around the world. Rapid and accurate flood detection is crucial for emergency response and timely mitigation of human and economic loss. The expanding availability of satellite data and advances in artificial intelligence have enhanced monitoring of environmental hazards, but many flood events remain challenging to detect because cloud cover obscures optical satellite imagery. Rambour et al. introduced the SEN12-FLOOD dataset and extracted per-image features using a ResNet-50 convolutional neural network backbone, then fed these features into a gated recurrent unit network to show that temporal information can substantially improve accuracy compared to single-image baselines. More recently, Chamatidis et al. showed that a vision transformer can achieve strong performance with popular convolutional architectures. However, these models typically function as opaque black boxes, making it difficult to interpret their decision boundaries, learned features, and internal reasoning, especially in safety-critical domains like remote sensing. In contrast, topological data analysis (TDA) provides a mathematically grounded framework for capturing global structural features of data. TDA has emerged as a powerful tool for analyzing complex imagery, especially imagery with geometrically interpretable structures, of which floods are a prime candidate. In this work, we systematically evaluate topological descriptors for flood detection using the open-source SEN12-FLOOD dataset. By extracting topological features from each image and incorporating them into neural networks, we demonstrate that topological descriptors carry meaningful flood signals independently and complement existing networks to yield more robust and interpretable flood detection systems.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically evaluates topological data analysis (TDA) descriptors for flood detection on the SEN12-FLOOD dataset, showing that topological features carry meaningful flood signals independently and complement neural networks to create more robust and interpretable flood detection systems.
AI.motivation: Existing flood detection models using deep learning (e.g., ResNet + GRU, vision transformers) are opaque black boxes with limited interpretability, which is problematic for safety-critical remote sensing applications where understanding model decisions is important.
AI.method: The authors extract topological descriptors from satellite images in the SEN12-FLOOD dataset using topological data analysis (TDA) and incorporate these features into neural network architectures, comparing performance against standard deep learning baselines.
AI.result: Topological descriptors carry meaningful flood signals independently and complement existing neural network features, leading to more robust and interpretable flood detection systems.
AI.conclusion: TDA provides a mathematically grounded framework for capturing global structural features of flood imagery, and integrating topological descriptors with neural networks yields improved performance and interpretability compared to opaque black-box models.

title: What Do Deepfake Benchmarks Measure? An Audit Using Frozen Self-Supervised Representations
summary: As deepfake generators approach perceptual indistinguishability, reliable detection becomes critical. Yet, detectors that score well on benchmarks routinely fail in the wild. A concerning feedback loop has emerged: benchmarks drive increasingly complex, engineered detectors, yet if those benchmarks do not reflect real-world deepfakes, this complexity may be solving the wrong problem entirely. This raises a prior question: what are these benchmarks actually measuring? We conduct an audit of video, image, and audio deepfake benchmarks using a deliberately simple diagnostic. If a linear probe on frozen, general-purpose self-supervised representations can approximate the performance of a bespoke detector, the benchmark is largely rewarding general modality understanding rather than forensic understanding. This has two implications: the benchmark may not reflect realistic threat models, and it raises the question of whether the bespoke detectors the probe approaches are truly learning forensic understanding. We observe, across three modalities, linear probes on general-purpose self-supervised representations closely approach the performance of bespoke detectors. We further show that generator-level difficulty is partly explained by Frechet geometry in the same representation space. Together, these results support a benchmark-audit view of deepfake detection: before high scores are read as evidence of forensic understanding, it is worth asking how much of the benchmark is already solved by general-purpose representations.

AI.tldr: The paper audits deepfake detection benchmarks across video, image, and audio modalities, finding that linear probes on frozen general-purpose self-supervised representations closely approximate the performance of bespoke detectors. This suggests benchmarks may reward general modality understanding rather than genuine forensic understanding, and that the difficulty of detecting different generators is partly explained by Fréchet geometry in representation space. The authors propose a benchmark-audit view to question whether high scores reflect true forensic capability.
AI.motivation: Current deepfake detectors that score well on benchmarks frequently fail in real-world settings, indicating a misalignment between benchmark evaluation and practical threat models. The authors aim to determine what these benchmarks are actually measuring, questioning whether they reward forensic understanding or merely general modality understanding.
AI.method: The authors audit video, image, and audio deepfake benchmarks using a simple diagnostic: they compare the performance of linear probes trained on frozen general-purpose self-supervised representations against that of bespoke detectors. They also analyze generator-level difficulty via Fréchet geometry in the same representation space.
AI.result: Across three modalities, linear probes on general-purpose self-supervised representations closely approach the performance of bespoke detectors. Generator-level difficulty is partially explained by Fréchet geometry in representation space.
AI.conclusion: The findings support a benchmark-audit view: before interpreting high benchmark scores as evidence of forensic understanding, one must consider how much of the benchmark is already solved by general-purpose representations. Current benchmarks may not reflect realistic threat models, and the complexity of engineered detectors may be solving the wrong problem.

title: The Verification Horizon: No Silver Bullet for Coding Agent Rewards
comment: Authors are listed alphabetically by their first names
summary: A classical intuition holds that verifying a solution is easier than producing one. For today's coding agents, this intuition is being inverted: as foundation models develop stronger reasoning capabilities and engineering harnesses grow more sophisticated, generating complex candidate solutions is no longer difficult -- reliably verifying them has become the harder problem. Every verifier we can build is only a proxy for human intent, never the intent itself. This makes verification subject to a twofold difficulty: first, intent is underspecified by nature, making it inherently hard to faithfully check whether it has been fulfilled; second, during model training, optimization widens the gap between proxy and intent -- manifesting as reward hacking or signal saturation. To address this, we characterize the quality of verification signals along three dimensions -- scalability, faithfulness, and robustness -- and argue that achieving all three simultaneously is the central challenge. We further study four reward constructions: a test verifier for general coding tasks, a rubric verifier for frontend tasks, the user as verifier for real-world agent tasks, and an automated agent verifier for long-horizon tasks. Across different task types and policy capability levels, we conduct in-depth analysis and experiments on the core challenges of reward design and how to more effectively leverage reward signals. Experiments show that targeted verification design can effectively suppress reward hacking, improve task completion quality, and achieve significant gains across multiple internal and public benchmarks. These experiences collectively point to a core observation: no fixed reward function can remain effective as policy capability continues to grow; and verification must co-evolve with the generator.

AI.tldr: Verification is becoming harder than generation for coding agents. The paper characterizes verification quality along scalability, faithfulness, and robustness, analyzes four reward constructions, and finds that verification must co-evolve with generator capability as no fixed reward function remains effective.
AI.motivation: The classical intuition that verifying is easier than producing is being inverted for coding agents: generating solutions has become easy while reliable verification has become the harder problem. Additionally, every verifier is only a proxy for human intent, making verification subject to the underspecification of intent and the widening gap between proxy and intent during optimization.
AI.method: Characterizes verification signal quality along three dimensions (scalability, faithfulness, robustness). Studies four reward constructions: test verifier (general coding), rubric verifier (frontend tasks), user as verifier (real-world agent tasks), and automated agent verifier (long-horizon tasks). Conducts experiments across task types and policy capability levels.
AI.result: Targeted verification design can effectively suppress reward hacking, improve task completion quality, and achieve significant gains across multiple internal and public benchmarks. No fixed reward function can remain effective as policy capability grows.
AI.conclusion: Verification must co-evolve with the generator; a static reward function becomes ineffective as policy capability advances.

title: Are LLMs Ready for Anti-Pattern Detection in Microservice Architectures?
summary: Microservice systems are prone to recurrent architectural anti-patterns (APs) that hinder maintainability, evolvability, and operational quality. Most existing AP detection approaches rely on static analysis and handcrafted rules, which can be effective but are often tool-dependent, limited to explicitly encoded detection logic, and difficult to adapt to heterogeneous repositories. In this paper, we investigate whether large language models (LLMs) are ready to support architectural anti-pattern detection in microservice architectures through a prompt-based analysis pipeline over static repository artifacts. We evaluate three general-purpose LLMs on a curated benchmark of microservice repositories annotated with 16 architectural anti-patterns, and compare their performance against the state-of-the-art static-analysis tool MARS using a uniform evaluation protocol based on precision and recall. Our results show that LLMs can provide useful support for anti-pattern detection, achieving competitive performance on several anti-patterns, especially when the relevant evidence is local, heterogeneous, or semantically rich.
