At the same time, they exhibit clear limitations on anti-patterns that require explicit structural or cross-service dependency evidence, where static analysis remains more reliable. These findings suggest that LLMs are not yet a replacement for traditional analyzers, but already represent a promising complementary aid for architectural assessment in microservice systems.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates whether LLMs can detect architectural anti-patterns in microservice systems via prompt-based analysis of static repository artifacts, finding that LLMs offer competitive but complementary performance to traditional static-analysis tools like MARS.
AI.motivation: Microservice systems suffer from recurrent architectural anti-patterns that hinder maintainability, evolvability, and operational quality. Existing detection approaches rely on static analysis and handcrafted rules, which are tool-dependent, limited to explicitly encoded logic, and difficult to adapt to heterogeneous repositories.
AI.method: The authors investigate LLM-based architectural anti-pattern detection via a prompt-based analysis pipeline over static repository artifacts. They evaluate three general-purpose LLMs on a curated benchmark of microservice repositories annotated with 16 architectural anti-patterns, comparing performance against the state-of-the-art static-analysis tool MARS using precision and recall.
AI.result: LLMs achieve competitive performance on several anti-patterns, particularly those with local, heterogeneous, or semantically rich evidence. However, they show clear limitations on anti-patterns requiring explicit structural or cross-service dependency evidence, where static analysis remains more reliable.
AI.conclusion: LLMs are not yet a replacement for traditional static analyzers but represent a promising complementary aid for architectural anti-pattern detection in microservice systems.

title: A General Framework for Learning Algebraic Properties from Cayley Graphs using Graph Neural Networks
summary: A Graph Neural Network (GNN) framework for predicting the solvability of finite groups from their Cayley graph representations was introduced in [1]. In the present work, we generalize this approach and develop a property-independent framework for learning algebraic properties of finite groups directly from Cayley graphs. As representative case studies, we consider abelianity, nilpotency, and solvability. Using a common GNN architecture and training pipeline, we investigate the extent to which algebraic structure can be recovered from graph-based representations alone. Results on a collection of finite groups drawn from several families demonstrate that the framework successfully learns and distinguishes multiple algebraic properties from their associated Cayley graphs. These findings suggest that substantial algebraic information is encoded in graph representations and can be extracted through GNNs. More broadly, the proposed framework provides a proof of concept for applying graph representation learning to the study of algebraic properties of finite groups.

AI.tldr: The paper generalizes a prior GNN framework to learn multiple algebraic properties (abelianity, nilpotency, solvability) of finite groups directly from Cayley graphs, showing that algebraic structure can be recovered from graph representations.
AI.motivation: To generalize the previous property-specific GNN approach for predicting solvability into a property-independent framework capable of learning various algebraic properties of finite groups directly from Cayley graph representations.
AI.method: The authors develop a property-independent GNN framework with a common architecture and training pipeline, applied to Cayley graph representations of finite groups drawn from several families. They test on three representative properties: abelianity, nilpotency, and solvability.
AI.result: The framework successfully learns and distinguishes multiple algebraic properties (abelianity, nilpotency, solvability) from Cayley graphs across diverse families of finite groups, demonstrating that algebraic information is encoded in graph representations.
AI.conclusion: Substantial algebraic information about finite groups is encoded in Cayley graphs and can be extracted via GNNs, providing a proof of concept for applying graph representation learning to study algebraic properties of finite groups.

title: Staying VIGILant: Mitigating Visual Laziness via Counterfactual Visual Alignment in MLLMs
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) extend large language models (LLMs) with visual perception, enabling joint reasoning over images and text. Despite inheriting strong reasoning capabilities from LLMs, they remain prone to hallucinations that contradict their visual inputs. Mechanistic studies indicate that this weakness stems from visual laziness: MLLMs encode the correct visual evidence internally, but overly rely on strong language priors during response. Existing alignment methods, such as direct preference optimization, primarily optimize outcome-level rewards based on text. This introduces an optimization bias toward linguistic shortcuts, leading to responses that often contradict the visual evidence. To address this, we propose Visual Information Gain In aLignment (VIGIL), a reinforcement-learning (RL) post-training framework that shifts the focus from numerical reward fitting to causal visual grounding. VIGIL introduces a geometric constraint that explicitly maximizes the mutual information between the visual input and the generated response. We achieve this by penalizing "blind confidence" instances where the model remains improperly certain even when textual-visual attention is masked to create a counterfactual blind state. Extensive experiments show that VIGIL consistently outperforms recent alignment methods across hallucination and reasoning benchmarks without compromising text-only capabilities. Our approach matches the full-data performance of state-of-the-art methods using only 25% of the preference data and even demonstrates emergent spatial grounding capabilities without explicit bounding box supervision.

AI.tldr: VIGIL is an RL-based post-training framework for MLLMs that reduces visual hallucinations by maximizing mutual information between visual input and generated response through a geometric constraint that penalizes "blind confidence" instances.
AI.motivation: MLLMs suffer from "visual laziness" — they encode correct visual evidence internally but over-rely on language priors during response generation. Existing alignment methods (e.g., DPO) optimize outcome-level rewards, introducing bias toward linguistic shortcuts that contradict visual evidence.
AI.method: VIGIL introduces a geometric constraint that maximizes mutual information between visual input and generated response by penalizing "blind confidence" instances where the model remains improperly certain even when textual-visual attention is masked to create a counterfactual blind state, using reinforcement learning for post-training alignment.
AI.result: VIGIL consistently outperforms recent alignment methods on hallucination and reasoning benchmarks without compromising text-only capabilities. It matches state-of-the-art full-data performance using only 25% of preference data and demonstrates emergent spatial grounding without explicit bounding box supervision.
AI.conclusion: Shifting alignment from outcome-level reward fitting to causal visual grounding effectively mitigates hallucinations in MLLMs while improving data efficiency and enabling emergent capabilities like spatial grounding.

title: How Do Tool-Augmented LLM Agents Perform on Real-World Energy Analytics Tasks?
summary:
Agentic benchmarks have emerged across general-purpose and domain-specific settings, including finance, coding, law, and drug discovery, yet energy-domain evaluations remain largely limited to static knowledge recall. This is a critical gap for a sector that requires live data retrieval, specialized regulatory and market knowledge, and multi-step quantitative reasoning under real-world constraints.
  We present an empirical study of tool-augmented LLM agents on real-world energy market analytics tasks. Our evaluation environment includes 243 expert-curated problems across three categories: (1) Market Data Retrieval and Analysis, (2) Knowledge Retrieval and Interpretation, and (3) Advanced Quantitative Modeling and Decision Analytics. Tasks include price and demand analysis, tariff impact modeling, asset revenue and returns estimation, hedging strategy analysis, and optimization modeling, with problems spanning multiple difficulty levels.
  Agents are equipped with a configurable suite of domain tools, including live electricity market APIs for major U.S. ISOs, regulatory docket search, utility tariff databases, asset optimization models, and retrieval-augmented generation over energy market documents. We assess agent responses using a multi-dimensional evaluation protocol that scores approach correctness, answer accuracy, attribute alignment, and source validity, with category-aware routing to match scoring criteria to question type. We evaluate both closed-source and open-source LLMs, providing a comparative analysis of how model capability and domain tooling interact in a high-stakes professional domain. Key artifacts are publicly released to support reproducibility and future research.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an empirical study of tool-augmented LLM agents on real-world energy market analytics tasks, introducing a benchmark of 243 expert-curated problems across three categories with domain-specific tools (live APIs, regulatory search, tariff databases, etc.) and a multi-dimensional evaluation protocol. It compares closed and open-source LLMs in this high-stakes domain.
AI.motivation: Existing agentic benchmarks in finance, coding, law, and drug discovery lack energy-domain evaluations, which remain limited to static knowledge recall. This is a critical gap for a sector requiring live data retrieval, specialized regulatory/market knowledge, and multi-step quantitative reasoning under real-world constraints.
AI.method: Created 243 expert-curated problems across three categories (Market Data Retrieval & Analysis, Knowledge Retrieval & Interpretation, Advanced Quantitative Modeling & Decision Analytics). Equipped agents with configurable domain tools (live ISO APIs, regulatory docket search, tariff databases, asset optimization models, RAG over energy documents). Evaluated using multi-dimensional protocol scoring approach correctness, answer accuracy, attribute alignment, and source validity with category-aware routing. Compared closed and open-source LLMs.
AI.result: Comparative analysis of how model capability and domain tooling interact in the energy sector, with key artifacts publicly released for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: Tool-augmented LLM agents can address real-world energy market analytics tasks, and the benchmark and evaluation framework provide a foundation for future research in domain-specific agentic evaluation.

title: CLIR: Liveness-Driven and Structure-Aware Fuzzing for the Cranelift Compiler
summary:
Modern compilers are complex software systems that must correctly translate high-level programming languages into machine code across multiple architectures. Cranelift, a fast and modern compiler backend originally developed for WebAssembly and recently adopted as an experimental backend for Rust, has gained increasing importance due to its superior compilation speed compared to LLVM and comprehensive multi-architecture support, including x86-64, AArch64, s390x, and RISCV64. However, despite decades of development in compiler testing, testing Cranelift still presents unique challenges, including (1) constructing valid IR under the strict enforcement of SSA form, (2) generating sequences with sufficient computational density to stress backend components, and (3) balancing broad backend coverage with efficient root cause analysis across heterogeneous architectures.
  To address these challenges, we propose CLIR, a differential testing framework that integrates a syntax-preserving hierarchical generation strategy to guarantee SSA validity, a liveness-guided instruction refinement mechanism to maximize computational density, and a diagnosis-guided cross-architecture adaptation scheme to facilitate efficient root cause analysis across heterogeneous backends. Our comprehensive evaluation demonstrates that CLIR significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art baselines, detecting 8x, 24x, and 8x more unique bugs than cranelift-fuzzgen, wasm-smith, and WASMaker, respectively, while RustSmith uncovered no bugs. Consequently, within 72 hours of testing, CLIR discovered 24 bugs spanning all target architectures, with 21 confirmed and 9 fixed.

AI.tldr: CLIR is a differential testing framework for Cranelift that uses syntax-preserving hierarchical generation, liveness-guided instruction refinement, and cross-architecture adaptation to detect 8x-24x more bugs than existing baselines, finding 24 bugs across all architectures within 72 hours.
AI.motivation: Despite advances in compiler testing, Cranelift poses unique challenges: constructing valid SSA-form IR, generating computationally dense instruction sequences, and achieving broad backend coverage with efficient root cause analysis across heterogeneous architectures.
AI.method: CLIR integrates three mechanisms: (1) syntax-preserving hierarchical generation to guarantee SSA validity, (2) liveness-guided instruction refinement to maximize computational density, and (3) diagnosis-guided cross-architecture adaptation for efficient root cause analysis.
AI.result: CLIR detects 8x, 24x, and 8x more unique bugs than cranelift-fuzzgen, wasm-smith, and WASMaker respectively (RustSmith found none). In 72 hours, it discovered 24 bugs across all target architectures, with 21 confirmed and 9 fixed.
AI.conclusion: The proposed CLIR framework effectively addresses Cranelift-specific testing challenges through targeted generation and adaptation strategies, substantially outperforming existing methods and demonstrating practical value by finding real, confirmed bugs in a production compiler backend.

title: Fast LeWorldModel
summary: Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs), including recent LeWorldModel (LeWM), have become a promising foundation for reconstruction-free visual world models. For visual planning, however, LeWM evaluates candidate action sequences by repeatedly applying a local one-step latent transition model. This autoregressive rollout makes planning computationally expensive and exposes the predicted trajectory to accumulated latent errors as the horizon grows. We propose Fast LeWorldModel (Fast-LeWM), a fast latent world model that replaces repeated local rollout with action-prefix prediction. Given the current latent and a candidate action sequence, Fast-LeWM encodes its prefixes and predicts the future latents reached after executing those prefixes in parallel. By making action prefixes the basic prediction unit, Fast-LeWM directly models action effects accumulated to different extents over multiple horizons. This prefix-level supervision forces the model to learn how states continuously evolve under different action prefixes, rather than only fitting one-step state transitions. During planning, the predictor can use the last prefix token from the encoded action sequence to evaluate the corresponding future latent without explicitly rolling through each intermediate imagined state. Across multiple tasks, Fast-LeWM improves average success over LeWM while substantially reducing planning time, achieving lower open-loop latent loss whose growth becomes significantly slower as the rollout horizon increases.

AI.tldr: Fast-LeWM replaces the expensive autoregressive rollout of JEPA-based world models with parallel action-prefix prediction, reducing planning time and accumulated latent errors while improving task success rates.
AI.motivation: Existing JEPA-based visual world models like LeWM rely on autoregressive one-step latent transitions for planning, which is computationally expensive and suffers from accumulated latent errors as horizon grows.
AI.method: Fast-LeWM encodes action prefixes and predicts future latents for all prefixes in parallel. The model learns state evolution under different action prefixes directly, and during planning uses the last prefix token to evaluate future latents without intermediate rollout.
AI.result: Fast-LeWM achieves higher average success rates than LeWM across multiple tasks, with substantially reduced planning time and lower open-loop latent loss whose growth significantly slows with increasing horizon.
AI.conclusion: Action-prefix prediction is an effective alternative to autoregressive rollout in JEPA-based world models, improving both efficiency and planning quality by enabling parallel evaluation and reducing error accumulation.

title: DinoLink: A Token-Centric Representation Compression Framework for Bandwidth-Constrained Collaborative V2X Perception
summary: High-precision remote perception is often hindered by the severe bandwidth constraints of Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) networks. We propose \textit{DinoLink}, a token-centric compression framework that replaces raw pixel streaming with discrete semantic communication for vehicle-cloud collaborative inference. DinoLink employs a dual-sparsity architecture: a saliency-aware selector prunes redundant background tokens, while a Residual Vector Quantization (RVQ) module collapses features into compact codebook indices. By transmitting only lightweight indices and positional priors, DinoLink achieves a $139\times$ bitrate reduction compared to uncompressed transmission while maintaining a competitive 32.8\% mAP on the nuScenes dataset. Deployment simulations further demonstrate a $34.5\times$ acceleration in narrow-band environments, such as LoRa. Our results substantiate DinoLink as a robust, bandwidth-efficient frontend for high-fidelity remote perception in constrained V2X scenarios. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/UGA-MOBILITY-LAB/dino_link.

AI.tldr: DinoLink is a token-centric compression framework for V2X remote perception that uses saliency-aware token pruning and Residual Vector Quantization to replace raw pixel streaming with discrete semantic communication, achieving 139× bitrate reduction and 34.5× acceleration in narrow-band environments while maintaining competitive accuracy.
AI.motivation: High-precision remote perception for V2X networks is severely hindered by bandwidth constraints, making raw pixel streaming impractical for vehicle-cloud collaborative inference.
AI.method: DinoLink employs a dual-sparsity architecture: (1) a saliency-aware selector that prunes redundant background tokens, and (2) a Residual Vector Quantization (RVQ) module that collapses features into compact codebook indices, transmitting only lightweight indices and positional priors.
AI.result: Achieves 139× bitrate reduction compared to uncompressed transmission while maintaining 32.8% mAP on nuScenes, and demonstrates 34.5× acceleration in narrow-band environments like LoRa.
AI.conclusion: DinoLink is a robust, bandwidth-efficient frontend for high-fidelity remote perception in constrained V2X scenarios; code is publicly available.

title: What We are Missing in Multimodal LLM Evaluation?
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) can process diverse inputs, e.g., text, images, audio, and video, and generate textual responses. While their capabilities have advanced rapidly, evaluation of such models has not kept pace. Most existing evaluation benchmarks are limited to isolated tasks and reveal little about whether a model integrates information across modalities. We examine current means for evaluating MLLMs and review the existing benchmark taxonomy to identify gaps, including temporal-spatial coherence, physical world understanding, multimodal consistency, and selective attention. Addressing these gaps is essential for measuring real progress in multimodal intelligence and exposing capability boundaries.

AI.tldr: This paper reviews evaluation benchmarks for Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), finding that most existing benchmarks focus on isolated tasks rather than cross-modal integration. It identifies key gaps in evaluation—temporal-spatial coherence, physical world understanding, multimodal consistency, and selective attention—and argues that addressing these is essential for measuring real progress in multimodal intelligence.
AI.motivation: Evaluation of MLLMs has not kept pace with their rapid advancement. Existing benchmarks are limited to isolated tasks and fail to assess whether models truly integrate information across modalities, motivating the need to identify gaps in the current evaluation landscape.
AI.method: The authors examine current evaluation approaches for MLLMs and review the existing benchmark taxonomy to identify specific gaps in assessment coverage.
AI.result: Four key evaluation gaps are identified: (1) temporal-spatial coherence, (2) physical world understanding, (3) multimodal consistency, and (4) selective attention.
AI.conclusion: Addressing these identified gaps is essential for measuring real progress in multimodal intelligence and exposing the capability boundaries of MLLMs.

title: To Run or Not to Run: Analyzing the Cost-Effectiveness of Code Execution in LLM-Based Program Repair
comment: Accepted to the ACM SIGSOFT International Symposium on Software Testing and Analysis (ISSTA 2026). 23 pages
summary: LLM-based agents for program repair are increasingly built on a "generate-run-revise" paradigm, iteratively executing tests to evaluate and refine patches. This execution-based approach has become standard practice in state-of-the-art systems. However, executions can be time-consuming and expensive, yet their impact on these agents remains underexplored. In this paper, we conduct a two-stage empirical study over execution behavior in LLM-based program repair. To characterize execution behavior at scale, we first analyze 7,745 agent traces from SWE-bench leaderboard submissions. Second, we evaluate 3,000 end-to-end repair attempts across 200 SWE-bench instances and three agents (Claude Code, Codex, and the open-source OpenCode) under four execution paradigms, which allows for a fine-grained comparison of performance and cost. Our analysis reveals three key observations: (1) Code execution is used across all agents and models analyzed, with an average of 8.8 test runs per task. Execution behavior varies substantially across agents and models, with frequency ranging from 2 to 19 per task, and late-stage executions consistently achieve higher success rates than early-stage ones. (2) Execution restrictions have little effect on repair success: on commercial agents with SOTA models the resolve-rate gap between Prohibited and Unrestricted is only 1.25 percentage points and not statistically significant, while Prohibited saves substantial token and wall-clock cost. (3) Execution benefit is concentrated rather than uniform. These patterns suggest that current agents apply execution indiscriminately, paying its cost on instances where it provides little benefit. Execution, therefore, should be treated as a resource with an explicit cost-benefit tradeoff, not a default capability.

AI.tldr: LLM-based program repair agents rely heavily on iterative code execution (avg 8.8 runs/task), but this execution is often wasteful. A study of 7,745 agent traces and 3,000 repair attempts shows that restricting execution barely impacts repair success (only 1.25 pp drop for SOTA models) while saving significant cost, and execution benefits are concentrated rather than uniform.
AI.motivation: Code execution is widely used in LLM-based program repair agents but its actual impact on performance and cost is underexplored. The authors aim to characterize execution behavior and evaluate whether the standard "execute all patches" approach is efficient or wasteful.
AI.method: Two-stage empirical study: (1) Analyzed 7,745 agent traces from SWE-bench leaderboard submissions to characterize execution behavior at scale. (2) Evaluated 3,000 end-to-end repair attempts across 200 SWE-bench instances using three agents (Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode) under four execution paradigms, comparing performance and cost.
AI.result: (1) Execution is used across all agents/models with avg 8.8 test runs/task; late-stage executions have higher success rates than early-stage. (2) Restricting execution has negligible effect on repair success (1.25 pp gap for SOTA models) but saves substantial token and wall-clock cost. (3) Execution benefits are concentrated rather than uniformly distributed across instances.
AI.conclusion: Current agents apply execution indiscriminately, paying its cost on instances where it provides little benefit. Execution should be treated as a resource with explicit cost-benefit tradeoffs, not a default capability.

title: Dataset Usage Inference without Shadow Models or Held-out Data
comment: Accepted at the 2nd Workshop on Synthetic & Adversarial ForEnsics (SAFE), CVPR 2026 (non-archival)
summary: How much of my data was used to train a machine learning model? Dataset Usage Inference (DUI) aims to answer this by estimating what fraction of a dataset contributed to a model's training. However, existing DUI methods rely on assumptions that rarely hold in practice: they require training expensive shadow models to imitate the target model, and they assume access to both known training samples and an in-distribution held-out set confirmed to be absent from training. These conditions make current approaches impractical for modern large models and real data ownership disputes. We introduce a practical DUI framework that removes these constraints. Our method requires neither shadow models nor real held-out data. Instead, it generates synthetic non-member samples, extracts diverse membership signals, and casts DUI as a mixture proportion estimation problem to estimate what share of the candidate dataset was used during training. Experiments on large image generative models show that our method reliably quantifies dataset usage, providing a practical tool for data owners to determine how much of their data was used to train a model.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a practical Dataset Usage Inference (DUI) framework that estimates what fraction of a dataset contributed to training a model, without requiring shadow models or real held-out data. It uses synthetic non-member samples, diverse membership signals, and mixture proportion estimation to quantify dataset usage, demonstrating effectiveness on large image generative models.
AI.motivation: Existing DUI methods are impractical because they rely on training expensive shadow models and require access to both known training samples and an in-distribution held-out set confirmed absent from training. These constraints make them unsuitable for modern large models and real data ownership disputes.
AI.method: The method generates synthetic non-member samples, extracts diverse membership signals, and formulates DUI as a mixture proportion estimation problem to estimate the fraction of a candidate dataset used during training.
AI.result: Experiments on large image generative models show that the method reliably quantifies dataset usage, providing a practical tool for data owners.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework removes key practical constraints of prior DUI methods (no shadow models, no real held-out data), offering a scalable and practical solution for dataset usage quantification in modern machine learning.

title: Neural Voxel Dynamics: Learning Implicit 3D Physics via Volumetric Feature Advection
summary: We present a self-supervised framework for learning implicit 3D physical dynamics directly from video-derived supervisory signals. While current generative video models achieve high visual fidelity, they lack a 3D geometric foundation, often resulting in physical inconsistencies and a failure to maintain object permanence. We address this by shifting the predictive bottleneck from 2D image space to a `lifted' 3D Volumetric Latent Space. Our method unprojects semantic features from a Video Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (V-JEPA) into a voxelized grid, grounded by monocular depth priors. This lifting enables a Volumetric Feature Advection to learn an action-conditioned transition operator that treats physics as a spatio-temporal state advection problem, i.e., learn implicit 3D physics. Unlike state-of-the-art hybrid models that rely on explicit classical simulators for training and/or inference, our architecture tracks material states implicitly within high-dimensional V-JEPA features. This allows for the emergent simulation of heterogeneous phenomena (e.g., rigid body motion in fluid flow) within a single, unified pipeline. Supervised solely via end-to-end video-derived signal plus action conditions, without access to physics engine internal states, labels, or surrogate models, our model demonstrates good long-term structural stability and physical plausibility on multiple benchmarks (CLEVERER, PhysInOne, PhysGaia). We believe that this work opens a scalable pathway toward general-purpose dynamic world models that internalize the 3D invariants of the physical world solely through passive observation of monocular videos.

AI.tldr: A self-supervised framework that learns implicit 3D physics from video by lifting V-JEPA features into a volumetric latent space, enabling physically plausible dynamics simulation without explicit physics engines or 3D supervision.
AI.motivation: Current generative video models lack 3D geometric foundations, leading to physical inconsistencies and failure to maintain object permanence. The authors aim to learn physically plausible 3D dynamics directly from monocular video without relying on explicit physics simulators.
AI.method: The method unprojects V-JEPA semantic features into a 3D voxel grid using monocular depth priors, creating a Volumetric Latent Space. A Volumetric Feature Advection learns an action-conditioned transition operator that treats physics as spatio-temporal state advection, all trained end-to-end from video-derived signals without physics engine internal states.
AI.result: The model demonstrates good long-term structural stability and physical plausibility on CLEVERER, PhysInOne, and PhysGaia benchmarks, including simulation of heterogeneous phenomena like rigid body motion in fluid flow within a unified pipeline.
AI.conclusion: This work opens a scalable pathway toward general-purpose dynamic world models that internalize 3D physical invariants solely through passive observation of monocular videos.

title: OpenFinGym: A Verifiable Multi-Task Gym Environment for Evaluating Quant Agents
summary: Although large language model agents are increasingly applied to quantitative-finance workflows, their evaluation remains fragmented across isolated tasks, while the financial relevance of benchmark tasks is often overlooked. Yet financial workflows are inherently multi-stage, spanning interdependent tasks such as forecasting, strategy construction, risk management, and trading. Existing platforms typically focus on a single task, and can therefore overstate agent competence and fail to reveal weaknesses in generalization, real-market interaction, and financially meaningful decision-making. We introduce OpenFinGym, a unified gym environment for quantitative-finance agent development that covers forecasting, market generation, real-time trading, and fraud detection under a single execution and verification interface. OpenFinGym additionally provides an automated task-construction pipeline that turns quantitative finance publications into executable task packages; a containerised runtime with a host-side verifier service that supports scalable agent rollouts and prevents runtime train-test leakage; a paper trading engine with a low-latency data-stream design; deferred-resolution support for long-horizon and event-market forecasts; and integration for SFT and RL post-training

AI.tldr: OpenFinGym is a unified gym environment for quantitative-finance agent development that covers multiple interdependent tasks (forecasting, market generation, real-time trading, fraud detection) under a single interface, with automated task construction from finance publications, containerized runtime, paper trading engine, deferred-resolution support, and SFT/RL integration.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluation of LLM agents in quantitative finance is fragmented across isolated tasks, often overlooking the inherently multi-stage and interdependent nature of financial workflows (forecasting, strategy construction, risk management, trading), leading to overstatement of agent competence and failure to reveal weaknesses in generalization, real-market interaction, and financially meaningful decision-making.
AI.method: OpenFinGym provides: (1) a unified execution and verification interface covering forecasting, market generation, real-time trading, and fraud detection; (2) an automated task-construction pipeline that converts quantitative finance publications into executable task packages; (3) a containerized runtime with host-side verifier for scalable agent rollouts and preventing train-test leakage; (4) a paper trading engine with low-latency data-stream design; (5) deferred-resolution support for long-horizon and event-market forecasts; and (6) integration for SFT and RL post-training.
AI.result: The paper introduces OpenFinGym as a platform but does not present specific experimental results in this abstract; it describes the system's capabilities and design.
AI.conclusion: OpenFinGym addresses the fragmentation in quantitative-finance agent evaluation by providing a comprehensive, integrated environment that supports multi-stage workflows, automated task generation, and scalable deployment, thereby enabling more realistic assessment of agent capabilities in financial settings.

title: How Much Static Structure Do Code Agents Need? A Study of Deterministic Anchoring
comment: Accepted to the ACM SIGSOFT International Symposium on Software Testing and Analysis (ISSTA 2026). 21 pages
summary: LLM-based code agents navigate repositories through keyword search but miss the structural relationships, such as call graphs, inheritance hierarchies, and configuration dependencies, that define how software actually works. This makes agent navigation stochastic and difficult to reproduce across runs. We investigate whether lightweight static analysis can provide deterministic anchors for these agents: stable structural facts injected as plain-text comments that constrain probabilistic exploration and make navigation more predictable. Starting from a strong baseline, Codex from OpenAI, we systematically inject varying granularities of structural annotations and measure their effects on localization, trajectory behavior, and run-to-run stability. Our study identifies what we call the deterministic anchoring effect: static structure helps less by making agents "smarter" and more by making their navigation disciplined and reproducible. Three observations support this finding: (1) Anchoring works: lightweight call/inheritance topology improves function-level localization (+2.2pp Func@5) and shortens trajectories (-1.6 interaction rounds); (2) Anchoring is scale-sensitive: the optimal granularity and directionality depend on repository characteristics, where denser semantics show diminishing returns and hub-heavy projects benefit from inverse-only links that expose "who-calls-me" without forward edges; (3) Anchoring stabilizes: tags raise link-following rate from 0.15-0.18 to 0.21-0.24, roughly halve run-to-run variance, and improve single-run reliability (Pass@1 +3.4 pp) on medium-scale repositories, at the cost of roughly 10% more input tokens. These observations suggest practical guidelines: default to lightweight topology on medium projects, prune forward edges in large repositories, and reserve dense tags for implicit-dependency cases.

AI.tldr: Lightweight static analysis annotations (e.g., call graphs, inheritance hierarchies) injected as plain-text comments into LLM-based code agents improve navigation determinism, reproducibility, and localization accuracy, though with diminishing returns on dense semantics and a ~10% token cost.
AI.motivation: LLM-based code agents rely on keyword search for repository navigation, which misses structural relationships (call graphs, inheritance, configuration dependencies), making navigation stochastic, unreproducible, and unreliable across runs.
AI.method: Starting from a Codex baseline, the authors systematically inject static analysis annotations at varying granularities (lightweight topology, dense semantics, inverse-only links) into the agent's context as plain-text comments, then measure effects on localization accuracy (Func@5), trajectory length (interaction rounds), link-following rate, run-to-run variance, and Pass@1.
AI.result: (1) Anchoring improves function-level localization by +2.2pp Func@5 and shortens trajectories by 1.6 rounds; (2) Optimal granularity depends on repo characteristics—denser semantics show diminishing returns, hub-heavy projects benefit from inverse-only (who-calls-me) links; (3) Anchoring raises link-following rate from 0.15–0.18 to 0.21–0.24, halves run-to-run variance, improves Pass@1 by +3.4pp, at a ~10% token overhead.
AI.conclusion: Static structure acts as a "deterministic anchor": it constrains probabilistic LLM navigation to be more disciplined and reproducible rather than making agents inherently smarter. Practical guidelines: use lightweight topology for medium projects, prune forward edges for large repos, reserve dense tags for implicit-dependency cases.

title: Equivariance and Augmentation for Bayesian Neural Networks
summary: Symmetries are important for many deep learning tasks, ranging from applications in the sciences to medical imaging. However, there is an ongoing debate about whether to impose symmetry constraints on the neural network architecture (yielding equivariant neural networks) or learn them from augmented training data. Although equivariant networks are well-studied theoretically, much less is known about data augmentation, since analyzing augmentation requires control over the training dynamics. Inspired by recent results that show that augmented infinite deep ensembles are exactly equivariant, we study data augmentation for Bayesian neural networks (BNNs) trained with variational inference. We focus on variational distributions in the exponential family and derive conditions under which exact equivariance is reached. We furthermore obtain bounds on the equivariance error and introduce three novel symmetrization techniques which boost the effect of data augmentation in this setting. We conduct extensive numerical experiments which show that one of our symmetrization methods (orbit expansion) outperforms the baseline in both equivariance and overall performance. Our code is available at github.com/dmw1998/augment-BNNs

AI.tldr: This paper studies data augmentation for Bayesian neural networks (BNNs) trained with variational inference, deriving conditions for exact equivariance, bounding equivariance error, and introducing three novel symmetrization techniques—with orbit expansion outperforming baselines.
AI.motivation: There is an ongoing debate about whether to impose symmetry constraints via equivariant networks or learn symmetries from augmented data. While equivariant networks are well-studied theoretically, much less is known about data augmentation due to the need to control training dynamics.
AI.method: The authors study BNNs with variational inference using exponential family variational distributions, derive conditions for exact equivariance, bound the equivariance error, and propose three novel symmetrization techniques (including orbit expansion) to boost data augmentation effectiveness.
AI.result: The orbit expansion symmetrization method outperforms baselines in both equivariance and overall performance, as demonstrated by extensive numerical experiments.
AI.conclusion: Data augmentation can be systematically analyzed for BNNs with variational inference, and the proposed symmetrization techniques—especially orbit expansion—effectively enhance equivariance and model performance.

title: Methane-Plume Segmentation From Hyperspectral Satellite Imagery Via Multimodal Deep Learning
comment: Accepted at IEEE International Geoscience and Remote Sensing Symposium (IGARSS) 2026
summary: Efficient detection of methane plumes is crucial for understanding and mitigating global warming, as accurately identifying and segmenting them in earth observation imagery remain essential for large-scale monitoring. In this work, we propose a multimodal deep learning model that integrates a feature-guided methane enhancement (FGME) mechanism which injects physically meaningful methane cues into transformer-based RGB representations at multiple semantic scales. Our method is evaluated on the MPDataset, where it outperforms the state-of-the-art with improvements of +0.92 in MIoU, +0.87 in MPrecision and +1.01 in Recall. Notably, these gains are obtained with a substantially lower computational cost than other high-performing architectures, resulting in a favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off for large-scale methane monitoring. These results highlight the potential of efficient multimodal fusion strategies for accurate and scalable methane plume segmentation in real-world remote sensing applications.

AI.tldr: A multimodal deep learning model with a feature-guided methane enhancement (FGME) mechanism integrates physically meaningful methane cues into transformer-based RGB representations, achieving state-of-the-art methane plume segmentation on MPDataset with +0.92 MIoU, +0.87 MPrecision, and +1.01 Recall improvements at lower computational cost.
AI.motivation: Efficient detection of methane plumes is crucial for understanding and mitigating global warming, but accurately identifying and segmenting them in earth observation imagery remains challenging for large-scale monitoring.
AI.method: A multimodal deep learning model integrating a feature-guided methane enhancement (FGME) mechanism that injects physically meaningful methane cues into transformer-based RGB representations at multiple semantic scales.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art on MPDataset with +0.92 MIoU, +0.87 MPrecision, and +1.01 Recall improvements, achieved with substantially lower computational cost.
AI.conclusion: Efficient multimodal fusion strategies show potential for accurate and scalable methane plume segmentation in real-world remote sensing applications, offering a favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off.

title: Instruction Bleed: Cross-Module Interference in Prompt-Composed Agentic Systems
comment: 8 pages, 2 tables. Accepted to the ICML 2026 Workshop on Failure Modes in Agentic AI (FAGEN), Seoul, South Korea
summary: Practitioners of prompt-composed agentic systems report a recurring failure mode: editing one prompt module silently shifts the behavior of others despite no shared variable or executable dependency. We formalize this as compositional behavioral leakage (CBL): interference between modules sharing a context window. CBL is enabled by architectural non-isolation: transformer self-attention provides no formal boundary between concatenated modules. We probe CBL on a deployed job-evaluation agent (Claude Sonnet 4.6, 144 trials) through a reusable three-channel protocol that perturbs non-focal modules along volume, content, and form. Only the content channel produces a detectable paired effect (Cohen's d = 0.63, bootstrap 95% CI excluding zero); no recommendation flipped -- a sub-threshold regime invisible to standard QA but compounding across the thousands of decisions a deployed agent makes. CBL is orthogonal to known agent-failure axes (adversarial injection, cognitive degradation, multi-agent fault propagation, privacy leakage). We contribute an operational definition, a reusable protocol, a falsifiable prediction set, and a system-class characterization, establishing cross-module interference measurement as a requirement for prompt-composed agent evaluation.

AI.tldr: Editing one prompt module in agentic systems silently alters behavior of other modules (compositional behavioral leakage / CBL) due to transformer self-attention lacking formal boundaries. A three-channel protocol on a deployed job-evaluation agent (144 trials) found detectable interference only via content changes (Cohen's d=0.63), sub-threshold for QA but compounding at scale. CBL is orthogonal to known failure modes.
AI.motivation: Practitioners observe that editing one prompt module in agentic systems unexpectedly changes behavior of other modules, a recurring failure mode lacking formal characterization.
AI.method: Experimental probe using a reusable three-channel protocol (volume, content, form) on a deployed job-evaluation agent (Claude Sonnet 4.6, 144 trials) to detect compositional behavioral leakage (CBL) by perturbing non-focal modules.
AI.result: Only the content channel produced detectable interference (Cohen's d=0.63, bootstrap 95% CI excluding zero). No recommendation flipped—effects are sub-threshold for standard QA but compound across many decisions. CBL is orthogonal to adversarial injection, cognitive degradation, multi-agent fault propagation, and privacy leakage.
AI.conclusion: CBL is a distinct failure axis requiring cross-module interference measurement as a standard component of prompt-composed agent evaluation.

title: Cleaning Logs for Downstream Tasks (Registered Report)
comment: This article supersedes arXiv:2004.07194: the tool name has been updated from LogCleaner to LogPurifier; the evaluation plan has been updated
summary: Background: Software systems generate logs during execution to record critical events and runtime information for troubleshooting and monitoring. However, in practice, logs often contain significant amounts of redundant and irrelevant information, which can negatively impact the performance of downstream analysis tasks, such as model inference and anomaly detection. Objective: The objective of this study is to clean log data by identifying and removing free-standing messages -- messages that are not relevant to the execution behaviors of interest and are interleaved with messages capturing the system's functional behavior. Method: To address this objective, we propose LogPurifier, a task-agnostic log-cleaning approach based on dependency relationships between log message templates. The paper presents a plan for an empirical evaluation using a controlled experimental design to assess the impact of LogPurifier on the effectiveness and efficiency of two downstream tasks: model inference and anomaly detection.

AI.tldr: LogPurifier is a task-agnostic log-cleaning approach that removes free-standing messages (irrelevant, interleaved log entries) based on dependency relationships between log templates, aiming to improve downstream task performance like model inference and anomaly detection.
AI.motivation: Logs contain redundant and irrelevant information (free-standing messages) that degrade the performance of downstream analysis tasks such as model inference and anomaly detection.
AI.method: LogPurifier, a task-agnostic approach that identifies and removes free-standing messages by analyzing dependency relationships between log message templates.
AI.result: The paper presents a plan for empirical evaluation using a controlled experimental design; no results are reported yet.
AI.conclusion: The proposed LogPurifier approach aims to improve the effectiveness and efficiency of downstream tasks (model inference and anomaly detection) by removing irrelevant log messages.

title: SSM Adapters via Hankel Reduced-order Modeling: Injection Site Determines Task Suitability in Long-Context Fine-Tuning
summary: While parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) typically targets attention projectors, its efficacy for tasks requiring sequential state accumulation remains under-explored. We examine if PEFT for such tasks can benefit from state space model (SSMs) adapters, and if MLP blocks are better injection sites. We introduce Hankel Reduced order Model (HRM) adapter, an SSM-based residual module initialized via Balanced Truncation of empirical Hankel Grammians. By leveraging the time-invariance of the system matrix $\bar{A}$, HRM enables an exact FFT-based parallel scan, achieving computational parity with LoRA across all context lengths. In iso-parametric evaluations on Mistral-7B (8.4M trainable parameters), HRM outperforms LoRA variants on LongBench tasks, including QuALITY (+34.8\% relative accuracy) and QMSum (+71.6\% relative ROUGE-1). HRM further demonstrates consistent superiority across 18 configurations of synthetic state-tracking (DFA, Parity) and character-level language modeling (enwik8). Gate analysis reveals that HRM adapters effectively learn to modulate recurrence, providing a robust architectural alternative to low-rank adaptation for long-context sequence modeling.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the Hankel Reduced order Model (HRM) adapter, an SSM-based PEFT method that uses Balanced Truncation of empirical Hankel Grammians for initialization. HRM achieves computational parity with LoRA via FFT-based parallel scan, and outperforms LoRA variants on LongBench tasks (e.g., +34.8% relative accuracy on QuALITY, +71.6% relative ROUGE-1 on QMSum) and synthetic state-tracking benchmarks, demonstrating that SSM adapters are effective alternatives to low-rank adaptation for long-context sequence modeling.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether PEFT for tasks requiring sequential state accumulation can benefit from SSM-based adapters, and whether MLP blocks are better injection sites than attention projectors.
AI.method: Proposes HRM adapter, an SSM-based residual module initialized via Balanced Truncation of empirical Hankel Grammians, with an FFT-based parallel scan enabled by the time-invariance of the system matrix.
AI.result: HRM outperforms LoRA variants on LongBench (QuALITY: +34.8% relative accuracy, QMSum: +71.6% relative ROUGE-1) and synthetic tasks (DFA, Parity, enwik8) with only 8.4M trainable parameters on Mistral-7B, while achieving computational parity with LoRA.
AI.conclusion: SSM-based adapters like HRM provide a robust architectural alternative to low-rank adaptation for long-context sequence modeling, effectively learning to modulate recurrence.

title: Active Adversarial Perturbation-driven Associative Memory Retrieval for RGB-Event Visual Object Tracking
summary: RGB-Event tracking improves localization robustness by fusing RGB appearance textures and dense temporal motion cues from event sensors. While this multi-modal scheme broadens tracking applicability, real-world scenes suffer diverse structured signal degradations that hinder traditional multi-modal fusion. In harsh environments, either modality can lose reliability drastically, and targets frequently appear incomplete due to occlusion, edge truncation and foreground clutter.To tackle the above challenges, we present a hierarchical perturbation and retrieval framework tailored for RGB-Event tracking with robustness against partial target missing and modal degradation, termed APRTrack. To mimic real-world signal corruption, APRTrack constructs structured degradation via two adversarial perturbation branches at the modality and spatial levels, which separately simulate full-modal failure and localized target region absence. A hierarchical routing mechanism is designed to disentangle the training pipelines of the two perturbation types, effectively eliminating feature collapse induced by superimposed degradation constraints. Furthermore, we devise Footprint-guided Channel-calibrated Hopfield Retrieval (FCHR) for reliable historical information compensation. This module evaluates retrieval confidence based on association footprints between queries and memory banks, and calibrates the retrieval metric space prior to Hopfield matching, realizing controllable historical feature compensation bounded to target regions. Extensive experiments on FE108, COESOT, VisEvent, and FELT datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed strategies for the RGB-Event visual object tracking. The source code and pre-trained models will be released on https://github.com/Event-AHU/OpenEvTracking

AI.tldr: APRTrack is a hierarchical perturbation and retrieval framework for RGB-Event tracking that handles partial target missing and modal degradation through adversarial perturbation branches (modal-level and spatial-level) and a Footprint-guided Channel-calibrated Hopfield Retrieval (FCHR) module for reliable historical feature compensation.
AI.motivation: Real-world RGB-Event tracking faces structured signal degradations where either modality can lose reliability and targets frequently appear incomplete due to occlusion, edge truncation, and foreground clutter, hindering traditional multi-modal fusion.
AI.method: APRTrack introduces: (1) two adversarial perturbation branches at modality and spatial levels to simulate full-modal failure and localized target absence; (2) a hierarchical routing mechanism to disentangle training pipelines and prevent feature collapse; (3) Footprint-guided Channel-calibrated Hopfield Retrieval (FCHR) for controllable historical information compensation based on association footprints.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on FE108, COESOT, VisEvent, and FELT datasets demonstrate effectiveness for RGB-Event visual object tracking.
AI.conclusion: The proposed hierarchical perturbation and retrieval framework improves robustness against partial target missing and modal degradation in RGB-Event tracking.

title: Accelerating Returns and the Qualitative Engine for Science
summary: Ray Kurzweil described a thesis of accelerating returns, which is the most influential narratives in discussions of technological progress. Its central claim is that advances in multiple technological fields, especially compute, artificial intelligence, brain science, and biotechnology, interact in such a way that progress becomes self-amplifying and approximately exponential. This paper gives a simple mathematical interpretation of that claim and then argues that, even if such acceleration is real, it does not by itself resolve the central problem of scientific discovery. The reason is that accelerating returns apply most naturally to executional and infrastructural capability, whereas genuine discovery often depends on a different capacity: qualitative reasoning about when a current framework is structurally inadequate and what conceptual move is needed next. Recent ARC-AGI-3 results sharpen this distinction: humans solve the benchmark at ceiling, whereas frontier AI systems remain below 1%, indicating that the gap between current AI and human flexible reasoning is still very large. At the same time, Demis Hassabis has emphasized that humans must retain their sense of meaning and what they choose to focus their lives on, a reminder that the future of AI is not only a technical forecast but also a question of what forms of human understanding are worth preserving and transmitting. This paper positions the Qualitative Engine for Science (QES) [3] as a response to that missing capacity. In this view, the Kurzweil theory helps explain why quantitative capability may accelerate, while QES addresses the central problem in scientific discovery that acceleration alone does not solve. Its value does not depend on when AGI arrives, but on the fact that the processes of scientific discovery themselves constitute a form of human wisdom worth preserving, organizing, and making accessible.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that while Kurzweil's accelerating returns theory explains exponential growth in computational and infrastructural capabilities, it does not address the core challenge of scientific discovery—qualitative reasoning about structural inadequacies in current frameworks. The authors position the Qualitative Engine for Science (QES) as a solution to this missing capacity, emphasizing that scientific discovery processes represent human wisdom worth preserving beyond AGI timelines.
AI.motivation: To examine whether Kurzweil's thesis of accelerating returns resolves the fundamental problem of scientific discovery, and to identify the missing capacity that acceleration alone cannot provide.
AI.method: Mathematical interpretation of accelerating returns theory, empirical analysis using ARC-AGI-3 benchmark results (humans at ceiling vs. frontier AI below 1%), and conceptual positioning of the Qualitative Engine for Science (QES) framework.
AI.result: Accelerating returns applies to executional/infrastructural capability but not to qualitative reasoning about structural framework inadequacy. ARC-AGI-3 results demonstrate a persistent gap between AI and human flexible reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Scientific discovery requires qualitative reasoning capacity that acceleration alone cannot provide. QES addresses this gap, and its value lies in preserving scientific discovery processes as a form of human wisdom, independent of AGI arrival timelines.

title: The Spec Growth Engine: Spec-Anchored, Code-Coupled, Drift-Enforced Architecture for AI-Assisted Software Development
summary:
AI coding agents dramatically accelerate implementation speed but introduce two structural failure modes that existing spec-driven approaches do not fully solve: (1) context explosion -- the agent must reason over an entire repository at once, degrading output quality as the context window fills; and (2) silent spec-code drift -- code evolves, the specification does not, and the divergence becomes invisible until it is costly to repair.
  We present the Spec Growth Engine, a lightweight framework that addresses both failure modes through a machine-readable spec graph whose nodes carry explicit contract/design separation, a Spine context assembler that scopes agent context to an ownership path, a vertical-slice growth protocol that enforces hardest-first ordering, and a drift gate that makes spec-code divergence a blocking merge condition.
  The design synthesises well-established software engineering principles (Parnas information hiding, C4, ADRs, Walking Skeleton, Reflexion Models, Fitness Functions) into a lean, code-coupled, machine-enforced whole -- without the overhead of heavy-weight frameworks such as RUP or MDA.

AI.tldr: The Spec Growth Engine is a lightweight framework that prevents context explosion and spec-code drift in AI coding agents through machine-readable spec graphs, scoped context assembly, hardest-first growth protocols, and merge-blocking drift gates.
AI.motivation: AI coding agents suffer from two structural failure modes: (1) context explosion when reasoning over entire repositories, degrading output quality; and (2) silent spec-code drift where code evolves but specifications don't, making divergence invisible until costly to repair.
AI.method: A Spec Growth Engine consisting of: (1) a machine-readable spec graph with explicit contract/design separation; (2) a Spine context assembler that scopes agent context to ownership paths; (3) a vertical-slice growth protocol enforcing hardest-first ordering; and (4) a drift gate making spec-code divergence a blocking merge condition.
AI.result: The framework synthesizes established SE principles (Parnas information hiding, C4, ADRs, Walking Skeleton, Reflexion Models, Fitness Functions) into a lean, code-coupled, machine-enforced whole without heavyweight overhead.
AI.conclusion: Existing spec-driven approaches are insufficient; the Spec Growth Engine provides a practical, low-overhead solution that enforces alignment between specs and code continuously, preventing costly late-stage divergence.

title: The Red Queen Gödel Machine: Co-Evolving Agents and Their Evaluators
comment: 12 pages main text + 21 pages appendix (37 pages total, incl. references); 10 figures (6 main text + 4 appendix); 10 tables (2 main text + 8 appendix). Preliminary preprint; work in progress. Keywords: self-improving agents, learned evaluation, multi-agent systems, auto- mated scientific discovery, controlled utility evolution, co-evolutionary search, autoresearch
summary: Self-improving agents are state-of-the-art (SOTA) on agentic coding benchmarks and have recently been extended to general domains. However, their search methods generally assume a stationary evaluation criterion: a fixed verifier, benchmark, or labeled dataset that remains valid as the agent improves. This ignores a central feature of evolution: species adapt as their environments change with them. We aim to bring the same principle to recursive self-improvement, making evaluation part of the improvement loop and opening search to evolving evaluators, adversarial objectives, and dynamic utilities that may surpass static benchmarks. We introduce the Red Queen Godel Machine (RQGM), an evolutionary framework for recursive self-improvement under non-stationary utilities. The RQGM makes this possible through controlled utility evolution: search is organized into epochs with a fixed within-epoch evaluation criterion, while the utility can be updated at epoch boundaries, so self-improvement guarantees hold per epoch as the objective evolves across them. We begin by showing that even on verifiable coding tasks, the RQGM improves test pass rate over the prior SOTA by adding a complementary agent-as-a-judge code-review signal. This signal is cheaper and the RQGM uses 1.35x-1.72x fewer tokens. We then turn to scientific paper writing and reviewing, and Olympiad-level proof writing and grading, where the RQGM improves performance over prior self-improving agents: co-evolved writers reach 1.78x-1.86x higher acceptance rates under a diverse agent-as-a-judge panel, while co-evolved graders reach 9% higher ground-truth accuracy. In paper reviewing, the strongest baseline reviewer over-accepts AI-generated papers at up to 1.91x the human rate. The RQGM corrects this by introducing an adversarial objective that discovers reviewers equally stringent on AI and human work.

AI.tldr: RQGM is an evolutionary framework for recursive self-improvement under non-stationary utilities that treats evaluation as part of the improvement loop, outperforming prior methods on coding, paper writing/reviewing, and Olympiad-level proof tasks while using fewer tokens.
AI.motivation: Existing self-improving agents assume stationary evaluation criteria (fixed verifiers/benchmarks), ignoring that real evolution involves changing environments. The authors aim to bring this principle to recursive self-improvement by making evaluation part of the improvement loop with evolving evaluators and dynamic utilities.
AI.method: RQGM organizes search into epochs with fixed within-epoch evaluation criteria, allowing utility updates at epoch boundaries so self-improvement guarantees hold per epoch while objectives evolve across them. It uses controlled utility evolution with complementary agent-as-a-judge signals.
AI.result: On coding tasks, RQGM improves test pass rate over SOTA using 1.35x-1.72x fewer tokens. Co-evolved writers achieve 1.78x-1.86x higher acceptance rates; co-evolved graders achieve 9% higher ground-truth accuracy. RQGM corrects reviewer bias (baseline over-accepts AI papers at 1.91x human rate) via adversarial objectives.
AI.conclusion: Non-stationary utility evolution enables more robust and adaptive self-improvement, correcting evaluation biases and outperforming static-baseline approaches across diverse domains.

title: Forget, Anticipate and Adapt: Test Time Training for Long Videos
comment: ECCV 2026. GLOM/APM's temporal binding now works for long videos
summary:
Test Time Training (TTT) is a mechanism in which a model adapts to an incoming test-sample by performing some self-supervised (SSL) task and updating its weights even during inference. This procedure does not require labels at test-time. This paper focuses on TTT for long-videos. A major concern with existing approaches is: 1) they perform TTT updates using a sliding window containing frames in the past, whose compute increases linearly with the size of window. This becomes computationally intractable when the videos are hours long. 2) TTT is performed even when temporally close frames look similar, thereby consuming a lot of compute.
  We present the Frame Forgetting Network (FFN) that: 1) operates on only three frames within the sliding window, namely the frame that exits, the current frame and the frame after that. The model still manages to retain temporal context and work for hours long-videos; 2) mathematically define a surprise metric: how much new information the incoming frame contains with respect to the past seen frame. This facilitates determining how to modify the effective window size during TTT and constitutes the core mechanism of an adaptive windowing algorithm. Additionally, we curate a dataset EpicTours containing up to 3 hour long videos of walking city-tours, whereas earlier datasets on this problem were only 5 min long. We demonstrate FFNs empirical effectiveness on dense-segmentation, video classification tasks, generalization to depth-estimation, and multi-hour long videos.

AI.tldr: The Frame Forgetting Network (FFN) efficiently performs Test Time Training (TTT) on long videos by operating on only three frames per sliding window and using a surprise metric for adaptive windowing, demonstrated on multi-hour videos with tasks like dense segmentation, classification, and depth estimation.
AI.motivation: Existing TTT approaches for videos are computationally intractable for long videos (hours long) because: 1) compute scales linearly with sliding window size, and 2) TTT updates are performed even when temporally close frames are similar, wasting compute.
AI.method: 1) FFN operates on only three frames within the sliding window: the exiting frame, current frame, and next frame, yet retains temporal context. 2) A mathematically defined surprise metric quantifies new information in an incoming frame relative to past frames, enabling an adaptive windowing algorithm that adjusts effective window size during TTT. 3) Curated EpicTours dataset with up to 3-hour long walking city-tour videos.
AI.result: FFN shows empirical effectiveness on dense segmentation, video classification, generalization to depth estimation, and multi-hour long videos, overcoming the computational limitations of prior TTT methods.
AI.conclusion: FFN enables practical TTT for hour-long videos by drastically reducing compute requirements via a three-frame operation and adaptive windowing based on surprise, without losing temporal context.

title: Narration-of-Thought: Inference-Time Scaffolding for Defeasible Ethical Reasoning in Large Language Models
comment: 24 pages, 8 figures, 16 tables. To appear at ACL 2026 (submitted via ARR)
summary: Standard chain-of-thought on moral dilemmas exhibits two failure modes: stakeholder collapse (the trace names at most one party with a stake in the outcome) and uncertainty suppression (no explicit unknowns or hedges before committing to an action). We introduce narration-of-thought (NoT), a system prompt that structures chain-of-thought into five sections: protagonist, stakeholders, two-step consequences, uncertainty, then commitment. NoT adds no training, parameters, or fine-tuning. On 100 DailyDilemmas scenarios across four generators from three vendors, NoT cuts stakeholder collapse from up to 31% to under 1% and uncertainty suppression from up to 72% to 1-24% on every model. A matched-budget verbose-CoT control rules out token spend as the active ingredient; NoT retains Cliff's delta advantages of +0.79 to +0.90 on stakeholder count and +0.65 to +0.93 on uncertainty score for three of four generators, and a section ablation attributes each shift to its specific sub-instruction. Textual-gradient descent initialised at NoT improves the scaffold further; a cross-family training judge (different vendor from the generator) dominates an in-family one on every measured axis. Extended to a five-round multi-stakeholder debate protocol, the scaffold converts a 6% standoff into 95% full consensus on a calibration set and 100% combined convergence on a DailyDilemmas replication. The resulting traces externalise the stakeholders, consequences, and uncertainty grounding each commitment, providing an auditable substrate for dependable agentic deployment.

AI.tldr: Narration-of-Thought (NoT) is a structured system prompt that organizes chain-of-thought into five sections (protagonist, stakeholders, two-step consequences, uncertainty, commitment), eliminating stakeholder collapse and uncertainty suppression in moral dilemma reasoning across multiple LLMs without any training or fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Standard chain-of-thought reasoning on moral dilemmas suffers from stakeholder collapse (naming at most one stakeholder) and uncertainty suppression (no explicit hedging before committing to an action), undermining the reliability and auditability of LLM decisions in ethically sensitive agentic deployment.
AI.method: Introduces NoT, a system prompt structuring CoT into five fixed sections (protagonist, stakeholders, two-step consequences, uncertainty, commitment). No ablation/training is added. Tested on 100 DailyDilemmas scenarios with four generators from three vendors. Controlled against verbose-CoT baseline. Extended to textual-gradient descent optimization and a five-round multi-stakeholder debate protocol.
AI.result: Stakeholder collapse reduced from up to 31% to under 1%; uncertainty suppression from up to 72% to 1-24%. Cliff's delta advantages of +0.79–+0.90 (stakeholder count) and +0.65–+0.93 (uncertainty score) for 3/4 generators. Cross-family training judge outperforms in-family. Debate protocol converts 6% standoff to 95% full consensus on calibration set and 100% convergence on replication.
AI.conclusion: NoT provides an effective, zero-training intervention that externalizes stakeholders, consequences, and uncertainty in moral reasoning traces, creating an auditable substrate suitable for dependable agentic deployment in ethically sensitive domains.

title: ATGBuilder: Feature-Assisted Graph Learning for Activity Transition Graph Construction with Seed Supervision
summary: Android applications are organized around activities that provide visual Graphical User Interface (GUI) containers that host the UI and handle user interaction events. Activity Transition Graphs (ATGs) have been widely used to model apps' GUI navigation. However, the construction of high-quality ATGs is challenging: ATGs based on static analysis may miss acceptable transitions and may extract infeasible ones; while dynamically explored ATGs can yield incomplete transitions. Recent learning-based approaches can treat ATG construction as a seed-supervised link-prediction task. However, the use of activity-layout and widget-trigger information for ATG construction remains limited. We propose ATGBuilder, a feature-assisted graph-learning approach for seed-supervised ATG construction. ATGBuilder uses a Large Language Model (LLM) to summarize UI activity metadata from layouts into compact textual functionality summaries. ATGBuilder explicitly models widget-trigger information into the edge attribute: It then uses an auxiliary widget-attribute reconstruction objective on this information during model training. ATGBuilder's performance was evaluated across a series of ablations on the frontmatter corpus, and an experiment on benchmark using manually-checked ground-truth ATGs. Experiments on multiple benchmarks show that ATGBuilder significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods. We further demonstrate its effectiveness by improving automated GUI exploration tools through better navigation guidance.

AI.tldr: ATGBuilder is a feature-assisted graph-learning approach for seed-supervised Activity Transition Graph (ATG) construction that leverages LLMs to summarize UI activity metadata and incorporates widget-trigger information via an auxiliary reconstruction objective, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art methods and improving automated GUI exploration tools.
AI.motivation: Constructing high-quality Activity Transition Graphs (ATGs) is challenging because static analysis may miss acceptable transitions and extract infeasible ones, while dynamic exploration yields incomplete transitions. Existing learning-based approaches also underutilize activity-layout and widget-trigger information.
AI.method: ATGBuilder uses a Large Language Model (LLM) to summarize UI activity metadata from layouts into compact textual functionality summaries, explicitly models widget-trigger information as edge attributes, and applies an auxiliary widget-attribute reconstruction objective during model training for seed-supervised ATG construction.
AI.result: ATGBuilder significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods on multiple benchmarks with manually-checked ground-truth ATGs, and improves automated GUI exploration tools through better navigation guidance.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating LLM-generated UI summaries and widget-trigger information into graph learning substantially improves ATG construction quality, enabling better navigation guidance for automated GUI testing tools.

title: High-Probability PL-SGD with Markovian Noise: Optimal Mixing and Tail Dependence
summary:
We study first-order methods for smooth objectives satisfying the Polyak-Łojasiewicz (PL) condition when gradient samples are generated by an exogenous Markov chain. In the light-tailed setting, prior uniform-in-time high-probability bounds for ordinary Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) under a standard growth envelope scale as $\widetilde{O}(t_{mix}^2/k)$, leaving a gap with the $\widetilde{O}(t_{mix}/k)$ expectation bounds. We close this gap using a lag-blocking argument to establish a uniform high-probability guarantee with a leading stochastic term of $\widetilde{O}(t_{mix}/(k+K_0))$ under geometric mixing. We prove this linear dependence on the mixing time is optimal via a matching $Ω(σ^2 t_{mix}/k)$ lower bound on a quadratic objective driven by a persistent two-state chain.
  We then extend this framework to heavy-tailed Markovian gradients satisfying a stationary finite-$p$-moment condition, $p \in (1,2]$. We design an all-samples clipped block method that uses every Markov transition while mitigating Markovian bias. Under a transition budget $T$, this algorithm achieves a high-probability stochastic error of $\widetilde{O}(σ_p^2(t_{mix}/T)^{2(p-1)/p})$. We establish a matching lower bound by reducing PL optimization to heavy-tailed mean estimation for a sticky Markov chain. Ultimately, this work tightly characterizes the optimal polynomial dependence on mixing time for light-tailed PL-SGD, and the optimal heavy-tail exponent and effective-sample-size dependence in the robust regime.

AI.tldr: This paper tightens high-probability bounds for SGD under the PL condition with Markovian gradient samples, achieving optimal $\widetilde{O}(t_{mix}/k)$ rates for light-tailed gradients via a lag-blocking argument, and extends to heavy-tailed Markovian gradients with a clipped block method achieving $\widetilde{O}(σ_p^2(t_{mix}/T)^{2(p-1)/p})$ rates, with matching lower bounds in both settings.
AI.motivation: Prior high-probability bounds for Markovian SGD under the PL condition had a $\widetilde{O}(t_{mix}^2/k)$ rate, leaving a gap with the $\widetilde{O}(t_{mix}/k)$ expectation bounds. The paper aims to close this gap and extend the analysis to the heavy-tailed Markovian setting.
AI.method: For light-tailed gradients: a lag-blocking argument to establish uniform high-probability guarantees with linear dependence on mixing time. For heavy-tailed gradients: an all-samples clipped block method that uses every Markov transition while mitigating Markovian bias.
AI.result: Light-tailed: $\widetilde{O}(t_{mix}/k)$ high-probability bound (matching lower bound $\Omega(\sigma^2 t_{mix}/k)$). Heavy-tailed: $\widetilde{O}(\sigma_p^2 (t_{mix}/T)^{2(p-1)/p})$ high-probability stochastic error with matching lower bound.
AI.conclusion: The work provides tight characterizations of optimal polynomial dependence on mixing time for light-tailed PL-SGD, and optimal heavy-tail exponent and effective-sample-size dependence in the robust regime.

title: From Hallucination to Grounding: Diagnosing Visual Spatial Intelligence via CRISP
summary: Current VLM evaluations often conflate language priors with genuine spatial reasoning. To address this, we introduce CRISP, a novel structural-diagnostic evaluation paradigm that assesses visual spatial intelligence through consistency, the alignment between implicit perception and explicit reasoning. Unlike traditional black-box QA, CRISP utilizes metric 3D Scene Graphs and an oracle intervention protocol to decouple latent reasoning capabilities from perceptual bottlenecks. This granular diagnosis uncovers a systematic perception-reasoning disconnect. Crucially, we reveal that while proprietary models possess robust latent reasoning engines, they suffer from inaccurate metric estimation and a critical failure to leverage their implicit structural representations. Conversely, open-source models remain fundamentally bottlenecked by their lack of multi-hop compositional reasoning. By shifting the focus from merely ``guessing correctly'' via language priors to genuinely ``perceiving, verifying, and reasoning,'' CRISP offers a rigorous roadmap for multimodal alignment beyond end-to-end post-training. The code and dataset are available at https://github.com/iiyamayuki/CRISP-Bench.

AI.tldr: CRISP is a structural-diagnostic evaluation paradigm that uses 3D Scene Graphs and oracle intervention to decouple visual perception from spatial reasoning in VLMs, revealing that proprietary models have strong latent reasoning but poor metric estimation, while open-source models lack multi-hop compositional reasoning.
AI.motivation: Current VLM evaluations conflate language priors with genuine spatial reasoning, failing to diagnose whether models actually perceive spatial relationships or simply guess correctly using linguistic shortcuts.
AI.method: CRISP uses metric 3D Scene Graphs and an oracle intervention protocol to decouple latent reasoning capabilities from perceptual bottlenecks, evaluating spatial intelligence through consistency between implicit perception and explicit reasoning.
AI.result: Proprietary models have robust latent reasoning engines but suffer from inaccurate metric estimation and fail to leverage implicit structural representations. Open-source models are bottlenecked by lack of multi-hop compositional reasoning.
AI.conclusion: CRISP shifts evaluation from guessing correctly via language priors to genuine perception-verification-reasoning, offering a rigorous roadmap for multimodal alignment beyond end-to-end post-training.

title: Geometry-Aware MCTS for Extremal Problems in Combinatorial Geometry
summary: We study certain extremal problems in combinatorial geometry that ask about configurations of points in an $n \times n$ grid that satisfy strict, global geometric constraints.
