Classical exact solvers suffer from combinatorial explosion for these types of problems, and standard reinforcement learning and transformer-based models struggle with the sparse reward "validity cliff" and quadratic token-consumption limits. To overcome these bottlenecks, we propose a Geometry-Aware Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) framework. Our approach strictly enforces geometric constraints through incremental updates to the feasible action space. For constraints about collections of collinear points, like those that occur in the classic No-Three-in-Line problem (Max-N3IL), this mechanism reduces the constraint checking complexity from $O(n^3)$ to $O(n^2)$. To improve search efficiency, we exploit geometric symmetries in two ways: canonical pruning during node expansion to reduce the branching factor, and symmetric batch transitions to accelerate the discovery of promising configurations. We perform extensive experiments and establish new best-known computational results on five out of six of the problems that we considered. Notably, for Max-N3IL we find configurations of size roughly $1.8 n$ for grids of size $82 \le n \le 119$. For the Smallest Complete Set problem, we find configurations of size roughly $0.95 n$, providing new upper bounds within the tested grids. This work establishes Geometry-Aware MCTS as a highly adaptable framework for discovering novel configurations in combinatorial geometry.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a Geometry-Aware Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) framework to solve combinatorial geometry problems on n×n grids with strict geometric constraints. It reduces constraint checking complexity from O(n³) to O(n²), uses canonical pruning and symmetric batch transitions, and achieves new best-known results on five out of six problems, including ~1.8n configurations for Max-No-Three-in-Line on grids 82≤n≤119.
AI.motivation: Classical solvers suffer from combinatorial explosion, while RL and transformer models struggle with sparse reward "validity cliffs" and quadratic token consumption when solving strict geometric constraint problems on n×n grids.
AI.method: A Geometry-Aware MCTS that strictly enforces geometric constraints via incremental feasible action space updates, reduces collinearity checking from O(n³) to O(n²), exploits symmetries via canonical pruning (reducing branching factor) and symmetric batch transitions (accelerating promising configuration discovery).
AI.result: New best-known computational results on five of six problems; for Max-N3IL, configurations of ~1.8n points for 82≤n≤119; for Smallest Complete Set, configurations of ~0.95n points, providing new upper bounds.
AI.conclusion: Geometry-Aware MCTS is a highly adaptable framework for discovering novel configurations in combinatorial geometry.

title: On the Reproducibility of Quantum Software Defect Datasets: A Case Study of Bugs4Q
comment: 10 pages plus 2 pages of references. Accepted to the Replication and Negative Results (RENE) Track of ICSME 2026
summary: The reproducibility of software defect datasets is essential for obtaining reliable and comparable research results. Zhu et al. have shown that defect datasets such as Defects4J suffer from reproduction failures (i.e., reported bugs become non-reproducible) as time passes since their creation. However, it remains unclear whether these findings generalize to quantum software defect datasets. We therefore conduct a replication study of the prior work using Bugs4Q, a widely used dataset of real-world bugs in quantum programs. Our analysis includes 77,700 quantum program executions of 37 Bugs4Q artifacts across 21 core-library versions. The experimental results showed that the reproducibility of Bugs4Q dropped from 62.2% on Qiskit v0.20.1 to 16.2% on v2.3.1, the latest version as of April 1, 2026. A manual inspection of the root causes further indicated that 93.6% of the failures were dependency-related. While these findings are consistent with those of the prior work, we also observed differences. In particular, most reproduction failures in Bugs4Q cannot be resolved merely by adjusting dependency versions; instead, they require source-code modifications such as migrating import paths and API invocations. Based on this observation, we curated Bugs4Q-Robust, a patched version of Bugs4Q to restore reproducibility. Bugs4Q-Robust increases reproducibility from 16.2% to 78.4% on Qiskit v2.3.1. Our findings highlight the importance of continuous dataset maintenance in the rapidly evolving quantum software ecosystem.

AI.tldr: This replication study investigates reproducibility of quantum software defect dataset Bugs4Q over time, finding that reproducibility dropped from 62.2% to 16.2% across Qiskit versions, with 93.6% of failures being dependency-related—though unlike prior work on Defects4J, most Bugs4Q failures require source-code modifications rather than just version adjustments. The authors curated Bugs4Q-Robust, restoring reproducibility to 78.4%.
AI.motivation: To determine whether findings from prior work on reproducibility failures in classical software defect datasets (Defects4J) generalize to quantum software defect datasets (Bugs4Q), given the rapid evolution of the quantum software ecosystem.
AI.method: A replication study involving 77,700 quantum program executions of 37 Bugs4Q artifacts across 21 core-library versions of Qiskit, with manual root-cause inspection of reproduction failures.
AI.result: Bugs4Q reproducibility dropped from 62.2% (Qiskit v0.20.1) to 16.2% (v2.3.1). 93.6% of failures were dependency-related, but most required source-code changes (import paths, API invocations) rather than simple version adjustments. The patched Bugs4Q-Robust restored reproducibility to 78.4%.
AI.conclusion: Quantum software defect datasets face similar reproducibility degradation as classical datasets, but with distinct root causes—necessitating source-code patches rather than dependency versioning—underscoring the need for continuous dataset maintenance in the rapidly evolving quantum software ecosystem.

title: EVOM: Agentic Meta-Evolution of Actor-Critic Architectures for Reinforcement Learning
summary: In actor-critic reinforcement learning, network architectures are typically manually designed. Automating this design is challenging because each candidate must be trained before evaluation, and the design space is open-ended. To address these challenges, we introduce EVOM, an agentic meta-evolution framework for discovering high-performance actor-critic architectures. We frame architecture search as a bi-level optimization: an inner loop trains weights via the low-fidelity proximal policy optimization (PPO), while an outer loop drives meta-evolution by iteratively refining architecture programs. Crucially, this outer loop is powered by an LLM-based design agent that operates purely as an architecture designer, completely decoupled from policy execution and environment control. Experiments reveal that EVOM outperforms the manually designed baseline, an LLM-guided random search, and the state-of-the-art LLM-guided programmatic policy search method MLES, delivering superior performance on Ant-v4 and HalfCheetah-v4. Ablation studies validate that both the meta-evolution loop and the LLM Design Agent are indispensable for final performance.

AI.tldr: EVOM is a meta-evolution framework that uses an LLM-based design agent to automatically discover high-performance actor-critic architectures via bi-level optimization (inner: PPO weight training; outer: LLM-driven architecture program refinement), outperforming manual baselines and prior methods on MuJoCo tasks.
AI.motivation: Manual design of actor-critic network architectures is tedious and suboptimal; automating this search is challenging due to the need to train each candidate and the open-ended design space.
AI.method: EVOM formulates architecture search as bi-level optimization: inner loop trains weights via low-fidelity PPO; outer loop uses an LLM-based design agent (decoupled from execution) to iteratively refine architecture programs through meta-evolution.
AI.result: EVOM outperforms manually designed baselines, LLM-guided random search, and the state-of-the-art MLES method on Ant-v4 and HalfCheetah-v4. Ablations confirm both meta-evolution and LLM Design Agent are essential.
AI.conclusion: LLM-driven meta-evolution with a decoupled design agent is an effective paradigm for automated actor-critic architecture discovery, surpassing both manual and prior automated approaches.

title: PhyEditBench: A Real-World Multi-Stage Benchmark for Physics-Aware Image Editing
comment: 19 pages, 6 figures, 2 tables. Accepted to ECCV 2026
summary: While instruction-based image editing, enabled by multi-modal generative models, has advanced significantly, existing benchmarks lack a comprehensive evaluation of physics-based reasoning, a critical capability for handling real-world scenarios. To address this, we introduce PhyEditBench, a benchmark designed to assess the physical understanding of editing models. Guided by a hierarchical taxonomy, we establish 4 primary classes and 12 subclasses. It comprises 238 high-quality, high-resolution, real-world instances meticulously extracted from videos to capture authentic physical dynamics, alongside 35 synthetic Anti-Physics instances. Our empirical analysis of current SOTA editing methods exposes substantial limitations in their physics-based reasoning. We further propose a training-free baseline named PhyWorld that uses test-time scaling and a latent reduction strategy. PhyWorld outperforms comparable models and suggests that the video generation process can effectively serve as a reasoning mechanism for image editing. The project page is available at https://github.com/Previsior/PhyEditBench.

AI.tldr: PhyEditBench is a benchmark for evaluating physics-based reasoning in instruction-based image editing models, featuring 238 real-world and 35 synthetic anti-physics instances across 12 subclasses. The authors find current SOTA models lack physical understanding and propose PhyWorld, a training-free baseline using test-time scaling and latent reduction that outperforms comparable models.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for instruction-based image editing lack comprehensive evaluation of physics-based reasoning, which is critical for handling real-world scenarios.
AI.method: PhyEditBench is built with a hierarchical taxonomy (4 primary classes, 12 subclasses) containing 238 high-quality real-world instances from videos and 35 synthetic Anti-Physics instances. PhyWorld uses test-time scaling and a latent reduction strategy without additional training.
AI.result: Current SOTA editing methods show substantial limitations in physics-based reasoning; PhyWorld outperforms comparable models, suggesting video generation can serve as a reasoning mechanism for image editing.
AI.conclusion: Physics-based reasoning remains a major weakness in image editing models, and leveraging video generation processes as a reasoning mechanism offers a promising direction for improvement.

title: When Agents Meet Electric Bus Fleet Operations: Pricing Behavior, Trade-offs, and Policy Implications in an Aggregator Framework
summary: Agentic systems are changing how complex operational tasks are coordinated, introducing a new paradigm for connecting heterogeneous data sources and automating processes. Electric bus fleets provide a relevant test case. Their operation requires continuous coordination between service reliability, battery state-of-charge, charger availability, electricity prices, route-energy uncertainty, and vehicle-to-grid (V2G) opportunities. This paper proposes an agentic aggregator framework that streamlines this decision environment by coupling an optimization-based electric bus scheduling model with supervisory agents for disturbance detection, tariff adaptation, and schedule evaluation. The optimization core enforces physical feasibility across routes, chargers, batteries, and V2G exchanges, while the agentic layer interprets changing operating conditions, triggers real-time re-optimization when needed, and defines how flexibility value is allocated between the aggregator and the public transport operator (PTO). A realistic depot case study evaluates day-ahead and real-time operations under profit-based and operation-based coordination modes, considering service delays, route-energy deviations, electricity price shocks, and combined disturbances. The results show that agentic aggregation can support adaptive fleet-grid coordination by maintaining feasible schedules, activating re-optimization selectively, and improving the use of charging and V2G flexibility. However, they also reveal a critical trade-off: the same agentic capability that reduces operational complexity can extract value from the PTO when configured around profit-oriented pricing. These findings suggest that agentic aggregators can become useful for managing electric bus V2G operations, but their deployment in public-fleet contexts requires transparent coordination modes, auditable tariff-setting, and explicit value-sharing rules.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an agentic aggregator framework that couples optimization-based electric bus scheduling with supervisory agents for disturbance detection, tariff adaptation, and schedule evaluation. Using a realistic depot case study under various disturbance scenarios, the framework maintains feasible schedules and improves V2G flexibility utilization, but reveals a critical trade-off where profit-oriented agentic configurations can extract value from the public transport operator, requiring transparent coordination and value-sharing rules for public-fleet deployment.
AI.motivation: Electric bus fleet operation requires continuous coordination between service reliability, battery state-of-charge, charger availability, electricity prices, route-energy uncertainty, and V2G opportunities. The paper aims to streamline this complex decision environment by proposing an agentic aggregator framework.
AI.method: The framework couples an optimization-based electric bus scheduling model (enforcing physical feasibility across routes, chargers, batteries, and V2G exchanges) with supervisory agents for disturbance detection, tariff adaptation, and schedule evaluation. The agentic layer interprets changing conditions, triggers real-time re-optimization, and defines flexibility value allocation between the aggregator and PTO.
AI.result: Under profit-based and operation-based coordination modes with various disturbances (service delays, route-energy deviations, price shocks, combined disturbances), agentic aggregation maintains feasible schedules, activates re-optimization selectively, and improves charging/V2G flexibility use. However, profit-oriented pricing enables the agentic aggregator to extract value from the PTO.
AI.conclusion: Agentic aggregators can be useful for managing electric bus V2G operations, but deployment in public-fleet contexts requires transparent coordination modes, auditable tariff-setting, and explicit value-sharing rules to prevent value extraction from the public transport operator.

title: Smaller Models, Unexpected Costs: Trade-offs in LLM Quantization for Automated Program Repair
comment: Accepted for publication in the Research Papers Track of the 42nd IEEE International Conference on Software Maintenance and Evolution (ICSME 2026), 14-18 September 2026, Benevento, Italy
summary:
Language Models (LLMs) are powerful toolsand have been increasingly adopted for complex software engineering tasks. As the number of parameters increases, results can often be improved, but this also imposes substantialmemory requirements. While quantization effectively reduces thememory footprint, its overall impact is often summarized onlyby benchmark scores, which mask changes in model behaviorand non-functional overheads. In this work, we conduct anempirical evaluation of LLM quantization using AutomatedProgram Repair (APR), a complex task in software engineering.We analyze 13 quantization configurations spanning differentbit-widths, methods, and target components (weights and KVcache) across six representative LLMs, evaluated on two APRbenchmarks (HumanEval-Java and Defects4J).
  Our findings reveal that base and quantized models can provide different sets of repaired problems with little overlap, whileretaining a comparable number of repaired problems. Althoughquantization successfully reduces memory footprints by up to85%, it increases both inference time and energy consumption,which we attribute to suboptimal hardware utilization. OurPareto trade-off analysis shows that 48% of the configurationsevaluated are strictly dominated by alternatives. Rather thanidentifying a superior quantization method, our findings highlightthat the trade-offs between effectiveness, memory footprint,and energy efficiency are sensitive to the underlying modelarchitecture and the complexity of the task.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates LLM quantization for Automated Program Repair (APR), testing 13 quantization configurations across 6 LLMs on 2 benchmarks. Quantization reduces memory up to 85% but increases inference time and energy. 48% of configurations are Pareto-dominated, and trade-offs depend on model architecture and task complexity.
AI.motivation: Quantization's overall impact on complex software engineering tasks like APR is often summarized only by benchmark scores, masking changes in model behavior and non-functional overheads (memory, energy, inference time). This work aims to provide an empirical understanding of these trade-offs.
AI.method: Empirical evaluation of 13 quantization configurations (different bit-widths, methods, weight/KV-cache targeting) across 6 LLMs on two APR benchmarks: HumanEval-Java and Defects4J.
AI.result: Base and quantized models repair different problem sets with little overlap but comparable counts. Quantization reduces memory by up to 85% but increases inference time and energy due to suboptimal hardware utilization. 48% of configurations are strictly Pareto-dominated.
AI.conclusion: No single superior quantization method exists; trade-offs between effectiveness, memory, and energy efficiency depend on model architecture and task complexity.

title: Mesh-RL: Coupled subgrid reinforcement learning
summary: Reinforcement learning in large or sparse-reward environments suffers from slow temporal-difference reward propagation, as value information spreads only locally across the state space. We propose Mesh-RL, a spatial domain-decomposition framework inspired by the finite element method and domain decomposition theory, which partitions the environment into overlapping subgrids and enforces boundary-consistent temporal-difference updates. Such an approach enables localized learning while ensuring globally coherent value propagation. Unlike hierarchical or model-based approaches, Mesh-RL accelerates long-range credit assignment without modifying the reward function, Bellman operator, or introducing explicit planning mechanisms. We evaluate Mesh-RL on hazard-dense grid-world environments with varying geometries and mesh resolutions. Across Q-learning, SARSA, and Dyna-Q, Mesh-RL consistently improves convergence speed, cumulative reward, and learning stability. Higher mesh resolutions sustain exploration, prevent premature convergence, and substantially accelerate value propagation to distant states. While Dyna-Q already benefits from internal planning, it still achieves additional gains under structured decomposition. Overall, Mesh-RL introduces a principled spatial domain-decomposition mechanism for accelerating temporal-difference learning. Our framework bridges finite element method-inspired boundary-consistency techniques from scientific computing with reinforcement learning to improve sample efficiency in sparse-reward environments. We will release source code of the study.

AI.tldr: Mesh-RL uses finite element method-inspired spatial domain decomposition with overlapping subgrids and boundary-consistent TD updates to accelerate value propagation and improve sample efficiency in sparse-reward RL environments.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement learning in large or sparse-reward environments suffers from slow temporal-difference reward propagation, as value information spreads only locally across the state space.
AI.method: Mesh-RL partitions the environment into overlapping subgrids and enforces boundary-consistent temporal-difference updates, inspired by finite element method and domain decomposition theory.
AI.result: On hazard-dense grid-world environments, Mesh-RL consistently improves convergence speed, cumulative reward, and learning stability across Q-learning, SARSA, and Dyna-Q. Higher mesh resolutions sustain exploration and accelerate value propagation.
AI.conclusion: Mesh-RL introduces a principled spatial domain-decomposition mechanism for accelerating temporal-difference learning, bridging finite element method-inspired techniques from scientific computing with RL to improve sample efficiency in sparse-reward environments.

title: Perception, Verdict, and Evolution: Hindsight-Driven Self-Refining Forensics Agent for AI-Generated Image Detection
summary: The rapid advancement of generative models presents a significant challenge to existing deepfake detection methods, particularly given the widespread dissemination of highly realistic AI-generated images. Although Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) show strong potential for this task, existing approaches suffer from two key limitations: insufficient sensitivity to fine-grained forensic artifacts and reliance on static synthetic supervision from frontier models, leading to limited flexibility and high-cost. To address these issues, we propose ForeAgent, an agentic forensics framework for AI-generated image detection with iterative self-evolution. First, ForeAgent adopts a Perception-Verdict architecture that aggregates multi-view cues spanning semantic, spatial, and frequency-domain features, and leverages an MLLM as a verdict module to fuse these signals for a logical-grounded verdict. Second, to enable continual self-improvement, we introduce a Hindsight-Driven Self-Refining strategy following a Sampling-Reflection-Evolution paradigm. The agent performs inference rollouts on training instances. Guided by ground-truth labels as hindsight, it reflects on failure cases and low-quality reasoning trajectories to regenerate higher-quality reasoning traces. These synthesized samples are then strictly filtered through a dual-expert quality gating module. ForeAgent continuously evolves via fine-tuning on self-curated high-quality samples. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ForeAgent achieves state-of-the-art performance on the Chameleon benchmark, reaching 82.18% accuracy (+16.41% over AIDE), and achieves 93.3% mean accuracy on AIGCDetect-Benchmark across 16 generators. In addition, external evaluation shows that ForeAgent produces more consistent and causally grounded reasoning compared to GPT-5 and GPT-5-mini.

AI.tldr: ForeAgent is an agentic forensics framework for AI-generated image detection that uses a Perception-Verdict architecture (aggregating semantic, spatial, and frequency-domain features) and a Hindsight-Driven Self-Refining strategy for iterative self-evolution. It achieves state-of-the-art performance on Chameleon benchmark (82.18% accuracy, +16.41% over AIDE) and 93.3% mean accuracy on AIGCDetect-Benchmark across 16 generators, outperforming GPT-5 in reasoning quality.
AI.motivation: Existing deepfake detection methods, particularly those based on MLLMs, suffer from insufficient sensitivity to fine-grained forensic artifacts and reliance on static synthetic supervision from frontier models, leading to limited flexibility and high cost.
AI.method: ForeAgent employs a Perception-Verdict architecture that aggregates multi-view cues (semantic, spatial, frequency-domain features) and uses an MLLM as a verdict module. It also introduces a Hindsight-Driven Self-Refining strategy following a Sampling-Reflection-Evolution paradigm, where the agent performs inference rollouts, reflects on failures using ground-truth hindsight, regenerates higher-quality reasoning traces, and filters them through a dual-expert quality gating module for continuous fine-tuning.
AI.result: ForeAgent achieves 82.18% accuracy on Chameleon benchmark (+16.41% over AIDE) and 93.3% mean accuracy on AIGCDetect-Benchmark across 16 generators. It also produces more consistent and causally grounded reasoning than GPT-5 and GPT-5-mini.
AI.conclusion: The proposed iterative self-evolution framework effectively addresses key limitations of existing MLLM-based deepfake detection, achieving superior accuracy and reasoning quality through multi-view perception and self-refinement.

title: Unbiased Canonical Set-Valued Oracles Via Lattice Theory
summary: A non-agentic "oracle" AI that estimates probabilities of future events faces a self-reference problem: once its answer is learned and acted upon, it can change the very probability it was asked to report. One response, advocated for the Scientist AI programme, is to ask only counterfactual questions, evaluated as if the answer had no influence. We observe that such answers tend to become irrelevant the moment they are learned, precisely because their premise is then false. We therefore explore a self-referential alternative in which the oracle reports not a single probability but a credal set that is simultaneously unbiased and self-consistent with the consequences of being learned. The naive self-consistency requirement is satisfied by too many sets (including the useless answer $[0,1]$), so the problem is to single out a canonical, nontrivial member. We do so with the Knaster--Tarski fixed-point theorem on the complete lattice of closed credal sets, taking the least fixed point of a suitably defined isotone operator; a variant instead reports the least fixed point that contains every self-consistent point estimate. We prove existence, self-consistency, and nonemptiness, show that the construction collapses to the classical point answer for non-performative questions, and that for a binary event the canonical answer is, under a natural hull-factoring assumption, an interval. The development is purely lattice-theoretic and extends unchanged from a binary event $B$ to an arbitrary random variable $X$, with $P(B\mid A,C)$ replaced by the conditional law $\mathcal{L}(X\mid A,C)$. We close with open questions, including whether the interval characterization itself survives that generalization.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a self-referential solution to the oracle AI self-reference problem: instead of reporting a single probability (which changes when acted upon), the oracle reports a credal set satisfying self-consistency. The Knaster–Tarski fixed-point theorem is used to select a canonical nontrivial such set as the least fixed point of an isotone operator.
AI.motivation: A non-agentic oracle AI that estimates probabilities faces a self-reference problem: once its answer is learned and acted upon, it changes the very probability it was asked to report. The existing counterfactual approach (Scientist AI programme) yields answers that become irrelevant the moment they are learned, motivating an alternative.
AI.method: The oracle reports a credal set (set of probabilities) rather than a single probability, satisfying simultaneous unbiasedness and self-consistency under learning. To single out a canonical nontrivial member from the many self-consistent sets (e.g., [0,1]), the Knaster–Tarski fixed-point theorem on the complete lattice of closed credal sets is applied, taking the least fixed point of an isotone operator; a variant takes the least fixed point containing every self-consistent point estimate.
AI.result: Proves existence, self-consistency, and nonemptiness of the canonical fixed point. Shows the construction collapses to the classical point answer for non-performative questions. For a binary event, under a natural hull-factoring assumption, the canonical answer is an interval. The framework extends from binary events to arbitrary random variables via conditional laws.
AI.conclusion: The development is purely lattice-theoretic and generalizes to arbitrary random variables. Open questions remain, including whether the interval characterization survives that generalization.

title: Beyond Objects
summary: A core principle of object orientation -- that the functionality of a system can be partitioned amongst objects that correspond to individuals in the problem domain -- has influenced how software has been specified, designed and implemented for more than fifty years. Later developments in software engineering sought to build on this principle. But in fact this partitioning is neither natural nor straightforward, and the problems that these later developments sought to mitigate -- the fragmentation and conflation of functionality -- were often, in fact, the inevitable consequences of this founding principle. An easier path to addressing these problems therefore starts by going back, abandoning object orientation, and replacing it with an alternative approach that decouples the individuals of the problem domain from the modules that partition functionality.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that object orientation's core principle of partitioning functionality by problem-domain individuals is flawed, causing fragmentation and conflation of functionality. The authors propose abandoning OOP and replacing it with an approach that decouples domain individuals from functional modules.
AI.motivation: The authors identify that object orientation's foundational principle of mapping software modules to individuals in the problem domain leads to inherent problems of fragmentation and conflation of functionality. Later software engineering developments attempted to mitigate these issues but were ultimately treating symptoms of the root cause.
AI.method: The paper takes a critical, analytical approach examining the fundamental principle of object orientation. It proposes abandoning OOP entirely and replacing it with an alternative approach that separates (decouples) the individuals in the problem domain from the modules responsible for partitioning functionality.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates that the problems addressed by later software engineering developments (fragmentation and conflation of functionality) are not incidental but are inevitable consequences of OOP's core principle, suggesting these issues cannot be fully resolved within the OOP paradigm.
AI.conclusion: A better solution requires rejecting object orientation and adopting an alternative paradigm where problem-domain individuals are decoupled from functional modules, rather than attempting to patch OOP with additional methodologies.

title: EMA-FS: Accelerating GBDT Training via Gain-Informed Feature Screening
summary:
Gradient Boosted Decision Trees (GBDT), exemplified by LightGBM, spend a dominant fraction of training time -- typically 65-70% -- constructing per-feature histograms. Existing approaches such as random feature subsampling (feature_fraction) discard features without regard for their predictive utility. We propose EMA-based Feature Screening (EMA-FS), an algorithm-level optimization that maintains an exponential moving average (EMA) of per-feature split gains across boosting iterations and, after a short warmup, restricts histogram construction to the top-K features ranked by historical gain. Unlike random subsampling, EMA-FS is informed: it retains high-gain features while screening out low-gain ones. Operating at the per-tree level, it preserves full compatibility with LightGBM's histogram subtraction trick, requiring no changes to core routines.
  We evaluate EMA-FS on datasets spanning financial fraud detection, advertising click-through prediction, industrial quality control, and synthetic benchmarks, with feature dimensionalities from 29 to 968. On dense, moderate-to-high-dimensional data it achieves significant speedups: 2.61x on a 500-feature synthetic benchmark and 1.45x on the 432-feature IEEE-CIS Fraud dataset at 30% retention. At 70% retention it improves AUC by 0.11 points while delivering a 1.34x speedup. On extremely sparse data (Bosch, >90% missing) it yields no speedup, as LightGBM's sparse bin optimization already bypasses empty values.
  We further introduce Stochastic EMA-FS (S-EMA-FS), which replaces deterministic top-K selection with gain-weighted random sampling controlled by a concentration parameter beta, unifying deterministic EMA-FS (beta -> infinity) and random subsampling (beta = 0) in one framework. Both are implemented in ~120 lines of C++ across all six LightGBM tree learners and are fully backward-compatible.

AI.tldr: EMA-FS is an algorithm-level optimization for LightGBM that uses exponential moving averages of per-feature split gains to selectively build histograms only for top-K features, achieving 1.34x-2.61x speedups on dense moderate-to-high-dimensional data without degrading accuracy, with a stochastic variant (S-EMA-FS) unifying it with random subsampling.
AI.motivation: GBDT training (e.g., LightGBM) spends 65-70% of time constructing per-feature histograms, and existing random feature subsampling ignores features' predictive utility, wasting computation on low-gain features.
AI.method: Maintains an EMA of per-feature split gains across boosting iterations; after a warmup period, restricts histogram construction to the top-K features ranked by historical gain. S-EMA-FS replaces deterministic top-K with gain-weighted random sampling controlled by a concentration parameter β, unifying deterministic EMA-FS (β→∞) and random subsampling (β=0).
AI.result: 2.61x speedup on 500-feature synthetic benchmark, 1.45x on IEEE-CIS Fraud (432 features) at 30% retention; at 70% retention, 0.11 AUC improvement with 1.34x speedup. No speedup on extremely sparse data (Bosch, >90% missing) due to LightGBM's sparse bin optimization.
AI.conclusion: EMA-FS provides an informed, algorithm-level feature screening method that preserves full compatibility with LightGBM's histogram subtraction trick, implemented in ~120 lines of C++ across all six tree learners, offering practical speedups on dense data.

title: Coarse-to-Fine: A Hybrid Self-Supervised Method for Non-rigid 3D Shape Matching
summary: Non-rigid 3D shape matching is a fundamental task in computer vision and graphics. In this paper, we propose a hybrid self-supervised method based on a coarse-to-fine strategy, which ensures consistency between the coarse mapping and the refined correspondence produced by our refinement module. The architecture features a dual-branch design, consisting of two symmetric functional map learning streams: one based on the Laplacian basis and the other utilizing the elastic basis. Extensive experiments show that our approach not only maintains computational efficiency, but also achieves state-of-the-art performance across a variety of challenging scenarios, including non-isometric deformations and topological noise. Finally, we rigorously demonstrate that contrastive energies promote feature discrimination. Furthermore, integrating these energies with existing methods yields consistent improvements, validating the overall efficacy of our approach. Our code is available at https://github.com/LuoFeifan77/Coarse-to-Fine-Hybrid-Self-Supervised-Matching.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a hybrid self-supervised, coarse-to-fine method for non-rigid 3D shape matching, featuring a dual-branch architecture using Laplacian and elastic basis functional map learning streams, achieving state-of-the-art performance on challenging non-isometric and topologically noisy scenarios while maintaining computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: Non-rigid 3D shape matching is a fundamental task in computer vision and graphics that remains challenging, particularly under non-isometric deformations and topological noise.
AI.method: A hybrid self-supervised method with a coarse-to-fine strategy, featuring a dual-branch design with two symmetric functional map learning streams — one based on the Laplacian basis and the other using the elastic basis — along with a refinement module that ensures consistency between coarse mapping and refined correspondence, and contrastive energies to promote feature discrimination.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across challenging scenarios including non-isometric deformations and topological noise, while maintaining computational efficiency. Contrastive energies are shown to promote feature discrimination and integrating them with existing methods yields consistent improvements.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach is effective for non-rigid 3D shape matching, validated through extensive experiments, and the code is publicly available.

title: Estimating Uncertainty in Classifier Performance with Applications to Large Language Models and Nested Data
summary: Researchers increasingly use text classification--supervised models or large language models--to measure constructs from natural language, providing metrics such as recall and precision as evidence of their validity. Yet, though these metrics are point estimates subject to sampling variation, measures of uncertainty are inconsistently reported alongside them. Further, when they are reported, they are often estimated with methods that are not appropriate when relevant labelled datasets are small or performance is high. To increase and improve confidence interval reporting in the field, this paper evaluates confidence interval methods for performance metrics under conditions typical of social science text classification: small to moderate sample sizes, infrequent constructs, and texts nested within individuals. Across simulations, default methods such as the Wald interval and the basic percentile bootstrap are the least accurate, with coverage sometimes far below the nominal 95% level. Accuracy is improved with the use of Agresti-Coull, Wilson, Clopper-Pearson, and a novel pseudo-count regularized bootstrap (which is particularly relevant to the calculation of F1). When texts are nested within individuals, we demonstrate that adjustment for both effective N and the appropriate degrees of freedom is necessary for producing accurate analytic intervals. Among bootstrap intervals, the hierarchical bootstrap is more accurate than the cluster bootstrap when individuals produce a moderate number of texts but overly conservative when individuals produce only a few. By providing guidance to the field on appropriate interval estimation, we aim to improve the transparency of machine learning applications, and to encourage greater attention to the validation sample size at the design stage.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates confidence interval methods for text classification performance metrics under social science conditions (small samples, infrequent constructs, nested texts), finding that Wald and basic percentile bootstrap are least accurate, while Agresti-Coull, Wilson, Clopper-Pearson, and a novel pseudo-count regularized bootstrap perform better, with adjustments needed for nested data.
AI.motivation: Performance metrics like recall and precision are used as validity evidence for text classification models, but their uncertainty (confidence intervals) is inconsistently reported, and when reported, methods are often inappropriate for small labelled datasets or high performance conditions typical in social science.
AI.method: Simulation study evaluating confidence interval methods (Wald, Agresti-Coull, Wilson, Clopper-Pearson, basic percentile bootstrap, cluster bootstrap, hierarchical bootstrap, and a novel pseudo-count regularized bootstrap) under conditions of small to moderate sample sizes, infrequent constructs, and nested texts.
AI.result: Wald interval and basic percentile bootstrap are least accurate with coverage below 95%. Agresti-Coull, Wilson, Clopper-Pearson, and pseudo-count regularized bootstrap (relevant for F1) improve accuracy. For nested data, adjustment for effective N and degrees of freedom is necessary. Hierarchical bootstrap outperforms cluster bootstrap for moderate text counts but is conservative for few texts.
AI.conclusion: Provides guidance on appropriate interval estimation to improve transparency of machine learning applications and encourage attention to validation sample size at the design stage.

title: Context-Aware Synthesis of Optimization Pipelines for Warehouse Optimization
summary:
Order fulfillment in manual picker-to-goods warehouses involves interconnected decisions such as item assignment, order batching, and picker routing. While integrated models capture interactions between these decisions, practical warehouse systems often require decomposed approaches due to organizational boundaries, differing responsibilities, or limited data availability. Existing studies primarily evaluate algorithms for isolated subproblems or fixed subproblem combinations for specific warehouse settings, but lack a general mechanism to determine applicable algorithm configurations, compose them into valid solution pipelines, and assess their performance.
  With Context-Aware Synthesis of Optimization Pipelines (CASOP), we propose a framework for constructing and evaluating context-specific optimization pipelines and apply these to order fulfillment. The framework comprises: (1) a modular repository of algorithms for common order fulfillment problems; (2) semantic data and algorithm cards to describe warehouse context and algorithm requirements; (3) a taxonomy that structures order fulfillment problems into relevant subproblems; (4) a pipeline synthesizer that identifies applicable algorithms for a given warehouse context and composes all valid optimization pipelines; and (5) a pipeline evaluator that assesses all resulting pipelines. We demonstrate the framework on 7 benchmark instance sets covering four problem classes, resulting in 1,063,044 valid pipelines. The framework supports researchers and practitioners in designing, automatically synthesizing, and selecting valid, high-performing algorithmic pipelines for warehouse operations. The software is open-source and available at https://github.com/kit-dsm/ware_ops_pipes and https://github.com/kit-dsm/ware_ops_algos.
  Keywords: Warehouse optimization, Algorithm selection, Pipeline synthesis, Order fulfillment

AI.tldr: CASOP is a framework for automatically constructing and evaluating context-specific optimization pipelines for order fulfillment in picker-to-goods warehouses, demonstrated on over 1 million valid pipelines across four problem classes.
AI.motivation: Existing studies lack a general mechanism to determine applicable algorithm configurations, compose them into valid solution pipelines, and assess their performance for warehouse order fulfillment, especially given practical constraints like organizational boundaries and limited data.
AI.method: CASOP includes: (1) a modular algorithm repository; (2) semantic data and algorithm cards describing warehouse context and algorithm requirements; (3) a taxonomy structuring fulfillment subproblems; (4) a pipeline synthesizer composing valid pipelines; and (5) a pipeline evaluator assessing all resulting pipelines.
AI.result: Demonstrated on 7 benchmark instance sets covering four problem classes, yielding 1,063,044 valid pipelines for evaluation.
AI.conclusion: CASOP supports researchers and practitioners in designing, automatically synthesizing, and selecting valid high-performing algorithmic pipelines for warehouse operations. Software is open-source.

title: Does Aurora Encode Atmospheric Structure? Latent Regime Analysis and Attribution
comment: Accepted at the FM4Science and Sci4DL workshops at ICLR 2026
summary: ML foundation models are able to emulate atmospheric dynamics accurately and efficiently but operate as opaque ``black boxes''. We investigate the internal representations of the Aurora model using spatially pooled PCA and layer-wise relevance propagation (LRP). We find evidence that Aurora's latent space is primarily organized by seasonal cycles, whereas extreme storm events do not form a linearly separable cluster. LRP indicates that the model attends to features consistent with the 3D vertical structure of the Great Storm of 1987. Perturbation tests show masking relevant regions degrades forecasts $3.31\times$ more than random masking. These findings suggest that Aurora learns meteorological coherence and vertical structure without explicit instruction.

AI.tldr: Aurora model's latent space is organized by seasonal cycles rather than extreme events, and it learns meteorological coherence and vertical structure without explicit instruction, as shown via PCA, LRP, and perturbation tests.
AI.motivation: ML foundation models emulate atmospheric dynamics accurately but operate as opaque black boxes; the authors aim to interpret the internal representations of the Aurora model to understand what meteorological knowledge it has learned.
AI.method: Applied spatially pooled PCA and layer-wise relevance propagation (LRP) to analyze Aurora's latent space; conducted perturbation tests by masking relevant regions to measure forecast degradation.
AI.result: Latent space is primarily organized by seasonal cycles; extreme storm events do not form a linearly separable cluster. LRP shows attention to features consistent with the 3D vertical structure of the Great Storm of 1987. Masking relevant regions degrades forecasts 3.31× more than random masking.
AI.conclusion: Aurora learns meteorological coherence and vertical structure without explicit instruction, suggesting foundation models capture physically meaningful atmospheric representations.

title: Data-driven Machine Learning Cannot Reach Symbolic-level Logical Reasoning -- The Limit of the Scaling Law
summary: Sphere neural networks have achieved symbolic level syllogistic reasoning without training data, raising the question of where the limit of the scaling law for logical reasoning lies, i.e., whether data-driven machine learning systems can achieve the same level by increasing training data and training time. We show two methodological limitations that prevent supervised deep learning from reaching the symbolic-level syllogistic reasoning: (1) training data can not distinguish all 24 types of valid syllogistic reasoning; (2) end-to-end mapping from premises to conclusion introduces contradictory training targets between neural components for pattern recognition and logical reasoning. Beside theoretical analysis, we experimentally illustrate that Euler Net cannot achieve rigorous syllogistic reasoning. We further challenge the most recent ChatGPTs (GPT-5-nano and GPT-5) to determine the satisfiability of syllogistic statements in four surface forms (patterns): words, double words, simple symbols, and long random symbols, showing that surface forms affect the reasoning performance and that ChatGPT GPT-5 may reach 100% accuracy but still provide incorrect explanations. As empirical training processes are stopped after achieving 100% accuracy, we conclude that supervised machine learning systems will not attain the rigour of symbolic logical reasoning.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that supervised deep learning systems cannot achieve symbolic-level syllogistic reasoning due to two fundamental limitations: indistinguishable training data across valid syllogism types and contradictory training targets between pattern recognition and logical reasoning. Even GPT-5 achieves 100% accuracy but provides incorrect explanations, suggesting that data-driven systems cannot attain the rigor of symbolic logical reasoning.
AI.motivation: To investigate the limits of scaling laws for logical reasoning—specifically, whether data-driven machine learning systems can achieve symbolic-level syllogistic reasoning by simply increasing training data and training time, challenging the capability of neural networks to match symbolic reasoning systems.
AI.method: The authors identify two methodological limitations of supervised deep learning for syllogistic reasoning: (1) training data cannot distinguish all 24 valid syllogism types, and (2) end-to-end mapping introduces contradictory training targets between pattern recognition and logical reasoning. They experimentally test Euler Net and evaluate ChatGPT models (GPT-5-nano, GPT-5) on syllogistic reasoning across four surface forms (words, double words, simple symbols, long random symbols).
AI.result: Euler Net fails to achieve rigorous syllogistic reasoning. ChatGPT GPT-5 can reach 100% accuracy on satisfiability determination, but still provides incorrect explanations. Surface forms significantly affect reasoning performance, indicating that the models rely on pattern recognition rather than genuine logical reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Supervised machine learning systems, even with scaling, will not attain the rigor of symbolic logical reasoning because empirical training stops once 100% accuracy is reached, masking the underlying lack of genuine logical understanding.

title: SOLAR: AI-Powered Speed-of-Light Performance Analysis
summary: How fast could a deep-learning model run on target hardware, and how far is today's implementation from that limit? These questions are central to software, hardware, and algorithm optimizations. Speed-of-Light (SOL) analysis answers them by computing a workload's theoretical minimum execution time on a given architecture. Yet deriving SOL bounds remains manual, error-prone, and disconnected from rapid model development. To close this gap, we introduce SOLAR, a framework that automatically derives validated SOL bounds from PyTorch and JAX source code. SOLAR leverages both generative and deterministic components in its flow: an LLM frontend translates any source programs into an executable Affine Loop IR, validated by output comparison; a deterministic flow lifts the IR into an einsum graph; and an analytical backend computes unfused, fused, and cache-aware SOL bounds. SOLAR provides comprehensive operator and language coverage, produces validated bounds with zero observed SOL violations, and offers multi-fidelity analysis that tightens bounds and surfaces optimization insights. We evaluate SOLAR across KernelBench, JAX/Flax models, and robotics workloads. These experiments demonstrate four use cases: headroom analysis at multiple fidelity levels, identifying optimization opportunities, cross-platform exploration, and inverse-roofline hardware provisioning.

AI.tldr: SOLAR is a framework that automatically derives validated Speed-of-Light (theoretical minimum execution time) bounds from PyTorch and JAX source code using an LLM frontend, deterministic IR lifting, and analytical backend for unfused, fused, and cache-aware analysis.
AI.motivation: Speed-of-Light (SOL) analysis is crucial for understanding how close implementations are to theoretical hardware limits, but deriving SOL bounds is manual, error-prone, and disconnected from rapid model development workflows.
AI.method: SOLAR uses a three-stage pipeline: (1) an LLM frontend translates PyTorch/JAX code into Affine Loop IR validated by output comparison; (2) a deterministic flow lifts the IR into an einsum graph; (3) an analytical backend computes unfused, fused, and cache-aware SOL bounds.
AI.result: SOLAR provides comprehensive operator/language coverage, zero observed SOL violations, multi-fidelity bound tightening, and is evaluated on KernelBench, JAX/Flax models, and robotics workloads.
AI.conclusion: SOLAR enables four use cases: headroom analysis at multiple fidelities, identifying optimization opportunities, cross-platform exploration, and inverse-roofline hardware provisioning, bridging the gap between theoretical performance limits and practical model development.

title: DiCoBench: Benchmarking Multi-Image Fine-Grained Perception via Differential and Commonality Visual Cues
comment: Accepted by ECCV 2026. Project page with code: https://github.com/PKU-ICST-MIPL/DICO_Bench_ECCV2026
summary: Recent advancements in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated impressive fine-grained perception capabilities. However, existing benchmarks predominantly rely on explicit textual cues or low-resolution inputs, failing to evaluate a model's ability to autonomously perceive implicit visual cues in high-resolution. To bridge this gap, we introduce DiCoBench, a comprehensive, multi-image high-resolution benchmark designed for cross-image fine-grained perception. DiCoBench consists of 765 meticulously curated samples categorized into two progressive tracks: Differential Visual Cues and Commonality Visual Cues, covering 8 distinct perception tasks. By formulating the benchmark as a multiple-choice question task and utilizing high-resolution imagery (approaching 2K), we eliminate evaluation metric bias and pose a substantial challenge to current state-of-the-art MLLMs. Our extensive evaluation of 18 diverse MLLMs reveals a striking performance gap compared to human accuracy (98.3\%), with top-performing models struggling significantly with micro-scale detail capture. We believe DiCoBench will serve as a challenging testbed to drive future research in autonomous, high-resolution multi-image perception.

AI.tldr: DiCoBench is a high-resolution multi-image benchmark for evaluating MLLMs on cross-image fine-grained perception (differential and commonality visual cues). Top models (18 tested) achieve much lower accuracy than humans (98.3%), especially on micro-scale details.
AI.motivation: Existing MLLM benchmarks rely on explicit textual cues or low-resolution inputs, failing to assess autonomous perception of implicit visual cues in high-resolution settings.
AI.method: 765 curated samples, two progressive tracks (Differential & Commonality Visual Cues), 8 perception tasks, multiple-choice format, high-resolution imagery (~2K). Evaluated 18 diverse MLLMs.
AI.result: Top-performing MLLMs significantly underperform compared to human accuracy (98.3%), especially struggling with micro-scale detail capture.
AI.conclusion: DiCoBench serves as a challenging testbed to drive future research in autonomous, high-resolution multi-image perception.

title: MKG-RAG-Bench: Benchmarking Retrieval in Multimodal Knowledge Graph-Augmented Generation
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) over knowledge graphs has emerged as a promising approach for grounding large language models, yet existing benchmarks largely overlook the challenges of retrieval in multimodal knowledge graph RAG (MKG-RAG). In practice, retrieval is a critical bottleneck: multimodal knowledge is heterogeneous, difficult to align across modalities, and often poorly served by retrievers designed for unstructured corpora. To address this gap, we introduce MKG-RAG-Bench, a cross-domain benchmark explicitly designed to evaluate retrieval in MKG-RAG. MKG-RAG-Bench is constructed from two multimodal knowledge graphs spanning general and medical domains, and includes carefully aligned question-answering datasets that support controlled evaluation of both retrieval and downstream generation. The benchmark is built using an LLM-based curation pipeline that filters low-utility knowledge, generates structurally grounded queries with exact supervision, and systematically covers diverse modality configurations. Through extensive experiments across representative retriever families and modality settings, we show that effective multimodal retrieval remains challenging yet crucial for end-to-end MKG-RAG performance, and that retrieval quality strongly determines generation outcomes. By isolating retrieval as a first-class evaluation target, MKG-RAG-Bench provides a principled foundation for diagnosing current limitations and advancing multimodal knowledge graph RAG systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MKG-RAG-Bench, a benchmark specifically designed to evaluate retrieval in multimodal knowledge graph retrieval-augmented generation (MKG-RAG), demonstrating through experiments that multimodal retrieval is a critical bottleneck for system performance.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks overlook retrieval challenges in multimodal knowledge graph RAG, where multimodal knowledge is heterogeneous, difficult to align across modalities, and poorly served by retrievers designed for unstructured corpora.
AI.method: Constructed from two multimodal knowledge graphs (general and medical domains), uses an LLM-based curation pipeline that filters low-utility knowledge, generates structurally grounded queries with exact supervision, and systematically covers diverse modality configurations.
AI.result: Effective multimodal retrieval remains challenging yet crucial for end-to-end MKG-RAG performance, and retrieval quality strongly determines generation outcomes.
AI.conclusion: By isolating retrieval as a first-class evaluation target, MKG-RAG-Bench provides a principled foundation for diagnosing current limitations and advancing multimodal knowledge graph RAG systems.

title: At the Edge of Understanding: Sparse Autoencoders Trace The Limits of Transformer Generalization
summary: Pre-trained transformers have demonstrated remarkable generalization abilities, at times extending beyond the scope of their training data. Yet, real-world deployments often face unexpected or adversarial data that diverges from training data distributions. Without explicit mechanisms for handling such shifts, model reliability and safety degrade, urging more disciplined study of out-of-distribution (OOD) settings for transformers. By systematic experiments, we present a mechanistic framework for delineating the precise contours of transformer model robustness. We find that OOD inputs, including subtle typos and jailbreak prompts, drive language models to operate on an increased number of fallacious concepts in their internals. We leverage this device to quantify and understand the degree of distributional shift in prompts, enabling a mechanistically grounded fine-tuning strategy to robustify LLMs. Expanding the very notion of OOD from input data to a model's private computational processes, a new transformer diagnostic at inference time is a critical step toward making AI systems safe for deployment across science, business, and government.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a mechanistic framework to understand transformer robustness under out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs, finding that OOD prompts (typos, jailbreaks) increase fallacious internal concepts, and uses this insight to develop a fine-tuning strategy for robustifying LLMs, extending OOD analysis from input data to internal model computations.
AI.motivation: Pre-trained transformers show strong generalization but lack explicit mechanisms to handle distributional shifts in real-world deployments, leading to reliability and safety concerns. A more disciplined study of OOD settings is needed.
AI.method: Systematic experiments are conducted to delineate transformer robustness contours. The authors identify that OOD inputs cause language models to operate on more fallacious internal concepts. They use this insight to quantify distributional shift and develop a mechanistically grounded fine-tuning strategy.
AI.result: OOD inputs (including typos and jailbreak prompts) increase the number of fallacious concepts in transformer internals. The proposed fine-tuning strategy robustifies LLMs against such shifts.
AI.conclusion: Expanding OOD analysis from input data to a model's private computational processes enables a new inference-time diagnostic for transformers, which is a critical step toward safer AI deployment.

title: LogicIR: Logic Gate Networks for Image Restoration
summary: Image restoration aims to reconstruct high-quality images from degraded low-quality inputs. As the computational demands of image restoration models continue to rise, there is growing interest in lightweight architectures optimized for fast and efficient inference. Logic gate networks (LGNs), which operate using fundamental logic operations such as NAND and XOR, have recently emerged as a promising direction for achieving highly efficient computation. However, their potential remains largely untapped in the domain of image restoration. In this work, we introduce LogicIR, the first LGN specifically designed for image restoration tasks. LogicIR incorporates a UNet-inspired architecture composed entirely of logic gates. In addition, we propose a differentiable bit decoding layer and an index shuffling mechanism that improves information propagation across logic gates. Experimental results across multiple image restoration benchmarks demonstrate that LogicIR achieves strong performance with significantly reduced computational cost, establishing LogicIR as a viable and efficient alternative for image restoration. The source code is available at https://github.com/jimmy9704/LogicIR

AI.tldr: LogicIR is the first logic gate network (LGN) for image restoration, using a UNet-like architecture composed entirely of logic gates with a differentiable bit decoding layer and index shuffling mechanism, achieving competitive performance with significantly reduced computational cost.
AI.motivation: To explore the untapped potential of logic gate networks (LGNs) in image restoration, offering a lightweight alternative to computationally expensive deep learning models.
AI.method: LogicIR proposes a UNet-inspired architecture built entirely from logic gates (NAND, XOR), a differentiable bit decoding layer, and an index shuffling mechanism to enhance information propagation across gates.
AI.result: Strong performance across multiple image restoration benchmarks with significantly reduced computational cost compared to conventional approaches.
AI.conclusion: LogicIR establishes logic gate networks as a viable and efficient alternative for image restoration tasks, balancing performance and computational efficiency.

title: auto-psych: Automating the science of mind using agent-driven theory discovery and experimentation
summary: AI-based scientific automation is increasingly possible by using agents to generate hypotheses, design experiments, and analyze data. Data collection is a major bottleneck in this pipeline, however. Psychology, and computational cognitive science in particular, is well-positioned to benefit from AI experimentation because theories are often represented as code and crowdsourcing platforms enable programmatic human data collection at scale. Here, we apply automated discovery techniques to the project of generating theories in computational cognitive science, with an agent-based system collecting human data independently through crowdsourced survey experiments. As a testbed, we use a classic case study from cognitive psychology: judging which sequences of coin flips seem subjectively more random. Our system, auto-psych, uses nested agent-based discovery loops to generate explanatory theories of human behavior. The inner loop conjectures, fits, and critiques probabilistic cognitive models; the outer loop designs experiments to test these models, launches them online, and analyzes the data. This system can quickly and reliably recover ground-truth theories from synthetic data via systematic experimentation, but the nested structure is critical to model performance. Further, in three independent sequences of human experiments, the system finds theories that fit the data better than theories generated from the scientific literature. This work thus demonstrates the feasibility of automated data collection and theory discovery in computational cognitive science.

AI.tldr: This paper presents auto-psych, an agent-based system that uses nested discovery loops to autonomously generate hypotheses, design and run crowdsourced experiments, and discover cognitive theories about human randomness perception, demonstrating feasibility of automated theory discovery in computational cognitive science.
AI.motivation: Data collection is a major bottleneck in AI-based scientific automation. Psychology and computational cognitive science are well-positioned to benefit from AI experimentation since theories are often code-based and crowdsourcing enables scalable human data collection. The authors aim to demonstrate fully automated hypothesis generation, experimentation, and theory discovery.
AI.method: The auto-psych system uses nested agent-based discovery loops: an inner loop conjectures, fits, and critiques probabilistic cognitive models; an outer loop designs experiments to test these models, launches them on crowdsourcing platforms, and analyzes the data. The testbed is a classic cognitive psychology case study of subjective randomness judgments in coin flip sequences.
AI.result: The system quickly and reliably recovers ground-truth theories from synthetic data via systematic experimentation, with the nested structure being critical for performance. In three independent sequences of human experiments, auto-psych discovers theories that fit human data better than existing theories from the published scientific literature.
AI.conclusion: This work demonstrates the feasibility of automated data collection and theory discovery in computational cognitive science, where an AI system can independently conduct the full cycle of scientific discovery from hypothesis generation to experimental validation.

title: Deterministic Pareto-Optimal Policy Synthesis for Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning
summary: Real-world decision-making often requires balancing multiple conflicting objectives, a challenge that standard Reinforcement Learning (RL) frequently addresses by aggregating rewards into a single scalar signal. While effective for simple tasks, this approach often fails to capture the full spectrum of optimal trade-offs, known as the Pareto frontier. In this paper, we introduce a novel preference-conditioned Bellman operator, motivated from the Chebyshev scalarization, designed to compute deterministic Pareto-optimal policies for Multi-Objective Markov Decision Processes (MOMDPs). We prove that this operator satisfies an enveloping property, where the estimated value functions upper-bound the true Pareto frontier, and demonstrate that it monotonically converges to a coverage set of this frontier. Furthermore, we also show how to extract deterministic policies from these converged Q-estimates. This ensures the agent can recover a policy for any given preference, capturing the entire Pareto-optimal frontier while guaranteeing each synthesized policy remains approximately Pareto-optimal. Experimental results validate that our algorithm successfully recovers complex trade-offs, providing a solution for deterministic Pareto-optimal policy synthesis.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a preference-conditioned Bellman operator based on Chebyshev scalarization to compute deterministic Pareto-optimal policies for Multi-Objective MDPs, proving convergence to a coverage set of the Pareto frontier and enabling policy recovery for any given preference.
AI.motivation: Standard RL aggregates multiple conflicting objectives into a single scalar reward, failing to capture the full spectrum of optimal trade-offs (Pareto frontier) in real-world decision-making.
AI.method: A novel preference-conditioned Bellman operator motivated by Chebyshev scalarization is proposed for Multi-Objective MDPs. The operator satisfies an enveloping property (value functions upper-bound the true Pareto frontier) and monotonically converges to a coverage set of this frontier. Deterministic policies are extracted from the converged Q-estimates.
AI.result: The algorithm successfully recovers complex trade-offs and synthesizes deterministic Pareto-optimal policies that are approximately Pareto-optimal for any given preference preference.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method provides a principled solution for deterministic Pareto-optimal policy synthesis in multi-objective decision-making, capturing the entire Pareto frontier.

title: TaskTok: Delving into Task Tokens for Task-driven Image Restoration
summary: While traditional image restoration focuses on perceptual quality, Task-Driven Image Restoration (TDIR) aims to maximize the performance of downstream high-level vision tasks. Recent approaches leveraging generative priors have shown promise for TDIR; however, they typically suffer from computational inefficiency and potential semantic alteration by indiscriminately updating all latent tokens. In this paper, we posit that not all visual information is equally important for machine perception. Through an analysis of the latent token space, we observe that task-relevant cues are unevenly distributed across the token sequence, exhibiting index-wise specialization. This suggests that selectively refining a subset of tokens can be sufficient for task-driven objectives. Leveraging this insight, we propose TaskTok, a novel framework that selectively restores only task-relevant tokens via a learnable token switch and a lightweight token refinement module. Extensive experiments across image classification, semantic segmentation, and object detection demonstrate that TaskTok significantly enhances task performance with high computational efficiency. The source code is available at https://github.com/jimmy9704/TaskTok

AI.tldr: TaskTok is a novel framework for Task-Driven Image Restoration that selectively refines only task-relevant latent tokens (rather than all tokens), improving downstream task performance with high computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: Traditional image restoration focuses on perceptual quality, but TDIR aims to maximize downstream task performance. Existing generative prior methods are computationally inefficient and risk semantic alteration by indiscriminately updating all latent tokens, despite the observation that not all visual information is equally important for machine perception.
AI.method: TaskTok introduces a learnable token switch that identifies task-relevant tokens based on observed index-wise specialization in the latent token space, coupled with a lightweight token refinement module that selectively restores only those tokens.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across image classification, semantic segmentation, and object detection demonstrate that TaskTok significantly enhances task performance while maintaining high computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Selectively refining a subset of task-relevant tokens is sufficient for task-driven restoration objectives, offering an efficient alternative to indiscriminate token updating approaches.

title: Clinical Harness for Governable Medical AI Skill Ecosystems
summary: Medical AI remains organized around isolated models, whereas clinical care requires accountable capabilities that persist across time. We propose clinical AI skills and the Clinical Harness: a runtime governance architecture for registering, orchestrating, guarding and monitoring AI-enabled clinical capabilities. Using osteoporosis as an exemplar, we show how knowledge-driven, data-driven and physics-enhanced skills can support lifecycle care under runtime governance.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes "clinical AI skills" and a "Clinical Harness" runtime governance architecture to move medical AI from isolated models to persistent, accountable clinical capabilities, demonstrated via osteoporosis lifecycle care using knowledge-driven, data-driven, and physics-enhanced skills.
AI.motivation: Current medical AI is organized around isolated models that lack persistence and accountability, whereas clinical care requires capabilities that operate reliably across time and care episodes.
AI.method: The authors propose the Clinical Harness—a runtime governance architecture for registering, orchestrating, guarding, and monitoring AI-enabled clinical skills. They demonstrate it using osteoporosis as an exemplar, combining knowledge-driven, data-driven, and physics-enhanced AI skills to support lifecycle care.
AI.result: A demonstration showing how multiple AI skill types (knowledge-driven, data-driven, physics-enhanced) can be composed under runtime governance to support continuous osteoporosis care.
AI.conclusion: The Clinical Harness architecture enables accountable, persistent AI capabilities suitable for clinical workflows, moving beyond isolated models toward integrated, governed AI skills that align with real-world care pathways.

title: Beyond Feedforward Networks: Reentry Neural Systems as the Fundamental Basis of Subjecthood and Intrinsic Safety of Next-Generation AGI
summary: We propose a complete architectural blueprint for safe artificial general intelligence based on a closed reentry loop (D <-> I cycle). In contrast to feedforward networks, which are directed acyclic graphs (C=0, S=0) incapable of self-reference, the proposed architecture contains a structural cycle (C >= 1) with self-sustaining amplification (rho > 1), mathematically guaranteeing the emergence of a self-model, instrumental self-preservation, and unprogrammed goal-directed behaviour. The agent's goals are encoded as a non-textual D-vector in the architecture itself, making them immune to reinterpretation and prompt injection. We present the S-measure -- a polynomial-time [O(N^3)] computable alternative to Tononi's NP-hard Phi -- with machine-verified Lean 4 proof that S>0 implies positive integrated information. The work provides full Python/NumPy implementations (Tarjan-based cycle complexity, Delta-S barrier), industrial horizontal scaling via Apache Kafka and Docker Compose, a taxonomy of six epochs of AI evolution, a zoo of future reentry architectures (RAS, diffusion attractors, fractal loops), gauge-invariant networks for safe swarms, fault-tolerance and recovery protocols, and eight falsifiable predictions. All formal proofs are machine-verified in Lean 4. This architecture is deployable today and represents a topologically protected, safe-by-design approach to AGI.

AI.tldr: Proposes a safe-by-design AGI architecture based on a closed reentry loop (D↔I cycle) with structural cycles guaranteeing self-model emergence, self-preservation, and unprogrammed goal-directed behavior, including formal proofs, polynomial-time integrated information measure, and full deployable implementations.
AI.motivation: Current feedforward networks (directed acyclic graphs) lack self-reference and are vulnerable to reinterpretation and prompt injection. The motivation is to create an AGI architecture that is intrinsically safe, topologically protected, and mathematically guaranteed to avoid these issues.
AI.method: Introduces a closed reentry loop architecture (D↔I cycle) with C ≥ 1 structural cycles and ρ > 1 self-sustaining amplification. Proposes the S-measure (O(N³) polynomial-time) as an alternative to Tononi's NP-hard Phi for integrated information. Provides Tarjan-based cycle complexity and Delta-S barrier implementations in Python/NumPy, with industrial scaling via Kafka and Docker Compose.
AI.result: Machine-verified Lean 4 proof that S>0 implies positive integrated information. Eight falsifiable predictions presented. Full architectural blueprint with fault-tolerance, recovery protocols, and taxonomy of six AI evolution epochs. Gauge-invariant networks for safe swarms and future reentry architectures (RAS, diffusion attractors, fractal loops) specified.
AI.conclusion: This safe-by-design AGI architecture is deployable today with topologically protected guarantees against unsafe emergent behaviors, immune to prompt injection via D-vector encoding, and backed by machine-verified formal proofs.

title: Position Rebinding Cache Reuse: Replay-Free Visual Revisiting for Interleaved Multimodal Reasoning
summary: Interleaved multimodal reasoning improves visual grounding by revisiting visual evidence during multi-step generation, yet existing methods typically rely on token replay, repeatedly forwarding selected visual tokens. A natural shortcut is to reuse the historical visual key-value (KV) cache directly. However, we identify a critical failure mode of this strategy: cached visual keys are already bound to their original positional context. Such stale positional binding distorts attention under later decoding contexts and can trigger severe autoregressive decoding collapse. This failure suggests that effective cache reuse requires reconstructing visual evidence under positions compatible with the current decoding state, rather than directly copying position-bound historical cache entries. To this end, we propose Position Rebinding Cache Reuse (PRCR), a cache-level framework for replay-free visual revisiting. PRCR stores raw visual KV cache together with their original spatial coordinates, then reassigns position-compatible coordinates to select entries and rebinds their keys before injecting the reconstructed cache into the active decoder cache. This design reuses historical visual evidence while preserving textual positional continuity and relative visual structure. Experiments across multiple multimodal reasoning benchmarks show that PRCR achieves replay-level or better performance, improving average accuracy by 5 percent and reducing visual-revisiting computation by up to tens of thousands of times.

AI.tldr: PRCR (Position Rebinding Cache Reuse) is a cache-level framework that enables replay-free visual revisiting in multimodal reasoning by storing raw visual KV cache with spatial coordinates and rebinding them to position-compatible coordinates before reuse, achieving replay-level performance with drastically reduced computation.
AI.motivation: Existing interleaved multimodal reasoning methods rely on token replay (repeatedly forwarding selected visual tokens) for visual revisiting. A natural shortcut is reusing historical visual KV cache, but this fails because cached visual keys are bound to their original positional context, causing stale positional binding that distorts attention and triggers autoregressive decoding collapse.
AI.method: PRCR stores raw visual KV cache together with original spatial coordinates. It reassigns position-compatible coordinates to select entries, rebinds their keys, and injects the reconstructed cache into the active decoder cache, avoiding direct copying of position-bound historical cache entries.
AI.result: Experiments across multiple multimodal reasoning benchmarks show PRCR achieves replay-level or better performance, improves average accuracy by 5%, and reduces visual-revisiting computation by up to tens of thousands of times.
AI.conclusion: Effective cache reuse requires reconstructing visual evidence under positions compatible with the current decoding state rather than directly copying position-bound historical cache entries.

title: Humans Disengage, Reasoning Models Persist: Separating Difficulty Registration from Deliberation Allocation
summary: Large reasoning models (LRMs) take longer on harder problems, just as humans do. This surface similarity hides an opposite pattern within items. When an LRM gets a problem wrong, it spends more tokens than when it gets the same problem right; humans do the reverse, spending less time on the trials they get wrong. We separate two levels of deliberation: how response time tracks difficulty across items (registration), and, with item identity held fixed, whether an agent spends more on its own failures or successes (allocation).
