AI.motivation: Public sector organizations, especially tax administrations, need to analyze growing volumes of multilingual service feedback to identify emerging quality issues and potential disparities, but traditional manual or static indicator-based approaches lack scalability and cannot capture complex textual patterns.
AI.method: Combines fine-tuned, quantized LLMs with statistical techniques and expert oversight in a human-in-the-loop framework to analyze multilingual customer feedback, balancing computational efficiency with context-aware analysis.
AI.result: Evaluated via similarity analysis and assessments from experienced tax officers; demonstrated stronger alignment with expert judgments than baseline models, with reduced LLM fabrication and improved reliability of generated insights.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework supports scalable, evidence-based decision-making in public sector organizations, contributing to responsible AI systems that enhance service quality, responsiveness, fairness, and public trust.

title: Localizing RL-Induced Tool Use to a Single Crosscoder Feature
comment: Accepted as a spotlight at the ICML 2026 Mechanistic Interpretability Workshop
summary: Fine-tuning through RL reshapes the internal representations of language models to enable agentic behaviors such as tool use, yet the mechanistic basis of these changes remains poorly understood. While RL substantially improves structured tool-call generation, it is unclear which features emerge, which are preserved, and whether identified features can be leveraged for retraining-free behavioral control. In this work, we show that $\textit{Dedicated Feature Crosscoders (DFC)}$ isolate a compact set of RL-specific features that mediate tool-calling capability in $\texttt{Qwen2.5-3B}$. Across a $48$-crosscoder hyperparameter sweep, encode-decode reconstruction improves the RL model's tool correctness by $+31.1 \pm {9.7}$ pp and passively transfers tool-calling ability to the frozen base model by $+6.8 \pm 5.0$ pp which we call a $\textit{capability spillover}$. Our findings show that DFC partitioning concentrates RL-introduced capability into a minimal, steerable feature set that enables runtime behavioral control of agentic LLMs.

AI.tldr: Dedicated Feature Crosscoders (DFC) isolate compact RL-specific features that mediate tool-calling in Qwen2.5-3B, improving tool correctness by +31.1 pp via reconstruction and enabling +6.8 pp capability spillover to frozen base models.
AI.motivation: Fine-tuning via RL reshapes LM internal representations to enable agentic behaviors like tool use, but the mechanistic basis of these changes is poorly understood—specifically which features emerge, which are preserved, and whether identified features enable retraining-free behavioral control.
AI.method: Propose Dedicated Feature Crosscoders (DFC) to isolate RL-specific features; conducted a 48-crosscoder hyperparameter sweep; encode-decode reconstruction and passive transfer experiments on Qwen2.5-3B.
AI.result: DFC reconstruction improves RL model's tool correctness by +31.1 ± 9.7 percentage points; passive transfer yields +6.8 ± 5.0 pp capability spillover to the frozen base model.
AI.conclusion: DFC partitioning concentrates RL-introduced capability into a minimal, steerable feature set that enables runtime behavioral control of agentic LLMs.

title: Full spectrum Unlearnable Examples via Spectral Equalization
summary: Unlearnable examples (UEs) protect training data by injecting imperceptible perturbations so that models fail to extract exploitable representations. In this paper, we reveal that existing UEs exhibit a critical failure once low-pass filtering is applied, indicating that the effective perturbation signals for unlearnability concentrate predominantly in high frequencies. Hence, we argue that reliable UEs should remain effective across the full spectrum. To this end, we propose Full-spectrum Unlearnable examples via Spectral Equalization (FUSE), which aims to generate spectrum-agnostic perturbations by equalizing the contributions from different bands and enforcing cross-band consistency. Specifically, FUSE adopts a Random Spectral Masking (RSM) strategy during generator training, which randomly removes a contiguous frequency band, forcing the remaining bands to maintain unlearnability. In addition, FUSE further integrates Cross-Band Guidance (CBG), which enforces mutual consistency between high- and low-frequency components, thereby further enhancing low-frequency unlearnability and regulating high-frequency perturbations to preserve the semantic fidelity of images. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets, architectures, and spectral filtering demonstrate the strong protection achieved by FUSE.

AI.tldr: Existing unlearnable examples (UEs) fail under low-pass filtering because perturbations concentrate in high frequencies. The authors propose FUSE, which uses Random Spectral Masking (RSM) and Cross-Band Guidance (CBG) to generate spectrum-agnostic perturbations that remain effective across the full frequency spectrum.
AI.motivation: Existing UEs fail under low-pass filtering because their effective perturbations are concentrated in high frequencies, making them unreliable; thus, there is a need for UEs that remain effective across the full spectrum.
AI.method: FUSE introduces (1) Random Spectral Masking (RSM) to randomly remove a contiguous frequency band during training, forcing remaining bands to maintain unlearnability; and (2) Cross-Band Guidance (CBG) to enforce consistency between high- and low-frequency components, enhancing low-frequency unlearnability while regulating high-frequency perturbations to preserve semantic fidelity.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across multiple datasets, architectures, and spectral filtering demonstrate strong protection achieved by FUSE.
AI.conclusion: Reliable unlearnable examples should be effective across the full frequency spectrum, and FUSE achieves this through spectrum-agnostic perturbation generation.

title: Autoformalization of Agent Instructions into Policy-as-Code
comment: Accepted at the Second Workshop on Agents in the Wild: Safety, Security, and Beyond (AIWILD), ICML 2026
summary: Agent safety in high-stakes domains requires formal policy enforcement, but most existing approaches either rely on probabilistic guardrails (fine-tuned classifiers, prompt-based steering) that offer no formal guarantees, or on hand-coded symbolic enforcement that does not scale to the breadth of real policy specifications. We present an autoformalization pipeline that translates agent prompts, MCP tool descriptions, and natural language policy documents into formally verified policies using an LLM-based generator-critic loop. The resulting policies are written in the Cedar Policy Language. On the MedAgentBench benchmark, our autoformalized policies cover substantially more of the source natural-language specification than the hand-coded symbolic enforcement in prior work.

AI.tldr: An LLM-based autoformalization pipeline translates natural language policies into formally verified Cedar policies, outperforming hand-coded symbolic enforcement on MedAgentBench.
AI.motivation: Agent safety in high-stakes domains requires formal policy enforcement, but existing approaches either offer no formal guarantees (probabilistic guardrails) or do not scale to real policy specifications (hand-coded symbolic enforcement).
AI.method: An autoformalization pipeline that uses an LLM-based generator-critic loop to translate agent prompts, MCP tool descriptions, and natural language policy documents into formally verified policies written in the Cedar Policy Language.
AI.result: On the MedAgentBench benchmark, the autoformalized policies cover substantially more of the source natural-language specification than the hand-coded symbolic enforcement in prior work.
AI.conclusion: Autoformalization via LLM-based generator-critic loops can produce formally verified policies that achieve better coverage of natural language specifications compared to manual symbolic encoding, offering a scalable approach to formal policy enforcement for agent safety.

title: Retrieval-Warmed Energy-Based Reasoning: A Five-Arm Ablation Methodology for Diffusion-as-Inference on Structured Reasoning Tasks
summary: Warm-started diffusion samplers accelerate iterative inference, but it is rarely clear which part of the pipeline carries the gain. We study \textbf{retrieval-warmed energy-based reasoning (RW-EBR)} -- an IRED energy-based diffusion model \cite{du2024ired} augmented with a Modern Hopfield trajectory memory -- and contribute a \textbf{five-arm ablation methodology} (oracle, best-constant, per-query-random, shuffled, aligned) that separates three confounded effects: class-prior bias shift, stochastic warm-starting, and graph-aligned value reuse. The diagnostic decomposition is adapted from LLM-RAG evaluation \cite{ru2024ragchecker}. On \textbf{connectivity-2} (Erdős--Rényi all-pairs reachability), the aligned-vs-shuffled-oracle swing reaches \textbf{$+35$\,pp} balanced accuracy on a fixed 1{,}000-graph validation-set diagnostic, with value distribution and retrieval mechanics fixed, only per-graph alignment destroyed, while per-query random initialisation falls below cold -- per-graph alignment, not bias shift or stochasticity, dominates. Yet the \emph{deployable} cold-prediction pipeline misses the acceptance gate at stored-value quality. The same diagnostic logic, stopped at the key-quality screen, applied to \textbf{Sudoku} with a task-specific key encoder produces a clean negative at a \emph{different} component -- key quality, under the current setup. The decomposition names the first blocking component on each task. The setting -- graph reachability refined by an iterative diffusion sampler, with explainability of failure modes as the lens -- places the work within structured and spatio-temporal reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RW-EBR (retrieval-warmed energy-based reasoning) and a five-arm ablation methodology to separate confounded effects (class-prior bias, stochastic warm-starting, graph-aligned value reuse) in warm-started diffusion samplers. On connectivity-2 tasks, per-graph alignment dominates performance (+35 pp balanced accuracy), while on Sudoku, the blocking component is key quality under the current setup.
AI.motivation: Warm-started diffusion samplers accelerate iterative inference, but which component of the pipeline contributes the gain is unclear. The authors aim to disentangle three confounded effects: class-prior bias shift, stochastic warm-starting, and graph-aligned value reuse.
AI.method: A five-arm ablation methodology (oracle, best-constant, per-query-random, shuffled, aligned) adapted from LLM-RAG evaluation, applied to an IRED energy-based diffusion model augmented with a Modern Hopfield trajectory memory (RW-EBR).
AI.result: On connectivity-2 (Erdős–Rényi all-pairs reachability), aligned-vs-shuffled-oracle swing reaches +35 pp balanced accuracy; per-query random initialization underperforms cold start. On Sudoku, the diagnostic identifies key quality as the blocking component.
AI.conclusion: Per-graph alignment, not bias shift or stochasticity, dominates performance on graph reachability. The decomposition can identify the first blocking component per task. Key quality is the bottleneck for Sudoku under the current setup.

title: Robust Onion: Peeling Open Vocab Object Detectors Under Noise
comment: Accepted at The 19th European Conference on Computer Vision (ECCV)
summary: The impact of real-world noise on Open Vocabulary Object Detectors (OV-ODs) remains poorly understood due to their architectural complexity. We present our comprehensive analysis Robust Onion, an empirical study that uses controlled synthetic visual degradations to peel OV-ODs layer-by-layer, revealing how, why, and where robustness degrades, systematically analyzing feature collapse. Our findings reveal that models with similar vision backbones exhibit comparable robustness, driven by similar feature collapse at similar layers, while factors such as pretraining strategy, architectural nuances, and caption supervision contribute little. Robustness is primarily governed by the image domain rather than annotations, explaining the similar robustness impact on COCO and LVIS, and why datasets like ODinW-13 can give an impression of inflated robustness due to large, isolated objects. Finally, we validate our insights by improving robustness on real-world BDD100K, WiderFace, and VisDRONE via our lightweight plug-and-play NN & TK0 approach, using 96x fewer trainable parameters than end-to-end training. We also explain the prior works' robustness observations.

AI.tldr: Robust Onion systematically analyzes how real-world noise degrades Open Vocabulary Object Detectors (OV-ODs) using controlled synthetic degradations, revealing that robustness is primarily determined by vision backbone similarity and image domain rather than annotations or supervision strategies. The authors propose a lightweight plug-and-play method (NN & TK0) that improves robustness with 96x fewer parameters than full fine-tuning, validated on real-world datasets.
AI.motivation: The impact of real-world noise on Open Vocabulary Object Detectors (OV-ODs) is poorly understood due to their architectural complexity. This work aims to systematically peel OV-ODs layer-by-layer to reveal how, why, and where robustness degrades under noise.
AI.method: Controlled synthetic visual degradations are applied to OV-ODs layer-by-layer to analyze feature collapse. The study examines the influence of vision backbones, pretraining strategies, architectural nuances, caption supervision, and image domains on robustness. A lightweight plug-and-play NN & TK0 approach is proposed for robustness improvements.
AI.result: Models with similar vision backbones exhibit comparable robustness due to similar feature collapse. Robustness is primarily governed by the image domain rather than annotations. The proposed NN & TK0 method improves robustness on real-world datasets (BDD100K, WiderFace, VisDRONE) using 96x fewer trainable parameters than end-to-end training. Previous works' robustness observations are also explained.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a systematic understanding of OV-OD robustness, showing that backbone similarity and image domain are key factors, and offers an efficient lightweight approach to improve robustness in real-world scenarios.

title: SKILL-DISCO: Distilling and Compiling Agent Traces into Reusable Procedural Skills
summary: Agents often repeatedly solve similar task instances from scratch, leading to unnecessary reasoning cost and long execution traces. Prior work has explored workflow reuse and executable skill induction, but it remains unclear which task scenarios admit procedural skills and how the shared procedural structure should be represented across successful traces. We study this problem in FSM-defined scenarios, where successful traces can be viewed as paths in an unknown transition graph, and formulate procedural skills as reusable parameterized control-flow subgraphs. Based on this view, we introduce SkillDisCo, a distillation-and-compilation framework that distills reusable PFSM subgraphs from successful traces and compiles them into callable, executable, and verifiable procedural skills. Experiments on ALFWorld and WebArena show that SkillDisCo improves success rates and reduces agent turns across benchmarks and model scales, demonstrating the benefits of representing shared experience as reusable execution structures.

AI.tldr: SkillDisCo distills reusable procedural skills from successful agent traces by identifying shared control-flow subgraphs in FSM-defined scenarios, improving success rates and reducing agent turns on ALFWorld and WebArena.
AI.motivation: Agents repeatedly solve similar task instances from scratch, incurring unnecessary reasoning cost and long execution traces. Prior work on workflow reuse and skill induction lacks clarity on which scenarios admit procedural skills and how shared procedural structure should be represented.
AI.method: SkillDisCo is a distillation-and-compilation framework that distills reusable parameterized PFSM (parameterized finite state machine) subgraphs from successful traces and compiles them into callable, executable, and verifiable procedural skills.
AI.result: Experiments on ALFWorld and WebArena show SkillDisCo improves success rates and reduces agent turns across benchmarks and model scales.
AI.conclusion: Representing shared experience as reusable execution structures (procedural skills) benefits agent performance by reducing redundancy and improving effectiveness.

title: What Survives When You Compress a Recursive Reasoner for the Edge?
summary: Recursive reasoning models can solve complex structured tasks with only a few million parameters by repeatedly updating a latent state. Deploying these models on edge hardware requires significant compression, but unlike conventional sequence models, quantization errors compound across recursive reasoning cycles rather than across output tokens. As a result, standard intuitions about compression fail to apply. In this work, we ask what survives when recursive reasoners are compressed. Across a full precision sweep, three tasks, and two recursive architectures, we find that aggressive compression preserves local prediction but destroys global reasoning: cell accuracy holds while puzzle-exact accuracy collapses to zero under naive INT4 pruning, distillation, and linear attention alike. Token-level objectives, including quantization-aware training, cannot repair it. The collapse is architectural -- it strikes MLP-mixing recursion but not attention on the same task -- and we reverse it with per-channel calibrated INT4 without retraining. We also introduce carry-trajectory fidelity, the cosine similarity to the full-precision reasoning path, as a label-free signal that predicts this damage and its recovery before a task evaluation. The combined result is a deployment recipe: flash-streamed embeddings remove a 99.4MB bottleneck, INT8 at one cycle matches full-depth accuracy at 6x fewer FLOPs (8MB SoC), and calibrated INT4 fits a 4MB microcontroller.

AI.tldr: Compressing recursive reasoning models destroys global reasoning ability (puzzle accuracy collapses to zero) even when local predictions survive. The damage is architectural, predicted by a novel "carry-trajectory fidelity" metric, and can be reversed with per-channel calibrated INT4 quantization without retraining. A deployment recipe achieves 4MB microcontroller fit.
AI.motivation: Recursive reasoning models are capable of solving complex structured tasks with few parameters, but deploying them on edge hardware requires aggressive compression. Unlike standard sequence models, quantization errors in these models compound across recursive reasoning cycles (not output tokens), making standard compression intuitions invalid. The authors investigate what survives when recursive reasoners are compressed to enable edge deployment.
AI.method: Full precision sweep across three tasks and two recursive architectures (MLP-mixing recursion and attention). Tested naive INT4 pruning, distillation, linear attention, and quantization-aware training. Introduced carry-trajectory fidelity (cosine similarity to full-precision reasoning path) as a label-free diagnostic signal. Proposed per-channel calibrated INT4 quantization without retraining.
AI.result: Aggressive compression preserves local prediction (cell accuracy) but destroys global reasoning (puzzle-exact accuracy collapses to zero). Token-level objectives cannot repair it. The collapse is architectural: MLP-mixing recursion fails but attention on same task does not. Per-channel calibrated INT4 reverses the collapse without retraining. Flash-streamed embeddings remove a 99.4MB bottleneck. INT8 at one cycle matches full-depth accuracy at 6x fewer FLOPs (8MB SoC). Calibrated INT4 fits a 4MB microcontroller.
AI.conclusion: Standard compression techniques fail for recursive reasoners due to error compounding across reasoning cycles. Per-channel calibrated INT4 is an effective deployment strategy. Carry-trajectory fidelity is a useful label-free diagnostic metric. A practical deployment recipe is provided: flash-streamed embeddings, INT8 for balanced efficiency, and calibrated INT4 for microcontroller-level deployment.

title: Do Image Editing Models Understand Lighting?
summary: While recent advancements in generative image editing models have achieved stunning visual fidelity, it remains an open question whether these systems possess an intrinsic knowledge of real-world lighting. Existing benchmarks typically evaluate high-level plausibility of perceptual light transport on curated internet imagery, using VLMs or human judgement, or they rely on synthetically generated datasets. In this work, we introduce the 3D-anchored Light Probe (3DLP) benchmark, for which we have captured a new high-fidelity HDR dataset of real-world lighting changes. The dataset consists of 1K image pairs of diverse indoor scenery in which light probes are physically turned on and off. To allow for a granular performance analysis, we annotated specific image regions such as cast shadows or metallic surfaces. With this data, we evaluate a range of state-of-the-art image editing models by measuring how well their light probe edits align with reality. The evaluation uses two new scores to compensate for AI-generated photographic effects, such as adjusted white balance. Our results show that the overall performance of models differs considerably, with differences slightly less pronounced for specular highlights. The best image editing models are remarkably consistent with real-world physics, however, they still leave room for improvement. We observe that image regions that receive less light from the light probe are more prone to errors for all models. Furthermore, building on their success in evaluating macroscopic lighting plausibility, we test VLMs on our task but find that they are unsuitable for pixel-level light transport analysis. We will make the benchmark, together with the real-world dataset, publicly available to encourage future research on this topic.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces 3DLP, a benchmark with a real-world HDR dataset of lighting changes (light probes on/off) to evaluate image editing models' physical lighting accuracy, finding that while top models align well with real-world physics, they still have room for improvement, and VLMs are unsuitable for pixel-level light transport analysis.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks evaluate lighting plausibility either using VLMs/human judgement on internet imagery (subjective) or rely on synthetic datasets (lacking realism). There is no rigorous benchmark with real-world lighting ground truth to assess whether generative image editing models intrinsically understand physical light transport.
AI.method: The authors captured 1K high-fidelity HDR image pairs of diverse indoor scenes with light probes physically turned on/off, annotated with specific regions (cast shadows, metallic surfaces). They proposed two new evaluation scores that compensate for AI-generated photographic effects (e.g., white balance adjustments). They tested multiple state-of-the-art image editing models and VLMs on this benchmark.
AI.result: (1) Model performance varies considerably, with smaller differences for specular highlights. (2) Best models are remarkably consistent with real-world physics but still have room for improvement. (3) Regions receiving less light from the probe are more error-prone across all models. (4) VLMs are unsuitable for pixel-level light transport analysis despite their success in macroscopic plausibility assessment.
AI.conclusion: Real-world HDR benchmarks like 3DLP are essential for evaluating physical lighting understanding in generative models. Current models show promise but need further improvement, especially in low-light regions. The benchmark and dataset will be released publicly to advance research in this direction.

title: NebulaExp-8B: An Empirical Post-Training Pipeline via Full-Scale Ablation Research
summary: Post-training alignment determines the reasoning and human preference following capabilities of large language models, yet most existing works withhold detailed data construction, filtering rules and training recipes, which hinders community reproducibility and lightweight model optimization. This work presents NebulaExp, a fully transparent, ablation-driven post-training pipeline built on Qwen3-8B-base, covering two orthogonal model branches: general instruct model and complex reasoning-specialized model. We curate a raw corpus of 3.84M multi-source SFT samples and a 200K verifiable RL candidate pool, and design an end-to-end data processing stack including response distillation, multi-dimensional cross-verification filtering, fine-grained difficulty grading, task classification and diversity-aware sampling. For the Instruct branch, our three-stage optimized supervised fine-tuning approach NebulaExp-Ins-SFT improves the average benchmark score from the 55.01 baseline of Qwen3-8B-nothink to 60.99. GRPO reinforcement learning then further elevates the average score to 61.85. For the Reasoning branch, medium-difficulty GRPO RL improves average reasoning score from 73.88 to 75.17. To address RL's dependency on task verifiers, we systematically investigate single-teacher and multi-teacher OPD (MOPD): utilizing merely 4K instruction-following samples and outperforms RL baseline by 3.26 points on IFEval with +4.43 average overall gain; MOPD fuses four domain-specialist teachers with merely 10K samples, lifting average performance by 4.18 over the base model. This report provides a fully reproducible empirical post-training recipe for 8B-scale LLMs, and comprehensively dissects the capability trade-offs among instruction adherence, mathematical reasoning, code generation and general knowledge.

AI.tldr: NebulaExp presents a fully transparent, ablation-driven post-training pipeline for Qwen3-8B-base, covering both general instruct and reasoning-specialized model branches. It includes a 3.84M SFT corpus, a 200K RL candidate pool, multi-stage SFT (+5.98 points), GRPO RL (+0.86), and OPD/MOPD methods that outperform RL baselines using only 4K/10K samples, achieving up to +4.43 average gain.
AI.motivation: Existing post-training alignment works withhold detailed data construction, filtering rules and training recipes, hindering reproducibility and lightweight model optimization. NebulaExp aims to provide a fully transparent, reproducible pipeline.
AI.method: The pipeline includes: (1) a 3.84M multi-source SFT corpus and 200K verifiable RL pool; (2) end-to-end data processing (response distillation, multi-dimensional cross-verification filtering, difficulty grading, task classification, diversity-aware sampling); (3) three-stage optimized SFT for the Instruct branch; (4) GRPO RL for both Instruct and Reasoning branches; (5) single-teacher OPD and multi-teacher MOPD using 4K-10K samples to replace RL's verifier dependency.
AI.result: Instruct branch: SFT improves from 55.01 to 60.99; GRPO RL further to 61.85. Reasoning branch: GRPO RL improves from 73.88 to 75.17. OPD: outperforms RL baseline by 3.26 on IFEval with +4.43 average overall gain using only 4K samples. MOPD: fuses four domain-specialist teachers with 10K samples, lifting average performance by 4.18 over base model.
AI.conclusion: The study provides a fully reproducible empirical post-training recipe for 8B-scale LLMs, comprehensively dissecting capability trade-offs among instruction adherence, mathematical reasoning, code generation and general knowledge. OPD/MOPD effectively address RL's dependency on task verifiers with far fewer samples.

title: Learning Probabilistic Filters with Strictly Proper Scoring Rules
summary: Bayesian filtering of partially and noisily observed dynamical systems seeks to infer the evolving conditional distribution of the state of a dynamical system, given observations, in an online fashion. This Bayesian filtering distribution is the natural object for uncertainty quantification, but it is rarely available as a supervised learning target. However, one can often use the forecast model to generate synthetic system trajectories, along with synthetic observations. We introduce the proper scoring ensemble filter (PSEF), an ensemble data assimilation method based on training an analysis map to approximate the filtering distribution using only synthetic state--observation trajectories. The analysis step is represented as a permutation-invariant, transformer-based map that takes as input a forecast ensemble and observations, producing an analysis ensemble. Training is based on strictly proper scoring rules -- with the energy score used in our implementation -- so that probabilistic accuracy is rewarded over the whole probability distribution. We prove that, under a realizability assumption, the population objective is minimized by the true Bayesian filtering distribution. We also derive the finite-ensemble empirical objective used in training and relate its single state--observation trajectory form to the population objective, using a mean-field consistency argument. Numerical experiments show that the learned filter accurately approximates challenging filtering distributions, including nonlinear, non-Gaussian, and multi-modal posteriors, and achieves stronger performance in data assimilation tasks than classical methods or learning-based methods with mean-squared-error objectives. For close-to-Gaussian problems, learning a correction to the EnKF is the best approach, while for highly non-Gaussian problems an end-to-end approach that discards this inductive bias is superior.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Proper Scoring Ensemble Filter (PSEF), a transformer-based ensemble data assimilation method that learns to approximate Bayesian filtering distributions from synthetic trajectories using strictly proper scoring rules (energy score), proving realizability-based convergence and demonstrating superior performance on nonlinear/non-Gaussian problems compared to classical and MSE-based learning methods.
AI.motivation: Bayesian filtering distributions are crucial for uncertainty quantification in partially/noisily observed dynamical systems, but are rarely available as supervised learning targets. The authors aim to approximate these distributions using only synthetic state-observation trajectories generated from the forecast model.
AI.method: PSEF trains a permutation-invariant transformer-based analysis map that takes a forecast ensemble and observations as input and produces an analysis ensemble. Training uses strictly proper scoring rules (energy score) to reward probabilistic accuracy over the whole distribution. The method can work as a correction to EnKF (for near-Gaussian problems) or as an end-to-end approach (for highly non-Gaussian problems).
AI.result: PSEF accurately approximates challenging filtering distributions including nonlinear, non-Gaussian, and multi-modal posteriors. It achieves stronger performance than classical methods and learning-based methods with MSE objectives. For near-Gaussian problems, learning a correction to EnKF is best; for highly non-Gaussian problems, an end-to-end approach without this inductive bias is superior.
AI.conclusion: The proper scoring ensemble filter provides a principled, data-driven framework for ensemble data assimilation that rewards probabilistic accuracy via proper scoring rules, with theoretical guarantees under realizability and practical superiority across diverse filtering regimes.

title: LiveEdit: Towards Real-Time Diffusion-Based Streaming Video Editing
comment: Accepted by ECCV 2026, Project page: https://live-edit.github.io
summary: Streaming video editing has made rapid progress, yet practical deployment is still limited by two core issues: maintaining stable backgrounds and non-edited regions over time, and achieving the low latency required for real-time interactive scenarios. Meanwhile, recent streaming video generation methods are mostly developed for synthesis and cannot be directly applied to editing due to the strict preservation requirement and region-specific control. In this work, we present a novel streaming video editing framework that performs causal, frame-by-frame editing with strong content preservation and real-time responsiveness. Our key design is a three-stage distillation pipeline that progressively transfers editing capability from a powerful bidirectional foundation model to an efficient unidirectional streaming editor, enabling stable long-horizon edits without sacrificing visual fidelity. To further support real-time deployment, we introduce an AR-oriented mask cache that reuses region-related computation across frames, substantially reducing redundant processing and accelerating inference. Finally, we establish a dedicated benchmark for streaming video editing. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art visual quality among streaming baselines while drastically boosting inference speed to 12.66 FPS, making it suitable for interactive and augmented reality applications.

AI.tldr: A streaming video editing framework using a three-stage distillation pipeline (bidirectional→unidirectional) and an AR-oriented mask cache achieves real-time (12.66 FPS), frame-by-frame editing with strong content preservation and stable backgrounds.
AI.motivation: Existing streaming video editing methods suffer from unstable backgrounds/non-edited regions over time and lack the low latency needed for real-time interactive scenarios; streaming video generation methods cannot be directly applied to editing due to strict preservation and region-specific control needs.
AI.method: A three-stage distillation pipeline progressively transfers editing capability from a powerful bidirectional foundation model to an efficient unidirectional streaming editor, plus an AR-oriented mask cache that reuses region-related computation across frames to reduce redundancy.
AI.result: State-of-the-art visual quality among streaming baselines, with inference speed boosted to 12.66 FPS, suitable for interactive and augmented reality applications.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework enables causal, frame-by-frame streaming video editing with strong content preservation and real-time responsiveness, addressing key bottlenecks for practical deployment.

title: Do Safety Guardrails Need to Reason? LeanGuard: A Fast and Light Approach for Robust Moderation
comment: 9 pages, 6 figures, 3 tables. Project page: https://ndb796.github.io/LeanGuard ; code and models: https://github.com/ndb796/LeanGuard
summary: In order to screen a prompt or a response, the recent guardrail methods generate a chain-of-thought (CoT) before they issue a verdict. This design follows a common belief that step-by-step reasoning improves a decision. However, CoT also makes the guard heavy and slow, because the model must generate many tokens before it decides. This may not match how guardrails are actually deployed. A guardrail sometimes should not be heavy and slow, and it often runs on-device, for example on an embodied robot. In this paper, we pose a question whether a safety guardrail really needs to reason. To answer this question, we train a lightweight bidirectional encoder and a reasoning guard on the same corpus, and we then remove only the reasoning while we keep everything else fixed. With this controlled same-base comparison, we show that the chain does not improve moderation accuracy. We name the resulting guard LeanGuard. A 395M label-only encoder reaches an average F1 of 82.90 $\pm$ 0.26 over public benchmarks. It matches a reasoning guard that is built on a much larger decoder, while it uses only a single forward pass over an input of at most 512 tokens. This is about a ~100x reduction in inference compute. We further show that this label-only encoder stays robust under training-label noise and retains far more recall at a strict false-positive rate than the reasoning guard, so a heavier reasoning guard is not the more robust choice either. Our finding suggests that the current guardrail benchmarks may not be hard enough to reward reasoning, and that the necessity of CoT for moderation is still not proven. We release all source codes and models including LeanGuard at https://github.com/ndb796/LeanGuard.

AI.tldr: LeanGuard shows that removing chain-of-thought reasoning from safety guardrails does not reduce accuracy (82.90 F1) while achieving ~100x inference compute reduction, challenging the necessity of CoT for moderation.
AI.motivation: Current guardrail methods use chain-of-thought reasoning before issuing a safety verdict, but this makes them heavy, slow, and unsuitable for on-device deployment (e.g., embodied robots). The authors question whether safety guardrails actually need reasoning.
AI.method: Train a lightweight bidirectional encoder (395M) and a reasoning guard on the same corpus, then remove only the reasoning while keeping everything else fixed for a controlled same-base comparison. The resulting model is called LeanGuard.
AI.result: LeanGuard achieves an average F1 of 82.90 ± 0.26 over public benchmarks, matching a much larger reasoning guard while using only a single forward pass over ≤512 tokens (~100x reduction in inference compute). It also stays robust under training-label noise and retains more recall at strict false-positive rates.
AI.conclusion: Current guardrail benchmarks may not be hard enough to reward reasoning, and the necessity of CoT for moderation remains unproven. A heavy reasoning guard is not necessarily more robust.

title: Multipath Adaptive Gated Bottleneck Latent ODE with Raman Data Fusion for Cell Culture Process Forecasting
summary: Mammalian cell-culture processes underpin the manufacture of many biopharmaceuticals, yet keeping a run on track is hard: critical process parameters drift over days, and an off-specification trend is often confirmed too late to intervene. Early-stage, multi-day forecasts could enable timely adjustment of feeding, sampling, and control, but bioprocess forecasting is challenging because measurements are sparse and irregularly sampled, operating conditions are heterogeneous across cell lines and media, and runs with near-identical early behaviour can diverge into different futures. We propose an adaptive framework combining a Gated Bottleneck Latent Ordinary Differential Equation (GB-Latent ODE) with Multi-Path Just-In-Time Fine Tuning (MP-JIT-FT). The GB-Latent ODE augments the stan dard Latent ODE with learnable variable-wise gating and a mask-aware bottleneck that compress high-dimensional sparse inputs, improving learning under limited data. Given a partially observed run, MP-JIT-FT retrieves similar historical trajectories, clusters the local neighbourhood into candidate regimes, and fine-tunes a separate model per regime to produce multiple plausible paths, each with a reconstruction-based confidence score, not a single averaged forecast. We further fuse Raman spectroscopy data: a machine-learning soft sensor turns dense Raman spectra into pseudo-observations that enrich the sparse offline measurements for more robust training. On 38 fed-batch 5L bioreactor runs spanning 14 conditions, MP-JIT-FT with Raman fusion achieves the best average rank and outperforms a global Latent ODE baseline on 8 of 9 target variables. Using local-divergence metrics, we show the multi-path gains are largest when locally similar prefixes diverge, whereas Raman fusion helps most when early dynamics are representative of later behaviour.

AI.tldr: Proposes an adaptive framework (GB-Latent ODE + MP-JIT-FT) with Raman fusion for multi-day forecasting of mammalian cell-culture bioprocesses, handling sparse, irregular measurements and divergent trajectories. Outperforms global Latent ODE baseline on 8 of 9 target variables across 38 bioreactor runs.
AI.motivation: Mammalian cell-culture bioprocesses are hard to keep on track due to drifting critical parameters, late detection of off-specification trends, sparse/irregular sampling, heterogeneous operating conditions, and runs with similar early behavior diverging into different futures—motivating early-stage, multi-day forecasts for timely intervention.
AI.method: (1) GB-Latent ODE: augments standard Latent ODE with learnable variable-wise gating and mask-aware bottleneck for sparse inputs. (2) MP-JIT-FT: retrieves similar historical trajectories, clusters into candidate regimes, fine-tunes separate models per regime for multiple plausible paths with confidence scores. (3) Raman fusion: ML soft sensor converts dense Raman spectra into pseudo-observations to enrich sparse offline measurements.
AI.result: On 38 fed-batch 5L bioreactor runs across 14 conditions, MP-JIT-FT with Raman fusion achieves best average rank and outperforms global Latent ODE baseline on 8 of 9 target variables. Multi-path gains are largest when locally similar prefixes diverge; Raman fusion helps most when early dynamics are representative of later behavior.
AI.conclusion: The adaptive framework with multi-path forecasting and Raman data fusion provides a promising approach for early-stage bioprocess forecasting, enabling timely control adjustments and improving robustness under data sparsity and trajectory divergence.

title: Depth-Semantic Alignment and Affinity-Guided Fusion for Structured Radar Point Cloud Generation
summary: Point clouds are an important carrier of three-dimensional spatial information, and their quality directly affects the performance of downstream perception tasks such as object detection and tracking. However, millimeter-wave radar point clouds are typically sparse, noisy, and structurally incomplete. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a multimodal point cloud generation method based on vision-radar fusion. The proposed method leverages image semantic information to impose structural constraints and achieve spatial alignment for radar point clouds, while incorporating a sparse completion strategy to enhance point density and recover missing structures. The generated point clouds are further evaluated in object detection and tracking tasks. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method effectively improves point cloud quality and enhances the detection accuracy and robustness of perception models in complex environments, providing a practical solution for multisensor point cloud generation and intelligent perception systems.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a multimodal point cloud generation method using vision-radar fusion to address the sparsity, noise, and structural incompleteness of millimeter-wave radar point clouds. It uses image semantic information for structural constraints and spatial alignment, along with a sparse completion strategy to enhance point density. Experiments show improved point cloud quality and downstream detection/tracking performance.
AI.motivation: Millimeter-wave radar point clouds are inherently sparse, noisy, and structurally incomplete, which degrades the performance of downstream perception tasks such as object detection and tracking.
AI.method: A multimodal point cloud generation method based on vision-radar fusion, using image semantic information for structural constraints and spatial alignment, combined with a sparse completion strategy to increase point density and recover missing structures.
AI.result: The proposed method effectively improves point cloud quality, enhances detection accuracy, and increases robustness of perception models in complex environments.
AI.conclusion: The vision-radar fusion approach provides a practical solution for multisensor point cloud generation and intelligent perception systems.

title: Kalman Prototypical Networks for Few-shot Fault Detection in Combined Cycle Gas Turbines
summary: Combined-cycle gas turbines (CCGTs) play a key role in modern power generation, offering both high efficiency and reduced environmental impact. However, their complex thermo-fluid and mechanical interactions complicate fault detection, particularly when labeled fault data are scarce. In this paper, we introduce the Kalman Prototypical Network (KPN), a metric-based few-shot learning (FSL) framework specifically tailored for CCGT fault diagnosis. We model the evolution of class prototypes as latent stochastic states in a dynamic system to reduce episodic variance and improve robustness in embedding representation. Synthetic data sets generated with a high-fidelity Modelica-based dynamic simulation of an offshore CCGT system were used, simulating both normal operation and progressive leak faults under transient conditions. Application of the proposed framework on simulated leak fault detection tasks demonstrate that KPN outperforms conventional FSL methods such as Matching Networks, Relation Networks, and MAML in both accuracy and stability under varying support and query configurations. The proposed framework significantly improves training convergence and generalization by stabilizing class representations, making it well-suited for real-world CCGT fault detection where labeled data is limited.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Kalman Prototypical Network (KPN), a few-shot learning framework for fault diagnosis in combined-cycle gas turbines that models class prototypes as latent stochastic states to reduce variance and improve robustness. It outperforms conventional FSL methods like Matching Networks, Relation Networks, and MAML on simulated leak fault detection tasks under transient conditions.
AI.motivation: CCGTs have complex thermo-fluid and mechanical interactions that complicate fault detection, and labeled fault data are scarce, making conventional diagnosis methods impractical.
AI.method: The Kalman Prototypical Network (KPN) models class prototype evolution as latent stochastic states in a dynamic system, using a metric-based few-shot learning framework to stabilize embedding representations and reduce episodic variance.
AI.result: KPN outperforms Matching Networks, Relation Networks, and MAML in both accuracy and stability under varying support and query configurations on simulated CCGT leak fault detection tasks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework significantly improves training convergence and generalization by stabilizing class representations, making it well-suited for real-world CCGT fault detection where labeled data is limited.

title: Theory-Scale Auto-Formalization of Logics for Computer Science
summary: Auto-formalization is critical for scalable formal verification, but existing progress largely focuses on isolated statements, while theory-scale auto-formalization, which coherently translates hundreds of interdependent definitions, lemmas, and theorems, remains open due to challenges in consistency, faithfulness, scalability, and correctness. In this paper, we introduce LCS-Bench, a stand-alone, theory-scale benchmark based on Logics for Computer Science. LCS-Bench is built through a novel semi-automated agentic pipeline that leverages concept graphs, formal signature planning, issue tracking, sorry-filling with counter-example search, complemented by faithfulness review from human experts. The resulting artifact covers 327 textbook items, over 4,076 Lean declarations, and more than 85K lines of Lean code. The dataset supports broad evaluation through a data engine that automatically derives five tracks of evaluation benchmarks, measuring different aspects of auto-formalization and theorem-proving capabilities. We also introduce a novel evaluation protocol featuring definitional equivalence checkers, enabling more fine-grained and faithful assessment. Through extensive evaluation on 14 models, we demonstrate that (1) LCS-Bench is of high quality, consistent, and faithful; (2) the benchmark is challenging, with state-of-the-art models achieving only 20.1% on auto-formalization tasks; and (3) our analysis reveals key findings regarding theory-scale auto-formalization and suggests promising directions for future work.

AI.tldr: LCS-Bench is a theory-scale auto-formalization benchmark covering 327 textbook items, 4,076 Lean declarations, and 85K+ lines of code, built via a semi-automated agentic pipeline. Evaluations on 14 models show high quality and challenge (SOTA only 20.1%).
AI.motivation: Existing auto-formalization research focuses on isolated statements; theory-scale formalization (coherently translating hundreds of interdependent definitions, lemmas, and theorems) remains unsolved due to challenges in consistency, faithfulness, scalability, and correctness.
AI.method: Introduces LCS-Bench built via a semi-automated agentic pipeline using concept graphs, formal signature planning, issue tracking, sorry-filling with counter-example search, and human expert faithfulness review. Includes a data engine for five evaluation tracks and a novel protocol with definitional equivalence checkers.
AI.result: Covers 327 textbook items, 4,076 Lean declarations, and over 85K lines of Lean code. Evaluated on 14 models; SOTA achieves only 20.1% on auto-formalization tasks. Demonstrates high quality, consistency, and faithfulness of the benchmark.
AI.conclusion: LCS-Bench is a challenging, high-quality benchmark for theory-scale auto-formalization. Key findings suggest promising future directions for theory-scale formalization research.

title: Capacity-Controlled Multi-View Stylization of 3D Gaussian Splatting
comment: Accepted to ECCV 2026. Project page: https://vcc2310.github.io/SceneStyler/
summary: While 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) provides an efficient and explicit representation for novel view synthesis, enforcing stylistic coherence across viewpoints remains challenging. Existing 3D stylization methods typically apply 2D feature-matching losses independently per rendered view, which leads to unstable style allocation, many-to-one feature reuse, and limited cross-view consistency. We propose a capacity-controlled framework for multi-view stylization of 3DGS, grounded in optimal transport. Specifically, we reformulate local style matching as a semi-balanced optimal transport problem. By introducing explicit column-capacity constraints with tunable strength, our formulation mitigates many-to-one matching and enables controllable allocation of style features. This transport-based objective provides a principled mechanism for balancing feature coverage and stylistic diversity while maintaining stable correspondences across viewpoints. To further enhance cross-view coherence, we incorporate a novel cross-view matching guidance to constrain correspondences between scene content and style patterns. In addition, we introduce several geometric regularizations to enhance the vanilla 3DGS, thereby enabling optimized Gaussian primitives to represent finer-grained textures during stylization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach significantly improves multi-view stylistic consistency and produces stable, expressive 3D stylizations while preserving the core semantic structure of the scene.

AI.tldr: A capacity-controlled optimal transport framework for multi-view 3DGS stylization that enforces cross-view consistency via semi-balanced transport with column-capacity constraints, cross-view matching guidance, and geometric regularizations.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D stylization methods apply 2D feature-matching losses independently per view, causing unstable style allocation, many-to-one feature reuse, and limited cross-view consistency.
AI.method: (1) Reformulates local style matching as a semi-balanced optimal transport problem with explicit column-capacity constraints for controllable style feature allocation. (2) Introduces cross-view matching guidance to constrain scene-content-to-style-pattern correspondences across viewpoints. (3) Adds geometric regularizations to the vanilla 3DGS for finer-grained texture representation.
AI.result: Significantly improves multi-view stylistic consistency and produces stable, expressive 3D stylizations while preserving core semantic structure.
AI.conclusion: The capacity-controlled optimal transport framework effectively balances feature coverage and stylistic diversity with stable cross-view correspondences for 3DGS stylization.

title: LithoDreamer: A Physics-Informed World Model for Multi-Stage Computational Lithography
comment: Correspondence to: Qi Sun <qisunchn \at zju \dot edu \dot cn>
summary: As semiconductor technology nodes scale, computational lithography is essential for ensuring yield and performance. However, lithography is a continuous physical process involving mask optimization, optical imaging, resist exposure, and development, which existing models fail to capture. To overcome this limitation, we present LithoDreamer, the first physics-informed World Model (WM) framework for computational lithography, which formulates the ``Layout-Mask-Resist Image-After Development Image (ADI)'' pipeline as a decision-driven multi-step evolution system. LithoDreamer captures feature changes between adjacent states to model stage-specific physics-informed latent spaces, in which it controls process intervention exploration and drives subsequent state transitions. To achieve interpretable intervention optimization without continuous supervision, we propose a contrastive variational optimization paradigm that contrasts the latent differences between intervention paths with variational evolution constraints, guiding the model to generate evolutions consistent with real lithography physics. Experiments show LithoDreamer achieves state-of-the-art performance in forward evolution and inverse planning. Our lithography dataset is publicly available at GitHub (https://github.com/7jiangyq/lithodreamer.git).

AI.tldr: LithoDreamer is the first physics-informed World Model for computational lithography, modeling the Layout-Mask-Resist-ADI pipeline as a multi-step evolution system, achieving state-of-the-art results in forward evolution and inverse planning via contrastive variational optimization.
AI.motivation: Existing computational lithography models fail to capture the continuous physical process (mask optimization, optical imaging, resist exposure, development). LithoDreamer addresses this by formulating lithography as a decision-driven multi-step evolution system.
AI.method: LithoDreamer uses a physics-informed World Model that captures feature changes between adjacent states in the Layout-Mask-Resist-ADI pipeline. It employs a contrastive variational optimization paradigm to contrast latent differences between intervention paths with variational evolution constraints, enabling interpretable intervention optimization without continuous supervision.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance in both forward evolution and inverse planning tasks. The dataset is publicly available on GitHub.
AI.conclusion: LithoDreamer effectively models the continuous lithography physics as a multi-step evolution system, outperforming existing methods and providing interpretable optimization.

title: Sample-efficient Transfer Reinforcement Learning via Adaptive Reward Shaping and Policy-Ratio Reweighting Strategy
summary: Transfer learning improves policy learning efficiency by reusing knowledge from source tasks, providing a feasible paradigm for safe and efficient autonomous highway lane changing decision-making. Existing methods frequently encounter transfer mismatch induced by distribution shifts between source and target domains, leading to training oscillation and performance decline. Besides, target domain adaptation depends on exploratory interactions, which struggles to guarantee training safety in safety-critical lane changing cases. To tackle these limitations, this paper proposes a safe transfer reinforcement learning framework for autonomous highway lane changing. First, we design an adaptive teacher intervention mechanism based on instantaneous safety cost to restrain risky exploration and fade intervention strength progressively, with theoretical analysis on return bounds for mixed behavior policy. This intervention also produces dual-source samples for joint training. Second, a teacher-guided safe transfer module embeds action evaluation information of teacher policy into student learning via reward shaping to boost training safety and efficiency, with teacher guidance decaying as policy safety rises. Third, a teacher-guided weighted optimization mechanism adjusts sample weights in policy optimization using a likelihood ratio factor to stabilize transfer performance. Experiments under varied traffic densities and validations on real-world NGSIM dataset reveal that our method surpasses baseline approaches by over 52.2% in safety and 5.0% in learning efficiency. Results verify the efficacy and robustness of our safety-aware transfer strategy for autonomous highway lane changing under various traffic conditions.

AI.tldr: Proposes a safe transfer reinforcement learning framework for autonomous highway lane changing, featuring adaptive teacher intervention, reward shaping, and weighted optimization to address distribution shift and training safety issues. Achieves 52.2% safety improvement and 5.0% efficiency gain over baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing transfer learning methods for lane changing suffer from transfer mismatch due to distribution shifts between source and target domains, causing training oscillation and performance decline. Additionally, target domain adaptation relies on exploratory interactions that compromise training safety in safety-critical scenarios.
AI.method: Three key components: (1) Adaptive teacher intervention mechanism using instantaneous safety cost to restrain risky exploration with gradual fade and return-bound analysis; (2) Teacher-guided safe transfer module embedding action evaluation via reward shaping to boost safety/efficiency with decaying guidance; (3) Teacher-guided weighted optimization using likelihood ratio factor to adjust sample weights for stable transfer.
AI.result: Outperforms baseline approaches by over 52.2% in safety and 5.0% in learning efficiency under varied traffic densities and real-world NGSIM dataset validation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed safety-aware transfer strategy demonstrates efficacy and robustness for autonomous highway lane changing across diverse traffic conditions.

title: ProtoKV: Streaming Video Understanding under Delayed Query with Summary-State Memory
summary: Streaming video understanding (SVU) must answer queries that arrive asynchronously while visual tokens stream continuously under strict GPU-memory and query-time latency budgets. A key challenge is delayed query: decisive cues may appear briefly, yet many subsequent updates occur before the query arrives, increasing the risk that those cues are evicted or diluted under bounded memory. We propose ProtoKV, a constant-footprint SVU memory that represents far history as a fixed-capacity summary state rather than retaining token instances. ProtoKV keeps an exact near-window KV cache and aggregates older content into a semantic-spatial prototype bank with residual statistics. At query time, each prototype is exposed through a bounded pseudo-token interface that is drop-in compatible with standard attention. Under matched budgets and comparable query-time cost, ProtoKV improves accuracy by up to 12.5 points over token-retention baselines on SVU benchmarks in the long-delay regime, with gains that grow as query delay increases.

AI.tldr: ProtoKV is a constant-footprint streaming video understanding memory that aggregates far history into semantic-spatial prototypes rather than retaining token instances, improving accuracy by up to 12.5 points under delayed-query conditions.
AI.motivation: Streaming video understanding faces the delayed query problem where decisive cues appear briefly but risk eviction or dilution under bounded GPU-memory budgets before the query arrives.
AI.method: ProtoKV maintains an exact near-window KV cache and aggregates older content into a fixed-capacity semantic-spatial prototype bank with residual statistics, exposing prototypes through a bounded pseudo-token interface compatible with standard attention.
AI.result: Under matched budgets and comparable query-time cost, ProtoKV improves accuracy by up to 12.5 points over token-retention baselines on SVU benchmarks in the long-delay regime, with gains growing as query delay increases.
AI.conclusion: ProtoKV effectively addresses the delayed query challenge in streaming video understanding by using prototype-based summary states that preserve critical cues under strict memory constraints, outperforming token-retention approaches particularly under long delays.

title: A Latent ODE Approach to Spatiotemporal Modeling of Cine Cardiac MRI
summary: Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (CMR) captures rich spatiotemporal information about ventricular structure and motion, but conventional risk models use only a few image-derived indices from selected cardiac phases. We present a latent dynamical model that encodes bi-ventricular anatomy and full-cycle cine motion as a continuous latent trajectory, using heart-rate-aware neural ordinary differential equation (ODE) dynamics and a graph-based mesh autoencoder to reconstruct anatomically consistent 3D+t ventricular motion. A covariate-conditioned prior defines the expected end-diastolic latent state, and a Cox proportional hazards model tests whether deviations from this prior predict incident heart failure. We studied 72,386 UK Biobank participants without baseline cardiovascular disease, including 367 incident heart failure events. In a held-out evaluation subset, adding the latent score to refitted pooled cohort equations improved the stratified C-index from 0.704 to 0.785, compared with 0.764 for seven established cardiac markers. Compared with non-graph and non-ODE approaches, the proposed model gave the best trade-off between reconstruction fidelity, generative realism, and downstream prognostic performance. These results suggest that continuous full-cycle modeling of ventricular motion provides informative cardiac phenotypes beyond conventional CMR summaries, while external validation in more representative patient cohorts is required before clinical risk-prediction use.

AI.tldr: A latent dynamical model using heart-rate-aware neural ODE and graph-based mesh autoencoder models continuous bi-ventricular motion from CMR, and its latent deviation score improves heart failure risk prediction (C-index 0.785) over conventional markers (0.764) in 72,386 UK Biobank participants.
AI.motivation: Conventional CMR risk models use only a few image-derived indices from selected cardiac phases, discarding the rich spatiotemporal information about ventricular structure and motion available in the full cine sequence.
AI.method: A latent dynamical model with three components: (1) a graph-based mesh autoencoder reconstructing anatomically consistent 3D+t ventricular motion, (2) heart-rate-aware neural ODE dynamics encoding continuous latent trajectories, and (3) a covariate-conditioned prior defining expected end-diastolic latent state, with a Cox proportional hazards model testing deviations from this prior for incident heart failure prediction.
AI.result: In 72,386 UK Biobank participants (367 HF events), the latent score improved stratified C-index from 0.704 to 0.785 when added to refitted pooled cohort equations, outperforming seven established cardiac markers (0.764). The proposed model achieved the best trade-off between reconstruction fidelity, generative realism, and prognostic performance versus non-graph and non-ODE approaches.
AI.conclusion: Continuous full-cycle modeling of ventricular motion provides informative cardiac phenotypes beyond conventional CMR summaries, though external validation in more representative cohorts is needed before clinical use.

title: CascadeFormer: Depth-Tapered Transformers Motivated by Gradient Fan-in Asymmetry
summary: Deep Transformers are composed of uniformly stacked residual blocks, yet their deepest layers often add little value. We present two efficiency methods that exploit this asymmetry. CascadeFormer tapers width with depth to match the uneven information flow across layers, achieving comparable perplexity to a uniform baseline at the same training budget while reducing latency by 8.6% and increasing throughput by 9.4%. CascadeFlow Pruning removes layers using accumulated training gradients, with no post hoc analysis. It outperforms standard heuristics on perplexity and rank-stability and stays competitive on downstream accuracy. To motivate these methods, we propose Gradient Fan-in Asymmetry (GFA) as a structural account of why deeper layers contribute less. In Pre-LayerNorm residual stacks, the gradient at a layer is the sum of an identity path and all downstream functional paths, producing a gradient fan-in that decays linearly with depth (and quadratically under deep supervision), yielding richer gradients for early layers and sparser ones for later layers. We provide correlational and interventional evidence for GFA on models trained from scratch up to 1.2B parameters. Across Transformers and ResNets, accumulated training gradients follow the theoretical fan-in and are associated with post hoc layer importance. Two interventions point to structure rather than magnitude as the bottleneck: equalizing per-layer gradient norms does not restore late-layer value, while increasing downstream path counts via parameter-shared repetition restores and elevates it. Whether gradient magnitude proxies fan-in beyond high-rank regimes, and how these dynamics behave at the 100B+ scale, remain open questions.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies Gradient Fan-in Asymmetry (GFA) in deep residual networks, where deeper layers receive sparser gradients due to architectural structure. Based on this, the authors propose two efficiency methods: CascadeFormer (tapering width with depth) and CascadeFlow Pruning (gradient-based layer pruning), achieving improved efficiency without sacrificing performance.
AI.motivation: Deep Transformers have uniform residual blocks, but deepest layers often contribute little, wasting computation. The authors aim to exploit this asymmetry for efficiency gains.
AI.method: CascadeFormer tapers layer width with depth to match uneven information flow. CascadeFlow Pruning removes layers using accumulated training gradients. GFA is proposed as a theoretical account: in Pre-LayerNorm residual stacks, gradient fan-in decays linearly with depth (quadratically under deep supervision).
AI.result: CascadeFormer achieves comparable perplexity with 8.6% lower latency and 9.4% higher throughput. CascadeFlow Pruning outperforms standard heuristics on perplexity and rank-stability. GFA is validated on models up to 1.2B parameters across Transformers and ResNets.
AI.conclusion: Structural gradient fan-in asymmetry, not gradient magnitude, is the bottleneck for late-layer contribution. Equalizing gradient norms fails to restore value, while increasing downstream path counts via parameter-shared repetition does. Open questions remain about gradient magnitude beyond high-rank regimes and at 100B+ scale.

title: Calibrated Harmonic Overlaid Implicit Neural Representations for Multi-Dimensional Data
summary: Implicit neural representation (INR) has emerged as a powerful prior for multi-dimensional data (e.g., multispectral images and videos). However, most INR methods employing periodic activation functions (e.g., Sine) predominantly rely on function composition. This mechanism introduces optimization instability as network depth increases, thereby limiting their performance. Meanwhile, these methods fail to incorporate proper physical priors to effectively alleviate spectrum bias. To address these issues, inspired by the commonalities between deep periodic networks and generalized Fourier series, we propose a novel Calibrated Harmonic Overlaid Implicit Neural Representation (CHOIR). Specifically, we utilize Coordinated Harmonic Superposition (CHS) to replace the conventional function composition used in most INRs, thereby ensuring optimization stability when scaling network depth. Furthermore, we introduce a Perceptual Spectrum Calibration (PSC) to mitigate spectrum bias. This calibration embeds the ubiquitous power-law spectrum prior of natural images and adjusts the globally fixed spectrum towards a physically plausible log-uniform distribution. Extensive experiments on various multidimensional data recovery problems demonstrate that our method achieves superior performance over state-of-the-art approaches. Code is available at https://github.com/chorl0229/CHOIR.

AI.tldr: CHOIR introduces Coordinated Harmonic Superposition (CHS) to replace function composition in deep periodic networks for stable optimization, and Perceptual Spectrum Calibration (PSC) leveraging power-law spectrum prior to mitigate spectrum bias, achieving SOTA on multidimensional data recovery.
AI.motivation: Existing INR methods using periodic activation functions rely on function composition, causing optimization instability as network depth increases. They also lack proper physical priors to alleviate spectrum bias.
AI.method: (1) Coordinated Harmonic Superposition (CHS) replaces conventional function composition in deep periodic networks for stable optimization when scaling depth. (2) Perceptual Spectrum Calibration (PSC) embeds power-law spectrum prior of natural images and adjusts globally fixed spectrum towards a physically plausible log-uniform distribution.
AI.result: Superior performance over state-of-the-art approaches on various multidimensional data recovery problems.
AI.conclusion: The proposed CHS and PSC effectively address optimization instability and spectrum bias in periodic INRs, achieving SOTA results.

title: Socratic agents for autonomous scientific discovery in high-dimensional physical systems
summary: The automation of scientific discovery has reached an inflection point. While AI systems now operate instruments, optimize parameters and generate hypotheses, most remain procedural: they execute workflows fixed by human designers. True autonomous science demands epistemic autonomy--the capacity to construct, challenge and revise physical explanations in response to evidence. Here we introduce AHOIS, a multi-agent AI scientist that embeds Socratic midwifery into closed-loop experimentation. A physics-critic agent interrogates hypotheses through causal questioning, constraint checking, counterexample generation and falsification-criteria formulation. We evaluate AHOIS on a real multimode-fibre optical platform, a high-dimensional system with complex wave transformations, indirect detection, environmental drift and multi-modal acquisition. Without prior encoding schemes, classifiers or speckle models, the system autonomously proposed and validated a random-interference encoding hypothesis, discovered task-adaptive sparse-measurement strategies, diagnosed distinct failure modes (encoding instability, fluorescence contamination and detector noise) and translated a published imaging protocol into an executable workflow on a non-original configuration. The discovered encoding yielded 16x16 measurements with effective rank 56.9 and classification accuracies of 76.97% on MNIST and 83.17% on Fashion-MNIST. Ablations show that Socratic interrogation improves physical consistency, hypothesis completeness, uncertainty calibration and experimental-plan validity. These results establish a route from workflow automation towards evidence-grounded, self-correcting autonomous discovery in complex physical environments.

AI.tldr: AHOIS is a multi-agent AI scientist with Socratic midwifery that autonomously discovers, tests, and validates physical hypotheses on a real multimode-fibre optical platform, achieving 76.97% MNIST and 83.17% Fashion-MNIST accuracy with 16x16 measurements (effective rank 56.9), outperforming ablations without Socratic interrogation.
AI.motivation: Existing AI systems for scientific discovery remain procedural, executing fixed workflows designed by humans, lacking epistemic autonomy—the ability to construct, challenge, and revise physical explanations based on evidence.
AI.method: AHOIS uses a multi-agent architecture where a physics-critic agent performs Socratic midwifery—interrogating hypotheses through causal questioning, constraint checking, counterexample generation, and falsification-criteria formulation—integrated with closed-loop experimentation on a real multimode-fibre optical platform.
AI.result: The system autonomously proposed a random-interference encoding hypothesis, discovered task-adaptive sparse-measurement strategies, diagnosed three failure modes (encoding instability, fluorescence contamination, detector noise), and translated a published protocol to a non-original configuration. Achieved 76.97% (MNIST) and 83.17% (Fashion-MNIST) with 16x16 measurements (effective rank 56.9). Socratic ablation improved physical consistency, hypothesis completeness, uncertainty calibration, and experimental-plan validity.
AI.conclusion: Socratic interrogation provides a viable route from workflow automation toward evidence-grounded, self-correcting autonomous discovery in complex physical environments, establishing epistemic autonomy as a tangible capability for AI-driven science.

title: Can Large Language Models Reliably Code Qualitative Humanitarian Data? A Benchmark Study Against Human Expert Adjudication
summary: Data from affected populations are crucial for informing humanitarian response, but their value depends on timely and consistent interpretation of nuanced accounts of need. Humanitarian organizations often lack the staff, time, and specialist expertise required to analyze this information at scale. Large language models (LLMs) may expand this capacity, but their reliability for coding qualitative humanitarian data has not been directly established. This benchmark study compares 46 LLMs to a human Gold Standard using 150 high-fidelity synthetic humanitarian transcripts. Evaluation combined inter-rater reliability testing with Krippendorff's alpha, discrepancy analysis distinguishing correct, near-correct, and incorrect codes, and qualitative assessment across humanitarian-specific criteria including discrimination, complex needs hierarchies, and non-standard communication styles. The authors find that multiple LLMs can perform deductive coding at reliability levels comparable to experienced human coders, especially when structured prompts and reasoning-enabled configurations are used. At the same time, aggregate reliability metrics alone are insufficient for deployment decisions. Models varied in recognizing needs expressed indirectly, needs outside predefined categories, and protection-relevant concerns such as physical safety and discrimination. These findings suggest that LLMs can materially expand humanitarian analytical capacity, but not as substitutes for human judgment. Appropriate use requires structured codebooks, reasoning-enabled models, attention to theme-specific performance, and tiered oversight focused on categories where miscoding would have the greatest programmatic consequences. For sensitive humanitarian data, open-weights models deployed on self-hosted infrastructure may offer a viable path for combining analytical scalability with stronger data governance.

AI.tldr: This benchmark study evaluates 46 LLMs against a human Gold Standard on deductive coding of 150 synthetic humanitarian transcripts, finding that multiple LLMs achieve human-comparable reliability with structured prompts and reasoning configurations, but aggregate metrics alone are insufficient—models vary on indirect needs, out-of-category needs, and protection concerns—concluding LLMs can expand humanitarian analysis capacity but require structured codebooks, reasoning-enabled models, theme-specific oversight, and tiered human review, with open-weights models on self-hosted infrastructure for sensitive data.
AI.motivation: Humanitarian organizations lack staff, time, and specialist expertise to analyze qualitative data from affected populations at scale; LLMs may expand capacity but their reliability for coding humanitarian data had not been directly established.
AI.method: Compared 46 LLMs to a human Gold Standard using 150 high-fidelity synthetic humanitarian transcripts, with evaluation combining Krippendorff's alpha inter-rater reliability, discrepancy analysis (correct/near-correct/incorrect codes), and qualitative assessment across humanitarian-specific criteria (discrimination, complex needs hierarchies, non-standard communication styles).
AI.result: Multiple LLMs perform deductive coding at reliability levels comparable to experienced human coders, especially with structured prompts and reasoning-enabled configurations; however, aggregate reliability metrics alone are insufficient—models varied in recognizing indirect needs, out-of-category needs, and protection concerns (physical safety, discrimination).
AI.conclusion: LLMs can materially expand humanitarian analytical capacity but not as substitutes for human judgment; appropriate use requires structured codebooks, reasoning-enabled models, theme-specific performance attention, and tiered oversight on high-consequence categories; open-weights models on self-hosted infrastructure offer a viable path for scalability with stronger data governance.

title: Anatomy-Guided Residual Motion Diffusion for Controllable 4D Cardiac MRI Synthesis
summary: Developing robust artificial intelligence models for 4D (3D + time) medical imaging is constrained by limited annotated data, inter-device domain shifts, and privacy restrictions. To address this, we propose a 4D controllable generative framework for anatomically consistent data augmentation. A semi-supervised variational autoencoder learns a compact latent representation of anatomical volumes while jointly predicting aligned segmentation masks in a unified framework. Anatomical structure is then disentangled from temporal dynamics through a cascaded latent diffusion model (LDM). A static LDM generates subject-specific anatomy conditioned on clinical priors (diagnosis and volumes measures) and a subsequent motion LDM estimates residual latent motions, ensuring strict temporal coherence across the 4D sequence. The proposed approach was evaluated on cine cardiac MRI as a representative 4D imaging application. Experiments across multiple datasets demonstrate high controllability of static anatomy (Pearson r > 0.8) and strong temporal coherence (FVD = 288.08). In cross-vendor generalization experiments, augmenting training sets with synthetic 4D sequences significantly improves downstream segmentation performance. Using nnU-Net, the proposed augmentation strategy improves the average Dice score by 1.4% and reduces the Hausdorff Distance by 3.0mm compared to training on real data alone, for the left ventricle, Dice improves by 2.8% with a 5.4mm reduction in boundary error. Overall, this framework provides a scalable and controllable solution for 4D medical image synthesis, supporting the development of more robust models with limited annotations and cross-vendor variability. Code available on https://github.com/cyiheng/4DCardiacMRISynthesis.
