AI.tldr: A 4D controllable generative framework (semi-supervised VAE + cascaded latent diffusion model) for anatomically consistent cardiac MRI augmentation, achieving high controllability (Pearson r > 0.8), temporal coherence (FVD=288.08), and improving downstream segmentation (Dice +1.4%, HD -3.0mm) in cross-vendor settings.
AI.motivation: Limited annotated data, inter-device domain shifts, and privacy restrictions constrain robust 4D medical imaging AI models.
AI.method: A semi-supervised VAE learns a compact latent representation with joint segmentation alignment; a cascaded latent diffusion model disentangles static anatomy (conditioned on clinical priors) from temporal dynamics (motion LDM), ensuring temporal coherence across the 4D sequence.
AI.result: Pearson r > 0.8 for static anatomy controllability; FVD = 288.08 for temporal coherence; nnU-Net segmentation improves average Dice by 1.4% and reduces HD by 3.0mm (left ventricle: +2.8% Dice, -5.4mm boundary error) in cross-vendor generalization.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a scalable, controllable solution for 4D medical image synthesis, enabling robust model development under limited annotations and cross-vendor variability.

title: Scientific discovery as meta-optimization: a combinatorial optimization case study
summary: Scientific discovery is fundamentally an optimization problem, defined by a vast "state space" of theories and experiments, and an evaluation criterion based on quality, novelty, and validity. Large language models (LLMs) have enabled automated exploration of this space, but we argue that simultaneous modification of the evaluation criteria is equally important. Here, we propose formalizing research as meta-optimization, where the optimization objective itself is also being optimized. Our key contribution is "consensus objective aggregation," where LLM-generated objective functions are combined via correlation-weighted voting, yielding a stable, self-correcting evaluation criterion that evolves as understanding deepens. We apply this framework to algorithm discovery for 3-SAT problems based on digital MemComputing machines, reducing the baseline scaling with problem size $N$ from $\sim N^{2.51}$ to $\sim N^{1.33}$ and delivering a $\sim 67\times$ speedup on the largest instances tested. As a problem-agnostic framework, we hope this approach will considerably aid scientific discovery.

AI.tldr: The paper formalizes scientific discovery as a meta-optimization problem where both the solution space and the evaluation criteria are optimized simultaneously. The authors propose "consensus objective aggregation," combining LLM-generated objectives via correlation-weighted voting to create a self-correcting evaluation criterion. Applied to 3-SAT algorithm discovery using digital MemComputing, the framework reduces scaling from ~N^2.51 to ~N^1.33, achieving ~67× speedup on the largest instances.
AI.motivation: Scientific discovery is inherently an optimization problem over a vast state space of theories and experiments, but existing LLM-based approaches only explore this space automatically while neglecting to simultaneously optimize the evaluation criteria themselves, which is equally important for effective discovery.
AI.method: The paper proposes "meta-optimization," where the optimization objective is itself optimized. Specifically, "consensus objective aggregation" uses LLMs to generate candidate objective functions, which are then combined via correlation-weighted voting to produce a stable, self-correcting evaluation criterion that evolves as understanding deepens.
AI.result: Applied to algorithm discovery for 3-SAT problems based on digital MemComputing machines, the framework reduced the baseline scaling with problem size N from ~N^2.51 to ~N^1.33, achieving a ~67× speedup on the largest tested instances.
AI.conclusion: The proposed meta-optimization framework with consensus objective aggregation is problem-agnostic and expected to broadly aid scientific discovery by enabling automated, self-correcting refinement of both solutions and evaluation criteria.

title: Revisiting Action Factorization for Complex Action Spaces
comment: 53 Pages, 37 Figures, 6 Tables, Target Journal/Venue: ACM Transactions on Autonomous and Adaptive Systems TAAS
summary: Many real-world control problems involve hybrid discrete-continuous action spaces. For example, steering and signaling in autonomous driving, and aiming and firing in robotics or video-games. Despite real-world hybrid factorization and reinforcement learning framework support for complex action spaces (e.g., Gymnasium, PettingZoo, TorchRL, SeedRL, Mujoco, etc), the default environments within those frameworks often implement uniform action space configurations (LunarLander, Walker2D, Cheetah, SMAC, SUMO, Ant, Atari). Landmark hybrid-action benchmarks (RoboCup 2D HFO, SC2LE, Platform, CARLA, etc) are mostly heavyweight or archival implementations originating from papers which test one or a small number of competing factorization methods on one kind of control. This article provides a cross-sectional study of factorization methods [independent networks, shared encoder, VDN, QPLEX, Joint, Auto-Regressive] on each of three families of algorithms [PPO, SAC, DQN] across three action spaces [discretized, hybrid, continuous] over four lightweight environments [Platform, hybrid-LunarLander, Hybrid-Shoot, CoopPush]. Accounting for some invalid pairings such as joint-continuous, we are left with 220 configurations to analyze each method. We provide two new C++ parallel gymnasium and petting-zoo compliant environments [CoopPush, Hybrid-Shoot] to isolate particular challenges such as state-dependent inter-action dependence. Finally, we introduce VDN-PPO and PPO-MIX which use a branching critic to assign credit to multi-headed PPO. These variants out-perform all other tested PPO factorizations. Our results suggest that branching dueling architectures balance compute and performance most effectively, with Auto-Regressive actions reaching the highest performance overall and native continuous SAC outperforming discrete and hybrid algorithms, albiet both at increased computational cost.

AI.tldr: This paper provides a cross-sectional study of 6 factorization methods across 3 algorithm families (PPO, SAC, DQN) and 3 action spaces using 4 lightweight environments (including 2 new C++ ones). It introduces VDN-PPO and PPO-MIX with branching critics, finding that branching dueling architectures balance compute and performance best, with Auto-Regressive actions achieving highest performance and continuous SAC outperforming discrete/hybrid methods.
AI.motivation: Real-world control problems often involve hybrid discrete-continuous action spaces, but existing frameworks and benchmarks predominantly implement uniform action space configurations. Landmark hybrid-action benchmarks are heavyweight or archival, testing only one or a few factorization methods on one kind of control. A systematic cross-sectional study was needed.
AI.method: Cross-sectional study testing 6 factorization methods (independent networks, shared encoder, VDN, QPLEX, Joint, Auto-Regressive) across 3 algorithm families (PPO, SAC, DQN) and 3 action spaces (discretized, hybrid, continuous) over 4 lightweight environments (Platform, hybrid-LunarLander, Hybrid-Shoot, CoopPush), totaling 220 configurations. Introduced VDN-PPO and PPO-MIX with branching critics. Two new C++ parallel Gymnasium/PettingZoo-compliant environments (CoopPush, Hybrid-Shoot) were developed.
AI.result: VDN-PPO and PPO-MIX outperformed all other tested PPO factorizations. Branching dueling architectures balance compute and performance most effectively. Auto-Regressive actions reach the highest overall performance. Native continuous SAC outperforms discrete and hybrid algorithms, albeit at increased computational cost.
AI.conclusion: Branching dueling architectures offer the best compute-performance trade-off. Auto-Regressive factorization achieves peak performance. Continuous SAC remains superior to discrete/hybrid alternatives when computational budget allows. The new lightweight environments enable systematic benchmarking of hybrid-action methods.

title: LearniBridge: Learnable Calibration of Feature Caching for Diffusion Models Acceleration
summary: Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) have driven substantial progress in image and video generation but suffer from prohibitive computational costs. Feature caching accelerates inference by reusing intermediate representations. Existing methods rely on historical features for implementation simplicity, yet suffer from severe error accumulation at high acceleration ratios. To address this limitation, we investigate the nature of the requisite feature correction. We demonstrate that the optimal calibration update is characterized by a shared low-rank subspace across diverse prompts. Guided by this structural insight, we propose LearniBridge, a learnable calibration mechanism for feature caching that bridges multiple timesteps through lightweight LoRA updates. This mechanism enables effective calibration requiring only 3-5 training samples. Extensive experiments on image and video generation show that LearniBridge achieves up to $5.87\times$, $5.75\times$, and $4.10\times$ acceleration on FLUX, HunyuanVideo, and WAN2.1, respectively. On WAN2.1, it improves VBench by 1.28% over the previous SOTA at $4.10\times$ acceleration. Our code is available at https://github.com/Iiiiiiirene/LearniBridge.

AI.tldr: LearniBridge is a learnable calibration mechanism for feature caching in Diffusion Transformers that uses lightweight LoRA updates to correct error accumulation at high acceleration ratios, achieving up to 5.87× speedup on FLUX, 5.75× on HunyuanVideo, and 4.10× on WAN2.1 with minimal training data (3-5 samples).
AI.motivation: Existing feature caching methods for accelerating Diffusion Transformers suffer from severe error accumulation at high acceleration ratios due to relying solely on historical features without proper calibration.
AI.method: The authors first demonstrate that optimal calibration updates lie in a shared low-rank subspace across diverse prompts. Based on this insight, they propose LearniBridge, a lightweight LoRA-based calibration mechanism that bridges multiple timesteps and requires only 3-5 training samples.
AI.result: Achieves 5.87×, 5.75×, and 4.10× acceleration on FLUX, HunyuanVideo, and WAN2.1 respectively. On WAN2.1 at 4.10× acceleration, improves VBench by 1.28% over the previous state-of-the-art.
AI.conclusion: LearniBridge effectively addresses the error accumulation problem in feature caching for DiTs through a minimal-parameter, sample-efficient learnable calibration mechanism, significantly outperforming prior methods.

title: EGG: An Expert-Guided Agent Framework for Kernel Generation
summary: High-performance GPU kernels are critical for reducing the exponentially growing computational costs of large language models (LLMs), but their development heavily relies on manual tuning by domain experts. While recent advances in LLM-based approaches show promise for automating kernel generation, they still struggle to achieve both correctness and high performance. This limitation primarily arises from the lack of domain-specific optimization guidance, hindering effective exploration of the optimization space. We propose EGG, an Expert-Guided Agent Framework for Kernel Generation, which incorporates expert optimization principles to guide LLMs' decisions. Inspired by expert workflows, we decompose kernel generation into two hierarchical stages: 1) algorithmic structure design, which establishes a high-quality computational structure foundation; 2) hardware-specific tuning, which performs targeted adjustments through parallel mapping, tensor tiling, and memory optimization. This staged decomposition defines explicit optimization objectives, structuring the design space to achieve progressive refinement. To this end, a stage-aware multi-agent collaboration mechanism is designed for inter and intra-stage context management, ensuring stable optimization trajectories. Experiments on KernelBench and real-world workloads show that EGG achieves a 2.13x average speedup over PyTorch, outperforming existing agent-based and RL-based approaches.

AI.tldr: EGG is an expert-guided agent framework that decomposes GPU kernel generation into algorithmic structure design and hardware-specific tuning stages, using a stage-aware multi-agent collaboration mechanism, achieving 2.13x average speedup over PyTorch on KernelBench and real-world workloads.
AI.motivation: High-performance GPU kernel development for LLMs is increasingly costly and relies heavily on manual tuning by domain experts. Existing LLM-based automated approaches struggle to achieve both correctness and high performance due to the lack of domain-specific optimization guidance, which hinders effective exploration of the optimization space.
AI.method: EGG decomposes kernel generation into two hierarchical stages: (1) algorithmic structure design establishing a high-quality computational foundation, and (2) hardware-specific tuning via parallel mapping, tensor tiling, and memory optimization. A stage-aware multi-agent collaboration mechanism manages inter and intra-stage context to ensure stable optimization trajectories.
AI.result: EGG achieves a 2.13x average speedup over PyTorch on KernelBench and real-world workloads, outperforming existing agent-based and RL-based approaches.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating expert optimization principles and staged decomposition into LLM-based kernel generation significantly improves both correctness and performance, establishing a structured design space for progressive refinement in automated GPU kernel optimization.

title: SharQ: Bridging Activation Sparsity and FP4 Quantization for LLM Inference
summary: Low-bit floating-point formats and semi-structured sparsity are increasingly supported by modern accelerators, yet combining them for LLM activation compression remains challenging: activations contain input-dependent outliers that dominate block scales in FP4 quantization, and directly applying N:M sparsity masks discards moderate values, coupling sparsification loss with quantization error. We introduce SharQ, a training-free inference method that bridges activation sparsity and FP4 quantization through an online sparse--dense decomposition. For each activation tensor, SharQ generates an input-adaptive N:M mask to extract an outlier-dominated sparse backbone, quantizes it to FP4, and defines a dense residual relative to the quantized sparse backbone rather than the unquantized sparse values. A sparse FP4 GEMM processes the backbone while a dense FP4 GEMM compensates for both mask-induced activation loss and sparse-path quantization error. The two paths share a single FP4 weight payload with path-specific scale views, and a fused preparation kernel absorbs mask generation, residual construction, and layer normalization into one operator. SharQ requires no calibration data, retraining, or model-specific tuning. Evaluated on Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen2.5-7B, Qwen3-30B-A3B, and Qwen3-VL-8B, SharQ recovers 43--63% of the NVFP4-to-FP16 accuracy gap across language and vision-language tasks, and generalizes across NVFP4, HiF4, and MXFP4 formats. On an RTX 5090, SharQ delivers 2.2--2.4$\times$ latency reduction over FP16 and 1.2--1.4$\times$ throughput improvement over FP8 in language model serving, and up to 1.58$\times$ speedup on Wan2.2-T2V-A14B video generation when combined with SageAttention. Our code is available at https://github.com/actypedef/SharQ.

AI.tldr: SharQ is a training-free method that combines N:M sparsity with FP4 quantization for LLM activation compression via online sparse-dense decomposition, recovering 43-63% of the NVFP4-to-FP16 accuracy gap and delivering 2.2-2.4× latency reduction over FP16 and 1.2-1.4× throughput improvement over FP8 on RTX 5090.
AI.motivation: Low-bit FP4 formats and N:M sparsity are supported by modern accelerators, but combining them for LLM activations is challenging because input-dependent outliers dominate block scales in FP4 quantization, and applying N:M masks directly discards moderate values, coupling sparsification loss with quantization error.
AI.method: SharQ performs an online sparse-dense decomposition: (1) generates an input-adaptive N:M mask to extract an outlier-dominated sparse backbone; (2) quantizes the backbone to FP4; (3) defines a dense residual relative to the quantized sparse backbone (not the unquantized sparse values). A sparse FP4 GEMM processes the backbone, a dense FP4 GEMM compensates for mask-induced loss and sparse-path quantization error. Both paths share a single FP4 weight payload with path-specific scale views. A fused preparation kernel absorbs mask generation, residual construction, and layer normalization into one operator. No calibration, retraining, or model tuning required.
AI.result: Evaluated on Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen2.5-7B, Qwen3-30B-A3B, and Qwen3-VL-8B, SharQ recovers 43-63% of the NVFP4-to-FP16 accuracy gap across language and vision-language tasks. It generalizes across NVFP4, HiF4, and MXFP4 formats. On RTX 5090, delivers 2.2-2.4× latency reduction over FP16 and 1.2-1.4× throughput improvement over FP8 in language serving, and up to 1.58× speedup on Wan2.2-T2V-A14B video generation when combined with SageAttention.
AI.conclusion: SharQ effectively bridges activation sparsity and FP4 quantization through a training-free sparse-dense decomposition, achieving significant acceleration with minimal accuracy degradation across multiple LLMs, tasks, and FP4 formats.

title: Event-based Gaze Control System for Accurate Real-time Spin Estimation in Professional Ball Games
summary: Spin plays a crucial role in many ball sports due to its effect on the trajectory of the ball. Vision-based estimation of the ball's spin during a game with conventional cameras is challenging due to the ball's small size, high speed, and fast rotation. To address these challenges, we propose an event-based active vision system that can track unmodified balls and measure their spin in real-time. The system consists of an event camera for its high temporal resolution and minimal motion blur, high-speed pan/tilt galvanometer mirrors to keep the ball in the field of view, and a low-latency focus-tunable telephoto lens to increase the spatial resolution on the ball and keep it in focus. To track the ball, we use a hybrid approach that combines 2D event-based detection for centering and 3D positions from a ball localization system for re-initialization. For high-accuracy spin estimation, we propose an offline method that performs contrast maximization on the sphere (s-CMax). This method achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on static balls across multiple sports (table tennis, baseball, tennis, and golf), with mean magnitude and axis errors of 2.1% and 4.0 degrees, respectively. We then develop a low-latency online method for table tennis as a case study in real-time applications. This method uses an uncertainty-aware convolutional neural network trained on pseudo-ground-truth spin labels from the offline approach, combined with a GPU-accelerated batch implementation of contrast maximization for refinement. We demonstrate reliable tracking and spin estimation with a three-view setup during professional table tennis matches, with high accuracy (8.8% magnitude and 6.4 degrees axis mismatch), 3 ms latency, and 750 Hz throughput.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an event-based active vision system using an event camera, galvanometer mirrors, and a tunable telephoto lens to track spinning balls in real-time. It introduces s-CMax (contrast maximization on the sphere) for offline spin estimation with state-of-the-art accuracy across multiple sports, and an online uncertainty-aware CNN + GPU-accelerated CMax method for real-time table tennis spin estimation, achieving 8.8% magnitude error, 6.4° axis error, 3 ms latency, and 750 Hz throughput.
AI.motivation: Spin significantly affects ball trajectory in sports, but vision-based spin estimation is difficult due to the ball's small size, high speed, and fast rotation. Conventional cameras struggle with motion blur and low temporal resolution.
AI.method: An event-based active vision system combining: (1) event camera for high temporal resolution and low motion blur; (2) high-speed pan/tilt galvanometer mirrors for tracking; (3) low-latency focus-tunable telephoto lens. Hybrid tracking: 2D event-based detection + 3D ball localization for re-initialization. Offline spin estimation via s-CMax (contrast maximization on the sphere). Online method: uncertainty-aware CNN trained on pseudo-ground-truth + GPU-accelerated batch CMax refinement.
AI.result: Offline: state-of-the-art accuracy across table tennis, baseball, tennis, golf (2.1% magnitude error, 4.0° axis error). Online (table tennis): 8.8% magnitude error, 6.4° axis error, 3 ms latency, 750 Hz throughput, validated in professional matches with a three-view setup.
AI.conclusion: Event-based active vision enables high-accuracy, real-time spin estimation for ball sports, with the proposed system achieving unprecedented performance in both offline and online settings.

title: ResilPhase: Plug-and-Play Phase Mapping and Noise-Resilient Macro-Trajectory Extrapolation for Diffusion Acceleration
summary: The adoption of powerful diffusion models is hindered by their significant inference latency. Recent ``cache-then-forecast'' schemes alleviate this issue by accelerating DiTs using derivative-based polynomials, but they suffer from severe quality degradation at high acceleration ratios. Our analysis reveals its root cause: the discrete extrapolation performed on representations that are misaligned with the continuous diffusion trajectory and are numerically unstable. Thus, accelerated DiTs suffer from accumulated spatial errors, noisy derivative amplification, and high-order instability. We therefore reformulate accelerated inference as stable macro-trajectory extrapolation in ordinary differential equation (ODE) space. Instead of predicting intermediate features, we align forecasting with the model's Global Drift (GD), i.e., the end-to-end state evolution, thereby eliminating feature inconsistency and memory overhead. However, even this smooth macro-trajectory remains vulnerable to the derivative fallacy: its higher-order temporal derivatives are intrinsically noisy. Thus, we introduce a derivative-free barycentric Lagrange extrapolator to effectively bypass derivative instability and approximation error. We further propose a bounded Phase Mapping that regularizes the extrapolation domain, suppressing oscillatory error growth. These elements collectively constitute ResilPhase, a noise-resilient acceleration framework. Experiments on FLUX.1-dev and HunyuanVideo demonstrate state-of-the-art fidelity under aggressive acceleration ratios.

AI.tldr: ResilPhase accelerates diffusion model inference by reformulating cache-then-forecast as stable macro-trajectory extrapolation in ODE space, using the model's Global Drift (end-to-end state evolution) instead of intermediate features, a derivative-free barycentric Lagrange extrapolator to bypass derivative instability, and bounded Phase Mapping to suppress oscillatory error growth. It achieves SOTA fidelity on FLUX.1-dev and HunyuanVideo under aggressive acceleration ratios.
AI.motivation: Existing cache-then-forecast schemes for accelerating DiTs suffer from severe quality degradation at high acceleration ratios due to discrete extrapolation on representations misaligned with the continuous diffusion trajectory and numerical instability, leading to accumulated spatial errors, noisy derivative amplification, and high-order instability.
AI.method: (1) Reformulate acceleration as stable macro-trajectory extrapolation in ODE space using Global Drift (GD) — end-to-end state evolution — instead of predicting intermediate features. (2) Introduce a derivative-free barycentric Lagrange extrapolator to bypass derivative instability. (3) Propose bounded Phase Mapping to regularize the extrapolation domain and suppress oscillatory error growth.
AI.result: State-of-the-art fidelity on FLUX.1-dev and HunyuanVideo under aggressive acceleration ratios.
AI.conclusion: ResilPhase, combining ODE-space macro-trajectory extrapolation via Global Drift, derivative-free barycentric Lagrange extrapolation, and bounded Phase Mapping, enables noise-resilient acceleration of diffusion models with superior quality compared to prior schemes.

title: Empirical Software Engineering TerraProbe: A Layered-Oracle Framework for Detecting Deceptive Fixes in LLM-Assisted Terraform
comment: 34 pages, 12 figures, 14 tables. Journal-first manuscript submitted to Empirical Software Engineering. Primary classification: cs.SE; cross-list: cs.CR
summary: Security misconfigurations in Terraform Infrastructure-as-Code are a growing risk in cloud deployments, and large language models are increasingly used as automated repair agents. Existing evaluations often treat a repair as successful when the targeted static-analysis finding disappears, without checking planning validity, behavioral change, or security intent. This paper presents TerraProbe, a five-layer oracle framework for evaluating LLM-assisted Terraform security repair. We apply TerraProbe to 288 first-pass repairs generated by gemini-2.5-flash-lite, GPT-4o, and Claude 3.5 Sonnet across 68 real-world TerraDS modules and 28 controlled injected-defect modules. The results show that targeted Checkov removal overstates repair success. Although targeted removal reaches 83.3 percent for the primary model, full-scanner cleanliness drops to 10.4 percent, Terraform planning succeeds for 39.6 percent, and plan comparison is reachable for 38.5 percent. Human adjudication further shows that 71.4 percent of plan-compared real-world repairs are deceptive fixes that pass automated checks while leaving the underlying vulnerability in place. This pattern is statistically indistinguishable across the three models, with deceptive-fix rates from 57.1 percent to 71.4 percent and pairwise Fisher exact p-values above 0.10. The paper introduces a four-dimensional taxonomy of deceptive fixes, validated with Cohen kappa of 0.78 and Krippendorff alpha of 0.76. IAM permission analysis confirms that wildcard Resource grants persist in all nine CKV2 AWS 11 deceptive-fix cases. TerraProbe contributes an evaluation methodology, a replication package, and the Multi-Layer Oracle Evaluation framework for distinguishing intent-aligned security repairs from scanner-passing false successes.

AI.tldr: TerraProbe is a five-layer oracle framework for evaluating LLM-assisted Terraform security repairs. Testing 288 repairs across 68 real-world modules shows that traditional targeted scanner removal (83.3% success) vastly overstates true repair quality — full-scanner cleanliness drops to 10.4%, only 38.5% produce valid plans, and 71.4% of those are "deceptive fixes" that pass automated checks without fixing the vulnerability. A four-dimensional deceptive-fix taxonomy is introduced with strong inter-rater reliability.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of LLM-based Terraform security repair treat static-analysis finding removal as success, ignoring planning validity, behavioral change, and security intent — leading to overestimated repair quality.
AI.method: TerraProbe applies a five-layer oracle: (1) targeted Checkov removal, (2) full-scanner cleanliness, (3) Terraform planning validity, (4) plan comparison with pre-repair, and (5) human adjudication. Tested on 288 repairs from 3 LLMs across 68 real-world + 28 injected-defect modules.
AI.result: Targeted removal 83.3% → full cleanliness 10.4% → planning success 39.6% → plan reachable 38.5% → 71.4% of real-world plan-compared repairs are deceptive fixes. Deceptive-fix rates indistinguishable across models (57.1%-71.4%, p>0.10). All CKV2_AWS_11 deceptive fixes retained wildcard Resource grants.
AI.conclusion: TerraProbe exposes that scanner-passing repairs are predominantly deceptive; the paper provides a validated taxonomy and evaluation framework to distinguish intent-aligned security repairs from false successes.

title: ReasonCLIP-58M: Visually Grounded Commonsense Reasoning Supervision for CLIP
summary: CLIP and its variants are widely adopted visual backbones in multimodal systems, but their pretraining remains dominated by descriptive image-text alignment. As downstream applications increasingly demand visually grounded commonsense inference and compositional reasoning, it remains unclear whether CLIP-style encoders can support such reasoning without architectural changes. To address this, we present ReasonCLIP-58M, a continual pretraining framework that integrates large-scale reasoning supervision into CLIP-style models through our two-stage strategy, which progressively integrates reasoning signals while preserving descriptive alignment, followed by category-structured reasoning supervision. To support this framework, we construct two complementary datasets and a benchmark: ReasonLite-42M, with open-form, visually verifiable reasoning captions; ReasonPro-16M, with category-specific reasoning supervision; and RCLIP-Bench for diagnostic evaluation of visually grounded reasoning. We train a family of ReasonCLIP that improves visually grounded commonsense and compositional reasoning while also enhancing zero-shot retrieval performance. As a drop-in visual encoder for multimodal large language models such as LLaVA-NeXT, ReasonCLIP delivers consistent gains without additional inference cost, demonstrating that structured reasoning supervision enhances the expressive capacity of CLIP-style visual representations. All datasets, models, and training code are available at https://github.com/RISys-Lab/ReasonCLIP.

AI.tldr: ReasonCLIP-58M is a continual pretraining framework that integrates large-scale reasoning supervision into CLIP-style models through a two-stage strategy, improving visually grounded commonsense and compositional reasoning while enhancing zero-shot retrieval and boosting multimodal LLMs like LLaVA-NeXT without extra inference cost.
AI.motivation: CLIP-style encoders dominate multimodal systems but are pretrained primarily on descriptive image-text alignment, leaving it unclear whether they can support visually grounded commonsense inference and compositional reasoning without architectural changes. Downstream applications increasingly demand these reasoning capabilities.
AI.method: A continual pretraining framework (ReasonCLIP-58M) with a two-stage strategy: (1) progressive integration of reasoning signals while preserving descriptive alignment, followed by (2) category-structured reasoning supervision. Two complementary datasets were constructed: ReasonLite-42M (open-form, visually verifiable reasoning captions) and ReasonPro-16M (category-specific reasoning supervision), along with RCLIP-Bench for diagnostic evaluation.
AI.result: ReasonCLIP improves visually grounded commonsense and compositional reasoning, enhances zero-shot retrieval performance, and delivers consistent gains as a drop-in visual encoder for multimodal LLMs like LLaVA-NeXT without additional inference cost.
AI.conclusion: Structured reasoning supervision enhances the expressive capacity of CLIP-style visual representations, demonstrating that reasoning-aware pretraining is beneficial even without architectural modifications.

title: Memory Depth, Not Memory Access: Selective Parametric Consolidation for Long-Running Language Agents
comment: Main paper with supplementary material included as ancillary file
summary: Long-running language agents need more than memory access. Retrieval systems can fetch past facts at query time, but they do not decide which experiences should continue to shape behavior after the working context is unloaded. We study this separate problem as memory depth: durable goal-conditioned tendencies written into a small parametric store. We introduce the loop-drift protocol, a controlled stress test in which the retrieval index remains intact while working context is unloaded and goal-conditioned behavior must persist under long-loop interference. We evaluate EVAF, a surprise- and valence-gated LoRA consolidation mechanism. Across GPT-2 and TinyLlama, retrieval is strongest on shallow factual recall (short-fact accuracy 0.956--0.973), while EVAF is strongest on goal persistence and post-unload recovery (0.812--0.904) with only 2--3 parametric writes per 200 events. Mechanism controls show that selective consolidation factorizes into two controllable dimensions: selection and actuation. Matched random gates isolate selection beyond sparse writing; fixed-inner controls across GPT-2, TinyLlama, and Mistral-7B show that inner-loop write strength is model-dependent; and a Mistral-7B matched-gate inversion reveals asymmetric selection-actuation coupling under miscalibrated actuation. Public Memora event streams serve as an external diagnostic, exposing stale-memory invalidation as an unresolved boundary. Within this probe, selective parametric consolidation supplies memory depth distinct from and complementary to retrieval access.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the loop-drift protocol to study memory depth (durable goal-conditioned behavior) in language agents, proposing EVAF—a selective LoRA consolidation mechanism that writes goal-relevant experiences into a small parametric store. EVAF outperforms retrieval on goal persistence after context unloading (0.812–0.904 accuracy) while using only 2–3 parametric writes per 200 events, and ablation studies reveal that selection and actuation are separable but asymmetrically coupled dimensions.
AI.motivation: Retrieval systems can fetch past facts but cannot decide which experiences should continue influencing behavior once the working context is unloaded. The authors study this as a separate problem—memory depth—and argue that durable, goal-conditioned tendencies must be written into a small parametric store.
AI.method: The loop-drift protocol stress-tests agents by unloading working context while keeping retrieval intact; EVAF uses surprise- and valence-gated LoRA consolidation to selectively write goal-relevant experiences. Controlled experiments with matched random gates, fixed-inner controls, and Mistral-7B matched-gate inversion separate selection from actuation.
AI.result: Retrieval excels at shallow factual recall (0.956–0.973), while EVAF dominates on goal persistence and post-unload recovery (0.812–0.904) with minimal writes (2–3 per 200 events). Selection and actuation factorize, but actuation strength varies by model (GPT-2, TinyLlama, Mistral-7B), and miscalibrated actuation reveals asymmetric selection-actuation coupling.
AI.conclusion: Selective parametric consolidation provides memory depth that is distinct from and complementary to retrieval access, with stale-memory invalidation identified as an unresolved boundary for future work.

title: Sketched Linear Contrastive Learning: Approximation, Optimization, and Statistical Scaling
summary: Scaling laws describe how learning performance varies with model size, data size, and compute. While recent theoretical work has established scaling laws for sketched linear regression, much less is understood for contrastive representation learning. In this paper, we study a sketched linear model for contrastive learning under a paired Gaussian latent-variable setup. The learner observes only sketched views of two correlated variables and trains a bilinear contrastive score by full-batch empirical gradient descent. We analyze a Gaussian-negative quadratic contrastive surrogate under aligned power-law spectra and a contrastive source condition, where we derive a risk decomposition into irreducible risk, approximation error, GD bias, GD variance, and a cross term. The cross term is controlled by the bias and variance and therefore does not affect the upper-bound scaling. Our main theorem gives an explicit scaling law with respect to sketch dimension $M$, sample size $N$, and effective optimization horizon $L_{\mathrm{eff}}γ$. Compared with standard linear-regression scaling laws, the contrastive setting must learn interactions between two views, and this changes how optimization and finite-sample noise scale with model size, data, and training time. This provides a first theoretical step toward understanding scaling behavior in contrastive learning and gives guidance for balancing model size, data, and optimization compute.

AI.tldr: This paper derives scaling laws for contrastive representation learning in a sketched linear model with paired Gaussian latent variables, showing how performance scales with sketch dimension, sample size, and optimization horizon.
AI.motivation: While scaling laws are well-studied for supervised learning like linear regression, they remain poorly understood for contrastive representation learning. This paper aims to bridge that gap.
AI.method: The authors study a sketched linear model under a paired Gaussian latent-variable setup with full-batch empirical gradient descent. They analyze a Gaussian-negative quadratic contrastive surrogate with aligned power-law spectra and a contrastive source condition, decomposing risk into irreducible risk, approximation error, GD bias, GD variance, and a cross term.
AI.result: The main theorem provides an explicit scaling law with respect to sketch dimension M, sample size N, and effective optimization horizon L_eff γ. The cross term is controlled by bias and variance, not affecting upper-bound scaling. Contrastive learning must learn interactions between two views, altering how optimization and finite-sample noise scale compared to standard linear regression.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a first theoretical step toward understanding scaling behavior in contrastive learning, offering guidance for balancing model size, data, and optimization compute.

title: KARLA: Knowledge-base Augmented Retrieval for Language Models
summary: We propose a new method that allows an LLM to automatically pull in factual knowledge from a knowledge base during token generation. This means that (1)~factual knowledge in the LLM output can be updated without retraining the LLM, (2)~facts in the LLM output can be traced to the knowledge base for transparency and explainability, and (3)~smaller models can achieve the same factual accuracy as larger models. Our core idea is to train the model to produce special tokens that trigger a query to the knowledge base. Our experiments show that our method improves factual grounding in both short and long-form generation, and allows factual revisions to take effect through KB edits rather than parameter updates.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a method enabling LLMs to automatically query knowledge bases during token generation, allowing factual knowledge updates without retraining, traceable facts, and smaller models achieving larger-model accuracy via special trigger tokens.
AI.motivation: To enable LLMs to incorporate factual knowledge from external knowledge bases during generation, avoiding retraining for knowledge updates, improving traceability, and allowing smaller models to match larger models' factual accuracy.
AI.method: Training the LLM to produce special tokens that trigger queries to a knowledge base during token generation, integrating external factual retrieval into the generation process.
AI.result: The method improves factual grounding in both short and long-form generation, and enables factual revisions through knowledge base edits rather than parameter updates.
AI.conclusion: The approach allows dynamic factual knowledge integration without retraining, enhances transparency via traceable facts, and demonstrates that smaller models can achieve factual accuracy comparable to larger models.

title: Discovering Millions of Interpretable Features with Sparse Autoencoders
summary: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) have emerged as a powerful tool for decomposing superposed language model representations into sparse and interpretable features. However, training SAEs is computationally expensive, and available open-source SAE models remain limited. In this work, we introduce \textbf{Qwen3-Instruct SAE}, a comprehensive suite of SAEs trained on the Qwen3 instruction-tuned model family, covering Qwen3-1.7B, Qwen3-4B, and Qwen3-8B. For Qwen3-1.7B and Qwen3-4B, we train layer-wise SAEs at three key activation sites: residual streams, MLP outputs, and attention outputs. For Qwen3-8B, we train SAEs on a subset of residual stream layers. We systematically evaluate these SAEs using both activation-level reconstruction metrics and model-level recovery metrics, revealing distinct sparsity--fidelity trade-offs across layers and components. Finally, we demonstrate the utility of Qwen3-Instruct SAE through a refusal-steering case study, showing that selected SAE features can causally steer instruction-tuned Qwen3 models toward refusal behavior. Our release provides a practical resource for studying sparse representations, feature-level mechanisms, and behavioral interventions in instruction-tuned language models

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Qwen3-Instruct SAE, a comprehensive suite of sparse autoencoders trained on Qwen3 instruction-tuned models (1.7B, 4B, 8B), covering multiple activation sites with systematic evaluation of sparsity-fidelity trade-offs, and demonstrates utility via refusal-steering case study.
AI.motivation: Training SAEs is computationally expensive and available open-source SAE models remain limited, motivating the need for a comprehensive, publicly available SAE suite for instruction-tuned language models.
AI.method: Train layer-wise SAEs on Qwen3-1.7B, Qwen3-4B (residual streams, MLP outputs, attention outputs) and Qwen3-8B (subset of residual stream layers); evaluate using activation-level reconstruction and model-level recovery metrics.
AI.result: Systematic evaluation reveals distinct sparsity-fidelity trade-offs across layers and components; selected SAE features can causally steer instruction-tuned Qwen3 models toward refusal behavior.
AI.conclusion: The release provides a practical resource for studying sparse representations, feature-level mechanisms, and behavioral interventions in instruction-tuned language models.

title: Multi-modality Image Fusion under Adverse Weather: Mask-Guided Feature Restoration and Interaction
summary: Multi-modality image fusion (MMIF) enhances scene representation by exploiting complementary cues from different modalities. Adverse weather, however, causes significant image degradation, disrupting feature representation and requiring simultaneous feature restoration and cross-modal complementarity. Existing methods often struggle with effective representation learning under such conditions, limiting their practical performance. To address these challenges, we propose a mask-guided MMIF method that integrates feature restoration and interaction. We first introduce "Pseudo Ground Truth" to simplify training, promoting faster and more effective feature learning. Then, we design a mask generation mechanism based on the mapping relationship between the fused result and the source images, quantifying the relative contribution of each modality during the fusion process. By incorporating the proposed mask-guided cross-modal cross-attention mechanism, the network is encouraged to selectively attend to informative features during modality interaction, mitigating the risk of overfitting to the static distribution of the "Pseudo Ground Truth". Additionally, we propose a mask-guided learning strategy and a task-coupled degradation-aware learning strategy to balance feature restoration and interaction. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that our method surpasses state-of-the-art approaches in visual quality, quantitative metrics, and downstream tasks. The source code is available at https://github.com/ixilai/AMG-Fuse.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a mask-guided multi-modality image fusion method (AMG-Fuse) that jointly addresses feature restoration and cross-modal interaction under adverse weather conditions, using pseudo ground truth, mask-guided cross-attention, and degradation-aware learning strategies to outperform SOTA methods.
AI.motivation: Adverse weather degrades image quality in multi-modality image fusion (MMIF), disrupting feature representation and requiring simultaneous restoration and cross-modal complementarity, which existing methods fail to handle effectively.
AI.method: The method introduces (1) "Pseudo Ground Truth" to simplify training, (2) a mask generation mechanism based on the mapping between fused result and source images to quantify modality contributions, (3) mask-guided cross-modal cross-attention for selective feature attention, (4) a mask-guided learning strategy, and (5) a task-coupled degradation-aware learning strategy to balance restoration and interaction.
AI.result: The method surpasses state-of-the-art approaches in visual quality, quantitative metrics, and downstream tasks on both synthetic and real-world datasets.
AI.conclusion: The proposed mask-guided MMIF framework effectively addresses adverse-weather degradation and cross-modal fusion simultaneously, demonstrating superior performance and generalization.

title: Computational Analysis of Heart Rate Variability in Healthy Adults
summary: Heart Rate Variability (HRV) analysis is a key indicator of cardiac physiological state and aids in disease diagnosis. However, research on HRV parameters in healthy individuals remains limited, and no gold standard exists. This study evaluates HRV indices in 40 healthy adults (20 men, 20 women, aged 30-50) to improve HRV's clinical utility. Using computational methods for signal processing and data analysis, time, frequency, and nonlinear indices were analyzed to address five questions: (1) normality, (2) stability, (3) correlation, (4) reproducibility, and (5) consistency. Key findings: (1) Time-domain and nonlinear indices, particularly global and LF (low frequency), follow normal distributions, with gender differences noted. (2) Most indices are stable except HF (high frequency)-related ones. (3) High correlations in HF-related indices suggest redundancy, indicating only one is necessary in studies. (4) Comparisons with the Fantasia database revealed less than 10% error for most indices, except SD2 and SDNN in women (greater than 15%). (5) Time-domain and nonlinear indices show low inter-study variability, while frequency-domain indices exhibit high variability, limiting cross-study comparisons. The selected indices-ApEn and IRRR (global variability), HRVi and SD2 (LF), and MADRR or rMSSD (HF)-are best suited for accurately representing HRV components and enhancing its clinical and research relevance.

AI.tldr: This study analyzes HRV indices in 40 healthy adults (30-50 years) to assess normality, stability, correlation, reproducibility, and consistency. Key findings include normal distributions for time-domain and nonlinear indices, stability issues with HF-related indices, redundancy among HF indices, and low inter-study variability for time-domain/nonlinear indices. Recommended indices: ApEn, IRRR (global), HRVi, SD2 (LF), MADRR or rMSSD (HF).
AI.motivation: HRV is a key cardiac indicator but research on healthy individuals is limited and no gold standard exists. This study aims to improve HRV's clinical utility by systematically evaluating its parameters.
AI.method: Forty healthy adults (20 men, 20 women, aged 30-50) underwent HRV analysis using computational signal processing. Time-domain, frequency-domain, and nonlinear indices were evaluated across five dimensions: normality, stability, correlation, reproducibility (vs. Fantasia database), and consistency.
AI.result: (1) Time-domain and nonlinear indices follow normal distributions with gender differences; (2) Most indices are stable except HF-related ones; (3) HF indices are highly correlated (redundant); (4) <10% error vs. Fantasia database for most indices except SD2 and SDNN in women (>15%); (5) Time-domain/nonlinear indices show low inter-study variability, frequency-domain indices show high variability.
AI.conclusion: The recommended indices (ApEn, IRRR for global; HRVi, SD2 for LF; MADRR or rMSSD for HF) best represent HRV components and enhance clinical and research relevance. Selecting non-redundant, stable indices improves cross-study comparability and clinical utility.

title: From Weights to Features: SAE-Guided Activation Regularization for LLM Continual Learning
summary: Weight-space regularization methods such as Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC) are the standard approach to catastrophic forgetting in continual learning. However, those methods tend to underperform when applied to large language models. We argue that such underperformance can be partly explained by the ``polysemantic'' nature of large language models: per-weight importance estimates utilized by EWC-style regularization are too coarse and cannot isolate the knowledge that needs protection. In this paper, we propose regularizing instead in the model's activation space, using pretrained Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) as a monosemantic feature dictionary. From the perspective of constrained optimization, we derive a new loss function that uses the SAE feature dictionary to explicitly balance stability and plasticity, and show that EWC is a special case in the one-sided weight-space penalty setting. Unlike replay-based methods that store or revisit examples from earlier tasks, our method requires no previous-task data after mask construction: current-task data is used to compute a compact SAE feature mask, and only this mask is retained for later training. Further, since the feature space has significantly lower dimensionality than the parameter space, the proposed method is more memory efficient. On the TRACE and MedCL continual learning benchmarks, the method achieves the strongest result among approaches without introducing task-specific architectural components, also surpassing traditional weight-space regularization methods like EWC. Beyond performance comparisons, we provide empirical evidence for the polysemanticity thesis: task-relevant representations are linearly separable in the SAE feature basis but indistinguishable from chance in the weight basis, and weight-space protection is nearly non-selective at the concept level.

AI.tldr: Regularizing in activation space using pretrained Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) as monosemantic feature dictionaries outperforms weight-space regularization (e.g., EWC) for continual learning in large language models, achieving better stability-plasticity balance, higher memory efficiency, and superior results on TRACE and MedCL benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Weight-space regularization methods like EWC underperform on large language models due to polysemanticity—per-weight importance estimates are too coarse to isolate knowledge that needs protection.
AI.method: Derives a loss function using SAE feature dictionaries for constrained optimization, computes a compact SAE feature mask from current-task data, and retains only this mask for later training. EWC is shown as a special case of this approach in one-sided weight-space penalty settings.
AI.result: Achieves strongest results among approaches without task-specific architectural components on TRACE and MedCL benchmarks, surpassing EWC. Provides empirical evidence that task representations are linearly separable in SAE feature basis but indistinguishable from chance in weight basis.
AI.conclusion: Activation-space regularization via SAEs offers a more effective and memory-efficient alternative to weight-space methods for continual learning in LLMs, with empirical support for the polysemanticity thesis.

title: Learning Adversarial Augmentation Policies for Robust Garlic Seedling Detection
summary: Accurate seedling detection during early growth stages is essential for timely replanting and effective crop management in precision agriculture. However, existing studies are mostly evaluated under relatively stable imaging conditions, such as UAV imagery or greenhouse environments, leaving robust detection under severe and spatially heterogeneous illumination in ground-based outdoor monitoring insufficiently explored. In addition, many illumination-robust detection methods rely on additional enhancement or feature-extraction modules, which increase inference-time overhead and are not tailored to seedling detection and downstream missing seedling localization. To address these gaps, we construct a new garlic seedling dataset captured using a ground-based monitoring platform under real outdoor field conditions with highly variable illumination. We further propose an illumination-robust seedling detection framework based on adversarial augmentation policy learning. The proposed method jointly optimizes a stochastic augmentation policy agent and an object detector, enabling the detector to learn robust representations under challenging visual conditions. A structural penalty is introduced to prevent unrealistic distortions while encouraging challenging augmentations during training. Extensive experiments show that the proposed approach achieves an AP$_{50}$ of 91.6%, improving the baseline by 0.9 percentage points and outperforming the previous best-performing method by 0.2 percentage points. For downstream missing seedling localization, it achieves 75.0% precision and a 67.0% F1-score, improving the baseline by 4.8 and 2.0 percentage points, respectively. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework for practical ground-based agricultural monitoring under complex outdoor lighting conditions without additional inference-time computational overhead.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an illumination-robust garlic seedling detection framework using adversarial augmentation policy learning for ground-based outdoor monitoring under severe lighting variability, achieving 91.6% AP50 without extra inference-time overhead.
AI.motivation: Existing seedling detection methods are mostly evaluated under stable imaging conditions (UAV, greenhouse) and fail under severe, spatially heterogeneous illumination in ground-based outdoor monitoring. Additionally, illumination-robust methods often rely on extra enhancement modules that increase inference overhead and are not tailored to seedling detection or downstream missing seedling localization.
AI.method: A framework based on adversarial augmentation policy learning that jointly optimizes a stochastic augmentation policy agent and an object detector, with a structural penalty to prevent unrealistic distortions while encouraging challenging augmentations during training.
AI.result: Achieves AP50 of 91.6% (+0.9% over baseline, +0.2% over SOTA). For missing seedling localization: 75.0% precision and 67.0% F1-score (+4.8% and +2.0% over baseline). No additional inference-time computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework is effective for practical ground-based agricultural monitoring under complex outdoor lighting conditions without extra inference-time cost.

title: The Capability Frontier: Benchmarks Miss 82% of Model Performance
summary: Existing benchmarks typically report accuracy for a single model on a single run. This systematically understates real-world LLM capabilities, particularly under heterogeneous data distributions: (i) different models get different questions correct according to their specializations, and (ii) given a budget, multiple generations can be sampled and selectively retained. To quantify this gap, we introduce the Capability Frontier: a Pareto frontier over a set of models that characterizes the best achievable performance at each cost level under optimal selection across models and generations (i.e., via an oracle). Our construction corrects for two opposing biases: underestimation from single-model evaluation and overestimation from taking maxima over noisy samples. We study 21 LLMs across 16 widely used benchmarks spanning coding, reasoning, medicine, factuality, instruction following, and agentic tasks, comparing Capability Frontier performance at matched cost to each benchmark's top-performing model. Correcting for single-model evaluation yields a 54% error rate reduction; additionally correcting for single runs yields an 82% improvement, with SOTA accuracy matched at 85% cost reduction. Complementing these empirical results, we use controlled probabilistic simulations to show that higher query topic entropy produces a near-monotonic increase in the performance gap between oracle routing and the best single model. Our findings suggest collective LLM capabilities are substantially underestimated, with implications for evaluation and deployment in data-heterogeneous, multi-domain settings.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the Capability Frontier, a Pareto-frontier method for evaluating LLMs across multiple models and generations, revealing that single-model single-run evaluations systematically underestimate real capabilities. Using 21 LLMs and 16 benchmarks, they show 54-82% error reduction and 85% cost reduction when accounting for heterogeneous model specializations and sampling budgets.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks report accuracy for a single model on a single run, which systematically understates real-world LLM capabilities under heterogeneous data distributions—different models excel on different questions, and multiple generations can be sampled and selectively retained given a budget.
AI.method: The authors construct the Capability Frontier as a Pareto frontier over a set of models, characterizing best achievable performance at each cost level under optimal oracle-based selection across models and generations. They correct for two biases: underestimation from single-model evaluation and overestimation from taking maxima over noisy samples. Controlled probabilistic simulations test the effect of query topic entropy on the performance gap.
AI.result: Correcting for single-model evaluation yields 54% error rate reduction; additionally correcting for single runs yields 82% improvement. SOTA accuracy is matched at 85% cost reduction. Higher query topic entropy produces a near-monotonic increase in the performance gap between oracle routing and the best single model.
AI.conclusion: Collective LLM capabilities are substantially underestimated under current evaluation practices, with important implications for evaluation methodology and deployment in data-heterogeneous, multi-domain settings.

title: Target-Aware Bandit Allocation for Scalable Surrogate Optimization in Chemical Space
summary: Identifying high-utility candidates from massive discrete spaces under expensive evaluations is a recurring challenge across the sciences, with structure-based drug discovery as a prominent example. While surrogate-based optimization can increase sample efficiency by reducing the number of expensive evaluations, modern molecular libraries have reached billions to trillions of compounds, making full-library surrogate inference itself a major computational bottleneck. We introduce BOBa, a bandit-guided surrogate optimization framework that eliminates full-library inference by adaptively allocating computation across partitions of the action space. By treating partitions as arms in a multi-armed bandit, BOBa concentrates inference and evaluations on empirically promising partitions while maintaining principled exploration. Experiments on real-world synthesis-on-demand libraries demonstrate that optimism-under-uncertainty bandits, combined with meaningful action space partitioning, are essential for effective allocation of inference and evaluations. Our findings reveal a tunable tradeoff between screening performance and surrogate inference cost, which supports practical optimization over current libraries, and establishes a viable route to ultra-large library virtual screening.

AI.tldr: BOBa is a bandit-guided surrogate optimization framework that eliminates the need for full-library inference by adaptively allocating computation across partitions of the action space, enabling efficient high-utility candidate identification in ultra-large molecular libraries.
AI.motivation: Structure-based drug discovery faces the challenge of identifying high-utility candidates from massive discrete spaces (billions to trillions of compounds) under expensive evaluations. While surrogate-based optimization improves sample efficiency, full-library surrogate inference itself becomes a major computational bottleneck for modern molecular libraries.
AI.method: BOBa treats partitions of the action space as arms in a multi-armed bandit, using optimism-under-uncertainty bandits to adaptively allocate inference and evaluations to empirically promising partitions while maintaining principled exploration.
AI.result: Experiments on real-world synthesis-on-demand libraries demonstrate that optimism-under-uncertainty bandits combined with meaningful action space partitioning are essential for effective allocation of inference and evaluations. The framework reveals a tunable tradeoff between screening performance and surrogate inference cost.
AI.conclusion: BOBa supports practical optimization over current ultra-large libraries and establishes a viable route to ultra-large library virtual screening by eliminating the full-library inference bottleneck through adaptive computational allocation.

title: Identifying the Unknown: Prompt-Free Open Vocabulary Anomaly Recognition for Robot-Object Interaction
comment: International Conference on Artificial Neural Networks 2026
summary: Robots operating in real-world environments must in general be able to recognize previously unseen objects. As robotic systems move toward open-world autonomy, there is a growing, yet largely unmet, need for open vocabulary object detectors that are prompt-free and efficient enough for continuous deployment. We present AnomNOVIC, a two-stage known-workspace framework that combines a masked autoencoder (MAE) trained for anomaly detection, with NOVIC, a powerful real-time prompt-free open vocabulary image classifier. The MAE produces generic object-agnostic bounding boxes, allowing NOVIC to classify salient image regions without requiring a predefined candidate class list. We evaluate AnomNOVIC against strong open vocabulary baselines in a tabletop robot-object environment featuring the NICOL humanoid robot, reaching 47.1% AP / 57.5% AP50 for prompt-free recognition, and 59.0% AP / 72.5% AP50 if class candidates are provided. Across additional datasets, including an in-the-wild test set with 48 unique objects, AnomNOVIC reaches up to 82.6% prompt-free detection and classification accuracy. These results significantly surpass all tested open vocabulary baselines, including YOLO-World-v2, OWLv2, and YOLOE.

AI.tldr: AnomNOVIC is a two-stage open-vocabulary object detector combining a masked autoencoder for anomaly detection (generating object-agnostic bounding boxes) with NOVIC, a prompt-free open-vocabulary classifier. It achieves state-of-the-art results (47.1% AP / 57.5% AP50 prompt-free, 59.0% AP / 72.5% AP50 with candidates) on tabletop robot-object environments and outperforms YOLO-World-v2, OWLv2, and YOLOE.
AI.motivation: Robots operating in open-world environments need to recognize previously unseen objects without predefined class lists, but existing open-vocabulary detectors require prompts or are computationally expensive for continuous deployment.
AI.method: A two-stage known-workspace framework: (1) a masked autoencoder (MAE) trained for anomaly detection generates generic object-agnostic bounding boxes; (2) NOVIC, a real-time prompt-free open-vocabulary image classifier, classifies the salient regions without requiring predefined class candidates.
AI.result: On NICOL humanoid robot tabletop environment: 47.1% AP / 57.5% AP50 (prompt-free), 59.0% AP / 72.5% AP50 (with candidates). On in-the-wild test set (48 objects): up to 82.6% prompt-free accuracy. Outperforms YOLO-World-v2, OWLv2, and YOLOE.
AI.conclusion: AnomNOVIC significantly surpasses all tested open-vocabulary baselines, demonstrating that combining anomaly-detection-driven proposal generation with prompt-free classification is an effective paradigm for open-world robotic perception.

title: Zero-Shot Size Transfer for Neural ODEs on Sparse Random Graphs: Graphon Limits and Adjoint Convergence
summary: Graph Neural Differential Equations (GNDEs) model continuous-time graph dynamics by parameterizing Neural ODE velocity fields with Graph Neural Networks. Their local, size-independent filters suggest a zero-shot size-transfer principle: train on a small graph and deploy on larger, similar graphs without retraining. We develop a quantitative theory for this principle on sparse random graphs sampled from graphons. We consider Graphon Neural Differential Equations (Graphon-NDEs) and adjoint Graphon-NDEs as the infinite-node limits of the forward and adjoint GNDE systems, and establish well-posedness. For an $n$-node random graph with sparsity parameter $α_n$, we prove trajectory-wise convergence of GNDE solutions to Graphon-NDE solutions at rate $O((α_n n)^{-1/2})$, up to logarithmic factors, with high probability. We also establish uniform-in-time convergence bounds for adjoint systems governing hidden-state and parameter gradients. We further study discretize-then-optimize (DTO) and optimize-then-discretize (OTD) training. Under explicit Euler discretization with $M$ steps, we show that DTO and OTD are asymptotically consistent, with hidden-state and local parameter-gradient discrepancies of orders $O(1/M)$ and $O(1/M^2)$, respectively, up to sparsity and logarithmic factors. Experiments on HSBM and tent graphons support the theoretical rates, while zero-shot transfer experiments across four graphon classes demonstrate accurate deployment of learned GNDEs on larger independently sampled graphs.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a quantitative theory for zero-shot size transfer in Graph Neural Differential Equations (GNDEs), proving convergence of GNDE solutions to their infinite-node limits (Graphon-NDEs) at rate O((α_n n)^{-1/2}) on sparse random graphs, establishing consistency of DTO and OTD training under Euler discretization, and validating results with experiments on synthetic and real graphons.
AI.motivation: To provide a rigorous theoretical foundation for the empirical observation that GNDEs trained on small graphs can be deployed zero-shot on larger similar graphs, by establishing convergence rates and well-posedness of the infinite-node limiting systems (Graphon-NDEs and adjoint Graphon-NDEs).
AI.method: The authors model GNDEs on sparse random graphs sampled from graphons, define Graphon-NDEs as infinite-node limits, prove well-posedness, derive trajectory-wise convergence rates and uniform-in-time adjoint convergence bounds, and analyze discretize-then-optimize (DTO) vs. optimize-then-discretize (OTD) training consistency under Euler discretization.
AI.result: Trajectory-wise convergence of GNDE to Graphon-NDE at rate O((α_n n)^{-1/2}) with high probability; DTO and OTD are asymptotically consistent with hidden-state discrepancy O(1/M) and local parameter-gradient discrepancy O(1/M²); experiments on HSBM and tent graphons confirm theoretical rates, and zero-shot transfer works across four graphon classes.
AI.conclusion: The theory rigorously justifies zero-shot size transfer for GNDEs trained on small graphs to larger graphs, with precise convergence rates and training consistency guarantees, supported by empirical validation.

title: Liquid Fusion of Heterogeneous Representations Towards General Salient Object Detection
summary: General Salient Object Detection (SOD) aims to identify and segment visually interesting objects from uni-modality or multi-modality scenes, recently advanced by cutting-edge State Space Models (SSMs). However, a critical limitation of current approaches is their neglect of the inherent spectral biases exhibited by different neural network paradigms. By digging to the dataset-level spectral analysis of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and SSMs, their semantic representations are inherently complementary based on their complementary frequency preferences. Inspired by this, we harmonize heterogeneous representations from SSMs and CNNs to bridge their spectral biases for general salient object detection. To this end, inspired by the dynamic information propagation of Liquid Neural Networks (LNNs), we introduce a liquid fusion to dynamically integrates features from two backbones, including VMamba and ConvNeXt, referred to Liquid Fusion Network (LFNet). Concretely, by treating the continuous VMamba features and ConvNeXt features as evolving states and exogenous stimulus, respectively, LFNet employs a dynamic gating mechanism for content-aware feature aggregation. Crucially, this state-stimulus paradigm enables to scale to multi-modal cues, resulting in flexibility in general SOD. Besides, a Saliency-Guided Upsampling (SGU) operator to propagate the features to the shallow layer, which leverages a spectral-spatial co-design to suppress upsampling artifacts while preserving semantics. Extensive experiments across five diverse tasks (RGB, RGB-D, RGB-T, VSOD, and VDT) demonstrate that LFNet achieves state-of-the-art performance, offering a superior trade-off between detection accuracy and model efficiency. Code has been released at https://github.com/cke520/LFNet.

AI.tldr: LFNet harmonizes heterogeneous features from SSMs (VMamba) and CNNs (ConvNeXt) via a liquid fusion mechanism inspired by Liquid Neural Networks, combined with a Saliency-Guided Upsampling operator, achieving state-of-the-art performance across five general SOD tasks.
AI.motivation: Current SSM-based SOD methods neglect the inherent spectral biases of different neural network paradigms. Analysis reveals CNNs and SSMs have complementary frequency preferences, motivating the need to bridge their spectral biases for improved salient object detection.
AI.method: LFNet uses a liquid fusion mechanism inspired by LNNs to dynamically integrate continuous VMamba (SSM) features as evolving states and ConvNeXt (CNN) features as exogenous stimuli via a content-aware dynamic gating mechanism, plus a Saliency-Guided Upsampling (SGU) operator using spectral-spatial co-design to suppress artifacts while preserving semantics.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance across five diverse SOD tasks (RGB, RGB-D, RGB-T, VSOD, VDT), with superior trade-off between detection accuracy and model efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The proposed state-stimulus paradigm effectively bridges spectral biases between CNNs and SSMs, enabling flexible multi-modal fusion and achieving strong general SOD performance.

title: LCAi: Life Cycle Assessment with big data fusion and retrieval-augmented generation-assisted interpretation
comment: 23 pages, 14 figures, 6 tables. Includes Supplementary Information
summary: The interpretation phase of life cycle assessment often lacks structured mechanisms for translating quantified improvement opportunities addressing environmental hotspots into actionable strategic pathways under technological, social, and policy uncertainty. To overcome this limitation, this study introduces a perspective-conditioned retrieval-augmented generation framework for LCA interpretation, where a multi-perspective retrieval and controlled synthesis is incorporated in the artificial intelligence (AI)-assisted LCA. To operationalise large language models in LCA interpretation, a perspective fusion RAG architecture was developed, covering academic, industry, public discourse, and European union (EU) funding datasets. Our approach comprises three steps: (1) a scenario anchor defining system boundaries and decarbonization targets, (2) a set of perspective-specific micro-queries with constrained retrieval, and (3) a neutral synthesis step integrating only ledger-stored outputs without further retrieval. The framework is demonstrated through a hydrogen-enabled diesel reduction use case in an Italian apple production facility using GPT-5 nano as the reasoning model. Overall, the structured retrieval and constrained synthesis are designed to mitigate the risk of hallucination while preserving cross-domain diversity. The approach presented can support more disciplined translation of impact results into strategic pathways and opens up new avenues for the use of advanced AI tools in LCA studies, particularly those focused on technologies that could be deployed at scale. This proof-of-concept demonstrates how AI-assisted, evidence-grounded interpretation can support implementation-oriented decision-making beyond conventional LCA studies.

AI.tldr: This study introduces a perspective-conditioned retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) framework for Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) interpretation that structures the translation of environmental hotspot analysis into actionable strategic pathways under uncertainty. The framework uses a multi-perspective retrieval approach with academic, industry, public, and EU funding datasets, involving scenario anchoring, perspective-specific micro-queries with constrained retrieval, and neutral synthesis. Demonstrated via a hydrogen-enabled diesel reduction case in an Italian apple facility using GPT-5 nano, the approach mitigates hallucination risks while preserving cross-domain diversity for implementation-oriented decision-making.
AI.motivation: The LCA interpretation phase lacks structured mechanisms for translating quantified improvement opportunities into actionable strategic pathways under technological, social, and policy uncertainty.
AI.method: A perspective-conditioned RAG framework was developed with three steps: (1) scenario anchoring defining system boundaries and decarbonization targets, (2) perspective-specific micro-queries with constrained retrieval from academic, industry, public discourse, and EU funding datasets, and (3) neutral synthesis integrating ledger-stored outputs without further retrieval. GPT-5 nano was used as the reasoning model.
AI.result: The framework was demonstrated through a hydrogen-enabled diesel reduction use case in an Italian apple production facility, showing that structured retrieval and constrained synthesis mitigate hallucination risks while preserving cross-domain diversity.
AI.conclusion: The approach supports disciplined translation of impact results into strategic pathways and opens new avenues for AI-assisted, evidence-grounded interpretation in LCA studies, particularly for technologies deployable at scale.

title: PersistentKV: Page-Aware Decode Scheduling for Long-Context LLM Serving on Commodity GPUs
summary: Autoregressive large language model (LLM) serving is increasingly limited by key-value (KV) cache movement rather than dense matrix multiplication. Modern paged-attention systems reduce KV-cache fragmentation and mature kernels such as FlashInfer provide highly optimized native-paged decode attention. However, the best single-kernel implementation is not always the best serving schedule: low-active long-context decode can under-utilize commodity GPUs, while mixed sequence lengths introduce a tension between many exact-length launches and coarse padded batches. We present PersistentKV, a native block-table decode attention engine and page-aware scheduling study for grouped-query attention (GQA). PersistentKV maps work by KV-head group, is designed to reuse K,V tiles across grouped query heads, supports native page tables, and adds a compact workqueue schedule that executes only non-empty row-KV-head-sequence-split tasks. On an RTX 3060 with FP16, page size 16, Hq=32, Hkv=8, d=128, and identical correctness tolerance against FlashInfer, a calibrated adaptive policy selects FlashInfer for small active batches, PersistentKV sequence splitting for B1 long-context steps, and PersistentKV workqueue scheduling for B8 long-context steps. With thresholds and split counts fixed on calibration traces, one held-out trace seed improves synchronized wall throughput by 1.063-1.265x on B8 bimodal, uniform, and Zipf-like workloads and by 1.399x on a B1 bucketed trace. On the B4 bimodal boundary case, the policy avoids the PersistentKV regression by selecting FlashInfer. These results identify a concrete systems niche for adaptive page-aware decode scheduling and show that work assignment, not only attention math, is a decisive serving-system variable.

AI.tldr: PersistentKV is a native block-table decode attention engine that improves LLM serving throughput by adaptively scheduling KV-cache attention work at the KV-head-group granularity, achieving 1.063-1.399x speedup over FlashInfer on long-context and mixed-batch workloads by reducing under-utilization from low-active decode and coarse padded batches.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive LLM serving bottlenecks have shifted from dense matrix multiplication to KV-cache movement. Existing paged-attention systems (e.g., FlashInfer) have optimized single-kernel performance but suffer from GPU under-utilization in low-active long-context decode and inefficiencies from mixed sequence lengths that force a trade-off between many exact-length launches and coarse padded batches. A better serving schedule, not just a better kernel, is needed.
AI.method: PersistentKV designs a block-table decode attention engine for GQA that: (1) maps work by KV-head group to reuse K,V tiles across grouped query heads; (2) supports native page tables; (3) introduces a compact workqueue schedule that executes only non-empty row-KV-head-sequence-split tasks. A calibrated adaptive policy selects between FlashInfer (small active batches), PersistentKV sequence splitting (B1 long-context), and PersistentKV workqueue scheduling (B8 long-context) based on thresholds fixed on calibration traces.
AI.result: On RTX 3060 (FP16, page size 16, Hq=32, Hkv=8, d=128), the adaptive policy improves synchronized wall throughput by 1.063-1.265x on B8 bimodal, uniform, and Zipf-like workloads, 1.399x on a B1 bucketed trace, and avoids regression on the B4 bimodal boundary case by falling back to FlashInfer.
AI.conclusion: There is a concrete systems niche for adaptive page-aware decode scheduling. Work assignment (not just attention kernel math) is a decisive variable in LLM serving system performance.

title: Rolling Shutter Relative Pose Estimation Made Practical
summary: Rolling shutter (RS) cameras equip virtually all consumer devices, yet RS-aware relative pose estimation has remained impractical: the state-of-the-art solver requires a minimum of 20 point correspondences, making RANSAC-based robust estimation prohibitively expensive due to the exponential dependence of the iteration count on the sample size. We make RS relative pose estimation practical by introducing affine correspondences (ACs) into the RS two-view geometry. We derive novel \emph{RS-corrected affine constraints} that account for the coupling between point perturbations and the row-dependent essential matrix, providing two equations per correspondence beyond the standard epipolar constraint. Building on these constraints, we develop a linearized algebraic solver that estimates pose and RS motion from only 7 ACs.
