summary: Reinforcement Learning like Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) has significantly advanced text-to-image post-training. However, current methods often favor superficial aesthetics, such as over-saturated colors, leaving critical flaws like AI artifacts and biological implausibilities unresolved. We attribute these limitations to two primary factors: (1) The absence of real images during post-training confines GRPO sampling to the original distribution, failing to break inherent generative boundaries; (2) the optimization process lacks specific rewards targeting fine-grained artifacts like overly oily skin and other AI artifacts. To address this, we propose PortraitGen, a novel framework tailored for photorealistic portrait generation. First, we break inherent generative boundaries by directly introducing real images into the GRPO sampling groups, where image inversion is employed to obtain their transition probabilities and latents. Second, to explicitly steer the model toward photorealism, we introduce a complementary dual-reward mechanism: OmniReward for general quality and AI-Portrait for human-centric fidelity. Furthermore, we curate PortraitBench, a comprehensive portrait-centric benchmark. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PortraitGen significantly outperforms existing baselines, effectively suppressing AI artifacts and achieving unprecedented photorealism.

AI.tldr: PortraitGen enhances text-to-image post-training by introducing real images into GRPO sampling and a dual-reward mechanism to suppress AI artifacts and improve photorealism in portrait generation.
AI.motivation: Current GRPO-based methods favor superficial aesthetics (e.g., oversaturated colors) but fail to address critical flaws such as AI artifacts and biological implausibilities due to (1) absence of real images in post-training locking the model into its original distribution, and (2) lack of specific rewards targeting fine-grained artifacts like overly oily skin.
AI.method: (1) Breaks generative boundaries by injecting real images into GRPO sampling groups using image inversion to obtain transition probabilities and latents. (2) Introduces a complementary dual-reward mechanism: OmniReward for general quality and AI-Portrait for human-centric fidelity. (3) Curates PortraitBench, a portrait-specific benchmark.
AI.result: PortraitGen significantly outperforms existing baselines, effectively suppresses AI artifacts, and achieves unprecedented photorealism on the proposed PortraitBench.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating real images into GRPO sampling and designing fine-grained, portrait-specific rewards are effective strategies to overcome the superficial optimization pitfalls of current reinforcement learning-based text-to-image post-training methods.

title: Adaptive Utility driven Resource Orchestration for Resilient AI (AURORA-AI)
comment: Accepted at IEEE Research and Technologies for Society and Industry 2026 conference
summary: Modern AI systems are increasingly deployed under non-stationary computational, demographic, and operational conditions in which static resource allocation strategies degrade both predictive performance and human-centric properties such as fairness and explainability. This paper presents AURORA-AI, an Adaptive Utility-driven Resource Orchestration framework for Resilient AI that unifies Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman feedback control, Lyapunov-based stability monitoring, and a fairness-aware composite utility into a single closed-loop policy.The framework continuously redistributes computational budget across a population of heterogeneous AI models so that the global utility, defined jointly over predictive performance, demographic parity, cost, latency, robustness, and interpretability, remains maximised under disruption. The framework is evaluated in a stress-rich discrete-time simulation that concurrently injects demographic bias shocks, gradual concept drift, and abrupt black-swan disruptions, and is compared against five established controllers including Static, Round Robin, Greedy, LinUCB, and a deep reinforcement-learning agent based on Proximal Policy Optimisation. AURORA-AI achieves immediate recovery from the black-swan event compared to eighty-eight time steps for the Static baseline and twenty-two for Proximal Policy Optimisation, lifts the alpha-quantile and the super-quantile by twenty-nine and twenty-five percent respectively, simultaneously reduces the mean and maximum demographic parity gap, and increases the fraction of Lyapunov-stable operating steps. These results indicate that fairness-aware adaptive orchestration grounded in stability theory is a practical and theoretically motivated path toward resilient human-centric AI deployment.

AI.tldr: AURORA-AI is an adaptive resource orchestration framework that uses Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman control, Lyapunov stability monitoring, and fairness-aware utility to dynamically allocate computational resources across AI models. It outperforms five baselines by achieving faster recovery from disruptions (immediate vs. 88 steps for Static, 22 for PPO), improving fairness metrics, and increasing Lyapunov-stable operating steps.
AI.motivation: Modern AI systems operate under non-stationary conditions where static resource allocation degrades both predictive performance and human-centric properties (fairness, explainability), motivating a need for adaptive, stability-grounded orchestration.
AI.method: The framework unifies Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman feedback control, Lyapunov-based stability monitoring, and a fairness-aware composite utility into a closed-loop policy that continuously redistributes computational budget across heterogeneous AI models.
AI.result: AURORA-AI achieves immediate recovery from black-swan disruptions (vs. 88 steps for Static, 22 for PPO), lifts alpha-quantile and super-quantile by 29% and 25%, reduces mean and maximum demographic parity gap, and increases Lyapunov-stable operating steps.
AI.conclusion: Fairness-aware adaptive orchestration grounded in stability theory offers a practical and theoretically motivated path toward resilient human-centric AI deployment.

title: Asymptotically Optimal Learning for Parametric Prophet Inequalities
summary: We study learning in prophet inequalities with i.i.d. rewards drawn from an exponential-type parametric family with an unknown parameter $θ$, a class that includes exponential, Pareto, and bounded-support power-family distributions. We first characterize the optimal full-information asymptotic competitive ratio for this family. In the unbounded-support case, the limit is $ {\left(θ/({θ-c_+})\right)^{c_+/θ}}/ {Γ(1-c_+/θ)},$ while in the bounded-support case, the limit is $1$. We then propose a confidence-based dynamic-programming policy for online learning. By exploiting the explicit parametric structure, the policy achieves the same optimal asymptotic competitive ratio using only online observations, without external offline samples. We further derive distribution-specific convergence rates for canonical examples. Finally, numerical experiments on synthetic instances illustrate the performance of our algorithm.

AI.tldr: The paper studies learning in prophet inequalities for i.i.d. rewards from exponential-type parametric families, characterizes the optimal asymptotic competitive ratio (unbounded-support: $(θ/(θ-c_+))^{c_+/θ}/Γ(1-c_+/θ)$; bounded-support: $1$), and proposes a confidence-based DP policy that achieves this ratio online without offline samples, with convergence rates and numerical validation.
AI.motivation: To bridge prophet inequalities and online learning under parametric uncertainty, characterizing optimal asymptotic competitive ratios and designing policies that achieve them without offline samples.
AI.method: Characterizes the optimal full-information asymptotic competitive ratio for exponential-type parametric families; proposes a confidence-based dynamic-programming policy for online learning that exploits explicit parametric structure.
AI.result: Optimal asymptotic competitive ratio: $(θ/(θ-c_+))^{c_+/θ}/Γ(1-c_+/θ)$ for unbounded-support, $1$ for bounded-support. The proposed policy achieves this ratio online without offline samples, with distribution-specific convergence rates.
AI.conclusion: Parametric structure can be exploited to achieve optimal asymptotic competitive ratios in online prophet inequalities, with theoretical guarantees and empirical validation.

title: Focusing on What Matters: Saliency-Harnessing Accurate Routing for Diffusion MoE
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures have emerged as a powerful paradigm for scaling diffusion models in visual generation. Recent advancements have focused on adaptively allocating computational resources across diverse tokens to improve efficiency and performance. However, we identify a routing assignment problem in existing diffusion MoE frameworks: the router fails to accurately allocate more computational resources to salient tokens. Our analysis attributes this failure to the router's reliance on noise-corrupted latent features throughout the denoising process. Such stochastic noise obscures the critical structural and textural information, thereby preventing the router from effectively distinguishing salient tokens. To address this, we propose SharpMoE, a post-training framework with a saliency-harnessing accurate routing mechanism, which utilizes clean latent features as a noise-free guidance signal for routing. By bypassing the noise-distorted inputs, SharpMoE provides the router with clear saliency guidance, enabling the identification of salient tokens even in high-noise stages. Furthermore, we introduce a trajectory routing loss to constrain the compute allocation throughout the multi-step denoising trajectory, ensuring precise resource allocation along the generation rollout. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SharpMoE serves as a versatile, plug-and-play solution that further enhances the pretrained, converged MoE models, achieving state-of-the-art performance in visual generation.

AI.tldr: SharpMoE is a post-training framework that solves the routing assignment problem in diffusion MoE models by using clean latent features as noise-free guidance, combined with a trajectory routing loss, achieving state-of-the-art visual generation performance.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion MoE frameworks suffer from a routing assignment problem where the router fails to allocate more computational resources to salient tokens because it relies on noise-corrupted latent features throughout the denoising process, where stochastic noise obscures critical structural and textural information.
AI.method: SharpMoE introduces (1) a saliency-harnessing accurate routing mechanism that uses clean latent features as noise-free guidance for routing, bypassing noise-distorted inputs; and (2) a trajectory routing loss that constrains compute allocation throughout the multi-step denoising trajectory for precise resource allocation.
AI.result: SharpMoE serves as a versatile, plug-and-play solution that further enhances pretrained, converged MoE models, achieving state-of-the-art performance in visual generation.
AI.conclusion: By providing clear saliency guidance even in high-noise stages, SharpMoE effectively addresses the routing assignment problem in diffusion MoE models, enabling better resource allocation and superior generation quality.

title: Semantic Early-Stopping for Iterative LLM Agent Loops
comment: 7 pages, 5 figures, 4 tables. Open implementation, machine-checked theory, and reproducible harness: github.com/SahilShrivastava-Dev/semantic-halting-problem
summary: Multi-agent large language model (LLM) loops, for example a Writer that drafts and a Critic that revises, are almost always terminated by a fixed iteration cap (max_iterations). This is a syntactic kill-switch: it is blind to whether the answer is still improving, so it over-spends tokens on easy inputs and truncates hard ones. We study semantic early-stopping: the loop halts when consecutive draft embeddings stop changing in meaning (cosine distance with a patience window) and the answer's measured quality stops improving. Our work makes three contributions. First, an honest theoretical footing: we prove deterministic termination and well-definedness and machine-check these claims, while treating the convergence of the distance sequence as an empirically tested conjecture rather than a (previously over-claimed) Banach contraction. Second, a judge-efficient evaluation protocol: we generate each question's full trajectory once, replay every stopping policy over the identical drafts, and cache every LLM-judge call, yielding a strictly paired efficiency-versus-quality comparison at low cost; we further separate operational tokens (charged to a policy) from evaluation tokens (a measurement instrument). Third, an empirical study on multi-hop retrieval-augmented question answering (HotpotQA). On the 60-question test split, a judge-free semantic stopper reduces operational tokens by 38% relative to max_iterations at parity quality (Delta-IS = -0.004, p = 0.81), whereas the full quality-gated variant is counter-productive because its per-round judging dominates cost. An oracle that selects the best round attains +0.115 Information Score over every practical policy (p ~ 4e-11), reframing the problem from "when to stop" (easy) to "which round is best" (open).

AI.tldr: The paper proposes semantic early-stopping for multi-agent LLM loops (e.g., Writer-Critic) to replace fixed iteration caps, proving deterministic termination, introducing a judge-efficient evaluation protocol, and showing on HotpotQA that a judge-free semantic stopper reduces tokens by 38% at parity quality.
AI.motivation: Fixed iteration caps (max_iterations) are syntactic kill-switches that waste tokens on easy inputs and truncate hard ones, motivating the need for a semantic early-stopping criterion that is aware of answer improvement.
AI.method: (1) Theoretical: prove deterministic termination and well-definedness using cosine distance with a patience window, treating convergence as an empirical conjecture rather than a Banach contraction. (2) Evaluation: generate full trajectories per question, replay all stopping policies over identical drafts, cache LLM-judge calls for paired comparisons. (3) Empirical: test on HotpotQA with judge-free semantic stopper and quality-gated variant.
AI.result: Judge-free semantic stopper reduces operational tokens by 38% over max_iterations at parity quality (Delta-IS = -0.004, p = 0.81). Quality-gated variant is counter-productive due to judging cost. An oracle selecting the best round achieves +0.115 IS over any practical policy (p ~ 4e-11).
AI.conclusion: Semantic early-stopping is feasible and token-efficient, but the bigger challenge is predicting which round is best (oracle gap shows room for improvement).

title: GEOALIGN: Geometric Rollout Curation for Robust LLM Reinforcement Learning
comment: Accepted as a conference paper at ICML 2026
summary: Online reinforcement learning is widely used to align large language models (LLMs) with reward signals, yet training can be unstable under noisy or misspecified rewards. We identify a failure mode we call directional inconsistency: within a batch, a small set of high-reward rollouts induces representation-space preference directions that sharply disagree with the batch majority, resulting in high-variance and destabilizing updates. We propose geoalign, a lightweight plug-in for rollout curation in iterative policy optimization. Geoalign (i) forms within-prompt preference pairs, (ii) learns an online projector on per-rollout hidden states to concentrate reward-ordered displacement directions, and (iii) detects directionally inconsistent rollouts via their angular deviation from a batch consensus prototype and rectifies them with within-prompt stable alternatives. Geoalign is forward-pass only and adds negligible overhead. Across dialogue alignment with a learned reward model and mathematical reasoning with binary verified rewards, Geoalign improves final performance and reduces training oscillation, outperforming PF-PPO, PAR, PODS, and Seed-GRPO. These results suggest latent directional consensus as an effective reliability signal for online LLM RL.

AI.tldr: Geoalign is a lightweight plug-in for online LLM RL that detects and rectifies directionally inconsistent high-reward rollouts (which cause unstable updates) by using angular deviation from a batch consensus prototype, improving performance and reducing training oscillation across dialogue and math reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Online RL for LLM alignment suffers from instability under noisy/misspecified rewards due to "directional inconsistency"—a small set of high-reward rollouts within a batch producing representation-space preference directions that sharply disagree with the batch majority, leading to high-variance destabilizing updates.
AI.method: Geoalign is a forward-pass-only plug-in that (i) forms within-prompt preference pairs, (ii) learns an online projector on per-rollout hidden states to concentrate reward-ordered displacement directions, and (iii) detects directionally inconsistent rollouts via angular deviation from a batch consensus prototype, rectifying them with within-prompt stable alternatives.
AI.result: Geoalign improves final performance and reduces training oscillation over PF-PPO, PAR, PODS, and Seed-GRPO across dialogue alignment with a learned reward model and mathematical reasoning with binary verified rewards.
AI.conclusion: Latent directional consensus serves as an effective reliability signal for online LLM RL, and Geoalign's lightweight curation mechanism successfully stabilizes training under noisy or misspecified rewards.

title: TraMP-LLaMA: Generative Interpretability with Decoupled Instruction Tuning for Facial Expression Quality Assessment
summary: Existing facial expression quality assessment (FEQA) methods typically produce only a severity score, without explicitly communicating the observable facial motion evidence that supports the prediction. This limits interpretability and makes it difficult to inspect the basis of model outputs in Parkinson's disease assessment. To address this gap, we propose TraMP-LLaMA, a unified multimodal framework that jointly predicts severity scores and generates structured textual reports from facial motion cues. The framework integrates RGB appearance and landmark trajectory cues, and adopts a decoupled instruction-tuning strategy to reduce task interference between severity prediction and language generation. To support this task, we further extend the PFED5 dataset with expert-guided textual motion descriptions and construct PFED5-plus. Experiments on PFED5-plus show that TraMP-LLaMA outperforms competitive video-language baselines in report generation and achieves the best severity prediction performance among the compared methods under joint multi-expression training, improving Spearman's rank correlation by at least 4.39 percent over all competing methods. The text annotations and code are available at https://github.com/shuchaoduan/TraMP-LLaMA.

AI.tldr: TraMP-LLaMA is a unified multimodal framework that jointly predicts facial expression severity scores and generates structured textual reports from RGB and landmark trajectory cues for Parkinson's disease assessment. It uses decoupled instruction-tuning to reduce task interference and extends the PFED5 dataset with expert-guided textual descriptions (PFED5-plus). It outperforms baselines in report generation and severity prediction (Spearman's ρ improved by ≥4.39%).
AI.motivation: Existing FEQA methods only output severity scores without explaining the observable facial motion evidence, limiting interpretability and model inspection in Parkinson's disease assessment.
AI.method: Proposes TraMP-LLaMA, a unified multimodal framework integrating RGB appearance and landmark trajectory cues with a decoupled instruction-tuning strategy to reduce task interference between severity prediction and language generation. The PFED5 dataset is extended to PFED5-plus with expert-guided textual motion descriptions.
AI.result: TraMP-LLaMA outperforms video-language baselines in report generation and achieves best severity prediction under joint multi-expression training, improving Spearman's rank correlation by at least 4.39% over all competing methods.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables interpretable facial expression quality assessment by jointly producing severity scores and structured textual reports, enhancing transparency in PD assessment.

title: How to evaluate clustering with ground truth?
comment: Preprint of a book chapter to appear: P. Fränti, "How to evaluate clustering with ground truth?", In Center-based clustering, Springer Nature, 2026
summary: External indexes can be used for cluster evaluation when ground truth is available. We review the most common external validity indexes focusing on set-matching-based measures. We recommend centroid index (CI), because it is an intuitive cluster-level measure with an explainable result. If we need a more fine-tuned, point-level measure, there are more choices. Pair-set index (PSI) provides a normalized score which is not biased by cluster sizes. If all points should matter equally, then clustering accuracy (ACC) or any other set-matching measure is suitable.

AI.tldr: This paper reviews external cluster validity indexes, particularly set-matching-based measures, and recommends the centroid index (CI) for its intuitive cluster-level interpretation, while also suggesting pair-set index (PSI) for fine-tuned point-level evaluation and clustering accuracy (ACC) when equal point importance is required.
AI.motivation: To review and compare common external validity indexes for cluster evaluation when ground truth is available, with a focus on set-matching-based measures.
AI.method: The authors review existing external validity indexes, focusing on set-matching-based measures, and compare their properties (cluster-level vs. point-level, bias, interpretability).
AI.result: Centroid index (CI) is recommended as an intuitive cluster-level measure with explainable results. Pair-set index (PSI) offers a normalized, unbiased score at the point level. Clustering accuracy (ACC) is suitable when all points should matter equally.
AI.conclusion: Different external validity indexes serve different needs: CI for cluster-level interpretation, PSI for unbiased point-level evaluation, and ACC for equal point importance.

title: Decision-Aligned Evaluation of Uncertainty Quantification
summary: Uncertainty estimates in machine learning are typically evaluated using generic metrics such as the negative log-likelihood and expected calibration error, yet good performance on such metrics does not necessarily imply high utility in downstream decisions. We introduce decision-alignment, a criterion that reveals which evaluation metrics meaningfully align with downstream utilities. Applying this framework, we show that many widely used uncertainty metrics are either misaligned with common decision problems or encode pathological prior beliefs about the downstream task. We then propose prior-weighted utility metrics, a special class of proper scoring rules that provides decision-aligned uncertainty evaluation. Across benchmark experiments and real-world case studies, our metrics consistently align with realized decision utility, while conventional metrics do not. Our results surface flaws in the current UQ evaluation protocol and offer a principled extension of existing metrics toward decision-relevant UQ evaluation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces "decision-alignment" as a criterion to evaluate whether uncertainty metrics meaningfully align with downstream decision utilities, showing that common metrics like NLL and ECE often misalign with real decision problems. The authors propose "prior-weighted utility metrics" (a class of proper scoring rules) as a decision-aligned alternative, demonstrating across benchmarks and real-world case studies that their metrics consistently correlate with realized decision utility while conventional metrics do not.
AI.motivation: Standard uncertainty quantification (UQ) evaluation metrics (e.g., negative log-likelihood, expected calibration error) are widely used, but good performance on these metrics does not guarantee high utility in downstream decision-making tasks. The authors aim to identify and address this disconnect by introducing a principled framework for decision-relevant UQ evaluation.
AI.method: The authors propose "decision-alignment" as a theoretical criterion to assess whether an evaluation metric reflects downstream decision utility. They then derive "prior-weighted utility metrics," a specialized class of proper scoring rules, and validate them against conventional metrics across benchmark experiments and real-world case studies.
AI.result: Many widely used uncertainty metrics are either misaligned with common decision problems or encode pathological prior beliefs about the downstream task. The proposed prior-weighted utility metrics consistently align with realized decision utility, whereas conventional metrics do not.
AI.conclusion: Current UQ evaluation protocols are flawed; the paper offers a principled extension of existing metrics toward decision-relevant UQ evaluation, advocating for the adoption of decision-aligned metrics in practice.

title: Scaling Multi-Reference Image Generation with Dynamic Reward Optimization
summary: While personalized image generation has achieved remarkable progress, multi-reference image generation (MRIG) remains a challenging task. Most existing benchmarks fail to adequately evaluate complex MRIG scenarios, hindering further progress in this area. To better assess model performance on complex MRIG tasks, we introduce OmniRef-Bench, a benchmark that covers complex combinations of reference image types and a large number of reference images. Evaluations on OmniRef-Bench show that mainstream open-source models struggle in complex MRIG scenarios, and their performance deteriorates significantly as the number of mixed-type reference images increases. To address this issue, we propose DyRef, a two-stage training framework. In the first stage, supervised fine-tuning equips the model with the basic capability to handle complex MRIG tasks. In the second stage, we introduce Difficulty-aware Advantage Reweighting (DAR) and Discriminative Reward Scaling (DRS). DAR dynamically adjusts the optimization objective to improve performance when handling a large number of mixed-type reference images. DRS enlarges intra-group reward differences for more effective policy optimization. Experiments demonstrate that DyRef significantly improves the performance of open-source models on OmniRef-Bench and single-image editing benchmarks, demonstrating the effectiveness and generalization capability of our approach.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces OmniRef-Bench, a benchmark for multi-reference image generation (MRIG), and proposes DyRef, a two-stage training framework with DAR and DRS mechanisms, which significantly improves open-source models' performance on complex MRIG and single-image editing tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks fail to adequately evaluate complex multi-reference image generation (MRIG) scenarios where combinations of reference image types and large numbers of reference images are involved, hindering progress in this area.
AI.method: Proposes DyRef, a two-stage training framework: (1) supervised fine-tuning to establish basic MRIG capability; (2) Difficulty-aware Advantage Reweighting (DAR) to dynamically adjust optimization objectives for handling many mixed-type references, and Discriminative Reward Scaling (DRS) to enlarge intra-group reward differences for more effective policy optimization.
AI.result: DyRef significantly improves performance of open-source models on OmniRef-Bench and single-image editing benchmarks, while mainstream open-source models struggle and deteriorate as the number of mixed-type reference images increases.
AI.conclusion: The proposed DyRef framework demonstrates effectiveness and generalization capability for complex MRIG tasks, validated on both multi-reference and single-image editing benchmarks.

title: Joint Learning of Experiential Rules and Policies for Large Language Model Agents
summary: For LLM agents in multi-step interactive environments, a key challenge is to make effective use of accumulated interaction experience. Existing work has typically separated two uses of such experience: keeping it outside the model as natural-language rules for later prompting, or using trajectories and feedback to update the model parameters. The former is easy to interpret but can fall out of sync with the evolving policy; the latter improves the policy more broadly but provides only limited correction for local mistakes in sparse-reward settings. We present Joint Learning of Experiential Rules and Policies for LLM Agents (JERP), which updates a long-term experiential-rule pool and the policy from the same interaction trajectories. At decision time, JERP retrieves task-relevant rules and conditions the agent on them together with the interaction history. After each episode, it uses the collected trajectories both to optimize the policy and to revise the rule pool by comparing current rollouts with reference successful trajectories. This coupling keeps the rule pool aligned with the evolving policy while allowing stable and effective behaviors to be gradually absorbed into the model itself. Experiments on AlfWorld and WebShop show that JERP yields consistent gains in decision performance for complex interactive tasks.

AI.tldr: JERP jointly updates an experiential-rule pool and LLM agent policy from the same interaction trajectories, keeping rules aligned with the evolving policy while gradually absorbing stable behaviors into the model, yielding gains on AlfWorld and WebShop.
AI.motivation: Existing work separates two uses of interaction experience—keeping it as natural-language rules for prompting or using trajectories for parameter updates—but the former can fall out of sync with the evolving policy while the latter provides limited local correction in sparse-reward settings. JERP aims to bridge this gap.
AI.method: JERP maintains a long-term experiential-rule pool updated via comparison of current rollouts with reference successful trajectories after each episode. At decision time, it retrieves task-relevant rules and conditions the agent on them alongside interaction history. The same trajectories are used to optimize the policy and revise the rule pool.
AI.result: Experiments on AlfWorld and WebShop demonstrate consistent gains in decision performance for complex interactive tasks.
AI.conclusion: Coupling rule pool updates with policy optimization keeps rules aligned with the evolving policy and allows stable behaviors to be gradually absorbed into the model, improving performance in multi-step interactive environments.

title: Uncertainty quantification via conformal prediction in data assimilation
comment: Submitted to Quarterly Journal of the Royal Meteorological Society
summary: Quantifying the evolution of uncertainty is critical to both probabilistic forecasting and data assimilation in numerical weather prediction. In this study, we investigate the applicability of conformal prediction (CP), a recent machine learning (ML) method, to quantify uncertainty in a controlled, idealized setting. We use the one dimensional modified shallow water model, designed to mimic the convective process. CP provides a set of possible outcomes with a chosen confidence level. Here, we compare and evaluate the average empirical coverage, the average interval length, miss low, miss high and average interval score loss (AISL) for three variants of CP, namely a) Standard CP, b) Normalized CP and c) Conformalized Quantile Regression. We further compare these CP-based uncertainty estimates with traditional ensemble-based measures such as standard deviation intervals and ensemble spread. In addition, we investigate the integration of CP-derived uncertainty within the data assimilation cycle through CP perturbations. Our results highlight the strengths and limitations of each approach, providing insight into the effectiveness of CP to complement common ensemble-based uncertainty quantification in simplified atmospheric models.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the use of conformal prediction (CP) methods for uncertainty quantification in numerical weather prediction using a 1D modified shallow water model. Three CP variants (Standard CP, Normalized CP, Conformalized Quantile Regression) are compared against traditional ensemble-based uncertainty measures, and CP-derived uncertainty is integrated into data assimilation cycles via CP perturbations.
AI.motivation: Quantifying the evolution of uncertainty is critical to both probabilistic forecasting and data assimilation in numerical weather prediction.
AI.method: Uses a 1D modified shallow water model mimicking convective processes. Compares three CP variants (Standard CP, Normalized CP, Conformalized Quantile Regression) against traditional ensemble-based measures (standard deviation intervals, ensemble spread) using metrics: average empirical coverage, average interval length, miss low, miss high, and AISL. Also integrates CP-derived uncertainty into data assimilation via CP perturbations.
AI.result: The results highlight the strengths and limitations of each CP approach, providing insight into CP's effectiveness to complement common ensemble-based UQ in simplified atmospheric models.
AI.conclusion: CP can complement traditional ensemble-based uncertainty quantification in simplified atmospheric models, but each variant has distinct trade-offs in performance.

title: Computer Vision for MOBA Analytics: A Dataset and Baseline for Visibility Analysis in Dota 2
comment: Accepted for presentation at the 2026 Simpósio Brasileiro de Jogos e Entretenimento Digital (SBGames)
summary: Introduction: Most Multiplayer Online Battle Arena (MOBA) analytics studies rely on structured data, which does not directly capture what each team could actually see during a match. Objective: This work introduces Dota2-Vis, a video-based dataset, and a baseline pipeline for visibility analysis in professional Dota 2 matches. Methodology: The dataset comprises all 144 matches from The International 2025, recorded from both team perspectives, totaling 288 Full HD videos, together with 2,477 manually annotated minimap images. We evaluate multiple variants of a modern object detector for player-icon detection and use the best-performing model to estimate opponent-visible player presence over time. Results: YOLO11l (large) achieved the best overall performance, reliably identifying player icons even in dense and visually cluttered minimap scenes. The resulting visibility curves reveal player, hero, role, and team-level patterns that complement conventional MOBA analytics, highlighting behavioral differences that are difficult to obtain from structured data alone. The dataset and code are publicly available at https://github.com/RicardoRCarvalho/dota2-vis/.

AI.tldr: Dota2-Vis is a video-based dataset of 288 Full HD professional Dota 2 match recordings from both team perspectives, with 2,477 annotated minimap images. Using YOLO11l for player-icon detection, the pipeline estimates opponent-visible player presence over time, revealing behavioral patterns not captured by structured data alone.
AI.motivation: Existing MOBA analytics rely on structured (logged) data that doesn't capture what teams can actually see during a match due to fog-of-war. This limits analysis of visibility-based strategic behavior.
AI.method: Collected 144 matches from The International 2025 recorded from both team perspectives (288 Full HD videos), with 2,477 manually annotated minimap images. Evaluated multiple YOLO variants for player-icon detection; used best-performing model to estimate opponent-visible player presence over time.
AI.result: YOLO11l (large) achieved the best detection performance, reliably identifying player icons in dense/cluttered minimap scenes. Visibility curves revealed player-, hero-, role-, and team-level behavioral patterns that complement conventional analytics.
AI.conclusion: Video-based visibility analysis provides behavioral insights difficult to obtain from structured data alone. Dataset and code are publicly available.

title: OpenRCA 2.0: From Outcome Labels to Causal Process Supervision
summary: Root cause analysis (RCA) poses a holistic test of LLM agentic capabilities, such as long-context understanding, multi-step reasoning, and tool use. However, existing datasets suffer from a fundamental gap: they label only the root cause, not the propagation path connecting it to the observed symptom, which largely simplifies the task to naive pattern matching. To support rigorous evaluation, we introduce PAVE, a step-wise labeling protocol that leverages known interventions from fault injection to reconstruct causal propagation paths. The mechanism is forward verification: reasoning from cause to effect rather than inferring backward from symptoms. Applying PAVE yields OpenRCA 2.0 (500 instances), the first cross-system RCA benchmark with step-wise causal annotations for LLM agents. Across 11 frontier LLMs, recovering the exact root-cause set succeeds in only 20.7% of cases on average. To locate where this difficulty lies, we relax the criterion and find what we call the ungrounded diagnosis: agents identify at least one correct root-cause service in 76.0% of cases, but ground that service in a verified causal propagation path to the observed symptom in only 61.5%. Outcome-only evaluation hides this failure mode; step-wise causal ground truth is the missing piece for trustworthy LLM-based RCA agents.

AI.tldr: PAVE introduces a step-wise causal annotation protocol for root cause analysis, producing OpenRCA 2.0 benchmark. LLM agents achieve only 20.7% exact root-cause recovery, and struggle with "ungrounded diagnosis"—identifying correct root causes (76.0%) but failing to ground them in verified causal propagation paths (61.5%).
AI.motivation: Existing RCA datasets only label the root cause without the causal propagation path, reducing the task to pattern matching. Step-wise causal ground truth is needed to rigorously evaluate LLM agentic capabilities in RCA.
AI.method: PAVE uses a forward verification mechanism: reasoning from cause to effect by leveraging known fault injection interventions to reconstruct causal propagation paths, producing step-wise annotations.
AI.result: Across 11 frontier LLMs, exact root-cause set recovery averages only 20.7%. Agents identify correct root-cause services in 76.0% of cases but ground them in verified causal paths in only 61.5%, revealing an "ungrounded diagnosis" failure mode.
AI.conclusion: Outcome-only evaluation hides the ungrounded diagnosis problem; step-wise causal ground truth is essential for trustworthy LLM-based RCA agents.

title: A Generalization Theory for JEPA-Based World Models
summary: Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs) have recently emerged as a promising paradigm for world modeling by learning predictive dynamics in a latent space rather than generating future observations at the input level. Despite their empirical success, the theoretical understanding of JEPA-based world models remains limited. In this paper, we develop the first generalization theory for JEPA-based world models. We formulate JEPA pretraining as a conditional spectral graph learning problem and show that the JEPA objective is equivalent to a low-rank factorization of an action-conditioned co-occurrence matrix. Building on this characterization, we establish a connection between JEPA pretraining error and downstream planning regret, leading to a finite-sample generalization bound for JEPA-based world models. Our analysis reveals an inherent trade-off between approximation and sample errors with respect to the latent dimension, providing theoretical insights into the advantages and limitations of latent predictive models compared with input-level predictive approaches.

AI.tldr: This paper develops the first generalization theory for JEPA-based world models, formulating JEPA pretraining as conditional spectral graph learning and establishing a connection between pretraining error and downstream planning regret with finite-sample generalization bounds.
AI.motivation: Despite empirical success of JEPAs for world modeling, theoretical understanding remains limited. This work aims to bridge the gap by providing a rigorous generalization theory.
AI.method: Formulates JEPA pretraining as a conditional spectral graph learning problem, showing equivalence to low-rank factorization of an action-conditioned co-occurrence matrix. Establishes connection between JEPA pretraining error and downstream planning regret.
AI.result: Derives a finite-sample generalization bound for JEPA-based world models, revealing an inherent trade-off between approximation and sample errors with respect to latent dimension.
AI.conclusion: Provides theoretical insights into the advantages and limitations of latent predictive models compared with input-level predictive approaches, highlighting the role of latent dimensionality.

title: Geometric Gradient Rectification for Safe Open-Set Semi-Supervised Learning
summary: Open-set semi-supervised learning aims to leverage unlabeled data that may contain out-of-distribution outliers while maintaining performance on in-distribution classes. Existing methods mainly follow two paradigms: filtering suspicious samples or incorporating unlabeled objectives with soft weighting. We argue that both face a common trade-off: aggressive filtering can discard informative but hard ID samples, whereas utilization can introduce auxiliary gradients that conflict with supervised learning when pseudo labels are wrong. We therefore shift the focus from sample selection to gradient-level control. We propose \textit{Geometric Gradient Rectification} (GGR), a plug-in framework that uses the supervised gradient as an anchor and projects conflicting auxiliary gradients onto an admissible region in gradient space. This makes the applied auxiliary update first-order non-opposing within the rectified coordinate block while preserving orthogonal components that may still carry useful representation signals. We further extend GGR with subspace-aware rectification to stabilize the anchor under noisy mini-batch gradients. Experiments on CIFAR and ImageNet benchmarks show that GGR improves representative OSSL baselines in most settings and yields gains in both closed-set generalization and open-set robustness. Code will be available at https://github.com/JiaheChen2002/GGR.

AI.tldr: GGR proposes gradient-level rectification for open-set semi-supervised learning, projecting conflicting auxiliary gradients onto an admissible region anchored by supervised gradients, avoiding the sample selection vs. utilization trade-off.
AI.motivation: Existing open-set SSL methods face a fundamental trade-off between aggressive filtering (discarding informative hard ID samples) and soft-weighting utilization (introducing conflicting auxiliary gradients from wrong pseudo labels). The paper aims to resolve this conflict at the gradient level rather than the sample level.
AI.method: Proposes Geometric Gradient Rectification (GGR), which uses the supervised gradient as an anchor and projects conflicting auxiliary gradients onto an admissible region in gradient space. This ensures applied auxiliary updates are first-order non-opposing in the rectified coordinate block while preserving orthogonal components. Extends with subspace-aware rectification to stabilize the anchor under noisy mini-batch gradients.
AI.result: GGR improves representative OSSL baselines on CIFAR and ImageNet benchmarks, yielding gains in both closed-set generalization and open-set robustness across most settings.
AI.conclusion: Shifting from sample-level selection to gradient-level control effectively resolves the trade-off in open-set SSL, providing a plug-in framework that enhances existing methods without architectural changes.

title: TOPS: First-Principles Visual Token Pruning via Constructing Token Optimal Preservation Sets for Efficient MLLM Inference
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have achieved strong multimodal reasoning capabilities, but their efficiency is limited by the large number of visual tokens, which introduces substantial computational overhead. Visual token pruning offers a natural solution, yet existing methods are imperfect: attention-based criteria tend to retain redundant tokens, while diversity-based criteria are often agnostic to user instructions. Even methods that combine multiple criteria still lack a principled formulation of the intrinsic objective of token pruning. In this paper, we revisit visual token pruning from a first-principles perspective and formulate it as constructing Token Optimal Preservation Sets. Through a top-down information-theoretic analysis, we identify three fundamental principles for effective token selection: Task Relevance, Information Coverage, and Semantic Diversity. Based on these principles, we propose TOPS, a training-free and model-agnostic pruning module that can be applied to various MLLMs. Extensive experiments on 7 MLLM backbones and 14 benchmarks demonstrate that TOPS outperforms prior methods under diverse pruning settings. Notably, on LLaVA-NeXT, TOPS removes 77.8% of visual tokens while preserving 100.0% and 100.6% performance on its 7B and 13B models, respectively, suggesting that pruning redundant visual tokens can sometimes mitigate hallucination and inspire future lightweight MLLM design.

AI.tldr: TOPS is a training-free, model-agnostic visual token pruning module for MLLMs that formulates token pruning as constructing Token Optimal Preservation Sets guided by three principles (Task Relevance, Information Coverage, Semantic Diversity), achieving up to 77.8% token reduction while maintaining or even improving performance across 7 backbones and 14 benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) suffer from high computational overhead due to the large number of visual tokens. Existing pruning methods are imperfect: attention-based criteria retain redundant tokens, diversity-based criteria ignore user instructions, and combined approaches lack a principled formulation of the intrinsic objective of token pruning.
AI.method: The paper revisits visual token pruning from a first-principles perspective, formulating it as constructing Token Optimal Preservation Sets via a top-down information-theoretic analysis. This yields three fundamental principles: Task Relevance, Information Coverage, and Semantic Diversity. Based on these, TOPS is proposed as a training-free and model-agnostic pruning module applicable to various MLLMs.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on 7 MLLM backbones and 14 benchmarks show TOPS outperforms prior methods under diverse pruning settings. On LLaVA-NeXT, TOPS removes 77.8% of visual tokens while preserving 100.0% and 100.6% performance on its 7B and 13B models, respectively, with pruning sometimes even mitigating hallucination.
AI.conclusion: TOPS provides a principled and effective solution for visual token pruning in MLLMs, demonstrating that aggressive token reduction can maintain or improve performance, suggesting potential for future lightweight MLLM design.

title: Just how sure are you? Improving Verbalized Uncertainty Calibration in Medical VQA
summary:
Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) applied to Medical Visual Question Answering (VQA) tend to produce overconfident outputs regardless of actual correctness, and existing verbalized confidence calibration methods, developed primarily for text only LLMs, do not account for the multimodal nature of medical image understanding.
  This work proposes a training based framework that finetunes MLLMs to improve their calibration using a composite loss function combining a Brier style calibration term, an anchor regularizer that prevents confidence collapse toward extreme values, a contrastive image text alignment term, and a KL based model stabilization term. The alignment signal is derived from a $2 \times 2$ factorial perturbation design that crosses image presence with text integrity, probing the reliance of the model on visual modality input versus language priors. Finally, a top K KL divergence regularizer is used to protect the answering ability of the model during finetuning.
  Across three Medical VQA benchmarks and two architectures (MedGemma 4B IT and Qwen2 VL 7B Instruct), our method reduces calibration error by 60% or more, and improves discrimination by 26% or more, while preserving predictive accuracy. On average across benchmarks, the technique outperforms prompting based, sampling based, and training based approaches, and ablation experiments confirm that each component of the loss function is indeed necessary for improving the calibration. All code for the experiments is publicly available.

AI.tldr: A training-based framework using composite loss functions (Brier calibration, anchor regularizer, contrastive alignment, KL stabilization) reduces calibration error by 60%+ and improves discrimination by 26%+ in Medical VQA MLLMs, outperforming existing methods across two architectures and three benchmarks.
AI.motivation: MLLMs applied to Medical VQA are overconfident regardless of actual correctness, and existing verbalized confidence calibration methods developed for text-only LLMs do not account for the multimodal nature of medical image understanding.
AI.method: A training-based framework finetuning MLLMs with a composite loss: Brier-style calibration term, anchor regularizer preventing confidence collapse, contrastive image-text alignment using a 2×2 factorial perturbation design, KL-based model stabilization, and a top-K KL divergence regularizer to protect answering ability.
AI.result: Reduces calibration error by 60%+ and improves discrimination by 26%+ across three Medical VQA benchmarks and two architectures (MedGemma 4B IT, Qwen2 VL 7B Instruct), while preserving predictive accuracy. Outperforms prompting-, sampling-, and training-based approaches.
AI.conclusion: Each component of the composite loss is necessary for improved calibration; the method effectively addresses multimodal overconfidence in Medical VQA and code is publicly available.

title: Unison: Benchmarking Unified Multimodal Models via Synergistic Understanding and Generation
summary: Unified multimodal models capable of both understanding and generation have achieved remarkable strides. However, despite their unified designs, existing evaluations typically assess understanding and generation capabilities in isolation, overlooking the synergy between comprehension and generation. To bridge this gap, we introduce Unison, a comprehensive benchmark comprising 2,169 high-quality unified task samples, designed to evaluate joint understanding and generation in unified multimodal models. Unison offers three key strengths: 1) Comprehensive Dimensions: Unison encompasses internal consistency, understanding-guided generation, generation-guided understanding, and mutual enhancement to enable holistic evaluation. 2) Diagnostic Evaluation: it provides both unified and decoupled tracks for understanding and generation, allowing fine-grained attribution of failure modes and quantitative analysis of the gains from unified modeling. 3) Human Alignment: we also introduce Unison-Judge, an evaluation model well aligned with human judgments to ensure reliable assessment. Based on systematic evaluations of state-of-the-art models on Unison, we uncover critical limitations in current unified multimodal systems and highlight promising directions for future research. Codes, Unison and Unison-Judge are publicly available at https://github.com/FudanCVL/Unison.

AI.tldr: Unison is a comprehensive benchmark with 2,169 samples for evaluating joint understanding and generation in unified multimodal models, featuring multi-dimensional assessment, diagnostic evaluation, and human-aligned judgment, revealing critical limitations in current models.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations assess understanding and generation in isolation, overlooking the synergy between them in unified multimodal models. Unison bridges this gap by providing a benchmark for joint evaluation.
AI.method: Unison includes 2,169 unified task samples covering internal consistency, understanding-guided generation, generation-guided understanding, and mutual enhancement. It provides unified and decoupled evaluation tracks for fine-grained attribution, and introduces Unison-Judge, a human-aligned evaluation model.
AI.result: Systematic evaluations of state-of-the-art models on Unison uncover critical limitations in current unified multimodal systems.
AI.conclusion: Highlighted promising directions for future research based on the identified limitations. Unison and Unison-Judge are publicly released.

title: A Process Harness for Uplifting Legacy Workflows to Agentic BPM: Design and Realization in CUGA FLO
summary: We introduce the process harness, a new mechanism for uplifting legacy workflows into Agentic Business Process Management (Agentic BPM) without replacing the underlying workflow engine. A process harness places a policy-governed agentic layer around a deterministic workflow engine, intercepting designated control points to contribute reasoning, adaptation, and oversight while the engine retains structural authority over the process. To define the process harness rigorously, we develop the Task-Decision-Flow (TDF) model, specifying both its data schema and its execution semantics. TDF decomposes LLM reasoning across three policy-governed agent types: a TaskAgent for knowledge-intensive task execution, a DecisionAgent for per-case gateway routing, and a FlowAgent that governs runtime flow adaptation through a principled hook mechanism. Each agent reasons within an explicit policy drawn from the process FRAME, the aggregate policy set governing all LLM calls in the system. We then present CUGA FLO as the design and implementation realization of the TDF model, and demonstrate it on a loan approval workflow that exercises all three agent types and hook-driven regulatory override. The process harness uniquely reconciles imperative requirements, realized through deterministic workflow execution that enforces structural compliance, with normative requirements, realized through policy-framed agentic autonomy invoked at designated control points wherever the process demands it.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the process harness, a mechanism that adds an agentic layer with LLM-powered agents (TaskAgent, DecisionAgent, FlowAgent) on top of deterministic workflow engines for Agentic BPM, formalized through the TDF model and implemented as CUGA FLO, demonstrated on a loan approval workflow.
AI.motivation: To uplift legacy deterministic workflows into Agentic BPM without replacing existing workflow engines, reconciling imperative (structural compliance) and normative (agentic autonomy) requirements.
AI.method: The Task-Decision-Flow (TDF) model with three policy-governed agent types (TaskAgent, DecisionAgent, FlowAgent), a hook mechanism for runtime flow adaptation, and the process FRAME policy set, implemented as CUGA FLO.
AI.result: Demonstrated on a loan approval workflow exercising all three agent types and hook-driven regulatory override.
AI.conclusion: The process harness uniquely reconciles imperative requirements (deterministic workflow execution enforcing structural compliance) with normative requirements (policy-framed agentic autonomy at designated control points).

title: Symplectic Neural Networks for learning Generalized Hamiltonians
summary: Hamiltonian Neural Networks (HNNs) integrate physical priors into neural models by learning a system's Hamiltonian, improving generalization and sample efficiency. Identifying the system Hamiltonian from noisy observations of state variables is a challenging task. For simulations to faithfully reflect the long-term behavior of Hamiltonian systems, especially energy conservation, it is essential to use symplectic integrators, which preserve the system's geometric structure. This fidelity comes at a cost: implicit symplectic integrators are more computationally intensive and make backpropagation through the ODE solver non-trivial. However, by leveraging the fact that symplectic discretizations of the adjoint system yield the same sensitivities associated by backpropagation, we obtain an efficient method of training the Neural Network parameters. In our work, we explore this alternate method of HNN training under noisy observation of trajectories with our HNN model based on an implicit symplectic integrator. Computationally, a predictor-corrector based ODE solver and fixed point iteration help to mitigate the computational cost of the implicit timestepping, resulting in more efficient generation of gradient updates. We showcase the numerical advantage, in experiments, in system identification and energy preservation on a range of non-separable, chaotic systems and the efficient computation and memory complexity of our method. We also observe that the post-processing of the learned Hamiltonian using backward error analysis yields a modified Hamiltonian that is a more accurate approximation of the true Hamiltonian without the need to use more accurate discretizations of the flow map.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an efficient method for training Hamiltonian Neural Networks (HNNs) using implicit symplectic integrators by exploiting adjoint system symplectic discretizations for backpropagation, with predictor-corrector and fixed-point iteration to reduce computational cost, achieving better energy preservation and system identification on chaotic and non-separable systems, and further improving accuracy via backward error analysis.
AI.motivation: Identifying the system Hamiltonian from noisy state observations is challenging. Simulating Hamiltonian systems faithfully requires symplectic integrators for energy conservation, but implicit symplectic integrators are computationally intensive and make backpropagation through ODE solvers non-trivial. The goal is to develop an efficient training method for HNNs under these constraints.
AI.method: The method leverages symplectic discretizations of the adjoint system to obtain the same sensitivities as backpropagation, enabling efficient gradient computation. A predictor-corrector ODE solver and fixed-point iteration are used to mitigate the computational cost of implicit timestepping. Additionally, backward error analysis post-processes the learned Hamiltonian to obtain a more accurate modified Hamiltonian.
AI.result: The method demonstrates numerical advantages in system identification and energy preservation on non-separable and chaotic systems, with improved computational and memory efficiency. The post-processing via backward error analysis yields a modified Hamiltonian that more accurately approximates the true Hamiltonian without requiring finer discretizations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach enables efficient training of HNNs with implicit symplectic integrators, preserving geometric structure and energy conservation while maintaining computational feasibility. Backward error analysis further refines the learned Hamiltonian, offering a practical path to accurate long-term simulation of Hamiltonian systems from noisy data.

title: Event-Aware Instructed Assistant for Referring Video Segmentation
summary: Existing referring video segmentation methods often treat a video as a single event consisting of multiple images, overlooking the fact that a video typically contains multiple distinct events. Under such a mechanism, the model needs to directly understand all the complex content in the video and text, which can easily lead to confusion and hallucinations. To address this issue, we propose to decompose a video to a set of simple events by learnable Event Query, and understand complex video content in an event-by-event, easy-to-understand manner. This is based on the observation that natural language expressions often divide a video into distinct, text-related segments, each representing a separate event within a compound event. We introduce EVIS, an Event-Aware Video Instructed Segmentation Assistant, which utilizes text-guided Event Queries to partition a video into simple events, extracting event-aware visual-text features to achieve a hierarchical understanding of the video. Additionally, we propose Object-Pixel-Hybrid Learning, which enables the MLLMs to track targets in long-term videos by integrating fine-grained pixel features with prior object queries. Extensive experimental results on 5 public benchmarks demonstrate EVIS's strong performance in addressing the referring video segmentation task.

AI.tldr: EVIS decomposes complex videos into simple events using learnable Event Queries for hierarchical understanding, combined with Object-Pixel-Hybrid Learning for long-term tracking, achieving strong performance on referring video segmentation.
AI.motivation: Existing referring video segmentation methods treat videos as single events with multiple images, overlooking that videos typically contain multiple distinct events, leading to confusion and hallucinations when models must directly understand all complex video and text content.
AI.method: 1) Decompose videos into simple events using learnable, text-guided Event Queries; 2) Extract event-aware visual-text features for hierarchical video understanding; 3) Object-Pixel-Hybrid Learning that integrates fine-grained pixel features with prior object queries to enable MLLMs to track targets in long-term videos.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on 5 public benchmarks demonstrate EVIS's strong performance on the referring video segmentation task.
AI.conclusion: Decomposing complex video content into event-by-event understanding via Event Queries, combined with object-pixel hybrid learning, effectively addresses referring video segmentation and reduces confusion/hallucination.

title: Vulnerability of Natural Language Classifiers to Evolutionary Generated Adversarial Text
summary: Deep learning models have achieved impressive performance across various fields but remain vulnerable to adversarial inputs, particularly in NLP, where such attacks can have significant real-world consequences. Adversarial attacks often involve small, semantically similar token replacements to fool NLP models, and recent methods have become more precise by targeting specific vulnerable words, often by exploiting some level of access to the model's internal structure. This paper proposes GAversary, a hybrid Genetic Algorithm (GA) to generate adversarial attacks on natural language models. The GA is able to treat the target model as a black box, requiring only the logit value output by the model to guide the search. GAversary differs from GAs previously proposed for this problem by using GloVe embeddings to propose word replacements (the mutation operator) to improve the semantic similarity of the adversarial examples. GAversary is applied to several benchmark data sets and well-known target models. GAversary is able to substantially reduce the target model's accuracy on test data compared to the BAE and A2T attacks compared against (in the best case, reducing a 76.8% accuracy to 5.8%, compared to BAE's 27.6%). The trade-off is that GAversary perturbs just under twice as many words as the other two methods, with a slightly lower semantic similarity to the original text and around a 5% increase in run-time.

AI.tldr: GAversary uses a hybrid Genetic Algorithm with GloVe embeddings to generate black-box adversarial attacks on NLP models, substantially reducing accuracy (e.g., from 76.8% to 5.8%) at the cost of more word perturbations and slightly lower semantic similarity compared to BAE and A2T.
AI.motivation: Deep learning NLP models are vulnerable to adversarial inputs with real-world consequences; existing attacks often require model internals or produce semantically dissimilar examples, motivating a black-box method that generates more semantically similar adversarial examples.
AI.method: GAversary is a hybrid Genetic Algorithm that treats the target model as a black box, using only logit outputs to guide the search, and employs GloVe embeddings in the mutation operator to propose semantically similar word replacements.
AI.result: GAversary substantially reduces target model accuracy (e.g., from 76.8% to 5.8%) compared to BAE (27.6%) and A2T, but perturbs nearly twice as many words, has slightly lower semantic similarity, and incurs about 5% higher run-time.
AI.conclusion: GAversary demonstrates superior attack effectiveness (accuracy reduction) against black-box NLP models compared to baselines, with trade-offs in perturbation rate, semantic similarity, and computational cost.

title: State Representation Matters in Deep Reinforcement Learning: Application to Energy Trading
summary: Energy trading decisions depend not only on current market prices, but also on expected future market conditions, and operational constraints. This makes the state representation given to a reinforcement learning agent an important design choice. We study this in HydroDam, a pumped-storage arbitrage environment, using a fixed Double DQN agent. The environment, action space, reward function, network, and training protocol are kept fixed; only the market features are changed. We compare absolute price/calendar features, relative features that compare current prices with recent market history, forecast features, and all combinations of these three feature families. Policies are trained and selected using 2007--2011 Belgian day-ahead prices and evaluated on two test settings: a later same-market test set from 2012--2025 and 39 other ENTSO-E market zones. Absolute features only reaches 28.8% on the test set and a median 5.7% across zones. Relative-only and forecast-only states also stay below a rolling price-score heuristic in the cross-zone median. Combining feature families is much stronger: absolute + relative reaches 49.9% on the test set and a 39.8% cross-zone median, while absolute + relative + forecast reaches 55.6% and 47.5%. These results suggest that state representation is not a minor preprocessing choice in storage-trading RL, but a central part of the policy design: robust transfer requires combining price scale, recent relative price context, and short-horizon forecast information, rather than relying on any single feature family.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how state representation design (absolute price features, relative features, forecast features) affects the performance and transferability of a Double DQN agent in a pumped-storage hydro arbitrage environment (HydroDam). Combining all three feature families yields the best results: 55.6% on the test set and 47.5% cross-zone median, while single feature families perform poorly, highlighting state representation as a central design choice in storage-trading RL.
AI.motivation: Energy trading decisions depend on current prices, expected future conditions, and operational constraints, making state representation a critical design choice for reinforcement learning agents. The authors aim to systematically study how different feature families affect agent performance and cross-market transfer in pumped-storage arbitrage.
AI.method: A fixed Double DQN agent is trained in the HydroDam pumped-storage arbitrage environment with controlled experimental setup: environment, action space, reward function, network, and training protocol are kept constant. Only market features are varied across three families: absolute price/calendar features, relative features (comparing current prices to recent history), and forecast features, plus all combinations. Training uses 2007-2011 Belgian day-ahead prices; testing is done on 2012-2025 same-market data and 39 other ENTSO-E zones.
AI.result: Absolute features only: 28.8% test set, 5.7% cross-zone median. Relative-only and forecast-only: below rolling price-score heuristic in cross-zone median. Absolute+relative: 49.9% test set, 39.8% cross-zone median. Absolute+relative+forecast: 55.6% test set, 47.5% cross-zone median. Combining families significantly outperforms single families.
AI.conclusion: State representation is not a minor preprocessing choice but a central part of policy design in storage-trading RL. Robust transfer across markets requires combining price scale (absolute), recent relative price context, and short-horizon forecast information rather than relying on any single feature family.

title: On-board Remote-Sensing Foundation Models for Unsupervised Change Detection of Disaster Events
summary: Remote Sensing Foundation Models (RSFMs) have emerged as a powerful alternative to supervised models for Earth Observation, allowing satellites to autonomously trigger high-resolution captures or adjust tasking parameters upon detecting an anomaly, thereby maximizing the utility of the mission's limited power and computational resources. RSFMs are versatile, unified encoders that optimize onboard storage for multiple orbital applications while ensuring high-fidelity feature extraction. In particular, unsupervised change detection with RSFMs offers a well-informed and transformative path for disaster monitoring without expensive labels. In this paper, we present a novel unsupervised detection method based on ResNet (RSFM) + FPN which identifies a wide spectrum of anomalies by detecting subtle semantic shifts in the latent space between successive orbital passes. By relying on an untrained FPN architecture and its intrinsic priors, the system achieves efficient image-level generation and higher resolution mapping with minimal effort (training-free) compared to previous proposals (patch-based, trained). And by replacing tailored models with RSFMs, we can achieve comparable results through an approach that eliminates the need for bespoke training and extensive development effort and adds customization, while ensuring high-performance generalization across diverse terrains and sensors.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a training-free unsupervised change detection method using RSFM (ResNet) + FPN for remote sensing anomaly detection, enabling efficient onboard satellite processing without labels and outperforming patch-based trained approaches.
AI.motivation: To develop a training-free unsupervised change detection framework for remote sensing that eliminates the need for expensive labels and bespoke training while maintaining high-performance generalization across diverse terrains and sensors, enabling efficient onboard satellite anomaly detection.
AI.method: The method uses a ResNet-based RSFM encoder with an untrained FPN architecture to detect semantic shifts in the latent space between successive orbital passes, enabling image-level generation and higher-resolution mapping without training.
AI.result: The approach achieves comparable or better results than previous patch-based, trained methods while being training-free, requires minimal effort, and generalizes well across diverse terrains and sensors.
AI.conclusion: RSFM + FPN provides a transformative, efficient path for onboard unsupervised change detection for disaster monitoring, maximizing limited satellite resources without requiring supervised labels or extensive development.

title: Ask, Don't Judge: Binary Questions for Interpretable LLM Evaluation and Self-Improvement
comment: Acceepted to the Second Workshop on Compositional Learning at ICML 2026, Seoul, South Korea
summary: Evaluating LLM outputs remains a major bottleneck in NLP: human evaluation is expensive and slow, lexical metrics correlate poorly with human judgments on open-ended generation, and holistic LLM judges often produce opaque scores that are hard to debug. We propose BINEVAL, a framework that decomposes evaluation criteria into atomic binary questions and aggregates the resulting verdicts into interpretable, multi-dimensional scores. Given a task prompt, a meta-prompt generates fine-grained evaluation questions, and an LLM answers them independently for each output, yielding transparent question-level feedback together with calibrated overall scores. This decomposition makes evaluation easier to inspect, easier to diagnose, and directly usable for prompt improvement. Across SummEval, Topical-Chat, and QAGS, BINEVAL matches or outperforms strong baselines including UniEval and G-Eval, with especially strong results on factual consistency benchmarks such as QAGS. Beyond competitive correlation with human judgments, BINEVAL better matches human score distributions and avoids the ceiling effects common in prior LLM judges, leading to better discrimination between borderline and clearly flawed outputs. We further show that the same question-level feedback supports iterative prompt optimization, improving evaluator prompts on summarization and generation prompts on IFBench under both self-update and cross-model update settings. Overall, BINEVAL provides a task-agnostic, training-free, and interpretable evaluation framework that combines strong empirical performance with practical diagnostic and optimization value.

AI.tldr: BINEVAL is an evaluation framework that decomposes LLM output assessment into atomic binary questions, producing interpretable multi-dimensional scores. It matches or outperforms strong baselines (UniEval, G-Eval) on SummEval, Topical-Chat, and QAGS, correlates well with human judgments, avoids ceiling effects, and supports iterative prompt optimization across both self-update and cross-model settings.
AI.motivation: Evaluating LLM outputs is a major NLP bottleneck: human evaluation is expensive and slow, lexical metrics correlate poorly with human judgments on open-ended generation, and holistic LLM judges produce opaque, hard-to-debug scores.
AI.method: BINEVAL decomposes evaluation criteria into atomic binary questions via a meta-prompt, then aggregates LLM verdicts into interpretable multi-dimensional scores, providing transparent question-level feedback and calibrated overall scores.
AI.result: BINEVAL matches or outperforms strong baselines (UniEval, G-Eval) on SummEval, Topical-Chat, and QAGS, excels on factual consistency (QAGS), better matches human score distributions, avoids ceiling effects, and supports iterative prompt optimization in self-update and cross-model settings.
AI.conclusion: BINEVAL is a task-agnostic, training-free, interpretable evaluation framework combining strong empirical performance with practical diagnostic and optimization value.

title: Finding Stationary Points by Comparisons
comment: 41 pages, 4 figures. To appear in the Forty-Third International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: We study the problem of finding stationary points of non-convex functions when access to the objective is provided only through a comparison oracle that, given two points, outputs which has the larger function value. For a twice differentiable $f\colon\mathbb R^n\to\mathbb R$ with Lipschitz gradient and Hessian, we develop an algorithm that visits an $ε$-stationary point using $\widetilde O(n^2/ε^{1.5})$ queries. Our approach uses a subroutine that estimates the normalized Hessian to accuracy $δ$ using $\widetilde O(n^2\log(1/δ))$ queries. We further study this problem with a quantum comparison oracle model where queries can be made in superpositions, and develop the first quantum algorithm that finds an $ε$-stationary point, which takes $\widetilde O(n/ε^{1.5})$ queries.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a comparison oracle-based algorithm for finding ε-stationary points of smooth non-convex functions, achieving $\widetilde O(n^2/ε^{1.5})$ queries classically and $\widetilde O(n/ε^{1.5})$ queries quantumly.
AI.motivation: To solve non-convex optimization problems where only comparison (order) queries are available, not gradient or function value queries, motivated by settings like human feedback, noisy measurements, or derivative-free optimization.
AI.method: The algorithm uses a subroutine that estimates the normalized Hessian to accuracy δ with $\widetilde O(n^2\log(1/δ))$ comparisons, then leverages this to navigate to an ε-stationary point. A quantum variant accelerates this using superposition queries.
AI.result: Classical algorithm: $\widetilde O(n^2/ε^{1.5})$ comparison queries. Quantum algorithm: $\widetilde O(n/ε^{1.5})$ comparison queries — a quadratic improvement in dimension dependence.
AI.conclusion: Comparison oracle-based optimization is feasible for smooth non-convex problems with rigorous query complexity bounds, and quantum computing offers provable speedups in this setting.

title: PanoImager: Geometry-Guided Novel View Synthesis and Reconstruction from Sparse Panoramic Views
summary: Panoramic sensing offers wide field-of-view coverage, yet 3D reconstruction from sparse panoramas remains challenging under rotation-dominant, weak-parallax motion. In such regimes, SfM/SLAM initialization is often ill-conditioned and unreliable. We present PanoImager, an SfM-free framework that combines feed-forward pose/depth priors, geometry-conditioned diffusion view completion, and depth-guided 3DGS optimization. Given only a few panoramic images, PanoImager decomposes them into local perspective views, synthesizes auxiliary observations to enrich sparse evidence, and stabilizes Gaussian optimization for improved cross-view consistency. Experiments on multiple benchmarks show improved stability under extreme sparsity, suggesting PanoImager as an offline/background component for map refinement when SfM/SLAM fails to initialize.

AI.tldr: PanoImager is an SfM-free framework for 3D reconstruction from sparse panoramic images under rotation-dominant, weak-parallax motion. It combines feed-forward pose/depth priors, diffusion-based view completion, and depth-guided 3DGS optimization to handle ill-conditioned initialization where SfM/SLAM fails.
AI.motivation: Panoramic sensing provides wide FOV, but 3D reconstruction from sparse panoramas under rotation-dominant, weak-parallax motion is challenging because SfM/SLAM initialization becomes ill-conditioned and unreliable.
AI.method: PanoImager decomposes panoramic images into local perspective views, uses feed-forward pose/depth priors, applies geometry-conditioned diffusion view completion to synthesize auxiliary observations, and performs depth-guided 3DGS optimization for cross-view consistency.
AI.result: Improved stability under extreme sparsity on multiple benchmarks, outperforming SfM/SLAM-based approaches when initialization fails.
AI.conclusion: PanoImager serves as an offline/background component for map refinement when SfM/SLAM fails to initialize under challenging rotation-dominant, weak-parallax conditions.

title: EO-WM: A Physically Informed World Model for Probabilistic Earth Observation Forecasting
summary: Earth Observation (EO) forecasting aims to predict future Earth surface dynamics from satellite observations under changing meteorological conditions.
