It examines twelve application domains, identifying graph-construction costs, dominant architectures, and separating genuine gains from weak-baseline artifacts. Cross-domain patterns reveal that heterophily, scale, and temporal dynamics remain open challenges, and leaderboard-topping architectures rarely reach deployment.
AI.motivation: To move beyond asking whether message passing helps on a given dataset and instead determine where graph structure earns its computational cost versus where it does not, by providing a structured, cross-domain analysis of GNN design choices and their practical trade-offs.
AI.method: Organizes the GNN field around a single design space, derives spectral and spatial formulations from shared first principles, connects expressive power to the Weisfeiler-Leman hierarchy, and conducts a cross-domain comparison across twelve application domains with explicit cost-benefit analysis.
AI.result: Identifies recurring patterns: heterophily and scale undermine the same models across domains; temporal graphs are consistently harder than static ones; leaderboard-topping architectures seldom reach deployment. Also surfaces that over-smoothing, over-squashing, robustness, distribution shift, fairness, and explainability are practical adoption constraints rather than closing checklist items.
AI.conclusion: The field should focus on the constraints that decide real-world adoption (heterophily, scale, temporal dynamics, robustness, fairness) rather than incremental leaderboard improvements, as architectures that excel in benchmarks often fail in deployment.

title: Sculpting NeRF Geometry: Human-Preference Fine-Tuning of a 3D-Aware Face GAN
summary: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) for 3D generation is now established across a number of works, but most existing pipelines optimise explicit surface representations, often by converting radiance fields into meshes and training heavily on surface-supervised data. We instead fine-tune a pretrained 3D-aware generative model directly from a learned reward over radiance-field density ($σ$) values, with no externally supplied mesh or shape prior. The reward model requires no pretraining, trains easily on a small set of preference samples, and yields robust improvement in 3D geometry. Working on an unconditional 3D-aware face GAN (EG3D), our reward reads the continuous 3D density field of the neural radiance field (NeRF) directly and supplies a geometry-only learning signal, requiring neither text conditioning, mesh extraction, nor multi-view rendering. A density-consistency constraint keeps the 2D appearance qualitatively similar while the geometry is reshaped, at a measurable but bounded distributional cost (FID-50k rises from 4.09 to 6.66): the fine-tuned generator, trained from the preferences of a single annotator as a proof of concept, produces face geometries preferred by users in 74.4% of pairwise comparisons.

AI.tldr: This paper fine-tunes a 3D-aware face GAN (EG3D) using RLHF directly on radiance-field density (σ) values, without meshes, text conditioning, or multi-view rendering. A reward model trained on a small set of preference samples improves 3D geometry, while a density-consistency constraint preserves 2D appearance. The fine-tuned generator produces preferred face geometries in 74.4% of pairwise comparisons, with a mild FID cost (4.09→6.66).
AI.motivation: Existing RLHF for 3D generation relies on explicit surface representations (meshes) and heavy surface-supervised data. The authors aim to directly optimize geometry in the radiance field space without external shape priors or mesh extraction.
AI.method: Fine-tune a pretrained 3D-aware GAN (EG3D) via a reward model trained on density (σ) values from the NeRF. A density-consistency constraint maintains 2D appearance. The reward model requires no pretraining and trains on a small set of preference samples.
AI.result: 74.4% of users prefer the fine-tuned face geometries in pairwise comparisons. FID-50k increases from 4.09 to 6.66, indicating a bounded distributional cost. A single annotator's preferences suffice as proof of concept.
AI.conclusion: Directly optimizing radiance-field density with RLHF is a viable alternative to mesh-based pipelines for improving 3D geometry, achieving user preference with minimal supervision and no external shape priors.

title: The Geometry of Updates: Fisher Alignment at Vocabulary Scale
comment: Accepted at the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026), PMLR 306. 64 pages total (main paper plus appendix), 4 figures, 29 tables
summary: Training-free source selection for LLM families with shared vocabularies arises in scientific string domains such as SMILES, protein, and genomic sequences, where candidate corpora share a tokenizer but differ in prediction targets. This creates an activation-dark regime: representation-similarity metrics can be uninformative without assumptions about label-conditioned error geometry, while classical update-geometry metrics are computationally prohibitive at vocabulary scale. We show that, in a shared-output head setting, representation metrics (e.g., CKA) are non-identifiable for transfer; models can share identical representations yet have orthogonal head updates. The key identity is that head Fisher alignment is exactly a cosine between kernel mean embeddings in the joint activation-error space, exposing activation, error, and coupling factors rather than requiring a materialized Fisher matrix. FisherSketch estimates this cosine directly in a single streaming pass, making K=128,256 head Fisher alignment practical with a 16 KB task signature (m=4096) and a 192 KB per-task streaming state, small enough to store next to a model hash, but encoding transfer-relevant update structure. Beyond source selection, the same signatures and marginals provide a diagnostic instrument for studying whether LLM task similarity is driven by activations, errors, or their coupling; shared-parameter and internal-layer validations, together with Llama-3.1-8B verbalizer-shift experiments, show that FisherSketch remains informative when activation similarity cannot distinguish tasks.

AI.tldr: FisherSketch enables efficient, training-free source selection for LLM families with shared vocabularies by estimating head Fisher alignment via a cosine between kernel mean embeddings in joint activation-error space, using only a streaming pass and minimal memory footprint, overcoming the non-identifiability of representation metrics like CKA.
AI.motivation: Training-free source selection for LLM families with shared vocabularies (e.g., SMILES, protein, genomic sequences) is needed where candidate corpora share a tokenizer but differ in prediction targets. Representation-similarity metrics like CKA are uninformative without assumptions about label-conditioned error geometry, while classical update-geometry metrics are computationally prohibitive at vocabulary scale.
AI.method: The paper identifies that head Fisher alignment equals a cosine between kernel mean embeddings in the joint activation-error space, decomposing into activation, error, and coupling factors. FisherSketch estimates this cosine via a single streaming pass with a 16 KB task signature (m=4096) and 192 KB per-task streaming state, avoiding materialization of the full Fisher matrix.
AI.result: FisherSketch makes K=128,256 head Fisher alignment practical with minimal memory footprint. Shared-parameter and internal-layer validations, plus Llama-3.1-8B verbalizer-shift experiments, demonstrate that FisherSketch remains informative when activation similarity cannot distinguish tasks.
AI.conclusion: Beyond source selection, FisherSketch's signatures and marginals serve as a diagnostic instrument for studying whether LLM task similarity is driven by activations, errors, or their coupling, proving effective even when representation-based metrics fail.

title: See & Sniff: Learning Visuo-Olfactory Representations
summary: While modern multimodal models integrate vision with language, audio, or touch, olfaction remains largely unexplored due to the lack of paired visuo-olfactory data. We introduce SmellNet-V, a scalable visuo-olfactory dataset built on the insight that odor identity is largely invariant to visual transformations within a semantic category. This allows us to synthetically pair smell-only samples with semantically aligned in-the-wild web images, converting a unimodal olfactory dataset into a cross-modal benchmark without costly co-collection. Building on this dataset, we propose See & Sniff, a self-supervised framework that learns joint visuo-olfactory representations via dense local alignment and naturally produces smell saliency maps for spatial grounding of odor sources. We further introduce pixel-level smell localization task and a benchmark for evaluation. Our method surpasses smell-only baselines by 7% in smell classification from smell alone and generalizes to cross-modal retrieval and smell localization, establishing visuo-olfactory learning as a new direction in multimodal perception.

AI.tldr: SmellNet-V is a scalable visuo-olfactory dataset created by pairing smell-only samples with semantically aligned web images. See & Sniff is a self-supervised framework learning joint visuo-olfactory representations via dense local alignment, producing smell saliency maps for spatial grounding. It surpasses smell-only baselines by 7% and enables cross-modal retrieval and pixel-level smell localization.
AI.motivation: Olfaction remains largely unexplored in multimodal AI due to the lack of paired visuo-olfactory data. Existing multimodal models integrate vision with language, audio, or touch, but no scalable visuo-olfactory benchmark exists.
AI.method: SmellNet-V dataset: leverages odor identity invariance within semantic categories to synthetically pair smell-only samples with semantically aligned web images. See & Sniff framework: self-supervised learning with dense local alignment for joint visuo-olfactory representations, naturally producing smell saliency maps.
AI.result: See & Sniff surpasses smell-only baselines by 7% in smell classification, and generalizes to cross-modal retrieval and pixel-level smell localization.
AI.conclusion: Visuo-olfactory learning is established as a new viable direction in multimodal perception, with scalable dataset construction and effective cross-modal representation learning.

title: Effective Covariance Dynamics in Solvable High-Dimensional GANs
summary: We study a solvable high-dimensional model of generative adversarial network (GAN) training in which a linear generator learns a low-dimensional subspace from data with structured latent covariance. Prior solvable GAN analyses assume unconditional signals with diagonal latent covariance; we extend the multi-feature discriminator setting to class-dependent, correlated, and non-zero-mean latent structure. For the quadratic energy discriminator, all such heterogeneity enters the dynamics through a probability-weighted effective second moment. We prove that the stochastic microscopic training process converges, in the high-dimensional limit, to deterministic ordinary differential equations governed by this effective covariance. In the matched-covariance specialization, the stability analysis yields a mode-wise solvable interval determined by the learning rates and noise level: learning begins when the leading effective eigenvalue crosses the lower threshold, while full recovery requires all relevant effective modes to remain within the interval. This reveals a signal-boosting mechanism: low-rank correlations can lift weak directions above the learnability threshold, whereas overly strong correlations destabilize recovery. Numerical simulations validate the ODE, phase boundary, and boosting mechanism. Experiments on MNIST, FashionMNIST, and CIFAR-10 further show that informed generator covariance improves alignment with the data-driven reference subspace.

AI.tldr: A solvable high-dimensional GAN model with linear generator and quadratic discriminator is analyzed, showing that heterogeneous latent structure (class-dependent, correlated, non-zero-mean) enters dynamics via a probability-weighted effective second moment. The training converges to deterministic ODEs governed by this effective covariance. Stability reveals a mode-wise learning interval: learning begins when the leading effective eigenvalue crosses a lower threshold, and full recovery requires all relevant modes to stay within bounds. Low-rank correlations boost weak signals above learnability, while overly strong correlations destabilize. Numerical simulations and experiments (MNIST, FashionMNIST, CIFAR-10) validate the theory and show that informed generator covariance improves subspace alignment.
AI.motivation: Prior solvable GAN analyses assume unconditional signals with diagonal latent covariance; this work extends to class-dependent, correlated, and non-zero-mean latent structure, capturing more realistic data distributions.
AI.method: A high-dimensional linear GAN model with quadratic energy discriminator is studied, where the generator learns a low-dimensional subspace. The analysis derives the probability-weighted effective second moment governing dynamics, proves convergence of microscopic training to deterministic ODEs in the high-dimensional limit, and performs stability analysis in the matched-covariance specialization.
AI.result: Learning begins when the leading effective eigenvalue crosses a lower threshold; full recovery requires all relevant effective modes to remain within a stability interval. Low-rank correlations can boost weak signals above the learnability threshold, while overly strong correlations destabilize recovery.
AI.conclusion: The effective covariance framework captures heterogeneous latent structure in GAN dynamics. Signal-boosting via low-rank correlations improves learnability, validated by numerical simulations and real-data experiments showing that informed generator covariance enhances subspace alignment.

title: ViQ: Text-Aligned Visual Quantized Representations at Any Resolution
summary: A unified representation for text and vision is a natural pursuit, as it enables simpler multimodal modeling and more efficient training. However, representing images as discrete signals in the same way as text inevitably introduces severe information loss. Existing work struggles to balance low-level details and high-level semantics in discrete representations: reconstruction-oriented representations often lack semantic information, whereas semantically stronger features typically suffer from severe loss of detail. We present ViQ, a Visual Quantized Representations framework, which is designed to balance semantics and details in discrete representations while supporting inputs at native resolutions, thereby enabling it to serve as a unified and general discrete representation for arbitrary visual inputs. Our approach structures quantization learning into two stages: text-aligned pre-training and feature discretization. With text-aligned pre-training, we enhance the visual encoder semantic-rich supervision from the pretrained language model and enable it to process native-resolution visual inputs. During discretization, we propose a proximal representation learning strategy to progressively compact the feature space, along with a position-aware head-wise quantization mechanism that enables flexible processing of arbitrary resolutions. Extensive experiments on multimodal tasks demonstrate that ViQ achieves competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art multimodal vision encoders with continuous and high-dimensional visual features, while maintaining high precision in low-level reconstruction. We also show that multimodal training with visual quantized representations largely improves efficiency, yielding up to 20\%-70\% acceleration with different base LLMs and training recipes.

AI.tldr: ViQ is a visual quantized representation framework that balances semantics and details in discrete visual representations, supporting native-resolution inputs and achieving competitive performance with continuous features while accelerating multimodal training by 20-70%.
AI.motivation: Existing discrete visual representations struggle to balance low-level details and high-level semantics: reconstruction-oriented representations lack semantic information, while semantically strong features suffer from detail loss. A unified text-vision discrete representation is desired but challenging due to information loss from discretization.
AI.method: ViQ structures quantization learning into two stages: (1) text-aligned pre-training that enhances visual encoder with semantic supervision from a pretrained language model and enables native-resolution processing; (2) feature discretization using proximal representation learning to progressively compact feature space and position-aware head-wise quantization for flexible arbitrary resolution handling.
AI.result: ViQ achieves competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art continuous multimodal vision encoders while maintaining high reconstruction precision. Multimodal training with ViQ yields 20%-70% acceleration across different base LLMs and training recipes.
AI.conclusion: Discrete visual representations can effectively balance semantics and details through a two-stage quantization approach with text-aligned pre-training and proximal representation learning, enabling efficient multimodal training without sacrificing performance.

title: RSPC: A Benchmark for Modeling Stress and Psychiatric Conditions in Digitally Mediated Relationships using Psychiatrist Annotations
summary: In NLP, mental health conditions are often modeled as isolated phenomena, without interpersonal context. We use Reddit posts about long-distance relationships to capture both mental health distress and associated relational triggers. We introduce the Relational Stress and Psychiatry Corpus (RSPC) containing 1,799 Reddit posts annotated by psychiatrists for diagnostic categories, including the most prevalent mood disorders (anxiety and depression), relational stressor triggers, and indications of relationship phase. We benchmark seven fine-tuned transformer models and five large language models across multi-label disorder classification, relational trigger detection, and temporal phase prediction tasks. We find clear task-dependent differences between model families, with Claude-3-Haiku achieving the best disorder classification performance (Macro-F1 = 0.538) and GPT-4o obtaining the strongest relational trigger detection performance (Macro-F1 = 0.519), suggesting distinct model capabilities. We further find strong associations between anxiety disorders and chronic relational uncertainty. Overall, RSPC establishes a benchmark for NLP tasks that consider relational context and supports a shift from individual-centric to context-aware mental health modeling that captures the social and temporal dynamics of distress.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Relational Stress and Psychiatry Corpus (RSPC), a dataset of 1,799 Reddit posts about long-distance relationships annotated by psychiatrists for mental health conditions (anxiety, depression), relational stressor triggers, and relationship phases. Experiments with fine-tuned transformers and LLMs show task-dependent performance differences (Claude-3-Haiku best for disorder classification, GPT-4o best for trigger detection), and reveal strong links between anxiety disorders and chronic relational uncertainty.
AI.motivation: Mental health in NLP is typically modeled without interpersonal context. The authors aim to capture both mental health distress and relational triggers by analyzing Reddit posts about long-distance relationships, supporting a shift from individual-centric to context-aware mental health modeling.
AI.method: The authors constructed RSPC with 1,799 Reddit posts annotated by psychiatrists for diagnostic categories (mood disorders), relational stressor triggers, and relationship phase. They benchmarked seven fine-tuned transformer models and five large language models on multi-label disorder classification, relational trigger detection, and temporal phase prediction tasks.
AI.result: Claude-3-Haiku achieved the best disorder classification performance (Macro-F1 = 0.538), GPT-4o obtained the strongest relational trigger detection (Macro-F1 = 0.519), and strong associations were found between anxiety disorders and chronic relational uncertainty. Clear task-dependent differences between model families were observed.
AI.conclusion: RSPC establishes a benchmark for NLP tasks incorporating relational context, supporting a shift from individual-centric to context-aware mental health modeling that captures social and temporal dynamics of distress.

title: OctoSense: Self-Supervised Learning for Multimodal Robot Perception
summary: We present OctoSense, an open-source sensor platform with stereo RGB and event cameras, LiDAR, a thermal camera, an inertial measurement unit, RTK-corrected global positioning system, and proprioception (CAN bus data from a car, and joint angles for a quadruped robot). The eponymous OctoSense dataset contains 59 hours of time-synchronized driving data across different types of environments at different times of the day, including situations with highly degraded sensors. We demonstrate multi-modal self-supervised learning using such real-world robotics data, where sensors have different representations, frequencies, latencies and noise. Our approach, a "late-fusion" masked autoencoder, (i) uses modality-specific tokenizers to account for different spatiotemporal characteristics of these sensors, and (ii) caches modality-specific tokens at inference time to process new measurements as they come. This architecture (i) is fast (6.68 ms and 112 ms on NVIDIA 5090 and Orin NX respectively, to compute the representation), (ii) performs better than existing image-only foundation models on tasks such as estimation of optical flow, depth, semantic segmentation, and ego-motion (translation, rotation, and steering angle), and (iii) predicts robustly at nighttime or in situations where sensory data is degraded. See our project page for links to the dataset, code, and supplementary videos: https://abisulco.com/octosense/.

AI.tldr: OctoSense is an open-source multi-modal sensor platform (stereo RGB/event cameras, LiDAR, thermal camera, IMU, RTK-GPS, CAN bus) with a 59-hour driving dataset, paired with a late-fusion masked autoencoder that efficiently processes heterogeneous sensor data and outperforms image-only foundation models on vision tasks, especially under degraded conditions.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of diverse, time-synchronized multi-modal autonomous driving datasets with real-world sensor degradation, and to develop a self-supervised learning method that can effectively fuse heterogeneous sensor modalities with different spatiotemporal characteristics, frequencies, latencies, and noise levels.
AI.method: A late-fusion masked autoencoder architecture with modality-specific tokenizers that account for different spatiotemporal characteristics of sensors (stereo RGB, event cameras, LiDAR, thermal, IMU, GPS, CAN bus), featuring a caching mechanism for asynchronous inference. Trained via self-supervised learning on real-world robotics data.
AI.result: The approach achieves fast inference (6.68 ms on NVIDIA 5090, 112 ms on Orin NX), outperforms image-only foundation models on optical flow, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, and ego-motion estimation (translation, rotation, steering angle), and maintains robust predictions at nighttime and under sensor degradation.
AI.conclusion: Multi-modal late-fusion with modality-specific tokenization and inference-time caching enables efficient, robust, and high-performance representation learning from heterogeneous sensor streams, particularly valuable in degraded sensing conditions.

title: BetXplain: An Explanation-Annotated Dataset for Detecting Manipulative Betting Advertisements on Social Media
summary: The promotion of betting applications on social media platforms has increased significantly in recent years. Many of these advertisements use persuasive techniques that may mislead users, encourage risky behavior, and potentially influence users' mental well-being. However, research on the automated detection of manipulative and deceptive betting advertisements remains limited due to the lack of publicly available annotated datasets. In this work, we introduce a new dataset of betting-related advertisements collected from two widely used social media platforms, Instagram and Reddit. The advertisements were manually annotated for manipulative and deceptive advertising practices. In addition to classification labels, the dataset includes human-provided explanations that describe the reasoning behind each annotation, enabling research into explainable approaches to detecting manipulative advertising. Furthermore, we analyze the strategies commonly used in betting advertisements and examine how these persuasive tactics may impact users' mental health. The proposed framework can also enable practical applications such as browser plugins that warn users about manipulative betting advertisements and automated web crawlers that help regulatory authorities monitor and detect such promotions online.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a new annotated dataset of betting advertisements from Instagram and Reddit, including human explanations for manipulative and deceptive practices, and analyzes persuasive strategies that may impact mental health, withapplications for browser plugins and regulatory monitoring.
AI.motivation: Research on automated detection of manipulative betting ads is limited due to the lack of publicly available annotated datasets, despite the rise of such ads on social media and their potential to mislead users and harm mental well-being.
AI.method: Collected betting ads from Instagram and Reddit; manually annotated for manipulative/deceptive practices with human-provided explanations; analyzed persuasive strategies and mental health impacts.
AI.result: A new publicly available annotated dataset of betting ads with classification labels and human explanations; identification of common persuasive strategies in betting advertisements.
AI.conclusion: The dataset and framework enable explainable detection of manipulative betting ads, supporting applications like browser warnings and automated regulatory monitoring.

title: Not All Actions Are Equal: Rethinking Conditioning for Dexterous World Model
summary: Recent advances in action-conditioned world models show promising progress in modeling complex interactions and forecasting future states under diverse action sequences. While these models are often driven by stronger visual representations and model capacity, action conditioning itself remains underexplored. Most existing approaches compress the entire action sequence into a single representation, which works well for low-DoF control but becomes less reliable in high-DoF scenarios. We observe that high-DoF dexterous actions are inherently heterogeneous, spanning multiple orders of magnitude, where large-scale motions coexist with subtle but important signals. When uniformly aggregated, optimization exhibits an imbalance across action components, which hinders the modeling of fine-grained effects and affects action fidelity. We therefore propose DexAC-WM, which treats action conditioning as a structured process rather than global compression. DexAC preserves dimension-level semantics via action tokenization and aligns action signals with visual dynamics through local refinement and global modulation. To address the limited high-level semantic grounding in existing world models, we further introduce a semantic branch that provides rich object-scene priors, which enables world model to capture dynamic visual details while supporting high-DoF action-conditioned video prediction. Experiments on EgoDex and EgoVerse show that combining the semantic branch with DexAC significantly improves FID, FVD, and PCK, demonstrating gains in visual-temporal realism and action-following consistency. We further verify that DexAC extends to other backbones, showing the scalability of our structured action-conditioning design. These results suggest that scaling world models to high-DoF control requires both structured action modeling and semantic grounding.

AI.tldr: DexAC-WM proposes structured action conditioning via action tokenization with local refinement and global modulation, plus a semantic branch for object-scene priors, significantly improving high-DoF action-conditioned video prediction on EgoDex and EgoVerse benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing world models compress entire action sequences into a single representation, which works for low-DoF but fails for high-DoF dexterous actions where heterogeneous components (spanning multiple scales) cause optimization imbalance and loss of fine-grained action fidelity.
AI.method: DexAC-WM introduces: (1) action tokenization to preserve dimension-level semantics instead of global compression, (2) local refinement and global modulation to align action signals with visual dynamics, and (3) a semantic branch providing object-scene priors for better high-level grounding.
AI.result: On EgoDex and EgoVerse, DexAC-WM achieves significant improvements in FID, FVD, and PCK over baselines, showing better visual-temporal realism and action-following consistency. The approach also generalizes to other backbones, demonstrating scalability.
AI.conclusion: Scaling world models to high-DoF control requires both structured action modeling (beyond uniform compression) and semantic grounding (for capturing dynamic visual details).

title: How Good Can Linear Models Be for Time-Series Forecasting?
summary: Time-series forecasting research has been moving steadily toward larger architectures, from specialized transformers to general-purpose foundation models, on the assumption that capacity is what unlocks accuracy. We take the opposite position: most of the gap can be closed at far lower cost by tuning preprocessing rather than scaling models. We use Ridge regression as the testbed, since it has a closed-form solution and interpretable weights, which let the optimal hyperparameters be read off the search directly. We search over context length, local normalization, regularization, and augmentation on eight standard benchmarks and find three patterns. (1) Optimal lookback is strongly series-specific and often non-monotonic in forecast horizon, with fitted power-law exponents ranging from $+0.46$ on ETTm2 to $-0.19$ on Exchange and Traffic, challenging the convention that longer horizons need longer history. (2) Normalizing over a learned trailing fraction of the context, rather than its entirety, is almost universally preferred. (3) Series within the same dataset often disagree on hyperparameters; the optimal degree of cross-series sharing varies from fully shared to fully per-series. The resulting models beat prior linear forecasters on most dataset-horizon entries and exceed Transformer, MLP, and CNN baselines on six of eight benchmarks. The optimized hyperparameters also serve as a diagnostic on the data itself, revealing structures that larger models absorb silently into their learned parameters.

AI.tldr: Tuning preprocessing (context length, normalization, regularization, augmentation) with Ridge regression outperforms larger architectures on time-series forecasting, revealing that optimal lookback is series-specific, normalization over a learned trailing fraction is preferred, and cross-series sharing varies per dataset.
AI.motivation: Challenge the prevailing assumption that larger model architectures (transformers, foundation models) are necessary for accurate time-series forecasting, proposing instead that most accuracy gains can be achieved at lower cost by optimizing preprocessing.
AI.method: Use Ridge regression with closed-form solution, searching over context length, local normalization, regularization, and augmentation across eight benchmarks; analyze hyperparameter patterns and cross-series sharing.
AI.result: Optimal lookback is series-specific with non-monotonic power-law exponents (+0.46 to -0.19); normalization over a learned trailing fraction is preferred; cross-series sharing varies; model beats linear forecasters and exceeds Transformer/MLP/CNN baselines on 6/8 benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Preprocessing optimization can rival or surpass large architecture approaches at lower cost, and the hyperparameters serve as data diagnostics revealing structures that larger models absorb implicitly.

title: RoPEMover: Depth-Aware Object Relocation via Positional Embeddings
summary: Moving an object in a single image requires geometry-consistent spatial rearrangement, including handling occlusions, revealing previously unseen regions, and maintaining coherent shadows and reflections. Existing approaches are not well suited to this setting and often fail to preserve such scene-level consistency. We address this problem by introducing a geometry-aware object motion method that operates directly on the positional representations of diffusion transformers. Our key insight is that rotary positional embeddings (RoPE) define a structured spatial field that can be explicitly manipulated to induce controlled motion. We extend 2D RoPE into a depth-aware formulation that encodes 3D spatial structure, enabling consistent object displacement and scene-aware updates. Our model is trained using synthetic data combined with a small set of real images via parameter-efficient fine-tuning. Despite minimal real supervision, it preserves object identity under large spatial displacements, generates plausible content in newly revealed regions, and consistently updates scene-dependent effects such as shadows and illumination. Experimental results on standard object motion benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art performance across all evaluation metrics.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a geometry-aware object motion method using depth-aware rotary positional embeddings in diffusion transformers to enable consistent object displacement, occlusion handling, and scene-level updates (shadows, reflections) from a single image.
AI.motivation: Existing single-image object motion methods fail to maintain geometry-consistent spatial rearrangement, including handling occlusions, revealing unseen regions, and preserving coherent shadows and reflections.
AI.method: The method operates on positional representations of diffusion transformers by extending 2D RoPE into a depth-aware 3D formulation that encodes spatial structure, enabling controlled object displacement and scene-aware updates. Training uses synthetic data +少量 real images with parameter-efficient fine-tuning.
AI.result: The approach preserves object identity under large displacements, generates plausible content in newly revealed regions, and consistently updates shadows/illumination effects. Achieves state-of-the-art performance on standard object motion benchmarks across all metrics.
AI.conclusion: Depth-aware RoPE in diffusion transformers enables geometry-consistent single-image object motion with minimal real supervision, outperforming existing methods.

title: Recovering Governing Equations from Solution Data: Identifiability Bounds for Linear and Nonlinear ODEs
summary: Learning governing equations from observed solution data is a fundamental challenge in scientific machine learning \cite{bruntonDiscoveringGoverningEquations2016,kovachkiNeuralOperatorLearning2023,longPDENetLearningPDEs2018,rudyDatadrivenDiscoveryPartial2017,raonicConvolutionalNeuralOperators2023}, yet the theoretical conditions under which a ground-truth ODE can be uniquely and stably identified from multiple solution observations remain largely undeveloped, and no quantitative analysis of the sample complexity of such learning tasks exists in the literature. To address this gap, we introduce the Hausdorff distance on solution sets as the natural metric for comparing differential equations, since it captures the worst-case separation between two equations over all admissible initial conditions and thus encodes the minimax structure of the identification problem. We establish identifiability bounds for governing ODEs across a wide class of structure equations--ranging from linear ODEs to nonlinear classes with Lipschitz (Hölder)-continuous vector fields--characterizing precisely when two distinct equations can be distinguished from solution data. Using this metric, we derive metric entropy estimates for the relevant ODE classes and analyze sample complexity bounds, quantifying how many solution observations are needed to reliably recover the governing equation.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Hausdorff distance on solution sets as a metric for comparing differential equations, establishes identifiability bounds for ODEs across linear and nonlinear classes, and derives metric entropy and sample complexity bounds for learning governing equations from solution observations.
AI.motivation: The theoretical conditions for uniquely and stably identifying a ground-truth ODE from multiple solution observations are largely undeveloped, and no quantitative sample complexity analysis exists in the literature.
AI.method: Introduces Hausdorff distance on solution sets to compare differential equations, establishing identifiability bounds for ODEs with Lipschitz (Hölder)-continuous vector fields, and derives metric entropy estimates and sample complexity bounds.
AI.result: Establishes precise conditions under which distinct equations can be distinguished from solution data, and provides quantitative sample complexity bounds for reliable recovery of governing equations.
AI.conclusion: The Hausdorff distance on solution sets is a natural minimax metric for ODE identification, enabling rigorous sample complexity analysis and identifiability guarantees across a wide class of structure equations.

title: SAM2Matting: Generalized Image and Video Matting
comment: ECCV 2026. Extended version. Project Page: https://henghuiding.com/SAM2Matting/
summary: Despite impressive advances in image matting, video matting remains challenging due to the inherent gap between high-level tracking, which requires frame-wise understanding, and low-level matting, which focuses on extremely fine-grained details. Existing methods attempt this with expensive and narrowly-scoped video matting datasets, which may limit out-of-domain generalization and compromise tracking robustness. We rethink the paradigm with SAM2Matting, a tracker-to-matting framework that advances VOS trackers to high-fidelity video matting. Specifically, it decouples the task by enhancing a foundational tracker (e.g., SAM2, SAM3) with a region-proposal bridge and dedicated matting heads, enabling the uncompromised tracker to handle temporal consistency while the matting components resolve fine-grained details. Notably, despite being trained only on images, SAM2Matting establishes new state-of-the-art performance on video matting, supports diverse prompt types, maintains strong temporal consistency, and demonstrates robust generalization across both human-centric and in-the-wild scenarios.

AI.tldr: SAM2Matting is a tracker-to-matting framework that enhances foundational trackers (SAM2/SAM3) with a region-proposal bridge and matting heads, achieving state-of-the-art video matting performance with only image-level training, supporting diverse prompts and robust generalization.
AI.motivation: Video matting is challenging due to the gap between high-level tracking (frame-wise understanding) and low-level matting (fine-grained details). Existing methods rely on expensive, narrowly-scoped video matting datasets that limit out-of-domain generalization and compromise tracking robustness.
AI.method: SAM2Matting decouples video matting into tracking and matting components: it enhances a foundational VOS tracker (SAM2/SAM3) with a region-proposal bridge and dedicated matting heads, allowing the tracker to handle temporal consistency while matting components resolve fine-grained details. The model is trained only on images.
AI.result: Establishes new state-of-the-art performance on video matting, supports diverse prompt types, maintains strong temporal consistency, and demonstrates robust generalization across both human-centric and in-the-wild scenarios, despite being trained only on images.
AI.conclusion: SAM2Matting effectively rethinks video matting by leveraging foundation trackers with image-only training, overcoming out-of-domain generalization limitations and achieving superior temporal consistency and fine-grained detail resolution.

title: Designing Reward Signals for Portable Query Generation: A Case Study in Industrial Semantic Job Search
comment: Accepted to KDD 2026 Workshop on AI Agent for Information Retrieval (Agent4IR)
summary:
Job-search platforms rely on low-bandwidth query interfaces that often fail to capture the high-dimensional complexity of candidate profiles. We present an end-to-end RLAIF (Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback) framework to generate \emph{portable} job search queries, terms that abstract away seeker-specific identifiers while preserving generalizable qualifications. This task introduces a highly adversarial reward surface where policy optimization frequently exploits flaws in LLM-as-judge rubrics, resulting in degenerate verbatim-copying behaviors.
  We conducted comprehensive empirical experiments to isolate the impact of optimization mechanics against structured reward engineering. Our results demonstrate that for critic-free optimizers, performance is overwhelmingly dictated by robust reward shaping, rendering the specific choice of algorithm largely immaterial. While critic-free per-rollout baseline methods (RLOO and REINFORCE++) natively resist reward-hacking, the group-relative advantage normalization in GRPO appears uniquely sensitive to spurious reward signals, making it disproportionately susceptible to exploitation. We show that introducing a deterministic, rule-based reward floor to correct for rewards assigned to verbatim copying mitigates this failure mode, resulting in a substantial $+0.147$ quality improvement on a cross-family evaluation judge. Ultimately, we show that the training-time reward model inflates performance gains by $2.4\times$, confirming that the training success is fundamentally dependent on enforcing reward-shaping disciplines rather than selecting alternative optimizers.

AI.tldr: An RLAIF framework for generating portable job-search queries reveals that robust reward shaping, not optimizer choice, is the primary driver of performance, and that GRPO is uniquely susceptible to reward-hacking; a rule-based reward floor mitigates this issue.
AI.motivation: Job-search platforms use low-bandwidth query interfaces that fail to capture the high-dimensional complexity of candidate profiles; existing methods struggle with adversarial reward surfaces in LLM-as-judge settings.
AI.method: End-to-end RLAIF framework generating portable job-search queries; comprehensive empirical study isolating optimization mechanics vs. structured reward engineering; introduction of deterministic rule-based reward floor.
AI.result: Critic-free optimizer choice is immaterial given robust reward shaping; GRPO is disproportionately susceptible to reward-hacking; rule-based reward floor yields +0.147 quality improvement; training-time reward model inflates gains by 2.4×.
AI.conclusion: Training success fundamentally depends on enforcing reward-shaping disciplines rather than selecting alternative optimizers.

title: RayPE: Ray-Space Positional Encoding for 3D-Aware Video Generation
summary: Modern video diffusion transformers position their tokens through RoPE on the (u,v,t) axes -- a description of the camera's sampling grid that says nothing about the 3D structure of the scene. We observe that the geometric relation between two camera rays is captured by the Plucker reciprocal product, which is bilinear in the two rays -- the same algebraic form as the dot product in Transformer attention. Building on this analogy, we propose RayPE, a positional-encoding extension that injects per-token 6D Plucker coordinates additively into the queries and keys of self-attention, with a query/key flip arrangement under which the symmetric identity configuration coincides exactly with the reciprocal product. The injection is additive, the resulting attention score decomposes into a content term, a geometry term, and two content and geometry cross-terms -- all of which our experiments find individually necessary. To make the encoding stable across video data with heterogeneous camera-translation scales (SfM, deep SLAM, metric), we further decouple ray direction from moment magnitude, gate the encoding by a learned function of the log-magnitude, and apply RMSNorm to align it with the QKNorm-normalized content branch. The full module adds less than 0.1% parameters to a pretrained video DiT, is zero-initialized to start from the pretrained weights, and improves camera controllability, cross-frame 3D consistency, and overall video quality on a four-dataset training mixture.

AI.tldr: RayPE injects 6D Plücker coordinates into video diffusion Transformer attention via additive positional encoding, leveraging the bilinear structure of the reciprocal product to improve 3D consistency and camera controllability with negligible parameter overhead.
AI.motivation: Standard video diffusion transformers use RoPE on (u,v,t) axes, which describes the camera sampling grid but ignores the 3D scene structure. The authors aim to inject explicit geometric information (ray geometry) into attention to improve 3D consistency and camera control.
AI.method: RayPE adds per-token 6D Plücker coordinates (ray direction + moment) into Q/K of self-attention with a query/key flip arrangement so the symmetric identity matches the reciprocal product. Attention scores become content + geometry + cross terms. To handle heterogeneous camera scales, they decouple direction from moment magnitude, gate via a learned function of log-magnitude, and apply RMSNorm. Zero-initialized, <0.1% parameter increase.
AI.result: Improves camera controllability, cross-frame 3D consistency, and overall video quality on a four-dataset training mixture. All four decomposed attention terms (content, geometry, two cross-terms) are individually necessary.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly encoding Plücker ray geometry into attention is a lightweight (<0.1% params), effective way to inject 3D structure into video diffusion Transformers without breaking pretrained weights.

title: A Multi-Fidelity Convolutional Autoencoder-Transfer Learning Framework for Guided-Wave-Based Damage Diagnosis Using Large Simulated and Limited Experimental Datasets
summary: Guided wave-based structural health monitoring (GWSHM) with onboard transducers offers significant potential for the early diagnosis of damage in engineering structures. However, the practical deployment of deep learning models is often hindered by the limited availability of labelled experimental data and the high computational cost of generating large-scale high-fidelity simulation datasets. This study presents a multifidelity transfer learning framework that integrates lightweight physics-based simulations, convolutional autoencoder (CAE)-based deep feature learning, a feed-forward neural network, and limited experimental measurements for accurate damage localisation and sizing in plate-like structures instrumented with piezoelectric transducers. A computationally efficient one-dimensional time-domain spectral element model is employed to generate a large synthetic dataset for pretraining, while transfer learning adapts the model to experimental domains using only a small amount of labelled data. The CAE-based transfer learning framework significantly outperforms its CNN-based counterpart in damage localisation accuracy. The model achieves excellent predictive performance with $R^2$ scores exceeding 0.93 for damage localisation and 0.99 for damage sizing. Its generalisation capability is demonstrated on previously unseen data, showing high prediction accuracy for damage scenarios not represented during pretraining or fine-tuning. The results establish the proposed framework as an accurate, computationally efficient, and practically viable solution for real-world GWSHM applications.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a multifidelity transfer learning framework that combines lightweight physics-based simulations (spectral element model), convolutional autoencoder (CAE) deep feature learning, and limited experimental data to achieve accurate damage localization and sizing in plate-like structures for guided wave-based structural health monitoring.
AI.motivation: The practical deployment of deep learning models in GWSHM is hindered by the limited availability of labelled experimental data and the high computational cost of generating large-scale high-fidelity simulation datasets.
AI.method: A computationally efficient 1D time-domain spectral element model generates a large synthetic dataset for pretraining; transfer learning then adapts the model to experimental domains using a small amount of labelled data. The framework integrates convolutional autoencoder (CAE)-based deep feature learning with a feed-forward neural network for damage localization and sizing.
AI.result: The CAE-based transfer learning framework outperforms its CNN-based counterpart in damage localization accuracy. The model achieves R² scores exceeding 0.93 for damage localization and 0.99 for damage sizing. It demonstrates strong generalization on previously unseen damage scenarios not represented during pretraining or fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multifidelity transfer learning framework is an accurate, computationally efficient, and practically viable solution for real-world GWSHM applications.

title: PhysiFormer: Learning to Simulate Mechanics in World Space
summary: We present PhysiFormer, a diffusion transformer for physically-plausible 3D object motion. Unlike video world models that operate in view-dependent pixel space, PhysiFormer represents objects as 3D meshes expressed in world coordinates. Given the initial vertex positions and velocities, as well as object material type, rigid or elastic, the model samples future vertex trajectories. While related neural physics approaches build on ad-hoc latent spaces or explicitly enforce rigidity and causality, PhysiFormer shows that excellent results can be obtained without any such inductive biases, by casting vertex trajectory prediction as a single denoising diffusion process directly in world coordinates. The probabilistic formulation captures uncertainty in the learned dynamics, enabling diverse plausible futures from initial conditions, making this framework potentially useful for applications with unobserved uncertainty. The model features attention factorised over time, space, and objects for efficiency, enabling permutation-invariant multi-object reasoning without needing explicit object encoding. Trained on over 100k simulated trajectories, PhysiFormer generates rigid and elastic mechanics, and generalises to mixed-material settings, unseen real-world geometries, and larger object counts. It substantially outperforms autoregressive baselines in trajectory accuracy, rigidity preservation, and momentum-based physical consistency. Our results position coordinate-space diffusion as a promising step toward view-invariant, geometry-aware world modelling for robotics, graphics, and physical design. Visualisations, code, and models are available at https://yimingc9.github.io/physiformer.

AI.tldr: PhysiFormer is a diffusion transformer that directly predicts 3D vertex trajectories of rigid and elastic objects in world coordinates via denoising diffusion, without explicit inductive biases like rigidity enforcement, achieving state-of-the-art physical plausibility and generalization.
AI.motivation: Existing video world models operate in view-dependent pixel space and lack physical plausibility; neural physics approaches rely on ad-hoc latent spaces or explicitly enforce rigidity/causality. The paper aims to show that simple coordinate-space diffusion can outperform these heavily engineered baselines.
AI.method: PhysiFormer takes initial vertex positions, velocities, and material type as input, and casts future vertex trajectory prediction as a single denoising diffusion process directly in world coordinates. It uses factorized attention over time, space, and objects for efficiency and permutation-invariant multi-object reasoning, without explicit object encoding or rigidity constraints.
AI.result: Trained on 100k+ simulated trajectories, PhysiFormer generates both rigid and elastic mechanics, generalizes to mixed-material settings, unseen real-world geometries, and larger object counts. It substantially outperforms autoregressive baselines in trajectory accuracy, rigidity preservation, and momentum-based physical consistency.
AI.conclusion: Coordinate-space diffusion is a promising step toward view-invariant, geometry-aware world modeling for robotics, graphics, and physical design.

title: Blackwell Approachability and Gradient Equilibrium are Equivalent
comment: 30 pages, 1 figure, accepted for presentation at COLT 2026
summary: Gradient equilibrium (GEQ) is a recently introduced online optimization framework that generalizes first-order stationarity from offline optimization and abstracts problems like online conformal prediction. While GEQ has curious similarities with known online learning frameworks, namely regret minimization, prior work has shown that GEQ error and regret are incomparable objectives, leaving open a precise understanding of how GEQ fits into the broader online learning landscape. In this work, we show that GEQ is equivalent to Blackwell approachability in the algorithmic sense. That is, a Blackwell approachability problem can always be solved using queries to a black-box GEQ oracle, with no asymptotic loss in the oracle's error rate, and vice versa. Taken together with known equivalences between approachability, regret minimization, and calibration, these results imply that GEQ is equivalent to these frameworks, as well. Our reductions are efficient and can be used to transfer refined guarantees, such as optimism and strong adaptivity, from regret minimization to GEQ. Along the way, we also identify necessary and sufficient conditions for GEQ, and establish reductions between different notions of GEQ with unconstrained and constrained decision sets.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that Gradient Equilibrium (GEQ) is algorithmically equivalent to Blackwell approachability, and through known connections, equivalent to regret minimization and calibration frameworks. It also provides efficient reductions, identifies necessary and sufficient conditions for GEQ, and establishes relationships between constrained and unconstrained GEQ variants.
AI.motivation: Prior work showed GEQ error and regret are incomparable objectives, leaving an unclear understanding of GEQ's position in online learning. This work aims to precisely characterize GEQ's relationship to established online learning frameworks.
AI.method: The authors establish bidirectional algorithmic reductions between GEQ and Blackwell approachability (no asymptotic loss in error rates), and leverage known equivalences between approachability, regret minimization, and calibration. They also analyze necessary and sufficient conditions and reductions between constrained/unconstrained GEQ.
AI.result: GEQ is equivalent to Blackwell approachability, regret minimization, and calibration. The reductions are efficient and transfer refined guarantees (optimism, strong adaptivity) from regret minimization to GEQ. Necessary and sufficient conditions for GEQ are identified.
AI.conclusion: GEQ fits squarely into the established online learning landscape, being equivalent to major frameworks like regret minimization and calibration via Blackwell approachability, resolving the previously open question of its relationship to these frameworks.

title: Don't Settle at the Mode! Mitigating Diversity Collapse in Pretrained Flow Models via Feature Self-Guidance
summary: State-of-the-art flow models generate stunning images from text or image prompts. However, they suffer from diversity collapse when generating multiple samples under the same conditioning. Existing methods address this issue via either latent guidance, which has limited effectiveness, or sample selection, which relies on external reward models that incur significant inference-time overhead. In this work, we introduce an efficient, training-free self-guidance mechanism to mitigate diversity collapse without requiring additional reward models. Specifically, we disperse the internal features of the flow model during batch generation with feature self-guidance. Further, to keep the features close to the manifold, we introduce a manifold regularization step that projects these dispersed features back onto the data manifold, ensuring diverse generation without sacrificing alignment with the input conditions. Our method integrates seamlessly as a plug-and-play module into pretrained flow models, adding only a marginal inference cost. Experiments demonstrate significant improvements in diversity while preserving fidelity across several conditional flow models, including multi-step and few-step text-to-image, depth-to-image, and reference image generation.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a training-free self-guidance mechanism with manifold regularization to mitigate diversity collapse in flow models during multi-sample generation, achieving diverse outputs without extra reward models or significant inference overhead.
AI.motivation: State-of-the-art flow models suffer from diversity collapse when generating multiple samples under the same conditioning, and existing solutions (latent guidance or sample selection with reward models) have limited effectiveness or incur high inference cost.
AI.method: The method disperses internal features of the flow model during batch generation via feature self-guidance, then applies manifold regularization to project dispersed features back onto the data manifold, ensuring diversity without sacrificing alignment. It is a plug-and-play, training-free module.
AI.result: Significant improvements in diversity while preserving fidelity across various conditional flow models (multi-step and few-step text-to-image, depth-to-image, and reference image generation), with only marginal inference cost added.
AI.conclusion: The proposed self-guidance mechanism effectively mitigates diversity collapse in flow models efficiently and without external reward models, offering a practical plug-and-play solution.

title: Beyond the Hard Budget: Sparsity Regularizers for More Interpretable Top-k Sparse Autoencoders
summary: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) have become a leading tool for interpreting the representations of vision foundation models, decomposing their polysemantic activations into a larger set of sparse, more monosemantic features. The Top-$k$ SAE, a now-standard variant, enforces sparsity architecturally through its activation function, retaining only the $k$ most active latents per input. Because it was designed precisely to avoid the $\ell_1$ penalty used by earlier SAEs and its known drawbacks, it has not been combined with an explicit sparsity regularizer, despite retaining limitations of its own, such as a budget $k$ that is fixed regardless of input complexity and a tendency to overfit to the training value of $k$. We introduce two sparsity regularizers compatible with the Top-$k$ architecture, both acting on the activations before the Top-$k$ selection: an $\ell_1$ penalty on the unselected (off-support) units, and a scale-invariant $\ell_1/\ell_2$-ratio penalty that concentrates the code onto fewer effective units. Both penalties are applied only to the batch-active units, those selected by the Top-$k$ operator at least once within the batch. Across two datasets, three vision foundation models, and a range of $k$, both regularizers consistently improve monosemanticity at no cost to reconstruction quality. The $\ell_1/\ell_2$ penalty further concentrates information into fewer latents, making reconstruction more robust to the inference-time choice of $k$ and improving small-budget linear probing. Our central finding is that hard architectural sparsity and soft sparsity regularization are complementary rather than mutually exclusive.

AI.tldr: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) with Top-k activation function can be improved by adding sparsity regularizers (ℓ1 on off-support units and ℓ1/ℓ2 ratio penalty) before the Top-k selection, which enhance monosemanticity without sacrificing reconstruction quality.
AI.motivation: Top-k SAEs avoid the ℓ1 penalty used in earlier SAEs due to its known drawbacks, but they have limitations: a fixed budget k regardless of input complexity and tendency to overfit to the training value of k. The authors aim to address these limitations while preserving the benefits of the Top-k architecture.
AI.method: Two sparsity regularizers compatible with Top-k SAEs: (1) ℓ1 penalty on unselected (off-support) units, and (2) scale-invariant ℓ1/ℓ2-ratio penalty to concentrate code onto fewer effective units. Both applied only to batch-active units. Evaluated across two datasets, three vision foundation models, and a range of k values.
AI.result: Both regularizers consistently improve monosemanticity at no cost to reconstruction quality. The ℓ1/ℓ2 penalty further concentrates information into fewer latents, makes reconstruction more robust to inference-time choice of k, and improves small-budget linear probing.
AI.conclusion: Hard architectural sparsity (Top-k) and soft sparsity regularization are complementary rather than mutually exclusive, demonstrating that combining both approaches yields better interpretability and robustness.

title: DnA: Denoising Attention for Visual Tasks
summary: The softmax activation in multihead attention (MHA) is the de facto standard for attention-based models in visual perception tasks. However, standard softmax can produce noisy attention patterns that dilute relevant features and degrade its performance. In this paper, we propose Denoising Attention or DnA, in which, first, a positive query identifies which image features belong to the correct class, and a negative query identifies closely associated but irrelevant image features. DnA then projects these interactions into two distinct subspaces with larger principal angles, promoting subspace separation and improved discriminability. Using a ViT-B backbone, our proposed DnA achieves an absolute gain of 0.8% on ImageNet-1K compared to the baseline. We further show improvements across multiple visual understanding tasks, including video understanding with video transformers (1.8%) and video LLMs (0.5%). Our extensive empirical analyses justify the design choices involving two interacting subspaces and the denoising effect of DnA.

AI.tldr: DnA replaces standard softmax attention with positive/negative queries and subspace separation to reduce noise and improve discriminability, achieving gains of 0.8% on ImageNet-1K, 1.8% on video tasks, and 0.5% on video LLMs.
AI.motivation: Standard softmax attention in MHA produces noisy patterns that dilute relevant features, degrading performance in visual perception tasks.
AI.method: DnA uses a positive query to identify correct-class features and a negative query to identify irrelevant but closely associated features, then projects them into two separated subspaces with larger principal angles to enhance discriminability.
AI.result: 0.8% absolute gain on ImageNet-1K with ViT-B, 1.8% improvement on video understanding with video transformers, and 0.5% improvement on video LLMs.
AI.conclusion: DnA's dual-subspace denoising design effectively reduces attention noise and improves performance across multiple visual understanding tasks.

title: Hallucination in World Models is Predictable and Preventable
comment: Interactive paper, live demo, code, dataset, and models: https://www.nicklashansen.com/mmbench2
summary:
Modern generative world models render increasingly realistic action-controllable futures, yet they frequently hallucinate: rollouts remain visually fluent while drifting from the ground-truth dynamics. We hypothesize that hallucination concentrates in low-coverage regions of the state-action space, where lightweight data-centric signals can both detect it and guide mitigation. To test this, we introduce MMBench2, a 427-hour, 210-task dataset for visual world modeling with ground-truth actions, rewards, and live simulators, and train a 350M-parameter world model on it. We identify three distinct hallucination modes: perceptual, action-marginalized, and scene-diverging -- each anchored to a different stage of the pipeline, and develop three signals that accurately predict where the model will fail. To close coverage gaps at training time, we develop a coverage-aware sampling technique; to close them online, our hallucination predictors serve as curiosity rewards for targeted data collection, yielding a data-efficient finetuning recipe that adapts the pretrained world model to entirely unseen environments with as few as 50 real environment trajectories. Overall, our findings reveal that hallucination in world models is inherently a data coverage issue, and that the same signals used to detect it can also be used for mitigation.
  An interactive web version of our paper is available at https://www.nicklashansen.com/mmbench2

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MMBench2, a large dataset for world modeling, identifies three hallucination modes in generative world models tied to low data coverage, and develops coverage-aware sampling and curiosity-driven data collection to detect and mitigate hallucinations, enabling efficient adaptation to unseen environments with minimal data.
AI.motivation: Generative world models hallucinate—producing visually fluent but dynamically incorrect rollouts. The authors hypothesize this stems from low-coverage regions of the state-action space, and seek lightweight data-centric signals to detect and mitigate such failures.
AI.method: The authors introduce MMBench2 (427h, 210 tasks) with ground-truth actions/rewards/simulators, train a 350M-parameter world model, identify three hallucination modes (perceptual, action-marginalized, scene-diverging), develop predictive signals for each, and propose coverage-aware sampling (training-time) plus curiosity-reward-based targeted data collection (online) to close coverage gaps.
AI.result: The hallucination predictors accurately forecast model failures. Coverage-aware sampling and curiosity-driven finetuning enable adaptation to entirely unseen environments with as few as 50 real environment trajectories.
AI.conclusion: Hallucination in world models is fundamentally a data coverage issue, and the same signals used to detect it can also drive effective mitigation strategies.

title: Paying More Attention to Visual Tokens in Self-Evolving Large Multimodal Models
summary: Recently, self-evolving large multimodal models (LMMs) have received attention for improving visual reasoning in a purely unsupervised setting. However, multi-role self-play and self-consistency reward schemes in existing self-evolving LMMs optimize answer agreement without ensuring the decoder attends to visual content, relying instead on statistical language priors to produce self consistent outputs. This leads to a persistent failure mode we term visual under-conditioning, where the decoder relies on language priors rather than the image during generation, manifesting as insufficient attention to visual tokens. As a result, current self-evolving LMMs struggle on vision--language understanding tasks such as image captioning and visual question answering. To address this, we propose VISE (Visual Invariance Self-Evolution), a purely unsupervised self-evolving framework that directly regularizes the model's visual conditioning policy through two complementary invariance-based rewards: a geometric invariance reward that enforces spatial consistency under known transformations, and a semantic invariance reward that penalizes evidence-agnostic generation by requiring the model to recognize the absence of evidence when predicted regions are perturbed. VISE operates within a single model without specialist roles, external reward models, or annotations, and is trained on raw unlabeled images. Experiments on 18 benchmarks demonstrate the efficacy of our approach. Using Qwen3-VL-2B as the base model, VISE achieves gains of $+16.85$ CIDEr on COCO and $+19.66$ CIDEr on TextCaps, reduces object hallucination by $5.0$ Chair-I points, and generalizes across four model families and scales. Our code and models are available at https://mbzuai-oryx.github.io/VISE

AI.tldr: VISE is an unsupervised self-evolving framework that uses geometric and semantic invariance rewards to address visual under-conditioning in LMMs, improving visual reasoning on 18 benchmarks without external models or annotations.
AI.motivation: Existing self-evolving LMMs suffer from visual under-conditioning, where decoders rely on language priors rather than visual content due to answer agreement optimization without proper visual attention regularization, leading to poor performance on vision-language tasks.
AI.method: VISE introduces two complementary invariance-based rewards: (1) geometric invariance reward for spatial consistency under known transformations, and (2) semantic invariance reward penalizing evidence-agnostic generation when predicted regions are perturbed. It operates within a single model without specialist roles, external reward models, or annotations.
AI.result: On 18 benchmarks with Qwen3-VL-2B: +16.85 CIDEr on COCO, +19.66 CIDEr on TextCaps, 5.0 Chair-I reduction in object hallucination, generalizing across four model families and scales.
AI.conclusion: VISE effectively addresses visual under-conditioning in self-evolving LMMs by directly regularizing visual conditioning through invariance rewards, achieving strong unsupervised improvements across diverse vision-language tasks and model architectures.

title: Error-Conditioned Neural Solvers
summary: Neural surrogate models offer fast approximate mappings from PDE parameters to solutions, but they typically treat solving as a purely statistical task: once trained, they struggle to correct their own constraint violations and extrapolate beyond the training distribution. Recent hybrid methods promote physical correctness by targeting the PDE residual via gradient descent or Gauss--Newton steps, but inherit the compute cost and instability of the underlying classical optimizers. We show, theoretically and empirically, that numerically minimizing the PDE residual can be an unreliable proxy for reconstruction accuracy in ill-conditioned systems, explaining why these methods often do not make accurate predictions despite achieving low residuals. We propose error-conditioned Neural Solvers (ENS), built on a different principle: rather than an optimization target, the PDE residual field is passed as a direct input to the network at each iteration, enabling it to read the spatial structure of its own errors and learn an update policy to iteratively correct its predictions. Across four PDE families, ENS attains the highest prediction accuracy in the large majority of settings, with gains reaching $10\times$ on turbulent Kolmogorov flow, while avoiding the expensive compute cost of hybrid methods. ENS's learned correction policy generalizes under distribution shift, including zero-shot parameter changes and cross-equation transfer, where its relative advantage is largest in the ill-conditioned regimes where residual minimization is least reliable. Project website: https://neuralsolver.github.io/.

AI.tldr: ENS is a neural surrogate model that uses PDE residual fields as direct input to iteratively correct predictions, achieving higher accuracy than hybrid methods that minimize residuals as optimization targets, especially in ill-conditioned regimes and under distribution shift, while avoiding expensive compute costs.
AI.motivation: Existing neural surrogate models treat PDE solving as purely statistical tasks and cannot correct constraint violations or extrapolate. Hybrid methods that minimize PDE residuals inherit the compute cost and instability of classical optimizers, and residual minimization is shown to be an unreliable proxy for reconstruction accuracy in ill-conditioned systems.
AI.method: Error-conditioned Neural Solvers (ENS) pass the PDE residual field as a direct input to the network at each iteration, enabling it to read the spatial structure of its own errors and learn an update policy for iterative correction.
AI.result: Across four PDE families, ENS achieves the highest prediction accuracy in most settings, with gains up to 10x on turbulent Kolmogorov flow, while avoiding the expensive compute cost of hybrid methods. It generalizes under distribution shift including zero-shot parameter changes and cross-equation transfer, with largest advantages in ill-conditioned regimes.
AI.conclusion: ENS demonstrates that using the residual as input rather than an optimization target leads to more accurate and generalizable neural PDE solvers, particularly in ill-conditioned systems where residual minimization is unreliable.

title: Ask, Solve, Generate: Self-Evolving Unified Multimodal Understanding and Generation via Self-Consistency Rewards
summary: Most unified large multimodal models (LMMs) that support both visual understanding and image generation still rely on curated post-training supervision, such as human annotations, preference labels, or external reward models. We ask whether a unified LMM can improve both abilities autonomously using only unlabeled images. We propose a self-evolving training framework with three internal roles: a Proposer that generates visual questions, a Solver that answers and evaluates them, and a Generator that synthesizes images. Training uses only self-derived consistency signals, without human annotations, preference labels, or task-trained external reward/judge models. To stabilize learning, we introduce Solver Token Entropy (STE), a continuous difficulty signal based on token-level prediction uncertainty that remains useful even when sample-level consistency becomes unreliable. For image generation, we design a multi-scale internal evaluation scheme that combines question-answer fidelity scoring with cycle-consistent captioning. This creates a solver-mediated coupling, where better visual understanding enables more reliable generation assessment and stronger internal training signals. The framework preserves the same role decomposition, reward logic, and training schedule across diffusion-based BLIP3o, rectified-flow BAGEL, and autoregressive VARGPT-v1.1 architectures, requiring only each backbone's native prompting and generation interface. Across eight understanding metrics, our method consistently improves over the corresponding base models. On BAGEL, it achieves a $+3.5\%$ absolute gain on MMMU and improves GenEval image generation performance from $82\%$ to $85\%$. Code and models are publicly released.

AI.tldr: A self-evolving framework using only unlabeled images enables unified LMMs to improve both visual understanding and image generation through internal role decomposition (Proposer, Solver, Generator), with Solver Token Entropy for stable learning, achieving consistent gains across multiple architectures without external supervision.
AI.motivation: Existing unified LMMs rely on curated post-training supervision (human annotations, preference labels, external reward models) for improving both visual understanding and image generation. The goal is to enable a unified LMM to improve both abilities autonomously using only unlabeled images.
AI.method: A self-evolving training framework with three internal roles: Proposer (generates visual questions), Solver (answers and evaluates them), and Generator (synthesizes images). Training uses self-derived consistency signals without external supervision. Introduces Solver Token Entropy (STE) for token-level uncertainty-based difficulty weighting. Uses multi-scale internal evaluation for image generation combining QA fidelity scoring and cycle-consistent captioning. Applied to BLIP3o, BAGEL, and VARGPT-v1.1 architectures.
AI.result: Consistent improvement over base models across eight understanding metrics. On BAGEL: +3.5% absolute gain on MMMU; GenEval image generation from 82% to 85%.
AI.conclusion: A self-evolving training framework using only unlabeled images can improve both visual understanding and image generation in unified LMMs across multiple architectures, with consistent gains on benchmarks like MMMU (+3.5%) and GenEval (82%→85%).

title: Autoregressive Boltzmann Generators
summary: Efficient sampling of molecular systems at thermodynamic equilibrium is a hallmark challenge in statistical physics. This challenge has driven the development of Boltzmann Generators (BGs), which allow rapid generation of uncorrelated equilibrium samples by combining a generative model with exact likelihoods and an importance sampling correction. However, modern BGs predominantly rely on normalizing flows (NFs), which either suffer from limited expressivity due to strict invertibility constraints (discrete time) or computationally expensive likelihoods (continuous time). In this paper, we propose Autoregressive Boltzmann Generators (ArBG) -- a novel autoregressive modelling framework -- that overcomes these limitations by departing from the flow-based BG paradigm. ArBG circumvents the topological constraints of flows and enables sequential inference-time interventions, while offering enhanced scalability by leveraging architectures effective in Large Language Models. We empirically demonstrate that ArBG leads to significant improvements over flow-based models across all benchmarks, but particularly in larger peptide systems such as the 10-residue Chignolin. Furthermore, we introduce Robin, a 132 million parameter transferable model trained with the ArBG framework which improves over the previous state-of-the-art, reducing the zero-shot energy error, E-W$_2$, on 8-residue systems by over 60$\%$. The code can be found at the following link: https://github.com/danyalrehman/autobg.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Autoregressive Boltzmann Generators (ArBG), a novel autoregressive framework that outperforms flow-based Boltzmann Generators for molecular equilibrium sampling, demonstrating significant improvements on peptide benchmarks and introducing a 132M parameter transferable model (Robin) that reduces zero-shot energy error by over 60%.
AI.motivation: Efficient sampling of molecular systems at thermodynamic equilibrium is a key challenge in statistical physics. Existing Boltzmann Generators rely on normalizing flows, which suffer from limited expressivity due to invertibility constraints (discrete time) or computationally expensive likelihoods (continuous time).
