AI.method: Proposes Autoregressive Boltzmann Generators (ArBG), an autoregressive modeling framework that departs from the flow-based paradigm, circumventing topological constraints of flows and enabling sequential inference-time interventions with enhanced scalability by leveraging architectures effective in Large Language Models.
AI.result: ArBG achieves significant improvements over flow-based models across all benchmarks, particularly in larger peptide systems like the 10-residue Chignolin. The Robin model (132M parameters) reduces zero-shot energy error (E-W$_2$) on 8-residue systems by over 60% compared to the previous state-of-the-art.
AI.conclusion: The autoregressive approach offers a promising alternative to flow-based Boltzmann Generators, overcoming their fundamental limitations and achieving state-of-the-art performance in molecular equilibrium sampling.

title: DanceOPD: On-Policy Generative Field Distillation
comment: Technical Report; 39 pages, 13 figures, 9 tables; Project Page at https://danceopd.github.io/
summary: Modern image generation demands a single model that unifies diverse capabilities, including text-to-image (T2I), local editing, and global editing. However, these capabilities are rarely naturally aligned and often conflict. For instance, editing tends to degrade T2I performance, while global and local editing interfere with each other. Consequently, effectively composing these capabilities has become a central challenge for image generation model training. To tackle this, we introduce DanceOPD, an on-policy generative field distillation framework for flow-matching models that routes each sample to one capability field, queries one low-noise student-induced state, and trains with a simple velocity MSE objective. With each capability source defined as a velocity field over the shared flow state space, the student learns from fields queried on its own rollout states to compose expert capabilities. This formulation also absorbs operator-defined fields such as classifier-free guidance. Comprehensive experiments on T2I, editing, realism-field absorption, and CFG absorption show that our approach improves multi-capability composition, strengthening target capabilities while preserving anchor generation quality. We believe this work establishes a practical route for generative field distillation in flow-matching models.

AI.tldr: DanceOPD is an on-policy generative field distillation framework for flow-matching models that composes multiple image generation capabilities (T2I, local/global editing) by routing each sample to one capability field, querying low-noise student states, and training with velocity MSE, improving multi-capability composition while preserving anchor generation quality.
AI.motivation: Modern image generation requires a single model unifying T2I, local editing, and global editing, but these capabilities naturally conflict (e.g., editing degrades T2I, global/local editing interfere). Effectively composing these capabilities is a central challenge.
AI.method: DanceOPD: an on-policy generative field distillation framework for flow-matching models. It routes each sample to one capability field, queries one low-noise student-induced state, and trains with a simple velocity MSE objective. Each capability source is defined as a velocity field over the shared flow state space; the student learns from fields queried on its own rollout states to compose expert capabilities, also absorbing operator-defined fields like classifier-free guidance.
AI.result: Experiments on T2I, editing, realism-field absorption, and CFG absorption show the approach improves multi-capability composition, strengthening target capabilities while preserving anchor generation quality.
AI.conclusion: DanceOPD establishes a practical route for generative field distillation in flow-matching models, effectively composing multiple conflicting image generation capabilities.

title: Reinforcement Learning without Ground-Truth Solutions can Improve LLMs
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) for training LLMs typically rely on ground-truth answers to assign rewards, limiting their applicability to tasks where the ground-truth solution is unknown. We introduce a \textbf{R}anking-\textbf{i}nduced \textbf{VER}ifiable framework (RiVER) that trains LLMs on score-based optimization tasks without ground-truth solutions, using deterministic execution feedback as continuous-valued supervision. When applying group-relative RL to such continuous rewards, we identify two key challenges: \emph{scale dominance}, where uncalibrated score magnitudes across test instances distort policy updates, and \emph{frequency dominance}, where repeatedly sampled suboptimal solutions can outweigh rare but stronger candidates. RiVER addresses these challenges with calibrated reward shaping that uses instance-wise comparisons and emphasizes top-ranked solvers while retaining bounded feedback for other valid solutions. We train on 12 AtCoder Heuristic Contest tasks and evaluate on Algorithm Engineering Benchmark (ALE-Bench), LiveCodeBench, and USACO. RiVER advances Qwen3-8B and GLM-Z1-9B-0414 by 8.9\% and 9.4\% in ALE rating rank. More importantly, despite training exclusively on score-based tasks without any ground-truth solutions, RiVER also improves the backbones across exact-solution benchmarks such as LiveCodeBench and USACO by an absolute average improvement of 2.4\% and 3.5\%. By contrast, baselines trained with raw execution scores improve ALE rating but fail to transfer to exact-solution benchmarks. These results suggest that score-based optimization tasks, combined with proper reward calibration, can serve as effective training environments for general coding ability without ground-truth solutions.

AI.tldr: RiVER trains LLMs on score-based optimization tasks without ground-truth solutions, using calibrated reward shaping to overcome scale and frequency dominance. It improves performance on both score-based and exact-solution coding benchmarks, outperforming baselines trained with raw scores.
AI.motivation: Existing RLVR methods rely on ground-truth answers for reward assignment, limiting applicability to tasks where correct solutions are unknown. The authors aim to train LLMs using score-based (continuous) feedback from heuristic optimization tasks (e.g., AtCoder) without requiring ground-truth solutions.
AI.method: RiVER introduces a ranking-induced verifiable framework that (1) uses deterministic execution feedback as continuous-valued supervision, (2) addresses scale dominance (uncalibrated score magnitudes) and frequency dominance (repeated suboptimal solutions outweighing rare strong ones) via calibrated reward shaping with instance-wise comparisons, and (3) emphasizes top-ranked solvers while retaining bounded feedback for other valid solutions.
AI.result: RiVER improves Qwen3-8B and GLM-Z1-9B-0414 by 8.9% and 9.4% in ALE rating rank. It also improves exact-solution benchmarks (LiveCodeBench +2.4%, USACO +3.5% absolute average), whereas baselines trained with raw scores only improve ALE rating without transferring.
AI.conclusion: Score-based optimization tasks, combined with proper reward calibration, can serve as effective training environments for general coding ability even without ground-truth solutions, enabling transfer to exact-solution benchmarks.
